topics.title: Quality Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Quality Planning** is the foundational activity in the **Juran Trilogy** of Total Quality Management (TQM). It is defined as the structured process of establishing quality goals and identifying the customers — both internal and external — along with their stated and unstated needs, translating those needs into measurable product, service, and process specifications, and developing the operational processes required to deliver those specifications consistently.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, Quality Planning is the *first and most strategic* pillar of TQM, sitting upstream of Quality Control and Quality Improvement. It answers the question: **"What must we do to satisfy our customers and meet our quality objectives before production even begins?"**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Quality Planning is NOT about inspecting defects after they occur. It is a *pre-emptive, design-stage* activity focused on **building quality in** rather than inspecting it in. This is the philosophical core of Deming's "System Optimization" and Juran's "Fitness for Use."

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference — Juran's Trilogy (Joseph M. Juran):**
> 1. **Quality Planning** → Identify customers → Determine needs → Develop products → Establish goals → Prove capability.
> 2. **Quality Control** → Evaluate actual performance → Compare to goals → Act on the difference.
> 3. **Quality Improvement** → Establish infrastructure → Identify improvement projects → Establish project teams.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are building a **custom house**.

Before laying a single brick, you don't just start digging. You sit down with an architect, understand what the family needs (number of rooms, sunlight direction, budget, future expansion plans), draw a **blueprint**, decide on the **materials**, estimate the **cost of quality** (better cement vs cheaper cement), and then hand the design to engineers to *prove the design works* (soil testing, structural analysis).

That entire *preparation stage* — understanding the family (customer), planning the layout (design), and proving the design works (process capability) — is exactly what **Quality Planning** does in an organization.

**The analogy mapping:**
- Family (Homeowner) = **Customer** (End user)
- Needs & Lifestyle = **Customer Requirements (Voice of Customer - VOC)**
- Blueprint = **Product/Service Design**
- Soil Test = **Process Capability Verification**
- Construction = **Production**
- Handover = **Delivery to Customer**

If Quality Planning is skipped, you build a house with a leaking roof, weak foundation, and a kitchen that faces the wrong direction — only to discover all these issues *after* the house is built. Similarly, poor Quality Planning means defects are discovered *after* the product reaches the customer.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

While Quality Planning is largely a management discipline (not a physical science), it uses several **standard performance metrics** that must be remembered for KTU examinations:

- **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** — Standard quality metric (Six Sigma level: **3.4 DPMO**)
- **Cp (Process Capability Index)** — A dimensionless ratio measuring process spread vs specification width
- **Cpk (Process Capability with Centering)** — A dimensionless ratio accounting for off-center processes
- **ROL (Return on Quality Investment)** — A financial ratio measuring payback from quality investments
- **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** — Used in FMEA, $RPN = S \times O \times D$
- **CTQ (Critical to Quality)** — Customer-facing quality characteristics

> [!NOTE]
> **Vocabulary Alert for KTU Students:**
> Frequently confused terms — make sure you know the difference:
> - **Quality of Design** vs **Quality of Conformance**: Design is *what* you plan; Conformance is *how well* you execute.
> - **Customer Satisfaction vs Customer Delight**: Satisfaction = meets expectations; Delight = exceeds expectations (Kano Model).
> - **Internal Customer** = next process in the workflow; **External Customer** = end user outside the organization.

---

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Six Universal Steps of Quality Planning (Juran's Roadmap)

Juran formalized Quality Planning into a **six-step sequential process** that is universally cited in KTU 2024 TQM textbooks. Every step has a deliverable and a logical purpose.

### Step 1 — Identify the Customers
- **Why?** You cannot satisfy someone you haven't defined.
- **How?** Segment internal and external customers. External = paying users; Internal = downstream departments who receive your output.
- **Deliverable:** A documented **Customer Map** (who receives your product/service?).

### Step 2 — Determine Customer Needs (Voice of Customer — VOC)
- **Why?** Customers often *don't know what they want* or *cannot articulate it technically*.
- **How?** Use VOC tools — interviews, focus groups, surveys, complaint logs, Kano model questionnaires, market research.
- **Deliverable:** A **List of Customer Needs** (stated, unstated, and delight needs).

### Step 3 — Translate Needs into Measurable Specifications
- **Why?** "Durable shoes" is a need; "heel can withstand 10,000 flex cycles" is a specification.
- **How?** Convert VOC into design specs using **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** — the "House of Quality" matrix.
- **Deliverable:** **Product/Service Specifications** (measurable, testable units).

### Step 4 — Develop Product/Service Features
- **Why?** Features are the *design choices* that meet the specifications.
- **How?** Brainstorming, TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving), benchmarking, design reviews.
- **Deliverable:** A **Design Document** describing features and how they meet specs.

### Step 5 — Establish Process Goals and Quality Standards
- **Why?** Without targets, you cannot measure success.
- **How?** Set **CTQs**, define **CTQs → CTPs (Critical to Process)**, set KPIs (e.g., <2 defects per 10,000 units), establish cost-of-quality budgets.
- **Deliverable:** **Process Control Plans**, **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**, and measurable **Quality Goals**.

### Step 6 — Prove Process Capability
- **Why?** A process that is *designed* to make a good product is meaningless until you *prove* it can run within specification.
- **How?** Conduct **Process Capability Studies** — calculate Cp, Cpk, control charts (X-bar, R-chart), pilot runs, and Design of Experiments (DoE).
- **Deliverable:** A **Capability Report** confirming the process can meet the CTQ targets before mass production.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Symbol | Equation | Units / Interpretation | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Process Capability Index | $C_p$ | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensionless; ≥ 1.33 generally acceptable | Step 6 — Prove Capability |
| Process Capability (Centered) | $C_{pk}$ | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Dimensionless; accounts for process mean shift | Step 6 — Prove Capability |
| Defects Per Million Opportunities | $DPMO$ | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per unit}}$ | Defects per 1,000,000 chances | Goal setting, Six Sigma |
| Rolled Throughput Yield | $RTY$ | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ where $Y_i$ = yield of each step | Decimal (0 to 1); first-pass yield across process chain | Process planning |
| Risk Priority Number (FMEA) | $RPN$ | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Score (1–1000); Severity × Occurrence × Detection | Design review |
| Return on Quality | $ROQ$ | $ROQ = \dfrac{\text{Quality Gain}}{\text{Quality Cost}}$ | Ratio; financial justification of planning | Investment approval |
| Customer Satisfaction Index | $CSI$ | $CSI = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Weight}_i \times \text{Satisfaction}_i)}{10}$ | Score (1–10); composite of weighted attributes | VOC measurement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Board Exams:**
> When writing the Cp formula, the denominator is $6\sigma$ (the *natural tolerance* of the process spanning 3σ above and 3σ below the mean). Do not confuse with $3\sigma$ — that is a one-sided control limit. This is a *very common KTU valuation trap*.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Quality Planning in Engineering

Quality Planning is not limited to manufacturing. Its principles drive:

- **Mechanical Engineering**: Tolerancing, GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing), Design for Manufacturability (DFM).
- **Civil Engineering**: Construction quality plans, mix-design verification, project quality manuals (ISO 9001).
- **Computer Science**: Software Quality Assurance plans, test planning, code review protocols, CI/CD pipeline design.
- **Electrical Engineering**: Reliability planning, MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) target setting, EMC compliance planning.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Connection — ISO 9001:2015:**
> Clause 8.1 of ISO 9001 explicitly mandates "**Operational Planning and Control**," which is the practical industrial embodiment of Juran's Quality Planning. Companies seeking ISO certification must document their planning process.

---

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Process Capability Index ($C_p$)

The $C_p$ index quantifies how well a process's natural variation fits within the specification limits set during Quality Planning.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let the specification limits be:} \quad & USL = \text{Upper Specification Limit} \\
                                             & LSL = \text{Lower Specification Limit} \\
\text{Let the process mean be:}              & \mu \\
\text{Let the process standard deviation be:} & \sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

The **total specification width** is:

$$
W_{spec} = USL - LSL
$$

The **natural process spread** (covering 99.73% of output under a normal distribution) is:

$$
W_{process} = 6\sigma
$$

The capability index is defined as the ratio of the specification width to the natural process spread:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \dfrac{W_{spec}}{W_{process}} \\
C_p &= \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation logic:**
- $C_p = 1.00$ → Process just barely fits (risk of defects = 0.27%).
- $C_p = 1.33$ → Industry minimum acceptable (risk of defects = 0.006%).
- $C_p = 1.67$ → World-class for existing products.
- $C_p = 2.00$ → World-class for new product introduction (Six Sigma territory).

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: $C_{pk}$ — Accounting for Off-Center Processes

The $C_p$ formula assumes the process mean is exactly at the center of the specification. In reality, processes drift. $C_{pk}$ corrects for this by measuring the **worst-case distance from the mean to the nearest specification limit**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Distance from mean to USL:} \quad & d_1 = USL - \mu \\
\text{Distance from mean to LSL:} \quad & d_2 = \mu - LSL
\end{aligned}
$$

The smaller of these two distances represents the tighter constraint. The $C_{pk}$ index uses this minimum distance, normalized by $3\sigma$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min\!\left( \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key relationship:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &\leq C_p \quad \text{(equality holds only when } \mu = \dfrac{USL + LSL}{2}\text{)} \\
\text{If } C_{pk} &< 0 \implies \text{process mean is outside specification → 100% defects}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:**
> A shaft diameter must be between $24.90\,\text{mm}$ (LSL) and $25.10\,\text{mm}$ (USL). After 50 sample measurements, the process mean is $\mu = 25.04\,\text{mm}$ and $\sigma = 0.02\,\text{mm}$. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$, and comment on the suitability of the process.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate $C_p$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \\
    &= \dfrac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.02} \\
    &= \dfrac{0.20}{0.12} \\
    &= 1.667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $C_{pk}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min\!\left( \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right) \\
       &= \min\!\left( \dfrac{25.10 - 25.04}{3 \times 0.02},\; \dfrac{25.04 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.02} \right) \\
       &= \min\!\left( \dfrac{0.06}{0.06},\; \dfrac{0.14}{0.06} \right) \\
       &= \min(1.000,\; 2.333) \\
       &= 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result

- $C_p = 1.667$ → The process *spread* is excellent (world-class).
- $C_{pk} = 1.000$ → But the *centering* is poor. The process mean is shifted toward the USL.
- **Conclusion:** The process is technically capable but misaligned. The planning team must **re-center the machine** to improve $C_{pk}$ to ≥ 1.33.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Juran's Steps to Tools

| Juran Step | Primary Tool | Output Document | Common Student Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Identify Customers | Stakeholder Mapping, SIPOC | Customer Register | Forgetting internal customers |
| 2. Determine Needs | VOC, Kano Model, Surveys | VOC Database | Treating complaints as "the" needs |
| 3. Translate to Specs | QFD / House of Quality | Design Specification Sheet | Confusing needs with specs |
| 4. Develop Features | Benchmarking, TRIZ, Brainstorming | Design Concept Document | Designing features customers didn't want |
| 5. Set Goals | SMART Goals, KPIs, SLOs | Quality Plan, SOPs | Setting unmeasurable goals |
| 6. Prove Capability | Cp/Cpk, Control Charts, DoE, Pilot Runs | Capability Report | Skipping this step → production disasters |

---

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Juran's Six-Step Quality Planning Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Identify Customers] --> B[Step 2: Determine Customer Needs VOC]
    B --> C[Step 3: Translate Needs to Measurable Specifications]
    C --> D[Step 4: Develop Product and Service Features]
    D --> E[Step 5: Establish Process Goals and Quality Standards]
    E --> F[Step 6: Prove Process Capability]
    F --> G{Process Capable?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Go to Quality Control Phase]
    G -- No --> I[Redesign Process and Return to Step 4]
    I --> D
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Position of Quality Planning in the Juran Trilogy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Quality Planning - Strategic Design] --> B[Quality Control - Operational Execution]
    B --> C[Quality Improvement - Continuous Optimization]
    C --> A
    A -.-> D[Establish Goals and Methods]
    B -.-> E[Measure and Compare to Goals]
    C -.-> F[Optimize Beyond Current Standards]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Quality Planning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VP[Voice of Customer VOC Capture] --> CR[Customer Requirements Database]
    CR --> HOU[House of Quality QFD Matrix]
    HOU --> DS[Design Specifications Output]
    DS --> PR[Process Design and Selection]
    PR --> PGO[Process Goals and CTQs Definition]
    PGO --> SOP[Standard Operating Procedures SOPs]
    SOP --> PCS[Process Capability Study Cp and Cpk]
    PCS --> CAP{Process Capable?}
    CAP -- Yes --> AP[Approval for Production Handoff to Quality Control]
    CAP -- No --> LP[Loop Back to Process Redesign]
    LP --> PR
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Voice of Customer (VOC) Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Customer Stated Needs - What they say] --> R[Real Needs - What they mean]
    R --> U[Unstated Needs - What they expect by default]
    U --> D[Delight Needs - What would surprise them positively]
    D --> K[Kano Model Categorization]
    K --> M[Must Be Quality - Basic]
    K --> O[One Dimensional Quality - More is better]
    K --> A[Attractive Quality - Excitement feature]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Architecture Note

The above four Mermaid diagrams collectively provide a complete **structural view of Quality Planning** — from the macro level (where it sits in Juran's Trilogy) to the operational level (VOC hierarchy and capability verification). This hierarchical block architecture is the standard KTU-recommended approach for visualizing management concepts that do not lend themselves to physical drawings.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Define Quality Planning. List its importance in Total Quality Management."**

**Model Answer:**
Quality Planning is the systematic process of identifying customers, determining their needs, translating those needs into measurable specifications, developing product/process features, establishing quality goals, and proving process capability.

**Importance in TQM:**
1. It is the *first pillar* of Juran's Trilogy, providing the foundation for all quality activities.
2. It enables **prevention-based quality management** — defects are avoided rather than detected after they occur.
3. It aligns products and services with **customer expectations**, improving satisfaction.
4. It reduces **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** by eliminating rework, warranty, and rejection costs.
5. It facilitates **ISO 9001:2015 compliance** and Six Sigma project success.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Briefly explain the concept of Voice of Customer (VOC) with two tools used to capture it."**

**Model Answer:**
Voice of Customer (VOC) is the structured process of capturing customers' stated, unstated, and unacknowledged needs, expectations, and feedback regarding a product or service. It forms the input to Step 2 of Juran's Quality Planning.

**Two Tools to Capture VOC:**
1. **Surveys and Questionnaires** — Structured quantitative data collection from a large customer base.
2. **Focus Groups** — A moderated discussion with 6–10 customers to uncover deep, qualitative insights about needs, perceptions, and expectations.

*(Other acceptable tools: Interviews, complaint analysis, Kano model, ethnographic observation, social listening.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Juran Trilogy of Quality** in detail. (7 marks)
**(b)** Discuss the **six universal steps of Quality Planning** with suitable examples. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
**Juran's Trilogy** is a three-part managerial framework developed by Joseph M. Juran that breaks Total Quality Management into three sequential, interdependent processes:

**1. Quality Planning:**
- Identify customers (internal and external).
- Determine their needs.
- Develop products/services to meet those needs.
- Establish quality goals.
- Develop processes to deliver those goals.
- Prove process capability.

**2. Quality Control:**
- Evaluate actual operating performance.
- Compare actual performance with quality goals.
- Act on the difference (corrective action).

**3. Quality Improvement:**
- Establish the infrastructure for continuous improvement.
- Identify improvement projects (Pareto analysis).
- Establish project teams with clear charters.
- Provide training, resources, and management support.

**Why the Trilogy Matters:**
- **Planning** prevents problems; **Control** detects problems; **Improvement** breaks through current performance ceilings.
- All three are essential — focusing only on control (inspection) leads to stagnation.
- The trilogy provides a **complete managerial roadmap** for TQM implementation, aligning with Deming's PDCA cycle and ISO 9001's process approach.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)
The six universal steps of Quality Planning (Juran) with examples:

| Step | Action | Example: Launching a New Smartphone |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify Customers | Target = age 18–35 urban professionals; internal = sales and after-sales teams |
| 2 | Determine Customer Needs (VOC) | Long battery life, fast charging, durable screen, low SAR radiation, premium feel |
| 3 | Translate Needs to Specifications | Battery: ≥ 5000 mAh; Charging: ≤ 30 min for 0–100%; Drop test: 1.5 m onto concrete, no crack |
| 4 | Develop Product Features | 6000 mAh battery, 120 W fast charge, Gorilla Glass Victus 2, ceramic back |
| 5 | Establish Process Goals | Assembly line first-pass yield ≥ 99.5%; DPMO ≤ 3400 (4-Sigma initially) |
| 6 | Prove Process Capability | Run 200 pilot units; compute Cp, Cpk on screen dimension; verify 6σ control on battery cell welding |

**Concluding remark:** Skipping any of these six steps results in the launch failures commonly seen in the consumer electronics industry — products that look great in marketing but fail in customer hands.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** and describe the **House of Quality** matrix. (7 marks)
**(b)** A manufacturing process has $USL = 12.06\,\text{mm}$, $LSL = 11.94\,\text{mm}$, process mean $\mu = 11.98\,\text{mm}$, and $\sigma = 0.015\,\text{mm}$. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Comment on process suitability and recommend corrective action. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
**Quality Function Deployment (QFD):**
QFD is a systematic planning tool that translates customer needs (the "Whats") into engineering specifications and process characteristics (the "Hows"). It was developed in Japan by Yoji Akao and Mizuno in the 1970s. The output of QFD is the **House of Quality (HOQ)** — a matrix that visualizes the relationship between customer requirements, technical requirements, and competitive benchmarking.

**House of Quality Matrix — Six Sections:**
1. **Customer Requirements (WHATS):** Listed on the left wall, weighted by importance.
2. **Technical Requirements (HOWS):** Listed across the top roof.
3. **Central Matrix (Relationship Matrix):** The "room" of the house, showing the strength of correlation between WHATS and HOWS using symbols (●=strong, ○=medium, △=weak).
4. **Roof (Correlation Matrix):** Shows how technical requirements interact with each other (positive or negative synergy).
5. **Competitive Benchmarking:** Compares our product against competitors on customer satisfaction and technical performance.
6. **Technical Priorities and Targets:** Output of the house — the engineering targets assigned to each technical requirement.

**Benefits of QFD:**
- Reduces rework by getting design right the first time.
- Cross-functional team alignment (marketing, engineering, manufacturing).
- Quantitative prioritization of engineering features.
- Strong alignment with **Step 3 (Translate Needs to Specs)** of Juran's Quality Planning.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)
**Given:**
$USL = 12.06\,\text{mm}$, $LSL = 11.94\,\text{mm}$, $\mu = 11.98\,\text{mm}$, $\sigma = 0.015\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 1 — Calculate $C_p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \\
    &= \dfrac{12.06 - 11.94}{6 \times 0.015} \\
    &= \dfrac{0.12}{0.090} \\
    &= 1.333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $C_{pk}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min\!\left( \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right) \\
       &= \min\!\left( \dfrac{12.06 - 11.98}{3 \times 0.015},\; \dfrac{11.98 - 11.94}{3 \times 0.015} \right) \\
       &= \min\!\left( \dfrac{0.08}{0.045},\; \dfrac{0.04}{0.045} \right) \\
       &= \min(1.778,\; 0.889) \\
       &= 0.889
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

- $C_p = 1.333$ → Process *spread* is just at the industry minimum acceptable level.
- $C_{pk} = 0.889$ → Process is **NOT capable** by industry standards (which require $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$).
- The process is **off-center** — the mean ($\mu = 11.98$) is closer to the LSL ($11.94$) than to the USL ($12.06$).
- **Process Mean Shift:** $\Delta = \mu - \text{Midpoint} = 11.98 - 12.00 = -0.02\,\text{mm}$ (shifted toward LSL).

**Step 4 — Corrective Action Recommendations:**

1. **Immediate re-centering:** Adjust the machine tool offset to shift the process mean to $\mu = 12.00\,\text{mm}$ (the midpoint).
2. **Reduce variation:** Improve tool rigidity, check for vibration, recalibrate the spindle, and use a more precise coolant to reduce $\sigma$ from $0.015$ to $\leq 0.010\,\text{mm}$.
3. **If $C_{pk} = 0.889$ persists:** The planning team should revisit **Step 6 of Juran's process** and consider tightening the process specification, redesigning the tool, or using Six Sigma DMAIC methods.
4. **Verification:** Recalculate $C_{pk}$ after correction. Goal: $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for production approval, $\geq 1.67$ for world-class quality.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$:** $C_p$ ignores the mean; $C_{pk}$ does not. Many students write $C_{pk} = C_p$ which is only true for perfectly centered processes.
> 2. **Forgetting the 6 in $C_p$:** The denominator is $6\sigma$, not $3\sigma$. This is the #1 valuation trap.
> 3. **Skipping interpretation:** A numeric answer without a process capability comment loses 2–3 marks. Always end with "is the process capable or not, and what is the corrective action?"
> 4. **Mixing Juran and Deming steps:** Juran's Quality Planning is a *product-oriented* process. Deming's PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) is a *process-control* cycle. Do not interchange them.
> 5. **Writing VOC without examples:** Always give one practical example (e.g., a hospital, a mobile app, an automotive part) to earn full marks.
> 6. **Skipping internal customers:** In any KTU answer about customer identification, the examiner expects you to mention **internal customers** (downstream departments) explicitly.

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Quality Planning (Module 1):**
> - Quality Planning is **Step 1 of Juran's Trilogy** (Planning → Control → Improvement).
> - The **6 Universal Steps** are: Identify Customers → Determine Needs → Translate to Specs → Develop Features → Establish Goals → Prove Capability.
> - **Voice of Customer (VOC)** is captured using surveys, focus groups, interviews, Kano model, and complaint analysis.
> - **QFD (Quality Function Deployment)** translates VOC into technical specifications using the **House of Quality** matrix.
> - The **House of Quality** has 6 components: WHATS, HOWS, Central Matrix, Roof (correlations), Benchmarking, and Technical Targets.
> - **Process Capability Formulas (MEMORIZE):**
>   - $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ (spread only)
>   - $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ (centered)
> - **Capability Benchmarks:** $C_p \geq 1.33$ acceptable, $\geq 1.67$ world-class. $C_{pk} \leq C_p$ always.
> - **DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities) is the Six Sigma standard metric. Six Sigma target = **3.4 DPMO**.
> - **FMEA's RPN** = Severity × Occurrence × Detection. Used in design review (Step 4 of Quality Planning).
> - **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 8.1** mandates operational planning, which is the industrial embodiment of Juran's Quality Planning.
> - **Common Pitfall:** Quality Planning is *design-stage* prevention — it is NOT inspection. Do not confuse with Quality Control.
> - **Distinguish:** Quality of **Design** (Planning) vs Quality of **Conformance** (Control).
> - **Kano Model categories:** Must-be (basic), One-dimensional (more is better), Attractive (delighters).
> - Always mention **internal and external customers** in any answer about customer identification.
> - **KTU favorite exam question type:** "Explain Quality Planning with reference to Juran's Trilogy" or "Calculate Cp and Cpk for the given data and comment on process capability."

---

> **End of Module 1 — Quality Planning Notes**
> *Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcomes, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Costs - Analysis Techniques for Quality Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Costs - Analysis Techniques for Quality Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Cost (Cost of Quality – COQ):**
> Quality cost is defined as *"the sum of conformance costs (the cost of achieving quality) and non-conformance costs (the cost of poor quality) incurred by an organization in the design, production, and delivery of products or services."*

As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 1), Quality Costs are systematically classified under the **PAF Model** (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure), originally formalized by **Joseph M. Juran** (1951) and later refined by the British Standards Institution (BS 6143) and the American Society for Quality (ASQ).

The four canonical categories are:

| # | Category | Type | Nature |
|---|----------|------|--------|
| 1 | **Prevention Costs (P)** | Conformance | Money spent to *avoid* defects |
| 2 | **Appraisal Costs (A)** | Conformance | Money spent to *detect* defects |
| 3 | **Internal Failure Costs (IFC)** | Non-Conformance | Cost of defects found *before* delivery |
| 4 | **External Failure Costs (EFC)** | Non-Conformance | Cost of defects found *after* delivery |

### 1.1.1 Detailed Component Breakdown

**Prevention Costs (P)** – Investments made to *prevent* the occurrence of defects:
- Quality planning and administration
- New product review / Design review
- Supplier capability surveys and audits
- Quality training and education of employees
- Quality improvement projects (Kaizen, Six Sigma projects)
- Process capability studies

**Appraisal Costs (A)** – Costs incurred to *detect* defects during production/service:
- Incoming material inspection
- In-process and final inspection / testing
- Test equipment calibration and maintenance
- Cost of inspection labour
- Quality audits of the system

**Internal Failure Costs (IFC)** – Costs of defects discovered *before* customer receipt:
- Scrap and rework
- Re-inspection and re-testing
- Failure analysis
- Downtime due to quality issues
- Material review / Material rejection

**External Failure Costs (EFC)** – Costs of defects discovered *after* the customer receives the product:
- Warranty claims and field service
- Returned goods and handling
- Liability costs and legal claims
- Loss of customer goodwill and brand damage
- Recall costs

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – The "Car Maintenance" Model:**
> Imagine owning a car. Every month you have two choices:
> 1. **Spend ₹2,000 on preventive servicing** (oil change, tyre rotation, brake check) – this is your **Prevention Cost**.
> 2. **Spend ₹500 on a quarterly car health check-up at a mechanic** – this is your **Appraisal Cost**.
> 3. If the car breaks down *before* you reach home (engine knock on the road, but you detect it) – towing + garage repair = **Internal Failure Cost**.
> 4. If the car breaks down *on a highway far from home* with your family – towing, hotel stay, lost work = **External Failure Cost**.

**The Managerial Insight:** Spending slightly more on Prevention (₹2,000 → ₹2,500) saves you lakhs of rupees in External Failure Costs (accidents, lawsuits). This is the **fundamental logic of investing in quality** — *a rupee spent on prevention saves many rupees in failure costs*.

In an engineering organization, the same principle applies:
- **1 unit on Prevention ≈ 10 units saved on Internal Failure**
- **1 unit on Prevention ≈ 100 units saved on External Failure**

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Industry Benchmarks

The following are widely accepted **global benchmarks** for Quality Cost ratios (ASQ Industry Surveys):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Industry-wide Quality Cost:** typically **5%–25%** of total sales revenue.
> - **Optimal Prevention + Appraisal:** **5%–10%** of total quality cost.
> - **Optimal External Failure:** should be **less than 25%** of total quality cost.
> - **Quality Cost Ratio** (P+A)/(IFC+EFC) ideally approaches **1.0** in mature quality systems.

These constants serve as diagnostic anchors during a KTU examination numerical analysis.

---

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PAF Cost Model – Mathematical Foundation

The Total Quality Cost (TQC) of any organization is mathematically expressed as the summation of all four cost categories:

$$TQC = P + A + IFC + EFC$$

Where each component is a **monotonic function of the level of prevention investment** $x$ (let $x$ represent the percentage of sales revenue invested in prevention activities):

| Component | Behaviour with increasing $x$ | Mathematical Type |
|-----------|-------------------------------|-------------------|
| Prevention Cost $P(x)$ | Increases monotonically | Linear or convex |
| Appraisal Cost $A(x)$ | Roughly constant, may slightly decrease | Nearly flat |
| Internal Failure $IFC(x)$ | Decreases monotonically | Convex decreasing |
| External Failure $EFC(x)$ | Decreases sharply (convex) | Convex decreasing |

The optimal investment $x^*$ is found by **minimizing TQC**:

$$\frac{d(TQC)}{dx} = \frac{dP}{dx} + \frac{dA}{dx} + \frac{dIFC}{dx} + \frac{dEFC}{dx} = 0$$

In equilibrium, the marginal increase in prevention cost equals the marginal decrease in combined failure costs.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula that may appear in a KTU 2024 ESE or internal examination question on this topic. **Memorize these.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $TQC = P + A + IFC + EFC$ | Total Quality Cost | Currency (₹/\$/€) |
| 2 | $COPQ = IFC + EFC$ | Cost of Poor Quality (Non-conformance only) | Currency |
| 3 | $COGQ = P + A$ | Cost of Good Quality (Conformance only) | Currency |
| 4 | $QCR = \dfrac{COQ}{Sales} \times 100$ | Quality Cost Ratio (as % of sales) | Percentage (%) |
| 5 | $Ratio_{P:A:F} = \dfrac{P + A}{IFC + EFC}$ | Prevention-to-Failure ratio (ideal $\approx 1$) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $E/I = \dfrac{EFC}{IFC}$ | External-to-Internal failure ratio (lower is better) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $Savings = COPQ_{old} - COPQ_{new}$ | Savings from a quality improvement project | Currency |
| 8 | $ROI_{Quality} = \dfrac{Savings}{Investment} \times 100$ | Return on Investment of quality program | Percentage (%) |
| 9 | $\eta_{Cumulative} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i} \times 100$ | Pareto Cumulative Percentage | Percentage (%) |
| 10 | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process Capability Index | Dimensionless ($\geq 1.33$ preferred) |

**Boundary / Performance Conditions:**

- $C_{pk} < 1.00$ → Process is **incapable** (high failure cost zone)
- $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ → Process is **marginally capable**
- $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ → Process is **capable** (low failure cost zone)
- $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ → **Six-Sigma quality** (near-zero failure cost zone)

---

## 2.3 Analysis Techniques for Quality Costs – Theoretical Framework

KTU Module 1 mandates study of the following **seven** analysis techniques. Each technique serves a unique purpose in the *Diagnose → Measure → Control* cycle of quality cost management.

### Technique 1: Pareto Analysis (80/20 Rule)

Vilfredo Pareto's principle, when applied to quality, states that roughly **80% of the quality problems arise from 20% of the defect types** (the "vital few"). This technique is used to **prioritize** which quality problems to attack first, ensuring maximum economic return on prevention investment.

### Technique 2: Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone / Ishikawa) Diagram

Developed by **Kaoru Ishikawa** (1968) in Japan. Used to identify the **root causes** of quality costs. The major branches are typically categorized as **5M + 1E**:
- **Man** (Workforce)
- **Machine** (Equipment)
- **Material** (Inputs)
- **Method** (Process)
- **Measurement** (Inspection systems)
- **Mother Nature** (Environment)

### Technique 3: Quality Cost Reporting & Trend Analysis

Time-series tracking of the four PAF cost categories, usually monthly/quarterly, to identify whether the organization is *moving toward* the optimal cost model (i.e., P and A rising while IFC and EFC fall).

### Technique 4: Cost-Benefit Analysis of Quality Programs

Comparison of the *monetary benefits* (savings in IFC + EFC) against the *costs* (P + A expenditure) of a proposed quality initiative, often expressed as a **payback period** or **Net Present Value (NPV)**.

### Technique 5: Process Capability Analysis ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$)

Statistical measurement of whether a process can consistently produce output within specification limits, directly influencing the Internal Failure Cost.

### Technique 6: Activity-Based Costing (ABC) for Quality

Allocates overhead quality costs to specific activities (e.g., inspection, rework) using cost drivers, providing a much more accurate picture of where quality cost is being consumed than traditional cost accounting.

### Technique 7: Benchmarking Quality Costs

Comparing an organization's Quality Cost ratios against *industry best-in-class* firms to identify improvement opportunities. Types: Internal, Competitive, Functional, and Generic benchmarking.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industries like **automotive (Toyota, Tata Motors)**, **semiconductor manufacturing (Intel, TSMC)**, and **aerospace (Boeing, HAL)**, the PAF cost model drives multi-million-dollar decisions:

- A **1% reduction in External Failure Cost** in the automotive industry can yield savings exceeding **₹500 crores annually** for a major OEM.
- Six-Sigma ($C_{pk} \geq 1.67$) translates to **only 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**, which virtually eliminates External Failure Costs.
- KTU graduates entering roles as **Quality Engineers, Production Managers, or Six Sigma Green Belts** will routinely use these formulas in **Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control (DMAIC)** projects.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimal Level of Prevention Investment (The PAF Optimum)

**Problem Statement:** Given mathematical models for the four cost components as functions of prevention investment $x$ (measured as a fraction of sales), derive the value of $x$ that minimizes Total Quality Cost.

**Given Models:**
$$P(x) = k_1 \cdot x \quad \text{(Prevention cost grows linearly)}$$
$$A(x) = k_2 \quad \text{(Appraisal cost is approximately constant)}$$
$$IFC(x) = \dfrac{k_3}{x} \quad \text{(Internal failure falls inversely)}$$
$$EFC(x) = \dfrac{k_4}{x^2} \quad \text{(External failure falls sharply with prevention)}$$

Where $k_1$, $k_2$, $k_3$, $k_4$ are positive constants determined empirically from the firm's historical data.

**Step 1 – Write the Total Quality Cost function:**

$$TQC(x) = P(x) + A(x) + IFC(x) + EFC(x)$$

Substituting the four models into this equation:

$$TQC(x) = k_1 \cdot x + k_2 + \dfrac{k_3}{x} + \dfrac{k_4}{x^2}$$

**Step 2 – Apply the first-order optimality condition (FOC):**

For a minimum, the first derivative with respect to $x$ must equal zero:

$$\dfrac{d(TQC)}{dx} = \dfrac{d}{dx}\left[k_1 x + k_2 + \dfrac{k_3}{x} + \dfrac{k_4}{x^2}\right] = 0$$

**Step 3 – Differentiate term by term:**

- $\dfrac{d}{dx}(k_1 x) = k_1$
- $\dfrac{d}{dx}(k_2) = 0$
- $\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac{k_3}{x}\right) = -\dfrac{k_3}{x^2}$
- $\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac{k_4}{x^2}\right) = -\dfrac{2 k_4}{x^3}$

Combining the derivatives into a single equation:

$$k_1 - \dfrac{k_3}{x^2} - \dfrac{2 k_4}{x^3} = 0$$

**Step 4 – Solve for the critical point $x^*$:**

Multiply both sides by $x^3$ to clear denominators:

$$k_1 \cdot x^3 - k_3 \cdot x - 2 k_4 = 0$$

This is a cubic equation in $x$. The unique positive real root is the optimal prevention investment level $x^*$.

**Step 5 – Verify that this critical point is a minimum using the second-order condition (SOC):**

$$\dfrac{d^2(TQC)}{dx^2} = \dfrac{2 k_3}{x^3} + \dfrac{6 k_4}{x^4}$$

Since $k_3, k_4 > 0$, this expression is **always positive** for any $x > 0$. Therefore, the critical point $x^*$ is confirmed to be a **global minimum** of the Total Quality Cost function.

**Step 6 – Economic Interpretation:**

At $x = x^*$, the marginal cost of an additional rupee of prevention equals the marginal reduction in failure costs. Beyond this point, the organization is over-investing in prevention (waste); before this point, it is under-investing (excessive failure cost).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pareto Cumulative Frequency Curve (Lorenz Construction)

**Given:** A list of $n$ defect categories with frequencies $f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_n$, sorted in **descending order**.

**Step 1 – Compute the total frequency:**

$$F_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i = f_1 + f_2 + f_3 + \cdots + f_n$$

**Step 2 – Compute the cumulative frequency up to the $k^{th}$ category:**

$$F_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i$$

**Step 3 – Compute the cumulative percentage:**

$$\eta_k = \dfrac{F_k}{F_{total}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 – Decision Rule (Pareto):**

If $\eta_{m} \geq 80\%$ for some small $m$ (typically $m \leq 4$), then the first $m$ defect categories are the **"vital few"** that should be prioritized for quality improvement. The remaining $n - m$ categories are the **"trivial many"** that can be addressed later.

**Step 5 – Plotting the Pareto Chart:**

- X-axis: Defect categories (sorted in descending frequency)
- Left Y-axis: Individual frequency (bar chart)
- Right Y-axis: Cumulative percentage (line plot)
- A horizontal reference line at 80% cuts the curve to identify the vital few.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)

**Given:** A normally distributed process with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, and specification limits $LSL$ (Lower) and $USL$ (Upper).

**Step 1 – Compute the one-sided capability indices:**

$$C_{pL} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$$

$$C_{pU} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$$

**Step 2 – $C_{pk}$ is the minimum of the two (worst-case capability):**

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pL},\ C_{pU}) = \min\left(\dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}\right)$$

**Step 3 – Yield and DPMO Conversions (for KTU reference):**

$$DPMO = (1 - \text{Yield}) \times 10^6$$

For a centered process with $C_{pk} = C_p$:

$$\text{Yield} = \Phi(3 C_p) - \Phi(-3 C_p)$$

Where $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the Standard Normal Cumulative Distribution Function.

**Step 4 – Relation to Quality Cost:**

A low $C_{pk}$ ($< 1.00$) indicates the process will generate a **high Internal Failure Cost**, while a $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ drives IFC close to zero.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Technique | Primary Purpose | Best Used When | Output Type | Linked Cost Category |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|-------------|----------------------|
| Pareto Analysis | Prioritize problems | Multiple defects exist | Ranked list + chart | IFC, EFC reduction |
| Fishbone Diagram | Identify root causes | Cause of a defect is unclear | Branch diagram | Prevention targeting |
| Trend Analysis | Track cost over time | Long-term control needed | Time-series chart | All categories |
| Cost-Benefit Analysis | Justify a project | New investment decision | NPV / Payback | ROI of P & A |
| Process Capability | Measure process health | Specifications exist | $C_{pk}$ value | IFC reduction |
| Activity-Based Costing | Accurate cost allocation | Overhead is large | Per-activity cost | Detailed PAF split |
| Benchmarking | Compare with peers | Industry comparison needed | Gap analysis | All categories |
