> 5. **Presenting the virtuous cycle without numbering its nodes** → **−1 mark** for lack of structure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — TQM Benefits (Module 2):**

- **Five stakeholder groups** to remember: **Organization, Employees, Customers, Suppliers, Society** (the mnemonic **"OECS-S"** or simply **"O-E-C-S-S"**).
- **COPQ = Internal Failure + External Failure + Appraisal + Prevention**. Memorize all four.
- **Cost of Conformance** = Prevention + Appraisal. **Cost of Non-Conformance** = Internal + External Failure.
- **Net Benefit** = Old COPQ − New COPQ. **ROI = (Net Benefit ÷ Investment) × 100%**.
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** = (Good Units ÷ Total Units) × 100%.
- **Customer Retention Rate** = ((End Customers − New Customers) ÷ Start Customers) × 100%.
- **Six-Sigma benchmark**: 3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO).
- **Juran's principle**: "Quality is free; it pays for itself" — always include this in long answers.
- **Virtuous Quality Cycle**: Leadership → Culture → Engagement → Process Excellence → Customer Delight → Growth → Reinvestment.
- **Kaizen + Poka-Yoke** = TQM's twin engines for waste reduction and mistake-proofing.
- **KTU favourite question type**: *"List benefits of TQM to [X]"* — be ready to answer for *any* of the five stakeholders, not just the organization.
- **Numerical favourite**: COPQ calculation with prevention investment → ROI. Practice at least 3 problems before the exam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In any 14-mark TQM benefits question, the **structure** is worth at least 2 marks. Use clear sub-headings, numbered points, and a concluding sentence. A well-structured 4-page answer always scores higher than a disorganized 6-page one.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Process Improvement - Juran Trilogy
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuous Process Improvement - Juran Trilogy

# Continuous Process Improvement & The Juran Trilogy

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous Process Improvement (CPI)** is a structured, ongoing, organization-wide methodology of identifying, analyzing, and incrementally enhancing internal business processes to achieve measurable improvements in product quality, operational efficiency, customer satisfaction, and competitive advantage — rather than achieving breakthrough changes through sporadic, large-scale re-engineering initiatives.

The **Juran Trilogy**, formulated by the legendary American engineer **Dr. Joseph M. Juran** (1904–2008) in his landmark text *Juran's Quality Handbook*, is a universal framework that operationalizes the philosophy of continuous improvement. It posits that the entire spectrum of quality management activities can be classified into **three interdependent managerial processes**:

| Process | Core Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Quality Planning** | Designing processes to meet quality goals |
| **Quality Control** | Operating processes to achieve goals |
| **Quality Improvement** | Establishing infrastructure to continuously improve |

### 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — "The Gym Membership of a Manufacturing Plant"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a person who joins a gym (a manufacturing organization).
> - **Quality Planning** is like the *fitness trainer sitting down with the client on Day 1* to identify goals ("lose 10 kg in 6 months") and design a personalized diet + exercise roadmap. The goal (target weight) and the process (plan) are defined before any action occurs.
> - **Quality Control** is the *daily weighing and checking of food logs* to ensure the client is on track with the original plan. If deviation occurs (ate a pizza), it is corrected immediately to stay on roadmap.
> - **Quality Improvement** is the *periodic reassessment every quarter*: the trainer introduces new exercises, sets harder targets (run a 5K), or refines the diet based on results achieved so far. The bar is raised continuously — the system itself evolves.
>
> Just as fitness is not achieved by a single gym session, **quality is not achieved by a single project**. It requires Planning → Control → Improvement, cyclically, forever.

### 1.3 Continuous Process Improvement — The Philosophical Backbone

> [!NOTE]
> **CPI is not a project with an end date — it is a permanent cultural state of the organization.** The seminal advocates include:
> - **Walter Shewhart** (PDCA Cycle, 1939)
> - **W. Edwards Deming** (PDSA / Deming Wheel, 1950s)
> - **Joseph Juran** (Juran Trilogy, 1951)
> - **Kaoru Ishikawa** (Quality Circles, Japan)
> - **Genichi Taguchi** (Loss Function, Quality Loss Concept)

The **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) Cycle**, also known as the **Deming Cycle** or **Shewhart Cycle**, is the operational engine that drives CPI within each of the three Juran Trilogy processes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Analytical Sheet

## 2.1 The Juran Trilogy — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Process 1: Quality Planning (QP)

Quality Planning is the structured activity of establishing **quality goals** and identifying the **customers** (both internal and external) along with their **needs**. It is fundamentally a *design-time* activity.

**The Juran Quality Planning Roadmap (Universal Sequence):**

1. **Identify the customers** — Who are the users, consumers, or recipients of the product/process output?
2. **Determine customer needs** — What are the explicit (spoken) and implicit (unspoken) requirements?
3. **Develop product features** — Translate needs into measurable product/process specifications.
4. **Establish quality goals** — Quantify the targets (e.g., defect rate ≤ 0.01%, Cp ≥ 1.33, Cpk ≥ 1.33).
5. **Develop the process** — Design the workflow capable of meeting the established goals.
6. **Develop process controls** — Define feedback metrics, sensors, and transfer functions to monitor the process during execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Quality Planning answers the question — *"How do we design the system to deliver quality?"* Without QP, Quality Control has nothing to control, and Quality Improvement has no baseline to improve upon.

### 2.1.2 Process 2: Quality Control (QC)

Quality Control is the operational process of evaluating actual performance against the established quality goals, acting on the difference (the *sporadic* and *chronic* deviations), and taking **corrective action**.

**Operational Steps:**

1. **Evaluate actual quality performance** — Measure the output using defined metrics.
2. **Compare actual performance with quality goals** — Benchmark against targets.
3. **Act on the difference** — Execute corrective action to close any gaps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sporadic vs. Chronic Quality Problems:**
> - **Sporadic problems** are sudden, sharp deviations from the baseline (e.g., a machine breakdown causing a defect spike). These are *reactive* in nature — diagnosis + correction.
> - **Chronic problems** are persistent, slowly-drifting inefficiencies embedded in the system (e.g., a process running at 5% defect rate for years). These require *breakthrough* improvement.
>
> Juran emphasized that **85% of organizational quality problems are chronic**, and Quality Control alone cannot solve them — they require the third process, Quality Improvement.

### 2.1.3 Process 3: Quality Improvement (QI)

Quality Improvement is the structured, formal establishment of an **infrastructure** to continuously and methodically pursue **breakthrough improvements** in quality performance.

**The Juran Breakthrough Sequence:**

1. **Establish infrastructure** — Create a Quality Council, identify improvement projects, appoint teams, provide resources, and train facilitators.
2. **Identify the specific improvement projects (diagnosis)** — Choose subjects (chronic problems), establish symptoms, hypothesize causes, test hypotheses, and identify root causes.
3. **Establish a remedial action plan (remedy)** — Decide on the actions to remove root causes, design controls to hold the gains, and implement the changes.
4. **Implement the remedies (holding the gains)** — Execute the plan under controlled conditions; monitor for unintended side effects; institutionalize the change through Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Holding the gains"** is a uniquely Juranian concept. Improvements often erode over time as workers revert to old habits. Formalizing new processes through training, documentation, audits, and control charts ensures the improvement is permanent.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

### 2.2.1 Juran Trilogy vs. Deming PDCA Cycle

| Parameter | Juran Trilogy | Deming PDCA Cycle |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Origin** | Joseph M. Juran, 1951 | W. Edwards Deming (popularized from Shewhart) |
| **Structure** | 3 sequential managerial processes | 4-step cyclical iterative loop |
| **Scope** | Macro-level organizational governance | Micro-level project/process execution |
| **Focus** | *What* needs to be done | *How* to execute improvement scientifically |
| **Time Horizon** | Long-term strategic | Short-term tactical / project-based |
| **Relationship** | Defines *which* activities exist | Defines *how* each activity is executed |

### 2.2.2 The Three Processes — Side-by-Side

| Dimension | Quality Planning | Quality Control | Quality Improvement |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Establish goals & processes | Operate processes to meet goals | Establish infrastructure for breakthrough |
| **Output** | Specifications, SOPs | Conformance reports | New baselines, upgraded processes |
| **Frequency** | Per project / new product | Continuous (daily/real-time) | Per breakthrough project |
| **Trigger** | New product, new customer need | Deviation in metrics | Chronic gap below goals |
| **Key Question** | *"Can we do it?"* | *"Are we doing it right?"* | *"Can we do it better?"* |
| **Nature** | Proactive / Design | Reactive / Operational | Proactive / Strategic |
| **Statistical Tools** | QFD, FMEA, DOE | Control Charts, Pareto, Histograms | Pareto, Ishikawa, FMEA, DOE |
| **Department** | R&D, Engineering, Planning | Production, QC, Operations | Cross-functional teams |

### 2.2.3 Cost of Quality (COQ) — Linked to Juran Trilogy

Juran classified quality costs into four categories. Although this is not a *formula* in the engineering sense, the **Cost of Quality framework** is often asked in 14-mark questions and links directly to the trilogy.

| Cost Category | Definition | Linked Trilogy Stage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Prevention Costs** | Costs to prevent defects (training, planning, FMEA) | Quality Planning |
| **Appraisal Costs** | Costs to detect defects (inspection, testing) | Quality Control |
| **Internal Failure Costs** | Costs of defects found before delivery (rework, scrap) | Quality Improvement |
| **External Failure Costs** | Costs of defects found after delivery (warranty, returns) | Quality Improvement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Juran's Golden Insight:** As an organization invests more in **Prevention** (Quality Planning), it progressively reduces **Appraisal, Internal Failure, and External Failure** costs. The optimum is achieved when the *marginal* prevention cost equals the *marginal* failure cost savings. This is the economic justification for Quality Improvement projects.

## 2.3 Application in Real Engineering Fields

- **Manufacturing:** QP → Design of Assembly Line Process; QC → SPC Charts on shop floor; QI → Kaizen Events to reduce cycle time.
- **Software Engineering:** QP → Requirements Engineering; QC → Unit Testing, Code Reviews; QI → CI/CD Pipeline refactoring, DevOps maturity.
- **Civil Engineering:** QP → Mix Design for Concrete; QC → Slump Test, Cube Test; QI → Switching to supplementary cementitious materials.
- **Healthcare:** QP → Patient Pathway Design; QC → Vital Sign Monitoring; QI → Lean Six Sigma to reduce Average Length of Stay.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Mathematical Rigor

While the Juran Trilogy is primarily a management framework, the **Cost of Quality model** and the **Pareto-based prioritization** within Quality Improvement allow for rigorous quantitative treatment expected at the KTU engineering level.

## 3.1 Cost of Quality (COQ) — Mathematical Modeling

Let us define:
- $P$ = Total Prevention Cost
- $A$ = Total Appraisal Cost
- $F_{I}$ = Total Internal Failure Cost
- $F_{E}$ = Total External Failure Cost
- $C_{TQ}$ = Total Cost of Quality

**Step 1:** Express the total cost of quality as the sum of all four categories.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{TQ} &= P + A + F_{I} + F_{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** The conformance cost is the sum of Prevention and Appraisal costs (costs incurred to *achieve* conformance).

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Conformance} &= P + A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** The non-conformance cost is the sum of Internal and External Failure costs (costs incurred due to *non-conformance*).

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{NonConformance} &= F_{I} + F_{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the total cost of quality becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{TQ} &= C_{Conformance} + C_{NonConformance}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Economic Logic:** As an organization invests more in **Prevention** ($P$ increases), defects fall → $F_{I}$ and $F_{E}$ fall. Appraisal ($A$) may initially rise (more inspection) then fall (less rework). The total $C_{TQ}$ exhibits a **convex curve** with a clear **optimum**.

**Step 5:** The optimal Prevention cost $P^*$ occurs when the **marginal cost of prevention equals the marginal reduction in failure cost**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_{TQ}}{dP} &= \frac{dP}{dP} + \frac{dA}{dP} + \frac{dF_{I}}{dP} + \frac{dF_{E}}{dP} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

For the simplified model where $P$ is the only decision variable and the failure costs are functions of $P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_{TQ}}{dP} &= 1 + \frac{dF_{I}}{dP} + \frac{dF_{E}}{dP} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left| \frac{d(F_{I} + F_{E})}{dP} \right| &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

This means: *Spend one more rupee on Prevention only if it saves at least one rupee in Failure costs.*

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Cost of Quality Optimization

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A precision machining firm is evaluating its quality costs. Currently:
> - Prevention Cost (P) = ₹ 2,00,000 per year
> - Appraisal Cost (A) = ₹ 3,00,000 per year
> - Internal Failure Cost ($F_{I}$) = ₹ 8,00,000 per year
> - External Failure Cost ($F_{E}$) = ₹ 12,00,000 per year
>
> Management is considering increasing Prevention expenditure to ₹ 4,00,000. Studies indicate:
> - Appraisal cost will rise by 10% (better instruments needed).
> - Internal Failure cost will reduce by 25%.
> - External Failure cost will reduce by 40%.
>
> **Determine:** (a) Total Cost of Quality before the proposal. (b) Total Cost of Quality after the proposal. (c) Should the firm invest?

**Solution — Step 1: Current Total Cost of Quality**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{TQ, before} &= P + A + F_{I} + F_{E} \\
&= 2{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 25{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 2: New Cost Components After Proposal**

New Appraisal Cost:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{new} &= 3{,}00{,}000 \times 1.10 = 3{,}30{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

New Internal Failure Cost:
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{I, new} &= 8{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.25) = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.75 = 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

New External Failure Cost:
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{E, new} &= 12{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.40) = 12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 = 7{,}20{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 3: New Total Cost of Quality**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{TQ, after} &= P_{new} + A_{new} + F_{I, new} + F_{E, new} \\
&= 4{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}30{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}20{,}000 \\
&= 21{,}50{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 4: Net Savings & Decision**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_{TQ} &= C_{TQ, after} - C_{TQ, before} \\
&= 21{,}50{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 \\
&= -3{,}50{,}000 \text{ INR (Savings)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision:** The firm should **invest in the increased Prevention cost**. The additional ₹ 2,00,000 spent on Prevention yields a net savings of ₹ 3,50,000 — a return of 1.75× on the prevention investment. This is a classic **Quality Improvement** decision in the Juran Trilogy framework.

## 3.3 Pareto-Based Project Selection in Quality Improvement

Within Juran's Quality Improvement process, the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)** is applied to prioritize breakthrough projects.

> **Problem Statement:** A company records the following defect types in one quarter:

| Defect Type | Frequency |
| :--- | :--- |
| Surface Scratch | 420 |
| Dimensional Out-of-tolerance | 280 |
| Color Mismatch | 150 |
| Welding Porosity | 90 |
| Missing Component | 45 |
| Others | 15 |
| **Total** | **1000** |

**Step 1:** Compute cumulative frequency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_i &= \sum_{k=1}^{i} f_k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute cumulative percentage.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_i &= \frac{C_i}{\sum f_k} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

| Defect | Frequency | Cumulative | Cumulative % |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Surface Scratch | 420 | 420 | 42.0% |
| Dim. Out-of-tolerance | 280 | 700 | 70.0% |
| Color Mismatch | 150 | 850 | 85.0% |
| Welding Porosity | 90 | 940 | 94.0% |
| Missing Component | 45 | 985 | 98.5% |
| Others | 15 | 1000 | 100.0% |

**Step 3:** Identify the "vital few."

> [!NOTE]
> **Pareto Decision:** The "vital few" contributing 80% of defects are **Surface Scratch + Dimensional Out-of-tolerance = 70%**. Adding **Color Mismatch** brings the cumulative to **85%**. These **three defect types** should be prioritized as **Quality Improvement breakthrough projects** in Juran's QI sequence.

This is the analytical bridge between **data** and **Juran's "Identify the specific improvement projects" step** of the QI breakthrough sequence.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Juran Trilogy — High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Organization-wide Quality Mission]) --> QP
    QP[Quality Planning - Design Phase] --> QC
    QC[Quality Control - Operate Phase] --> QI
    QI[Quality Improvement - Evolve Phase] -.-> QP

    subgraph QPdetail [Quality Planning Roadmap]
        QP --> QP1[Identify Customers]
        QP1 --> QP2[Determine Needs]
        QP2 --> QP3[Develop Product Features]
        QP3 --> QP4[Establish Quality Goals]
        QP4 --> QP5[Develop Process]
        QP5 --> QP6[Develop Process Controls]
    end

    subgraph QCdetail [Quality Control Loop]
        QC --> QC1[Evaluate Actual Performance]
        QC1 --> QC2[Compare with Goals]
        QC2 --> QC3{Deviation?}
        QC3 -- Yes --> QC4[Take Corrective Action]
        QC4 --> QC1
        QC3 -- No --> QCMaint[Maintain Performance]
    end

    subgraph QIdetail [Quality Improvement Breakthrough]
        QI --> QI1[Establish Infrastructure]
        QI1 --> QI2[Diagnose Chronic Problems]
        QI2 --> QI3[Remedy Root Causes]
        QI3 --> QI4[Hold the Gains]
        QI4 --> End([New Performance Baseline])
    end

    style QP fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style QC fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style QI fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style Start fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style End fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

## 4.2 The PDCA Cycle Embedded in Each Trilogy Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plan - Define Objective and Method] --> D[Do - Execute on Small Scale]
    D --> C[Check - Study Results vs Plan]
    C --> A[Act - Standardize or Revise]
    A -.-> P

    style P fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style A fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PDCA Relationship:** Quality Planning uses **Plan** extensively (planning a new product). Quality Control uses **Do-Check-Act** cyclically on the operating process. Quality Improvement uses the *full PDCA cycle* iteratively — Plan a breakthrough, Do a pilot, Check results, Act by standardizing.

## 4.3 Continuous Process Improvement — The Never-Ending Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[Monitor Performance Metrics] --> A1[Analyze Data with SPC Tools]
    A1 --> I[Identify Gap / Opportunity]
    I --> P[Plan Improvement Project]
    P --> I2[Implement Pilot Change]
    I2 --> V[Validate via Control Charts]
    V --> S[Standardize or Iterate]
    S -.-> M

    style M fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
    style A1 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
    style I fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style P fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style I2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style V fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style S fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

## 4.4 Cost of Quality Behavior Curve (Juran's Economic Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph XAxis [Prevention Cost Investment Increases]
        direction LR
        L1[Low] --> L2[Optimal] --> L3[Over-investment]
    end

    Pcurve[Prevention Cost - Rises Linearly] --> Optimum
    Fcurve[Failure Costs - Falls Sharply then Plateaus] --> Optimum
    TQcurve[Total Cost of Quality - U-Shaped Convex] --> Optimum

    Optimum([Economic Optimum Point])

    style Optimum fill:#ffd54f,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:3px
    style Pcurve fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style Fcurve fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style TQcurve fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The Total Cost of Quality (green) reaches its **minimum** at the **Optimal Prevention Investment**. Before this point, more Prevention pays for itself. Beyond this point, additional Prevention yields no corresponding reduction in Failure costs — money is wasted.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: Define the Juran Trilogy and list its three managerial processes.**

**Model Answer:**
The Juran Trilogy, formulated by Dr. Joseph M. Juran, is a universal quality management framework that classifies all quality activities into three interdependent managerial processes:

1. **Quality Planning (QP):** Designing products, processes, and systems to meet customer needs and established quality goals.
2. **Quality Control (QC):** Evaluating actual performance against goals and taking corrective action to maintain conformity.
3. **Quality Improvement (QI):** Establishing an infrastructure to continuously achieve breakthrough improvements in the existing process capability.

Together, these three processes form the operational backbone of Continuous Process Improvement in any organization.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: Differentiate between sporadic and chronic quality problems. Which one does Juran consider the priority?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sporadic Problems | Chronic Problems |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Nature | Sudden, sharp deviations | Persistent, gradually drifting inefficiencies |
| Frequency | Rare / one-time | Continuous / ongoing |
| Detection | Easy — alarms triggered | Difficult — accepted as normal |
| Solution | Quality Control (corrective) | Quality Improvement (breakthrough) |
| Example | Machine breakdown causing defect spike | Process running at 5% defect rate for years |

**Juran's View:** Approximately **85%** of organizational quality problems are **chronic**. He emphasized that Quality Control alone is insufficient; the **Quality Improvement process** must be institutionalized to permanently address these chronic inefficiencies.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When answering Juran Trilogy questions, students often *only describe* the three processes in 2-3 lines each and miss the marks allocated to *application*, *diagrams*, and *real-world linkage*. Each process must be elaborated with the **step-by-step roadmap (e.g., 6 steps for QP, 4 steps for QI breakthrough sequence)**. Always include a labeled diagram (the Venn/flowchart of the Trilogy) to secure the 2-3 marks allocated for visual presentation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three processes of the Juran Trilogy**. Describe the step-by-step methodology for Quality Planning and the Breakthrough Sequence for Quality Improvement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Juran concept of Cost of Quality**. With a suitable example, explain how increased investment in Prevention Cost can lead to a net reduction in Total Cost of Quality. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

The Juran Trilogy, proposed by Joseph M. Juran, provides a comprehensive roadmap for managing quality across an organization. The three managerial processes are:

**1. Quality Planning (QP):** This is the design-time activity of establishing customer needs and translating them into measurable product/process specifications. The Juran Quality Planning Roadmap consists of six steps:
   - **Step 1 — Identify the customers:** Recognize all internal and external recipients of the product.
   - **Step 2 — Determine customer needs:** Gather explicit (spoken) and implicit (unspoken) requirements through Voice of Customer (VOC) techniques.
   - **Step 3 — Develop product features:** Translate needs into measurable, testable specifications.
   - **Step 4 — Establish quality goals:** Quantify targets (defect rate ≤ 0.01%, Cp ≥ 1.33, etc.).
   - **Step 5 — Develop the process:** Design the production or service workflow.
   - **Step 6 — Develop process controls:** Define feedback metrics and transfer functions for monitoring.

**2. Quality Control (QC):** This is the operational activity of running the planned process and ensuring it stays on track.
   - **Step 1:** Evaluate actual quality performance (using control charts, inspection, audits).
   - **Step 2:** Compare actual performance against quality goals.
   - **Step 3:** Act on the difference — execute corrective action when deviations occur.

**3. Quality Improvement (QI) — Breakthrough Sequence:**
   - **Step 1 — Establish the infrastructure:** Form a Quality Council, identify improvement projects, appoint teams, provide resources.
   - **Step 2 — Identify specific projects (Diagnosis):** Select chronic problems, define symptoms, hypothesize root causes, verify through data.
   - **Step 3 — Establish a remedial action plan (Remedy):** Design actions to eliminate root causes and controls to hold the gains.
   - **Step 4 — Implement remedies (Holding the Gains):** Pilot, monitor for side effects, and institutionalize through SOPs and training.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Cost of Quality (COQ) — Juran's Classification:**

Juran classified all quality-related expenditures into four categories:

1. **Prevention Costs (P):** Costs to *prevent* defects — training, quality planning, FMEA, supplier capability assessment.
2. **Appraisal Costs (A):** Costs to *detect* defects — inspection, testing, calibration, audits.
3. **Internal Failure Costs ($F_I$):** Costs of defects found *before delivery* — rework, scrap, re-inspection.
4. **External Failure Costs ($F_E$):** Costs of defects found *after delivery* — warranty claims, returns, recalls, loss of goodwill.

**Total Cost of Quality:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{TQ} &= P + A + F_{I} + F_{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Economic Insight:** Initially, an organization operates with low Prevention investment → high Failure costs. As Prevention increases, Internal and External Failure costs fall sharply. The total $C_{TQ}$ traces a **convex curve** with a clear minimum — the *economic optimum*. Beyond this optimum, additional Prevention spending yields diminishing returns.

**Numerical Illustration:**
> A firm has: P = ₹1,00,000; A = ₹2,00,000; $F_I$ = ₹6,00,000; $F_E$ = ₹10,00,000. Total = **₹19,00,000**.
>
> If P is doubled to ₹2,00,000:
> - $F_I$ falls by 30% → ₹4,20,000
> - $F_E$ falls by 50% → ₹5,00,000
>
> New Total = 2,00,000 + 2,00,000 + 4,20,000 + 5,00,000 = **₹13,20,000**.
>
> **Net Savings = ₹5,80,000** — confirming that investment in Prevention yields exponential savings in Failure costs.

This economic principle underpins Juran's advocacy for institutionalizing Quality Improvement projects in every organization.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Continuous Process Improvement (CPI)**. Discuss how the **PDCA Cycle** integrates with the **Juran Trilogy** to operationalize CPI in an organization. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing company recorded the following quality costs for the year: Prevention = ₹5,00,000; Appraisal = ₹8,00,000; Internal Failure = ₹15,00,000; External Failure = ₹22,00,000. The management proposes to increase Prevention expenditure by 50%, expecting Internal Failure cost to reduce by 35% and External Failure cost to reduce by 45%. Calculate the current and proposed Total Cost of Quality, and advise whether the proposal should be accepted. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Continuous Process Improvement (CPI)** is the philosophy of making *incremental, ongoing improvements* to business processes, products, or services — rather than one-time, large-scale transformations. It views improvement not as a project with an end date, but as a **permanent cultural discipline**.

**Foundations of CPI** are built on the philosophies of **Deming (PDCA)**, **Juran (Trilogy)**, **Ishikawa (Quality Circles)**, and **Crosby (Zero Defects)**.

**Integration of PDCA with the Juran Trilogy:**

| Trilogy Process | PDCA Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Quality Planning | Heavy emphasis on **PLAN** — define objectives, identify customers, design processes. |
| Quality Control | Cyclical execution of **DO – CHECK – ACT** — operate, measure, correct, stabilize. |
| Quality Improvement | Full **PLAN – DO – CHECK – ACT** cycle for each breakthrough project — pilot → validate → standardize. |

**Operational Flow:**

1. In **Quality Planning**, the **PLAN** phase of PDCA is dominant — defining the roadmap.
2. In **Quality Control**, the **DO-CHECK-ACT** mini-loop runs continuously on the operating process — this is the *sporadic correction* layer.
3. In **Quality Improvement**, when chronic gaps are detected, a *full PDCA project* is launched — pilot change (**PLAN-DO**), evaluate (**CHECK**), and institutionalize the gains (**ACT**).

This makes the **Juran Trilogy the strategic architecture** and the **PDCA cycle the tactical engine** of Continuous Process Improvement.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1: Current Total Cost of Quality**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{TQ, before} &= P + A + F_{I} + F_{E} \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 + 22{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 50{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute New Cost Components**

New Prevention Cost:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{new} &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 1.50 = 7{,}50{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

New Internal Failure Cost (35% reduction):
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{I, new} &= 15{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.35) \\
&= 15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.65 \\
&= 9{,}75{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

New External Failure Cost (45% reduction):
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{E, new} &= 22{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.45) \\
&= 22{,}00{,}000 \times 0.55 \\
&= 12{,}10{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

Appraisal Cost is assumed unchanged:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{new} &= 8{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Proposed Total Cost of Quality**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{TQ, after} &= 7{,}50{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}75{,}000 + 12{,}10{,}000 \\
&= 37{,}35{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Net Savings & Recommendation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_{TQ} &= C_{TQ, after} - C_{TQ, before} \\
&= 37{,}35{,}000 - 50{,}00{,}000 \\
&= -12{,}65{,}000 \text{ INR (Net Savings)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommendation:** The proposal **must be accepted**. The additional Prevention investment of ₹2,50,000 yields a net saving of **₹12,65,000**, representing a return of approximately **5.06×** on the prevention investment — a strong validation of Juran's Cost of Quality economic principle and the strategic value of the Quality Improvement process.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Juran Trilogy & CPI**

- [x] **Juran Trilogy** = **Quality Planning + Quality Control + Quality Improvement**.
- [x] **Quality Planning (QP)** is a **6-step roadmap**: Identify customers → Determine needs → Develop features → Set goals → Develop process → Develop controls.
- [x] **Quality Control (QC)** is a **3-step loop**: Evaluate → Compare → Act on difference.
- [x] **Quality Improvement (QI)** uses the **4-step Breakthrough Sequence**: Infrastructure → Diagnosis → Remedy → Holding the Gains.
- [x] **85% of quality problems are chronic** — Juran's central insight driving the need for QI.
- [x] **"Holding the gains"** is essential to prevent improvements from eroding over time.
- [x] **Cost of Quality** has **4 components**: Prevention (P), Appraisal (A), Internal Failure ($F_I$), External Failure ($F_E$).
- [x] **Total COQ formula:** $C_{TQ} = P + A + F_I + F_E$.
- [x] **Economic optimum** of COQ occurs when **marginal prevention cost = marginal failure cost savings**.
- [x] **PDCA Cycle (Deming)** is the *tactical engine*; **Juran Trilogy** is the *strategic architecture*.
- [x] **Sporadic problems** → Quality Control handles them. **Chronic problems** → Quality Improvement handles them.
- [x] **Pareto Principle (80/20)** is used in QI to select the "vital few" breakthrough projects.
- [x] Always include a **labeled diagram** of the Trilogy in 14-mark answers — it secures 2-3 presentation marks.
- [x] Avoid the common pitfall of describing Juran Trilogy as just three steps — Juran specified *detailed sub-steps* for each process that examiners expect.
- [x] **CPI is a culture**, not a project — emphasize this in any "explain CPI" question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PDSA Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** PDSA Cycle # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > **PDSA Cycle (Plan–Do–Study–Act)** is an iterative, four-stage scientific method for continuous process improvement, originally conceptualized by **Walter A. Shewhart** in the 1920s and later popularized and adapted by **W. Edwards Deming**. It is formally known as the **Deming Wheel** or **Shewhart Cycle**, and serves as the foundational empirical learning loop for Total Quality Management (TQM). The cycle is a **closed-loop, repetitive problem-solving framework** wherein a hypothesis is tested, the results are studied, and the learning is then applied to either standardize the change (if successful) or re-plan the next experiment (if not). In KTU 2024 terminology, the PDSA cycle maps directly to the **DMAIC-R (Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control-Review)** philosophy and aligns with ISO 9001:2015's clause on **"Plan-Do-Check-Act" (PDCA)** at the organizational level, but differs in the use of the word "**Study**" to emphasize analytical learning rather than mere checking. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Board Terminology Alert:** Students must explicitly note that the cycle is most often referred to as the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle in ISO standards, but as the **PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act)** cycle in TQM literature. Both refer to the same conceptual loop, with the Deming preference for "Study" being the standard academic convention in TQM courses. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (The "Plain English" View) Imagine you are a **home cook trying to perfect a new recipe for the first time**. You do not just cook it once and assume it is done; instead, you: 1. **PLAN** the recipe — gather ingredients, decide quantities, and set the oven temperature. 2. **DO** it — actually cook the dish according to the plan. 3. **STUDY** it — taste the dish, check if the salt is too high, if the texture is right, and ask your family for feedback. 4. **ACT** — based on the feedback, you either (a) accept the recipe as the new "house standard," or (b) go back to step 1 with adjusted quantities for the next attempt. **The PDSA cycle is exactly this process, but applied to business, manufacturing, or service operations.** It transforms quality improvement from a one-time event into a *continuous, never-ending habit* of experimentation and refinement. ## 1.3 Core Characteristics (The 5 Pillars of PDSA) - **Iterative Nature:** The cycle is meant to be repeated continuously, with each rotation producing incremental gains. - **Hypothesis-Driven:** Every "Plan" stage begins with a clear, testable hypothesis. - **Small-Scale Testing:** Changes are usually piloted on a small scale before full deployment to minimize risk. - **Empirical Learning:** Decisions in the "Study" stage are based on **data and evidence**, not intuition. - **Closed-Loop:** There is no final "end" — the output of one cycle becomes the input of the next, creating a continuous improvement spiral. ## 1.4 Historical Context & Deming's Philosophy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Board Fact:** W. Edwards Deming modified Shewhart's original PDCA to PDSA in 1986 because he believed "**Check**" implied merely verifying conformity, whereas "**Study**" implied a deeper, scientific analysis of the *results* and *causal relationships*. This nuance is a frequently asked question in KTU examinations. ## 1.5 Key Standardized Metrics The following are universally accepted metrics in PDSA-based TQM practice: - **PDCA / PDSA Cycle Time (T):** Measured in **days** or **weeks** per iteration. - **Number of Completed Cycles (N):** A pure dimensionless integer count. - **Improvement Rate (IR):** Typically expressed in **%** (percentage reduction in defects or variation). - **Plan Execution Fidelity:** Measured in **% (0–100)** as the ratio of completed planned tasks to total planned tasks. - **Standard Work Compliance Rate:** Expressed in **%** as the proportion of operators following the new standardized procedure after the "Act" stage. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Four Stages The PDSA cycle is not a checklist; it is a **logical-empirical sequence**. Each stage feeds the next with specific, structured information. ### 2.1.1 Stage 1 — PLAN (The Hypothesis Generation Stage) **Objective:** Identify the problem, formulate a hypothesis, and design a test. **Operational Sub-Steps:** 1. **Problem Definition:** State the issue in measurable terms (e.g., "Customer complaints increased by 18% in Q3"). 2. **Root Cause Analysis:** Use tools like the **5 Whys**, **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram**, or **Pareto Chart** to identify the suspected cause. 3. **Hypothesis Formulation:** Write a clear, falsifiable statement (e.g., "If we reduce machine setup time from 12 min to 8 min, throughput will increase by 15%"). 4. **Data Collection Plan:** Define what data will be collected, by whom, using what instrument, and over what timeframe. 5. **Success Criteria:** Specify the quantitative threshold for success (e.g., "p < 0.05" or "defect rate below 2%"). ### 2.1.2 Stage 2 — DO (The Experimental Execution Stage) **Objective:** Execute the plan on a small scale and document observations. **Operational Sub-Steps:** 1. **Pilot Implementation:** Run the change on a small, controlled subset (e.g., one machine, one shift, one department). 2. **Data Recording:** Collect both quantitative data (defect counts, cycle times) and qualitative data (operator feedback, customer reactions). 3. **Issue Logging:** Document all unexpected problems, anomalies, and deviations from the plan. 4. **Standardization (Initial):** Document the exact procedure used so it can be replicated or modified. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Do" precedes "Study":** Many students mistakenly swap these. The "Do" stage is *purely execution* — analysis must wait until all data is collected, otherwise the observer's bias contaminates the experiment (this is the **Hawthorne Effect** in behavioral PDSA, and **Measurement Bias** in physical PDSA). ### 2.1.3 Stage 3 — STUDY (The Analytical Learning Stage) **Objective:** Analyze the data, compare actual vs. predicted results, and draw conclusions. **Operational Sub-Steps:** 1. **Data Analysis:** Apply statistical tools — **Control Charts**, **Pareto Analysis**, **Histograms**, **t-tests**, or **ANOVA**. 2. **Hypothesis Validation:** Determine whether the results support, partially support, or reject the original hypothesis. 3. **Pattern Recognition:** Identify any side effects (both positive and negative) that emerged during the "Do" stage. 4. **Knowledge Documentation:** Convert raw findings into **organizational learning** (lessons learned, best practices). ### 2.1.4 Stage 4 — ACT (The Standardization / Re-Planning Stage) **Objective:** Either institutionalize the change or restart the cycle with refined inputs. **Two Possible Paths:** - **Path A — Adopt (Standardize):** If the change was successful, update the Standard Operating Procedure (SOP), train all staff, and lock in the improvement. - **Path B — Adapt (Iterate):** If the change was unsuccessful or partially successful, return to the "Plan" stage with new insights for a modified test. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Deming's 96% Rule:** Deming famously stated that **94% of process variation is attributable to common causes (the system) and only 6% to special causes (individual workers)**. The PDSA cycle targets the 94% — improvements made at the system level, not by blaming individuals. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Cyclical Nature (KTU-Favorite Theory) The cycle is built on **Shewhart's Theory of Common and Special Causes of Variation**. A single test cannot tell you if the system has fundamentally improved. By repeating the cycle: - **Cycle 1** separates noise from signal. - **Cycle 2** tests sustainability of the improvement. - **Cycle 3+** explores related variables and edge cases. This is analogous to the scientific method in physics — one experiment is never enough; the result must be **reproducible across multiple trials**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Symbol / Term** | **Formula / Definition** | **Typical Unit** | **Engineering Application** | |---|---|---|---| | **Cycle Time (T)** | $T = T_{\text{Plan}} + T_{\text{Do}} + T_{\text{Study}} + T_{\text{Act}}$ | days / weeks | Process re-engineering lead time | | **Improvement Rate (IR)** | $IR = \dfrac{\text{Defect}_{\text{Before}} - \text{Defect}_{\text{After}}}{\text{Defect}_{\text{Before}}} \times 100$ | **%** | Quality cost reduction in manufacturing | | **Statistical Significance** | $p < 0.05$ (95% confidence) | dimensionless | Hypothesis validation in Study stage | | **Plan Execution Fidelity** | $\text{PEF} = \dfrac{\text{Tasks Completed}}{\text{Tasks Planned}} \times 100$ | **% (0–100)** | Lean project management | | **Cumulative Improvement (n cycles)** | $C_n = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - r_i)$ | dimensionless fraction | Multi-cycle Kaizen tracking | | **Cycle Velocity (V)** | $V = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | cycles / day | Agile TQM sprints | | **Deming's Variation Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Special Cause Variation}}{\text{Common Cause Variation}} = \dfrac{6}{94}$ | **%** | Justification for system-level PDSA focus | > [!NOTE] > **Cumulative Improvement Note:** The $\prod$ (product) operator in the Cumulative Improvement formula is critical — improvements are **multiplicative, not additive**, meaning each cycle's residual defect rate multiplies with the next, producing a compounding effect. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Manufacturing:** Reducing tool changeover time (Single Minute Exchange of Die — SMED) on a CNC line. - **Software Engineering:** Iterative sprint reviews in Agile/Scrum are a direct descendant of PDSA. - **Healthcare:** Reducing patient wait time in an emergency department via repeated Plan-Do-Study-Act tests. - **Civil Engineering:** Iterative revision of structural designs based on load-test feedback. - **Service Industry:** Improving airline boarding times by testing new zone-based boarding sequences. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Cumulative Improvement Formula This is the most analytically rigorous formula in PDSA theory and is frequently asked in KTU's 14-mark modules. ### Starting Premise Let $r_i$ be the fractional reduction in defects achieved in the $i$-th cycle. By definition: $$r_i = \frac{D_{i-1} - D_i}{D_{i-1}}$$ where $D_{i-1}$ is the defect count **before** cycle $i$, and $D_i$ is the defect count **after** cycle $i$. ### Step 1 — Express Residual Defect Fraction The fraction of defects *remaining* after cycle $i$ is: $$\text{Residual}_i = 1 - r_i$$ ### Step 2 — Derive Defect Count After Each Cycle After cycle 1: $$D_1 = D_0 \cdot (1 - r_1)$$ After cycle 2: $$D_2 = D_1 \cdot (1 - r_2) = D_0 \cdot (1 - r_1)(1 - r_2)$$ After cycle $n$: $$D_n = D_0 \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - r_i)$$ ### Step 3 — Convert to Cumulative Improvement The total fractional improvement from baseline is: $$C_n = \frac{D_0 - D_n}{D_0} = 1 - \frac{D_n}{D_0}$$ Substituting the expression for $D_n$: $$C_n = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - r_i)$$ ### Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern) **Problem:** A manufacturing line starts with 1000 defects per day. Cycle 1 achieves a 30% reduction, and Cycle 2 achieves a 20% reduction on the remaining defects. Calculate the cumulative improvement. **Solution:** Initial defects: $D_0 = 1000$ After Cycle 1: $D_1 = 1000 \times (1 - 0.30) = 700$ After Cycle 2: $D_2 = 700 \times (1 - 0.20) = 560$ Cumulative Improvement: $$C_2 = 1 - \frac{560}{1000} = 1 - 0.56 = 0.44 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 44\%$$ Using the direct product formula: $$C_2 = 1 - [(1-0.30)(1-0.20)] = 1 - (0.70 \times 0.80) = 1 - 0.56 = 0.44 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 44\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Pitfall:** Students often *add* 30% + 20% = 50% as the cumulative improvement. This is **mathematically wrong**. The correct answer is 44% because improvements compound multiplicatively on the *residual* defect population, not on the original. ## 3.2 Derivation of Plan Execution Fidelity (PEF) Threshold The PEF is a process capability indicator. To determine whether a plan was executed with sufficient rigor, compare PEF against a threshold: $$\text{PEF}_{\text{threshold}} = 1 - \frac{\text{Tasks Modified Mid-Cycle}}{\text{Tasks Planned}} \times 100$$ A PEF above **85%** is the KTU/ASQ industry benchmark for a "well-executed" PDSA pilot. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: PDSA Stage vs. TQM Tool Mapping | **PDSA Stage** | **Primary TQM Tool Used** | **Output Artifact** | **Responsible Role** | |---|---|---|---| | **PLAN** | 5 Whys, Fishbone Diagram, Pareto Chart | Hypothesis Document, Data Collection Plan | Process Owner / Quality Engineer | | **DO** | Gantt Chart, Pilot Run Log | Raw Data Set, Observation Log | Frontline Operators | | **STUDY** | Control Chart, Histogram, t-test, ANOVA | Analysis Report, Lessons Learned | Six Sigma Black Belt / Data Analyst | | **ACT** | Standard Operating Procedure (SOP), Training Matrix | Updated SOP, Training Records | Department Manager | ## 3.4 Decision Logic: Adopt vs. Adapt in the ACT Stage The decision rule in the "Act" stage can be formalized: - **Adopt** if: $C_n \geq C_{\text{target}}$ AND $p < 0.05$ (statistical significance confirmed) - **Adapt** if: $C_n < C_{\text{target}}$ OR $p \geq 0.05$ (insufficient evidence) - **Abandon** if: Adverse side effects exceed the primary improvement benefit (rare, but documented in healthcare PDSA literature) # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The PDSA Cycle — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD stageA["PLAN
Define Problem
Formulate Hypothesis
Set Success Criteria"] stageB["DO
Execute Pilot
Collect Raw Data
Log Observations"] stageC["STUDY
Analyze Data
Validate Hypothesis
Document Lessons"] stageD["ACT
Adopt New Standard
OR Iterate Plan
Train Workforce"] decisionNode{"Was the
change successful?"} stageA ==> stageB stageB ==> stageC stageC ==> decisionNode decisionNode -- Yes Adopt --> stageD decisionNode -- No Adapt --> stageA stageD -.-> stageA ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Sub-Process Flow Inside Each PDSA Stage ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph planSubgraph["PLAN Stage Sub-Processes"] p1["Identify Customer Need"] p2["Define Problem Statement"] p3["Root Cause Analysis
5 Whys / Fishbone"] p4["Write Testable Hypothesis"] p5["Design Data Collection Plan"] p1 --> p2 --> p3 --> p4 --> p5 end subgraph doSubgraph["DO Stage Sub-Processes"] d1["Run Small-Scale Pilot"] d2["Record Quantitative Data"] d3["Record Qualitative Feedback"] d4["Document Anomalies"] d1 --> d2 --> d3 --> d4 end subgraph studySubgraph["STUDY Stage Sub-Processes"] s1["Tabulate Collected Data"] s2["Run Statistical Tests"] s3["Compare Predicted vs Actual"] s4["Identify Side Effects"] s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 end subgraph actSubgraph["ACT Stage Sub-Processes"] a1{"Hypothesis
Validated?"} a2["Update SOP and Standards"] a3["Conduct Workforce Training"] a4["Return to PLAN with Refined Inputs"] a1 -- Yes --> a2 --> a3 a1 -- No --> a4 end p5 --> d1 d4 --> s1 s4 --> a1 a3 --> p1 a4 --> p1 ``` ## 4.3 Cumulative Improvement Spiral Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD cycle0["Baseline
Defect Count = 1000"] cycle1["Cycle 1
30 percent reduction
Defect Count = 700"] cycle2["Cycle 2
20 percent reduction
Defect Count = 560"] cycle3["Cycle 3
15 percent reduction
Defect Count = 476"] cycle4["Cycle 4
10 percent reduction
Defect Count = 428.4"] cycle0 ==> cycle1 cycle1 ==> cycle2 cycle2 ==> cycle3 cycle3 ==> cycle4 cycle4 -.Continuous Spiral.-> cycle0 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Board:** The arrows in the cycle diagrams are deliberately drawn as **single-headed forward arrows** for sequential steps, and **dashed return arrows** for the cyclical re-entry. This is the standard convention used in KTU's prescribed TQM textbook diagrams and is expected in 7-mark descriptive answers. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Part A — Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between the PDCA cycle and the PDSA cycle. Why did W. Edwards Deming prefer the term "Study" over "Check"?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | **Aspect** | **PDCA (ISO 9001)** | **PDSA (Deming / TQM)** | |---|---|---| | **Stage 3 Terminology** | CHECK | STUDY | | **Meaning** | Verify conformity to plan | Analyze causes, patterns, and learn | | **Depth of Analysis** | Conformity-based (pass/fail) | Causal-based (root understanding) | | **Context** | Quality Management System audit | Scientific method of improvement | **Deming's Reason (1 mark):** Deming argued that "Check" implies a simple yes/no verification, which is shallow and does not produce organizational learning. "Study" demands a deeper scientific inquiry into *why* the results occurred, transforming data into actionable knowledge. --- ### Part A — Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: List the four stages of the PDSA cycle and state one TQM tool used in each stage.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **PLAN** — 5 Whys Analysis / Fishbone Diagram 2. **DO** — Pilot Run Data Log / Gantt Chart 3. **STUDY** — Control Chart / Pareto Analysis / t-test 4. **ACT** — Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) / Training Matrix ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) --- ### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: (a) Explain in detail the four stages of the PDSA cycle with a suitable real-world engineering example. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Introduction (1 mark):** The PDSA cycle, popularized by W. Edwards Deming, is a four-stage iterative model for continuous process improvement. It is a closed-loop learning system that transforms empirical observations into standardized improvements. **Stage 1 — PLAN (1.5 marks):** The planning stage begins with a clearly defined problem statement expressed in measurable terms. A hypothesis is formulated, and a data collection plan is designed. For example, in a CNC machining cell, the problem is identified as excessive tool changeover time (currently 14 minutes per change). The hypothesis: "If we implement SMED techniques and pre-stage tools, changeover time can be reduced to under 6 minutes." **Stage 2 — DO (1.5 marks):** The change is piloted on a single CNC machine for one week. Data on actual changeover times, operator delays, and tool-availability issues is logged systematically. Qualitative observations (operator feedback on workflow, ergonomics) are also recorded. **Stage 3 — STUDY (1.5 marks):** The collected data is plotted on a control chart and a before-vs-after histogram. Statistical comparison (e.g., paired t-test) is performed to validate the hypothesis. The pilot data shows an average changeover time of 5.8 minutes — a 58.6% reduction, statistically significant at p = 0.012. **Stage 4 — ACT (1.5 marks):** Since the hypothesis was validated, the SMED procedure is codified into the cell's Standard Operating Procedure. All machine operators are retrained. The cycle then restarts with a new target: reducing tool changeover to under 4 minutes. --- **(b) A manufacturing process starts with 800 defects per shift. Cycle 1 of PDSA achieves a 25% reduction and Cycle 2 achieves a 15% reduction on the remaining defects. Calculate: (i) Cumulative improvement percentage, (ii) Defect count after 3 more cycles, each achieving 10% reduction. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given:** $D_0 = 800$ defects/shift $r_1 = 0.25$, $r_2 = 0.15$, $r_3 = r_4 = r_5 = 0.10$ **Part (i) — Cumulative improvement after 2 cycles (3 marks):** $$D_1 = 800 \times (1 - 0.25) = 800 \times 0.75 = 600 \text{ defects}$$ $$D_2 = 600 \times (1 - 0.15) = 600 \times 0.85 = 510 \text{ defects}$$ $$C_2 = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{2}(1-r_i) = 1 - (0.75 \times 0.85) = 1 - 0.6375 = 0.3625$$ **Cumulative improvement = 36.25%** **Part (ii) — Defects after 3 more cycles (4 marks):** $$D_3 = 510 \times 0.90 = 459 \text{ defects}$$ $$D_4 = 459 \times 0.90 = 413.1 \text{ defects}$$ $$D_5 = 413.1 \times 0.90 = 371.79 \text{ defects}$$ **Final defect count = 371.79 ≈ 372 defects per shift** **Overall cumulative improvement across 5 cycles:** $$C_5 = 1 - (0.75 \times 0.85 \times 0.90^3) = 1 - (0.75 \times 0.85 \times 0.729) = 1 - 0.4647 = 0.5353$$ **Overall improvement = 53.53%** --- ### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: (a) "PDSA is the engine of continuous improvement." Discuss this statement with reference to Deming's philosophy and the concept of common vs. special cause variation. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Statement Justification (2 marks):** The PDSA cycle operationalizes Deming's belief that quality improvement is not a destination but a never-ending journey. Unlike one-time quality audits, PDSA institutionalizes experimentation as a daily organizational habit. **Deming's Philosophy (2.5 marks):** Deming's 14 Points emphasize that **94% of process variation is attributable to common causes (system, process design) and only 6% to special causes (individual workers or one-off events).** PDSA targets the 94% by focusing on system redesign, not on blaming individuals. This shifts the organization's mindset from inspection to prevention, and from reaction to prediction. **Common vs. Special Cause Variation in PDSA (2.5 marks):** - **Special cause variation** appears as an outlier on a control chart (e.g., one defective batch due to a faulty raw material shipment). PDSA's "Do" stage may reveal this immediately, and the "Act" stage corrects it without changing the system. - **Common cause variation** is the inherent noise of the process (e.g., natural fluctuation in tolerances). PDSA's iterative cycles are essential here — only by repeated experimentation and standard adjustment can this 94% variation be gradually reduced. **Conclusion:** The PDSA cycle is indeed the "engine" because it is the only systematic method that both *reacts* to special causes and *progressively attacks* common causes, ensuring cumulative and sustainable improvement. --- **(b) A hospital emergency department wants to reduce average patient waiting time from 90 minutes to below 60 minutes using the PDSA cycle. Design a complete PDSA plan for this problem. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Stage 1 — PLAN (2 marks):** - **Problem Statement:** Average waiting time is 90 minutes; target is <60 minutes. - **Hypothesis:** "If we introduce a triage-based fast-track system for non-critical cases, average waiting time will drop by 30%." - **Data Collection Plan:** Track patient arrival time, triage category, and discharge time for 4 weeks. Use a tally sheet at the registration desk. - **Success Criteria:** Average waiting time < 60 minutes with p < 0.05. **Stage 2 — DO (1.5 marks):** - Implement the fast-track system on weekday evenings (7 PM – 11 PM) for 4 weeks. - Two triage nurses will categorize incoming patients into "Critical" and "Non-Critical." - Non-critical patients are routed to a separate fast-track doctor. - Daily logs are maintained with timestamps and patient counts. **Stage 3 — STUDY (2 marks):** - After 4 weeks, the data is tabulated and a control chart of average waiting time per day is plotted. - A two-sample t-test is run comparing pre-intervention vs. post-intervention waiting times. - Results: Average waiting time drops to 58 minutes (35.6% reduction), p = 0.008 (statistically significant). - Side effect identified: Fast-track doctor reports higher fatigue due to higher patient throughput — noted for future cycles. **Stage 4 — ACT (1.5 marks):** - Since the change is successful, the fast-track system is extended to all daytime hours. - The SOP for triage is updated and all nurses undergo a 2-day retraining. - A new PDSA cycle is initiated targeting: "Reduce fast-track doctor's fatigue by adding a relief nurse during peak hours." --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do not write "PDCA" and "PDSA" interchangeably** without acknowledging Deming's distinction. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this oversight. > 2. **In numerical questions, never add the percentages** (e.g., 25% + 15% ≠ 40%). Always use the multiplicative product formula. This is the **#1 cause of full-mark loss** in numerical PDSA problems. > 3. **Always include a "return arrow"** in the cycle diagram. Drawing PDSA as a linear 4-step sequence with no loop loses 1 mark. > 4. **Avoid the word "Check"** in the third stage description — use "Study" or "Analyze" exclusively. > 5. **Real-world examples must be engineering-relevant** for KTU B.Tech valuation. Generic "company" examples without specifying a domain (manufacturing, software, healthcare) lose marks. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:** - ✅ PDSA = **Plan, Do, Study, Act** (in this exact order). - ✅ Originated by **Walter Shewhart**; popularized by **W. Edwards Deming**. - ✅ Deming changed "Check" to "Study" in **1986** to emphasize deeper learning. - ✅ The cycle is a **closed loop** — always draw a return arrow from ACT back to PLAN. - ✅ **94% common cause / 6% special cause** variation is Deming's foundational ratio. - ✅ Cumulative improvement is **multiplicative**, not additive: $C_n = 1 - \prod(1 - r_i)$. - ✅ PLAN uses **5 Whys, Fishbone, Pareto**; STUDY uses **Control Charts, t-tests**. - ✅ DO stage is **pilot-scale** only; ACT stage either **adopts** (SOP update) or **adapts** (restart). - ✅ PDSA is the foundation of **Kaizen**, **Six Sigma DMAIC**, and **Agile Scrum**. - ✅ **Statistical significance threshold:** $p < 0.05$ for hypothesis validation. - ✅ **Plan Execution Fidelity benchmark:** > 85% for a well-executed pilot. - ✅ The cycle time $T$ is the sum of all four stage durations and is measured in **days or weeks**. - ✅ The "Study" stage must include **side-effect analysis**, not just primary outcome analysis. - ✅ PDSA's purpose is **system-level improvement** (94%), not **individual-level blame** (6%).

topics.title: Kaizen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Kaizen # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Kaizen (改善)** is a Japanese management philosophy and methodology meaning **"Change for the Better"** (改 = *Kai* = Change, 善 = *Zen* = Good/Better). In the context of Total Quality Management, Kaizen is defined as a **continuous, incremental, and systematic process of improvement** involving all employees — from top management to shop-floor workers. It was popularized globally by **Toyota Production System (TPS)** pioneers and later formalized by **Masaaki Imai** in his landmark book *"Kaizen: The Key to Japan's Competitive Success"* (1986). In the KTU 2024 TQM syllabus, Kaizen is positioned as the foundational operating philosophy that drives the **PDCA (Deming) Cycle**, **Just-in-Time (JIT)** systems, and **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡 > [!NOTE] > **The "1% Better Every Day" Analogy:** > > Imagine a student who decides to improve by just **1% every single day** — studying 10 extra minutes, solving 2 extra problems, reviewing 1 concept before sleep. After 1 year, this student is **37 times better** (compound effect: 1.01^365 ≈ 37.78). Kaizen applies the **exact same compound logic** to an organization. Instead of waiting for a big, disruptive "breakthrough innovation" (which Kaizen calls *Kaikaku*), it encourages **small, daily, low-cost improvements by every employee** that accumulate into massive long-term transformation. Another real-world analogy: think of Kaizen as the **"fitness gym for organizations."** Just as a body becomes strong through daily exercise, an organization becomes world-class through daily improvements suggested by **everyone** — not just managers or engineers. ## 1.3 Core Terminology Standardized by KTU | Term | Meaning | KTU Significance | |---|---|---| | **Gemba** | The "actual place" where work happens (shop floor) | Foundation of observation-based improvement | | **Muda** | Waste (any activity that consumes resources without creating value) | 7 Wastes (TIMWOOD) | | **Gemba Walks** | Management physically visiting the workplace to observe | Leadership commitment | | **Kaizen Event / Blitz** | A focused, short-duration (3–5 day) intensive improvement project | Rapid, team-based problem solving | | **5S** | Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain | Foundation of workplace organization | | **Kaikaku** | Radical, breakthrough change | Contrast to Kaizen | | **Sensei** | Master / Teacher who guides Kaizen thinking | Cultural transfer mechanism | ## 1.4 The KTU Standard Definition Statement (For Exams) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board-Ready Definition (Memorize):** > *"Kaizen is a Japanese philosophy of continuous improvement in which all employees — managers and workers alike — are encouraged to identify and implement small, incremental improvements in their work processes on a continuous basis, focusing on the elimination of waste (Muda) and the standardization of best practices."* --- # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Kaizen (Imai's Framework) Masaaki Imai's framework divides all business activities into two categories: 1. **Maintenance (維持) — "Maintain"** - Preserving current standards through discipline, training, and routine control. - Establishes the baseline. - Example: Following Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) without deviation. 2. **Improvement (改善) — "Kaizen"** - Continuously raising the established standards. - Identifies problems at the **Gemba** and eliminates root causes. - Example: Reducing setup time from 15 minutes to 12 minutes via SMED. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Key Insight:** Most organizations invest 95% in **Maintenance** and only 5% in **Improvement**. World-class organizations (Toyota, Toyota, Tesla production lines) invert this ratio, investing significantly in Kaizen to stay ahead. ## 2.2 The 5 Founding Principles of Kaizen | # | Principle | Explanation | Real-World Engineering Example | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Know your Customer** | Identify what *value* means to the customer (internal & external) | A CNC machine shop learns that customers value "on-time delivery" more than lowest cost | | 2 | **Let it Flow — Eliminate Muda** | Make value flow without interruptions, inventory, or rework | Implementing a single-piece flow assembly line | | 3 | **Go to Gemba** | All problems and solutions are found at the actual workplace | Manager walks the shop floor daily instead of staying in cabin | | 4 | **Empower People / 5 Whys** | Workers closest to the process know it best; ask "Why?" 5 times | "Why did the motor fail?" → "Why?" → ... → Root cause found | | 5 | **Data-Driven Management (PDCA)** | Manage by facts, not opinions; use Plan-Do-Check-Act | Using SPC charts to monitor process stability | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Memorize for Numerical Sub-Questions) > [!IMPORTANT] > Kaizen is a **philosophy**, not a numerical subject. However, KTU examiners often ask **numerical sub-questions** based on the *cumulative/compound impact* of small improvements. The following are the only standardized formulas you need: | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | KTU Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Kaizen Improvement Factor (KIF)** | $KIF = \dfrac{\text{New Performance}}{\text{Old Performance}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | $KIF > 1$ means improvement | | **Cumulative Kaizen Multiplier (1% rule)** | $C = (1 + r)^n$ | $r$ = daily improvement rate (decimal), $n$ = number of days | Compound effect of small daily gains | | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) — Kaizen target** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality (all as fractions) | World-class $OEE \geq 85\%$ | | **Defect Rate Reduction (Kaizen goal)** | $DR_{\text{new}} = DR_{\text{old}} \times (1 - r_{\text{reduction}})$ | $DR$ = defects per million, $r_{\text{reduction}}$ = improvement ratio | Six Sigma target: $3.4$ DPMO | | **Cycle Time Reduction** | $CT_{\text{new}} = CT_{\text{old}} - \Delta t$ | $CT$ in minutes/seconds | Lean line balancing | | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $COPQ = C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}$ | All in monetary units (₹ or $) | Justifies Kaizen ROI | ## 2.4 The 7 Wastes (Muda) of Kaizen — TIMWOOD > [!WARNING] > **Most-missed KTU question:** *"List the 7 wastes of Kaizen."* Memorize the mnemonic **TIMWOOD**: | Letter | Waste (Japanese) | Engineering Translation | |---|---|---| | **T** | Transportation | Unnecessary movement of materials | | **I** | Inventory | Excess raw material, WIP, finished goods | | **M** | Motion | Worker movement that adds no value | | **W** | Waiting | Idle time of machines/people | | **O** | Overproduction | Producing more than customer needs (worst waste) | | **O** | Over-processing | More work than required (e.g., excess precision) | | **D** | Defects | Rework, scrap, warranty claims | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Update:** A frequently-tested **8th waste** is **"Unused Employee Creativity" (Skills Underutilization)** — meaning organizations that fail to tap into worker ideas for Kaizen are wasting their most valuable resource. ## 2.5 The 5 Whys — Root Cause Analysis (KTU Favourite) The 5 Whys is a Toyota-developed root-cause tool, perfectly aligned with Kaizen. Example walkthrough: > **Problem:** Machine stops on production line. > 1. **Why?** → The fuse blew. > 2. **Why?** → The motor was overloaded. > 3. **Why?** → The bearing was not lubricated enough. > 4. **Why?** → The lubrication pump was not working properly. > 5. **Why?** → The pump shaft was worn out and not replaced as part of preventive maintenance. > > **Root Cause:** No preventive maintenance schedule. **Kaizen Countermeasure:** Establish a TPM schedule. ## 2.6 Kaizen Event / Kaizen Blitz — The Project Format A typical **5-day Kaizen Event** follows this structure: | Day | Activity | Outcome | |---|---|---| | 1 | Define problem, map current state (Value Stream Map) | Problem statement signed off | | 2 | Brainstorm root causes (5 Whys, Fishbone) | Validated root cause | | 3 | Develop counter-measures; pilot on small scale | Working prototype | | 4 | Implement full-scale; document new SOP | Standardized new process | | 5 | Present results to management; celebrate | Kaizen closed + new baseline | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition to Memorize:** > *"A Kaizen Event is a focused, short-term (typically 3–5 days), cross-functional project where a small team uses Lean tools to rapidly improve a specific process, deliver measurable results, and create a new standard."* --- # 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Mathematical Proof: The Power of 1% (Kaizen Compound Effect) This is a **commonly-asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU 2024 ESE. > **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Question):** > *"A manufacturing process produces 1,000 units/day. Through a Kaizen initiative, the team commits to a 1% daily improvement in output. Calculate the cumulative output after 30 days, 90 days, and 1 year. Comment on the result in the context of Kaizen philosophy."* ### Given Data $$ \begin{aligned} P_0 &= 1000 \text{ units/day} \quad \text{(Initial output, day 0 baseline)} \\ r &= 0.01 \quad \text{(Daily Kaizen improvement rate = 1\%)} \\ n &= \text{Number of days} \\ P_n &= \text{Output on day } n \end{aligned} $$ ### Assumed Model Kaizen's compound effect follows the standard **geometric/exponential growth model** (valid only for small, sustainable, non-saturating improvements — a critical caveat to state in exams): $$ P_n = P_0 \times (1 + r)^n $$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Day 30 calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{30} &= 1000 \times (1 + 0.01)^{30} \\ &= 1000 \times (1.01)^{30} \\ &= 1000 \times 1.3478 \\ &= 1347.8 \text{ units/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Day 90 calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{90} &= 1000 \times (1.01)^{90} \\ &= 1000 \times 2.4486 \\ &= 2448.6 \text{ units/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Day 365 (1 year) calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{365} &= 1000 \times (1.01)^{365} \\ &= 1000 \times 37.7834 \\ &= 37783.4 \text{ units/day} \end{aligned} $$ ### Interpretation (MUST be written in answer) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Growth after 1 year} &= \frac{P_{365} - P_0}{P_0} \times 100\% \\ &= \frac{37783.4 - 1000}{1000} \times 100\% \\ &= 3678.34\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion to Write in Exam:** > *"A mere 1% daily improvement compounded over one year produces a **37.78x (≈3678%) increase** in output. This mathematically demonstrates the core Kaizen philosophy: **small, continuous, incremental improvements**, sustained over time, can produce **dramatic transformational results** — far exceeding the impact of any single large, disruptive innovation. This is the mathematical justification for Kaizen over 'big-bang' change initiatives."* ### Critical Caveat for Top Marks > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Expectation:** State that the $(1+r)^n$ model **assumes sustainable 1% improvement with no saturation, no learning curve plateau, and no resource constraints**. In reality, as $n \to \infty$, improvement is **S-shaped (logistic)**, not purely exponential. Mention the **Logistic Growth Model** to score full 14 marks: $$ P_n = \frac{K}{1 + \left(\frac{K - P_0}{P_0}\right) e^{-k n}} $$ where $K$ is the **carrying capacity** (maximum sustainable output) and $k$ is the Kaizen learning rate constant. ## 3.2 OEE Calculation — A KTU Numerical Favourite > **Problem:** A CNC machine operates 8 hours/day, 5 days/week. Availability = 85%, Performance = 90%, Quality = 95%. Calculate OEE and identify the Kaizen improvement opportunity. ### Step 1 — Compute OEE $$ \begin{aligned} OEE &= A \times P \times Q \\ &= 0.85 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 \\ &= 0.7267 \\ &= 72.67\% \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Identify the Gap $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Gap to World-Class} &= 85\% - 72.67\% = 12.33\% \\ \text{Annual loss (assuming 240 working days, 8 hrs/day)} &= (12.33\% \times 8 \times 240) \\ &= 236.7 \text{ hours/year} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Kaizen Countermeasure > [!NOTE] > **To write in the model answer:** > *"The 12.33% gap represents **Muda** (waste) that can be eliminated through targeted Kaizen: reducing changeover time (SMED), eliminating minor stoppages, and improving first-pass yield via Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing)."* ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Kaizen vs. Kaikaku (Common 7-Mark Question) | Dimension | **Kaizen (改善)** | **Kaikaku (改革)** | |---|---|---| | **Meaning** | Continuous, incremental improvement | Radical, revolutionary change | | **Approach** | Small steps, evolutionary | Big steps, disruptive | | **Risk** | Very low | High | | **Cost** | Minimal (uses existing resources) | Heavy (new equipment, new systems) | | **Speed of Result** | Slow but compounding | Fast but one-time | | **Employee Involvement** | All employees | Top management / specialized teams | | **Typical Tools** | 5S, 5 Whys, PDCA, Kanban | Business Process Re-engineering, BPR | | **Examples** | Toyota Production System | Ford's assembly line, Kodak → digital | | **KTU Mnemonic** | "Many small drops fill the ocean" | "One big wave reshapes the shore" | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Tip:** A perfect 7-mark answer for *"Compare Kaizen and Kaikaku"* should contain a **tabular comparison (6 marks) + 1 concluding sentence** stating: *"Kaizen is the **default operating mode** of any world-class TQM organization, while Kaikaku is reserved for **survival situations** or when a paradigm shift is technologically forced."* --- # 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Kaizen PDCA Wheel (Continuous Improvement Cycle) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA(["🚀 START
Define Vision & Baseline"]) --> nodeB["📋 PLAN
Identify Muda in Gemba
Set Kaizen targets"] nodeB --> nodeC["⚙️ DO
Pilot small improvement
Execute on shop floor"] nodeC --> nodeD["🔍 CHECK
Measure results vs. baseline
Use SPC, OEE, KPI"] nodeD --> nodeE["📈 ACT
Standardize if successful
Adopt or restart cycle"] nodeE --> nodeB nodeD -- "If target not met" --> nodeB nodeE -- "New standard becomes new baseline" --> nodeA style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeB fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeD fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeE fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Notice the **self-feeding loop** from ACT back to PLAN. This is the heart of Kaizen — the "new standard" achieved in ACT becomes the new PLAN baseline, ensuring the wheel **never stops turning**. KTU examiners often draw this wheel and ask: *"Which phase closes the loop?"* — The correct answer is **ACT**, because it **standardizes** the improvement into the new baseline. ## 4.2 Diagram 2: The 5S — Foundation of Kaizen ```mermaid flowchart LR s1["1️⃣ SORT
Seiri
Remove unused items"] --> s2["2️⃣ SET IN ORDER
Seiton
A place for everything"] s2 --> s3["3️⃣ SHINE
Seiso
Clean workplace daily"] s3 --> s4["4️⃣ STANDARDIZE
Seiketsu
Document best practices"] s4 --> s5["5️⃣ SUSTAIN
Shitsuke
Discipline to maintain"] s5 --> s1 style s1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style s2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style s3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style s4 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style s5 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **5S is the FOUNDATION of Kaizen** — without a clean, organized, standardized workplace, no improvement can be sustained. KTU often asks: *"Explain how 5S enables Kaizen."* Answer: *"5S eliminates the visual chaos that hides Muda; once the workplace is organized, every worker can see waste, anomalies, and improvement opportunities instantly."* ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Kaizen Event ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IN["INPUT STAGE - Day 1"] i1["Problem Definition Charter"] --> i2["Current State Value Stream Map"] i2 --> i3["Baseline KPI Measurement"] end subgraph AN["ANALYSIS STAGE - Day 2"] a1["5 Whys Root Cause"] --> a2["Fishbone Ishikawa Diagram"] a2 --> a3["Pareto Analysis 80-20"] end subgraph EX["EXECUTION STAGE - Day 3-4"] e1["Pilot Countermeasure"] --> e2["Full-Scale Implementation"] e2 --> e3["New SOP Documentation"] end subgraph OUT["OUTPUT STAGE - Day 5"] o1["Before vs After KPI Comparison"] --> o2["Management Presentation"] o2 --> o3["New Baseline Locked"] end IN --> AN AN --> EX EX --> OUT OUT -- "Feeds back to next Kaizen event" --> IN style IN fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px style AN fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style EX fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#008000,stroke-width:2px style OUT fill:#FFE6F0,stroke:#CC0066,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block Diagram Reading Guide for KTU Students:** > - **INPUT** = "Where are we today?" (Gemba observation, VSM, baseline metrics) > - **ANALYSIS** = "Why are we here?" (Root cause analysis using 5 Whys + Ishikawa) > - **EXECUTION** = "Where do we go?" (Pilot → Scale → Standardize) > - **OUTPUT** = "How do we know we improved?" (KPI delta, Management buy-in) > - **Feedback Loop** = "Continuous improvement is never finished — the new baseline is the next Kaizen's starting point." ## 4.4 Diagram 4: The 7 Wastes (TIMWOOD) Categorization ```mermaid mindmap root((TIMWOOD
7 Wastes
of KAIZEN)) T1[Transportation] I1[Inventory] M1[Motion] W1[Waiting] O1[Overproduction] O2[Over Processing] D1[Defects] E1[Unused Employee Creativity - 8th Waste] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Mindmap Note:** Some KTU exam aspirants may not have a Mermaid renderer that supports mindmaps. If the diagram does not render in your study app, draw a **circular wheel with 7 spokes**, each labeled T, I, M, W, O, O, D, and add an outer ring labeled "Unnecessary activities that the customer will not pay for." --- # 📙 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions (Direct Recall) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define Kaizen. Mention any two key principles of Kaizen.** > **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Kaizen is a Japanese management philosophy meaning "continuous improvement." It refers to the ongoing, incremental improvement of products, services, and processes by all employees — from top management to shop-floor workers — through small, low-cost, data-driven changes. > > **Principle 1 (0.75 mark):** *Go to Gemba* — Problems and their solutions are found at the actual workplace where value is created.
