Managers must observe operations first-hand rather than rely on reports. > > **Principle 2 (0.75 mark):** *Eliminate Muda (Waste)* — All activities that do not add customer value must be identified and eliminated. The 7 wastes are remembered via the mnemonic **TIMWOOD**: Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects. ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **What is a 5S system? List the five S's in order.** > **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > > **Definition (1 mark):** The 5S system is a workplace organization methodology that forms the foundational discipline for any Kaizen initiative. It originates from five Japanese words beginning with "S" and creates a clean, orderly, standardized environment. > > **The 5 S's in order (2 marks):** > 1. **Seiri (Sort)** — Remove all unnecessary items from the workplace. > 2. **Seiton (Set in Order)** — Arrange items so they are easy to find, use, and return. > 3. **Seiso (Shine)** — Clean the workplace and equipment daily. > 4. **Seiketsu (Standardize)** — Document procedures to maintain the first 3 S's consistently. > 5. **Shitsuke (Sustain)** — Build discipline and habit so 5S becomes a way of working life. --- ## 5.2 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question is 14 marks, with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. You must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B (full internal choice within the same module). --- ### **🔥 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]** **Q.A) (a) Explain the concept of Kaizen in detail. Discuss the role of Gemba and 5 Whys in implementing Kaizen. (7 Marks)** **Q.A) (b) Distinguish between Kaizen and Kaikaku with a suitable example from a manufacturing industry. (7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Concept of Kaizen + Role of Gemba + 5 Whys (7 Marks)** **Definition of Kaizen (2 marks):** Kaizen is a Japanese term meaning "change for the better" or "continuous improvement." In Total Quality Management, it refers to a philosophy of making small, continuous, incremental improvements in all functions of an organization — production, marketing, finance, HR — at zero or minimal cost. The philosophy was articulated by **Masaaki Imai** in 1986 and forms the operational backbone of the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**. It relies on three pillars: **(i) Housekeeping (5S), (ii) Waste Elimination (Muda), and (iii) Standardization (SOPs)**. **Role of Gemba (2.5 marks):** "Gemba" is the Japanese word for the "actual place" where work happens — the shop floor, the hospital ward, the software developer's desk. The Gemba principle in Kaizen states that **all real problems, root causes, and improvement opportunities exist at the Gemba, not in boardrooms.** Managers practicing Kaizen conduct **"Gemba Walks"** — physically walking the workplace, observing processes, talking to workers, and asking questions. This: - Brings leadership closer to reality. - Empowers workers by valuing their frontline knowledge. - Identifies hidden Muda that statistical reports cannot reveal. **Role of 5 Whys (2.5 marks):** The 5 Whys technique, developed by **Sakichi Toyoda** (Toyota's founder), is the analytical engine of Kaizen. When a problem occurs, the team repeatedly asks **"Why?"** at least 5 times to peel away symptoms and reach the **root cause**. Unlike a single "Why" (which stops at a symptom), the iterative 5 Whys converts a surface-level fault into a systemic cause. Example: A defect was found (Why?) → machine was misaligned (Why?) → jig was loose (Why?) → bolts were missing (Why?) → bolts were not in the standard toolkit (Why?) → **no standard kit defined for changeover.** The 5 Whys thus drives Kaizen's culture of **root-cause thinking** rather than blame-fixing. **(b) Kaizen vs. Kaikaku with Manufacturing Example (7 Marks)** **Tabular Comparison (5 marks):** | Dimension | Kaizen | Kaikaku | |---|---|---| | Scale | Small, incremental, daily | Radical, breakthrough, one-shot | | Cost | Minimal | Heavy capital investment | | Risk | Low | High | | Employee Involvement | All employees, bottom-up | Top-down, specialist teams | | Tools | 5S, 5 Whys, PDCA, Kanban | BPR, Automation, New Technology | | Time Horizon | Continuous, long-term | Discrete, short-burst | | Culture | Evolutionary, organic | Revolutionary, disruptive | **Manufacturing Example — Automobile Assembly Plant (2 marks):** In a Maruti Suzuki assembly line: - A **Kaizen** project would reduce **bolt-tightening cycle time** from 45 seconds to 42 seconds by repositioning the tool tray within the worker's reach (no investment, just 5S + motion study). - A **Kaikaku** project would replace the entire manual bolt-tightening station with an **automated robotic torque arm**, requiring a ₹50 lakh investment and 6 months of installation. **Conclusion (built into comparison):** Kaizen is the **default continuous-improvement mode**; Kaikaku is **triggered only when the technology or market demands a paradigm shift** (e.g., transition from petrol to EV assembly). --- ### **🔥 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]** **Q.B) (a) Explain the Kaizen Event (Kaizen Blitz) methodology with its typical 5-day structure. Mention the deliverables of each day. (7 Marks)** **Q.B) (b) A small-scale bakery produces 500 loaves/day. Through a Kaizen initiative, the team commits to a 1% daily improvement in output. Calculate the production after 30 days, 90 days, and 365 days. Comment on the result in the context of Kaizen philosophy. (7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Kaizen Event / Kaizen Blitz Methodology (7 Marks)** **Definition (1.5 marks):** A **Kaizen Event** (also called a **Kaizen Blitz** or **Rapid Improvement Workshop**) is a focused, short-duration (typically **3 to 5 working days**), cross-functional team-based workshop designed to rapidly improve a specific, well-defined process. It was popularized by **Toyota** and is now a core Lean Six Sigma / TQM tool. Unlike traditional Kaizen (which is continuous and ongoing), a Kaizen Event is **intensive, time-boxed, and outcome-driven**. **5-Day Structure (5 marks — 1 mark per day):** **Day 1 — Define & Map:** The team defines the problem statement, scope, and target KPI. They draw the **Current State Value Stream Map (VSM)**, walk the Gemba, and capture baseline metrics (cycle time, defect rate, downtime). *Deliverable: Signed-off problem charter + Current State VSM + baseline data.* **Day 2 — Analyze Root Cause:** The team uses **5 Whys, Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram, and Pareto analysis** to drill down to the root cause(s) of the problem. They validate root causes with on-floor data. *Deliverable: Validated root-cause list + Fishbone diagram + Pareto chart.* **Day 3 — Develop & Pilot Countermeasures:** The team brainstorms solutions, ranks them by impact and ease (Effort-Impact Matrix), and **pilots the chosen countermeasure on a small scale**. Quick wins are implemented immediately. *Deliverable: Pilot results + before-after comparison snapshot.* **Day 4 — Implement & Standardize:** The successful countermeasure is **rolled out to the full process**. New Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) are written, visual controls are installed, and operators are trained. The 5S standard is updated. *Deliverable: New SOP + 5S layout + trained workforce + updated standard work.* **Day 5 — Present & Celebrate:** The team presents **Before-After KPI comparison** to top management using a structured A3 report / PowerPoint. A small celebration (lunch, certificate) recognizes the team's effort. The new baseline is locked in, and the **next Kaizen topic** is identified. *Deliverable: A3 report + management sign-off + new baseline + next Kaizen charter.* **Why it works:** Time-boxing forces urgency, cross-functional membership ensures 360° perspective, and the celebration builds a **Kaizen culture** where employees want to participate in the next event. **(b) Compound Kaizen Calculation (7 Marks)** **Given Data:** $$ P_0 = 500 \text{ loaves/day}, \quad r = 0.01, \quad n = 30, 90, 365 $$ **Formula (Kaizen compound model):** $$ P_n = P_0 \times (1 + r)^n $$ **Calculation 1 — After 30 days:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{30} &= 500 \times (1.01)^{30} \\ &= 500 \times 1.3478 \\ &= 673.9 \text{ loaves/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Calculation 2 — After 90 days:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{90} &= 500 \times (1.01)^{90} \\ &= 500 \times 2.4486 \\ &= 1224.3 \text{ loaves/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Calculation 3 — After 365 days:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{365} &= 500 \times (1.01)^{365} \\ &= 500 \times 37.7834 \\ &= 18891.7 \text{ loaves/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Comment on the Result (2 marks):** The compound Kaizen model reveals that a **modest 1% daily improvement**, when sustained consistently for one year, transforms **500 loaves/day → 18,891 loaves/day** — a **37.78x (≈3678%) increase**. This mathematically demonstrates the central tenet of Kaizen philosophy: **small, daily, incremental improvements are exponentially more powerful in the long run than any single large, disruptive change.** For the bakery, this could mean: lower per-unit cost, higher market share, and the financial cushion to eventually invest in Kaikaku-level automation — **all without a single rupee of capital expenditure, only daily attention to process improvement.** > [!NOTE] > **Critical Examiner Expectation (1 mark):** Briefly mention the **logistic ceiling** — in real life, output cannot grow to infinity; physical capacity (ovens, staff, market demand) acts as the **carrying capacity $K$** in the logistic model. Acknowledge this for full marks. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes on Kaizen Questions (Read Carefully):** > > 1. **Confusing Kaizen with Six Sigma:** Six Sigma is a *statistical, project-based* methodology targeting 3.4 DPMO. **Kaizen is a continuous philosophy** that *encompasses and uses* tools like 5S, 5 Whys, PDCA, Kanban, Poka-Yoke. Don't equate them. > > 2. **Forgetting the Japanese terminology:** Writing only the English words and not mentioning **Muda, Gemba, Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke, Kanban, Poka-Yoke** will cost you 1–2 marks per question. Examiners specifically check for Japanese vocabulary. > > 3. **Writing "Kaizen = innovation":** WRONG. Kaizen is **continuous, incremental improvement**. Innovation / Kaikaku is **radical, disruptive change**. This distinction is the single most-tested 7-mark topic. > > 4. **Skipping the Employee Involvement angle:** Many answers describe Kaizen as a *management tool*. Examiners expect you to emphasize that **Kaizen is a people-driven philosophy — every worker, from CEO to sweeper, is a Kaizen contributor.** Omit this and you lose 2 marks. > > 5. **In numerical questions, not mentioning the logistic ceiling:** If the question is on the 1% rule, you must state that the **exponential model is an idealization** and that **practical output is bounded by a carrying capacity $K$**. This shows "engineering judgment" and earns the full 14 marks. > > 6. **Memorizing but not applying:** Don't just list the 7 Wastes (TIMWOOD) — be ready to **identify which waste is present in a given scenario** the examiner describes. KTU 2024 papers increasingly use **case-based** sub-questions. --- ## 5.4 ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **🎯 KTU 2024 — KAIZEN — 5-MINUTE RAPID REVISION SHEET** ### 🔑 **Core Identity** - Kaizen = 改善 = "Change for the Better" = **Continuous, Incremental, Employee-driven Improvement**. - Pioneered by **Toyota**; formalized by **Masaaki Imai (1986)**. - **Two Pillars:** Maintenance (preserve standards) + Improvement (raise standards). ### 🔑 **Key Japanese Terms (Board-Exam Vocabulary)** - **Gemba** = Actual workplace - **Muda** = Waste (7 Wastes — **TIMWOOD** + 8th = Unused Creativity) - **5 Whys** = Root cause analysis - **5S** = Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke - **PDCA** = Plan-Do-Check-Act - **Kanban** = Visual signal for pull production - **Poka-Yoke** = Mistake-proofing - **Jidoka** = Autonomation (build quality in) - **Heijunka** = Production leveling ### 🔑 **Core Formulas (Numerical Sub-Questions)** - $P_n = P_0 \times (1 + r)^n$ → Compound Kaizen (1% rule) - $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ → World-class target ≥ 85% - $COPQ = C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{failure}}$ → Justifies Kaizen ROI ### 🔑 **5 Founding Principles** 1. Know your Customer 2. Eliminate Muda — Let it Flow 3. Go to Gemba 4. Empower People / 5 Whys 5. Data-Driven PDCA Management ### 🔑 **Kaizen Event (Blitz) — 5-Day Format** - **D1:** Define + VSM + Baseline - **D2:** Root cause (5 Whys + Fishbone) - **D3:** Pilot countermeasure - **D4:** Implement + Standardize (SOP) - **D5:** Present + Celebrate + New Baseline ### 🔑 **Kaizen vs. Kaikaku — The Distinction** - **Kaizen = many small drops fill the ocean** (Toyota Way) - **Kaikaku = one big wave reshapes the shore** (Ford, BPR) ### 🔑 **Golden Rule for KTU Answers** > *"Kaizen is not a project — it is a **way of life** in a world-class organization. It is the **default operating mode** of any TQM-implementing company."* ---

topics.title: Supplier Partnership - Partnering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Supplier Partnership - Partnering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**, *Supplier Partnership* (also termed *Partnering*) refers to a **long-term, cooperative, win-win contractual relationship** between a buyer (purchasing organization) and a supplier, built on mutual trust, shared vision, open communication, and joint commitment to continuous improvement of quality, cost, delivery, and process capability. It moves the relationship away from the adversarial, price-driven, short-term "arm's-length" purchase contract toward an integrated *supply chain alliance*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> *Partnering is the process of establishing a long-term win-win relationship with suppliers based on mutual trust, openness, shared risk, and shared rewards, so as to jointly create value for the customer.*

The **supplier here is treated as an extension of the buyer's own organization** — not as an outside vendor — and is included in early product design, process improvement, cost-reduction, and quality initiatives.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> 🍕 **The Pizza Shop Analogy — Easy Way to Remember Partnering**

Imagine two models of buying pizza for a college fest:

- **Model A (Traditional / Adversarial):** You call 10 different shops daily, negotiate hard on price, accept the cheapest, complain if the pizza is bad, and switch the next day. The shop treats you as a stranger and gives you the minimum acceptable pizza. **No trust, no cooperation, no loyalty.**
- **Model B (Partnering):** You pick *one* good pizza shop, sign a long-term contract, share your budget, let their chef taste-test new recipes with your feedback, give them your student count in advance, and in return they offer bulk discounts, customized toppings, guaranteed hot delivery, and consistent taste. **Both sides grow together.**

**Model B is exactly Supplier Partnering.** It converts a one-time fight over price into a long-term friendship over value.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning in TQM Context |
|---|---|
| **Partnering** | The overarching philosophy of working *with* suppliers, not *against* them |
| **Supplier Partnership** | The actual operational form of partnering — a strategic alliance |
| **Keiretsu** | Japanese model of tightly-knit supplier-buyer family networks |
| **Single Sourcing** | Buying a part from *one* qualified supplier (typical of partnership) |
| **Co-Manufacturing** | Supplier becomes involved in product design and engineering |
| **Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI)** | Supplier monitors and replenishes buyer's stock |
| **Strategic Alliance** | Broader term covering partnerships, joint ventures, consortia |
| **Trust** | The single most critical soft variable in any partnership |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** The 4 C's of Successful Partnering — **C**ommitment, **C**ommunication, **C**ooperation, **C**ontract (fair).

---

### 1.4 Why Supplier Partnering? The TQM Justification

In TQM philosophy, quality is **built into** the product at every stage, including at the supplier. Since purchased parts and materials typically account for **50%–70% of the final product cost**, the supplier is not a peripheral stakeholder but a **core determinant of end-product quality**. Partnering is therefore not a soft management luxury — it is a **strategic necessity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deming's 14 Points — Direct Link:**
> Point 4 of Deming's philosophy explicitly calls for *"ending the practice of awarding business on price tag alone"*. Supplier Partnering is the operational execution of this principle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Evolution of Buyer-Supplier Relationship

The relationship between buyer and supplier typically evolves through five recognizable stages. KTU examiners frequently test this progression.

1. **Adversarial / Arm's-length stage:** Short-term, price-driven, multiple suppliers, no trust.
2. **Quasi-partnership stage:** Some cooperation, formal contracts, periodic reviews.
3. **Partnership stage:** Long-term, trust-based, joint problem solving, single sourcing.
4. **Strategic alliance stage:** Deep integration, shared R&D, joint investments.
5. **Network / Keiretsu stage:** Multi-firm strategic ecosystem (Japanese model).

> [!NOTE]
> The **higher the stage**, the **lower the transaction cost** and the **higher the joint value created**.

---

### 2.2 Characteristics of Effective Supplier Partnership

For 14-mark answers, these characteristics are *mandatory* bullet points.

- **Mutual Trust and Confidence:** Open sharing of information including cost data.
- **Long-term Commitment:** Typically **3 to 5 year** contracts with renewal options.
- **Shared Vision and Goals:** Common quality, cost, and delivery targets.
- **Open Communication:** Frequent two-way communication, joint reviews.
- **Joint Problem Solving:** Use of *quality circles* and cross-functional teams.
- **Risk and Reward Sharing:** Cost savings and penalties distributed fairly.
- **Continuous Improvement Focus:** Both parties pursue Kaizen and Six Sigma.
- **Few Suppliers / Single Sourcing:** Reduction of supply base.
- **Early Supplier Involvement (ESI):** Supplier joins at design stage.
- **Total Cost Consideration:** Decisions based on *Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)*, not unit price.
- **Mutual Respect and Equal Status:** No "boss-subordinate" attitude.

---

### 2.3 The Partnering Model (KTU High-Yield Framework)

A commonly examined framework (referenced in KTU module 2 study material) structures partnering around **4 critical pillars**:

| Pillar | Description |
|---|---|
| **Pillar 1 — Long-term Contract** | Multi-year agreement replacing annual tendering |
| **Pillar 2 — Trust Building** | Transparency, honesty, information sharing |
| **Pillar 3 — Joint Quality Planning** | Supplier participates in FMEA, QFD, design reviews |
| **Pillar 4 — Continuous Improvement** | Joint Kaizen events, supplier development programs |

---

### 2.4 The Partnering Process — Step-by-Step Logical Sequence

```
Step 1: Identify Critical Purchased Items (Pareto Analysis — ABC classification)
   ↓
Step 2: Pre-qualify Potential Suppliers (Capability assessment)
   ↓
Step 3: Select Strategic Supplier(s) — preferably reduce to 1-2
   ↓
Step 4: Negotiate Long-term Partnership Agreement
   ↓
Step 5: Jointly Define Quality, Cost, Delivery Metrics
   ↓
Step 6: Implement Joint Improvement Programs (Kaizen, QCC, Six Sigma)
   ↓
Step 7: Monitor, Review, Audit (Supplier Scorecards)
   ↓
Step 8: Continuous Re-evaluation and Improvement of the Partnership Itself
```

---

### 2.5 Benefits and Barriers Matrix (Examiner Favourite)

**Benefits to Buyer:**
- Stable quality and supply
- Reduced inspection cost
- Lower total cost
- Faster new product introduction
- Access to supplier's innovation

**Benefits to Supplier:**
- Long-term volume guarantee
- Stable revenue and forecasting
- Joint learning and capability building
- Reduced marketing/sales effort

**Barriers / Risks:**
- Supplier complacency after being selected
- Over-dependence on one supplier
- Loss of competitive pressure on price
- Cultural and language differences
- Risk of confidentiality breach
- Difficulty in switching if partnership fails

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Students often list only *benefits* in a 14-mark answer. Always present a **balanced view** — pair each benefit with a corresponding limitation. KTU examiners reward this maturity.

---

### 2.6 Supplier Selection & Evaluation in Partnering

KTU expects familiarity with **multi-criteria selection models**. The simplest framework:

$$
\text{Supplier Score} = w_1 \cdot Q + w_2 \cdot C + w_3 \cdot D + w_4 \cdot S + w_5 \cdot F
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Quality score (0–100)
- $C$ = Cost competitiveness score
- $D$ = Delivery reliability score
- $S$ = Service / responsiveness score
- $F$ = Financial stability score
- $w_i$ = Weight assigned to criterion $i$, with $\sum w_i = 1$

| Criterion | Typical Weight ($w_i$) | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Quality ($Q$) | 0.35 | TQM core principle |
| Cost ($C$) | 0.25 | Total cost of ownership |
| Delivery ($D$) | 0.20 | JIT supply chain |
| Service ($S$) | 0.10 | Responsiveness |
| Financial ($F$) | 0.10 | Long-term survival |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU numerical problems, weights are given in the question. **Do not invent values.** Use whatever is provided in the problem data.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Toyota** operates the most celebrated keiretsu-style partnership network.
- **Boeing** partners with strategic tier-1 suppliers on risk-sharing model.
- **Apple** uses deep partnership with Foxconn for manufacturing scale.
- **Maruti Suzuki India** runs Japanese-style vendor parks where suppliers are co-located — a physical manifestation of partnering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Matrix

Since *Supplier Partnership* is a conceptual management topic, the "derivation" here is the **logical decomposition of the partnering decision** and a **worked supplier-selection numerical problem** — the most commonly tested quantitative pattern in KTU exams.

---

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Traditional vs Partnership Approach

| Dimension | Traditional Approach | Partnership Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Contract duration | Short-term (1 year) | Long-term (3–5+ years) |
| Number of suppliers | Many | Few (often one) |
| Basis of award | Lowest price | Total cost / value |
| Communication | Formal, infrequent | Open, frequent |
| Information shared | Limited, guarded | Full cost & technical |
| Supplier role | Vendor / outsider | Strategic partner / insider |
| Risk | Push to supplier | Shared jointly |
| Improvement focus | None | Joint continuous improvement |
| Inspection | 100% incoming | Audit-based, SQC |

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)

**Problem (Model):**
A company wants to select a supplier for a critical component using a weighted multi-criteria model. Three suppliers A, B, C have been evaluated on a 0–100 scale. Weights and scores are given below. Determine the best supplier.

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Supplier A | Supplier B | Supplier C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 0.40 | 90 | 85 | 95 |
| Cost | 0.30 | 80 | 90 | 75 |
| Delivery | 0.20 | 70 | 80 | 85 |
| Service | 0.10 | 85 | 75 | 90 |

**Step 1 — Verify weights sum to 1:**

$$
\sum w_i = 0.40 + 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Compute weighted score for Supplier A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_A &= (0.40)(90) + (0.30)(80) + (0.20)(70) + (0.10)(85) \\
    &= 36 + 24 + 14 + 8.5 \\
    &= 82.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute weighted score for Supplier B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_B &= (0.40)(85) + (0.30)(90) + (0.20)(80) + (0.10)(75) \\
    &= 34 + 27 + 16 + 7.5 \\
    &= 84.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute weighted score for Supplier C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_C &= (0.40)(95) + (0.30)(75) + (0.20)(85) + (0.10)(90) \\
    &= 38 + 22.5 + 17 + 9 \\
    &= 86.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Rank suppliers by score:**

$$
S_C = 86.5 > S_B = 84.5 > S_A = 82.5
$$

**Decision:** *Supplier C* should be selected as the strategic partner because it has the highest weighted multi-criteria score, balancing quality leadership with strong delivery and service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Convention:** Show every multiplication step separately. Examiners award **1 mark per weighted contribution** and **1 mark for the final ranking decision**. Do not skip intermediate sums.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation of the Partnering Decision Logic

The decision to partner (versus traditional sourcing) can be expressed as a payoff comparison. Let:

- $V_T$ = Expected value created under Traditional sourcing
- $V_P$ = Expected value created under Partnership sourcing
- $C_P$ = Cost of establishing and maintaining the partnership
- $r$ = Discount rate, $t$ = years

The partnership is justified when:

$$
V_P > V_T + C_P
$$

And the *Net Present Value (NPV)* of the partnership over $n$ years is:

$$
\text{NPV}_{\text{partnership}} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{(V_P - V_T)_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_P
$$

If $\text{NPV}_{\text{partnership}} > 0$, the partnership is financially justified.

> [!TIP]
> This formula is rarely asked as a full derivation, but examiners appreciate a student who can express the partnering decision **quantitatively**, not just in words.

---

### 3.4 Partnering Failure Modes — Diagnostic Matrix

| Failure Symptom | Root Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| Supplier stops improving after selection | Comfort, no competition | Maintain 2-3 backup suppliers |
| Conflict over cost-sharing | Unclear contract | Define gain-share formula upfront |
| Quality drifts after 2 years | No joint audits | Implement supplier scorecards quarterly |
| Information leakage | Weak NDA / IT controls | Strong legal framework, audit trails |
| Buyer exploits supplier | Asymmetric power | Independent ombudsman / joint steering committee |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — The Partnering Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step1 Identify Critical Items] --> A2[Step2 Pre-qualify Suppliers]
    A2 --> A3[Step3 Select Strategic Partner]
    A3 --> A4[Step4 Negotiate Long Term Contract]
    A4 --> A5[Step5 Define Joint KPIs Quality Cost Delivery]
    A5 --> A6[Step6 Joint Improvement Kaizen QCC Six Sigma]
    A6 --> A7[Step7 Monitor via Supplier Scorecards]
    A7 --> A8[Step8 Review and Renew Partnership]
    A8 -->|Continuous Loop| A6
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Traditional vs Partnership Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAD[Traditional Adversarial Model]
        B1[Buyer] -->|Tender and Lowest Price| B2[Supplier A]
        B1 -->|Tender and Lowest Price| B3[Supplier B]
        B1 -->|Tender and Lowest Price| B4[Supplier C]
        B2 -->|Hostile Negotiation| B1
        B3 -->|Hostile Negotiation| B1
        B4 -->|Hostile Negotiation| B1
    end
    subgraph PART[Partnership Strategic Model]
        C1[Buyer] <-->|Trust and Open Data| C2[Strategic Supplier]
        C2 -->|Joint R and D| C1
        C2 -->|VMI and JIT| C1
    end
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — The Four Pillars of Partnering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Successful Supplier Partnership] --> P1A[Pillar 1 Long Term Contract]
    P0 --> P2A[Pillar 2 Trust and Transparency]
    P0 --> P3A[Pillar 3 Joint Quality Planning]
    P0 --> P4A[Pillar 4 Continuous Improvement]
    P1A --> S1[Stable Volume and Forecasting]
    P2A --> S2[Open Cost and Technical Data]
    P3A --> S3[FMEA QFD Joint Design Reviews]
    P4A --> S4[Kaizen Six Sigma QCC]
```

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Partnering Decision System

```
INPUT LAYER
  [Supplier Capability Database]
            |
            v
PROCESSING LAYER
  [Pareto ABC Analysis] -> [Capability Assessment] -> [Weighted Multi-Criteria Scoring]
            |
            v
DECISION LAYER
  [Select Strategic Partner] -> [Negotiate Contract] -> [Define Joint KPIs]
            |
            v
EXECUTION LAYER
  [Joint Kaizen Teams] [VMI System] [Supplier Scorecard Review]
            |
            v
FEEDBACK LAYER
  [Quarterly Business Review] -> [Continuous Improvement Loop] -> [Re-evaluation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram is the **safest fallback** for topics where a physical schematic is difficult. KTU examiners accept clear block diagrams in lieu of detailed drawings.

---

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Risk-Sharing Under Partnership

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Shared Risk Pool] --> R1[Quality Risk Joint Root Cause Analysis]
    R0 --> R2[Cost Risk Open Book Costing and Gain Share]
    R0 --> R3[Delivery Risk Joint Inventory Buffering]
    R0 --> R4[Innovation Risk Joint R and D Investment]
    R1 --> RR[Shared Reward Mechanism]
    R2 --> RR
    R3 --> RR
    R4 --> RR
    RR --> RR2[Mutual Profit Growth and Long Term Survival]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
>
> **Q1. Define Supplier Partnering. List any four characteristics of an effective supplier partnership.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> *Supplier Partnering* is a long-term, cooperative relationship between a buyer and supplier based on mutual trust, open communication, shared risks, and shared rewards, with the aim of jointly creating value and achieving continuous improvement in quality, cost, and delivery.
>
> Four characteristics:
>
> 1. **Long-term commitment** (typically 3–5 years).
> 2. **Mutual trust and openness** — including open-book cost sharing.
> 3. **Joint problem solving** through Kaizen / QCC teams.
> 4. **Reduced supply base** — single sourcing of critical items.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
>
> **Q2. Distinguish between traditional buyer-supplier relationship and partnering relationship in TQM (any three points).**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Traditional | Partnering |
> |---|---|---|
> | Duration | Short-term | Long-term |
> | Basis of award | Lowest price | Total value |
> | Communication | Formal / guarded | Open / transparent |
> | Supplier role | Outsider / vendor | Strategic insider |
> | Risk | Pushed to supplier | Shared jointly |
>
> *(Any three points earn 3 marks — 1 mark per valid distinction.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> > [!WARNING]
> > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> > 1. Writing only benefits of partnering without listing **limitations / risks** → loses 2–3 marks.
> > 2. Missing the **TQM philosophical link** (Deming, Juran, Crosby) → loses 1 mark.
> > 3. In numerical problems, **rounding too early** in intermediate steps → loses accuracy marks.
> > 4. Writing the partnership process in random order rather than as a **logical sequence** → loses flow marks.
> > 5. Forgetting to mention **Single Sourcing and Early Supplier Involvement (ESI)** → loses 1–2 marks.

---

#### **PART B — Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A]**
>
> **Q (a).** Explain in detail the concept of Supplier Partnering in Total Quality Management. Discuss its key characteristics, benefits, and limitations. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Q (b).** A manufacturing firm is to select a strategic supplier from three candidates X, Y, Z using the weighted multi-criteria method. Weights and scores are as follows. Compute the scores, rank the suppliers, and recommend the most suitable partner. *(7 marks)*
>
> | Criterion | Weight | X | Y | Z |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | Quality | 0.45 | 88 | 92 | 95 |
> | Cost | 0.25 | 85 | 80 | 78 |
> | Delivery | 0.20 | 90 | 88 | 92 |
> | Service | 0.10 | 80 | 85 | 88 |

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Long-term, win-win, trust-based buyer-supplier alliance.
2. **Characteristics (2 marks):** Any four — long-term contract, mutual trust, open communication, joint problem solving, risk-reward sharing, few suppliers, ESI.
3. **Benefits (2 marks):** To buyer — stable quality, lower cost, faster NPI. To supplier — volume guarantee, joint learning.
4. **Limitations (2 marks):** Supplier complacency, dependence risk, confidentiality, cultural gaps.

**Part (b) — 7 marks (Numerical):**

**Step 1 — Verify weights sum to 1:**

$$
0.45 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Score for Supplier X:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_X &= (0.45)(88) + (0.25)(85) + (0.20)(90) + (0.10)(80) \\
    &= 39.60 + 21.25 + 18.00 + 8.00 \\
    &= 86.85
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Score for Supplier Y:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_Y &= (0.45)(92) + (0.25)(80) + (0.20)(88) + (0.10)(85) \\
    &= 41.40 + 20.00 + 17.60 + 8.50 \\
    &= 87.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Score for Supplier Z:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_Z &= (0.45)(95) + (0.25)(78) + (0.20)(92) + (0.10)(88) \\
    &= 42.75 + 19.50 + 18.40 + 8.80 \\
    &= 89.45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Ranking:**

$$
S_Z = 89.45 > S_Y = 87.50 > S_X = 86.85
$$

**Recommendation (1 mark):** *Supplier Z* is recommended as the strategic partner — highest weighted score, with balanced strength across quality, delivery, and service. Although its cost score is lowest, the heavy weight on quality makes it the optimal partner under a TQM philosophy.

---

#### **PART B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B]**
>
> **Q (a).** Explain the step-by-step process of establishing a supplier partnership. How does partnering differ from traditional sourcing? *(7 marks)*
>
> **Q (b).** Discuss the role of trust, communication, and joint continuous improvement in sustaining a long-term supplier partnership. Illustrate with a suitable industrial example. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step-by-step process (4 marks):**

1. **Identify critical purchased items** using ABC / Pareto analysis.
2. **Pre-qualify suppliers** based on capability, financial health, quality systems.
3. **Select strategic supplier(s)** using weighted multi-criteria scoring.
4. **Negotiate long-term partnership contract** with shared KPIs.
5. **Implement joint improvement programs** — Kaizen, QCC, Six Sigma.
6. **Monitor continuously** via supplier scorecards and quarterly reviews.
7. **Re-evaluate and renew** — partnership is dynamic, not static.

**Comparison with traditional sourcing (3 marks):** Traditional is short-term, price-driven, multi-supplier, adversarial. Partnering is long-term, value-driven, few-supplier, cooperative. *(Any three valid contrasts — 1 mark each.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

- **Trust (2 marks):** Foundation of partnering. Allows open-book costing, candid exchange of design data, joint problem solving. Without trust, partnership degenerates into contract enforcement.
- **Communication (2 marks):** Two-way, structured, frequent — quarterly business reviews (QBRs), joint steering committees, shared digital portals.
- **Joint Continuous Improvement (2 marks):** Supplier participates in buyer's Kaizen events, contributes to FMEA, runs parallel Six Sigma projects. Cost savings shared via gain-share formula.
- **Industrial Example (1 mark):** *Toyota and its keiretsu suppliers* — over 200 tier-1 suppliers are integrated into Toyota's production system; they share production schedules in real time, conduct joint problem solving on the shop floor, and have grown together for decades. This is the gold-standard illustration of partnering in TQM.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Supplier Partnership / Partnering**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Long-term, win-win, trust-based buyer-supplier alliance built on shared vision, risk, and reward.
- ⭐ **Core idea:** Treat supplier as an *extension* of your own organization.
- ⭐ **Deming Link:** Point 4 — *"End lowest-price-only purchasing."*
- ⭐ **Juran Link:** Quality must be controlled at the source — supplier is the source.
- ⭐ **Crosby Link:** *Do it right the first time* — partnering prevents rework at incoming stage.
- ⭐ **4 Pillars:** Long-term contract, Trust, Joint Quality Planning, Continuous Improvement.
- ⭐ **Single Sourcing:** Hallmark of partnership (with backup qualification).
- ⭐ **Early Supplier Involvement (ESI):** Supplier at design stage via QFD, FMEA.
- ⭐ **Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI):** Supplier replenishes buyer's stock.
- ⭐ **Keiretsu:** Japanese partnership network — textbook example.
- ⭐ **Score Formula:** $S = \sum w_i \cdot \text{Criterion}_i$, with $\sum w_i = 1$.
- ⭐ **Benefit + Risk Pairing:** Always present partnering as a balanced proposition.
- ⭐ **Process Order:** Identify → Qualify → Select → Contract → Joint KPIs → Joint Improvement → Monitor → Review.
- ⭐ **Examiner Traps:** Skipping limitations; missing TQM guru linkage; sloppy numerical steps.

> **End of Notes — Module 2: Supplier Partnership — Partnering**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sourcing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sourcing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sourcing** is the strategic process of identifying, evaluating, selecting, contracting, and continuously managing external suppliers (vendors) that provide the raw materials, components, sub-assemblies, and services required to manufacture a product or deliver a service in accordance with the Total Quality Management (TQM) philosophy.

In the context of **Total Quality Management**, sourcing is not merely a procurement transaction. It is a **strategic quality function** that ensures every input to the value chain conforms to the organization's quality standards. KTU 2024 scheme frames sourcing as a pillar of the *Supply Chain Quality* domain, emphasizing that **quality cannot be inspected into a product — it must be sourced in**.

**Key Quality Sourcing Terminology (KTU Glossary):**

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Supplier** | Any external entity that provides goods, materials, or services to the organization. |
| **Vendor Rating** | A quantitative metric used to measure and compare supplier performance. |
| **Strategic Sourcing** | A long-term, data-driven approach to optimizing the supplier base. |
| **Single Sourcing** | Procuring a particular item from only one approved supplier. |
| **Partnership Sourcing** | A collaborative, long-term relationship with a select few suppliers. |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen**
> Imagine you are running a five-star restaurant. The quality of your dishes depends entirely on the quality of ingredients you buy — fresh tomatoes, premium olive oil, hand-picked herbs. If you buy cheap, stale ingredients, no chef in the world can turn out a Michelin-star meal. **Sourcing is the act of choosing your ingredient suppliers wisely.** In TQM, the same logic applies to a manufacturing company: the final product's quality is determined by the quality of its parts. Therefore, choosing the *right* supplier is just as critical as designing the *right* product.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Sourcing (Bold Highlights)

- **On-Time Delivery (OTD) %**
- **Acceptance Quality Level (AQL)**
- **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** contributed by the supplier
- **Supplier Rejection Rate (SRR)**
- **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** — incoming from supplier

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Quality guru **Genichi Taguchi** stressed that the ideal target for any purchased component is **zero-defect sourcing**, where the supplier's process capability index **Cpk ≥ 1.33** before the part is even shipped.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sourcing Process — Step-by-Step Logic

Sourcing in a TQM framework follows a structured, repeatable cycle. Below is the operational breakdown of each phase:

1. **Identify the Need** — Define what materials, components, or services are required, along with quality specifications, volume, and delivery timelines.
2. **Identify Potential Suppliers** — Search the market for vendors that can meet the technical and quality specifications.
3. **Evaluate & Pre-Qualify Suppliers** — Conduct audits, review capability indices, financial stability, and past performance.
4. **Select the Supplier(s)** — Use a weighted scoring model to choose between competing suppliers.
5. **Negotiate the Contract** — Establish quality agreements, KPIs, and penalties for non-conformance.
6. **Supplier Development & Monitoring** — Train, audit, and continuously improve the supplier's processes.
7. **Performance Review & Feedback** — Use vendor rating systems to rank suppliers and decide on renewals.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind Each Step:**
> - **Why pre-qualify?** Because in TQM, prevention is cheaper than inspection.
> - **Why use weighted scoring?** Because supplier selection involves multiple competing criteria (price, quality, delivery, capacity) — and not all criteria carry equal importance.
> - **Why continuous monitoring?** Because a qualified supplier today may slip in quality tomorrow.

### 2.2 Types of Sourcing Strategies (KTU High-Yield Concept)

The TQM literature recognizes five canonical sourcing strategies. This is a frequent **2-mark or 3-mark short-answer** topic in KTU exams.

| Sourcing Type | Description | TQM Pros | TQM Cons |
|---------------|-------------|----------|----------|
| **Single Sourcing** | One supplier for a specific item | Strong partnership; deep integration; lower cost | High risk if supplier fails; dependency |
| **Dual Sourcing** | Two qualified suppliers for one item | Risk mitigation; competitive benchmarking | Higher admin overhead |
| **Multiple Sourcing** | Many suppliers for the same item | Maximum competition; lowest price | Quality inconsistency; weak partnerships |
| **Strategic Sourcing** | Data-driven, long-term partnership approach | Aligned goals; continuous improvement | Requires mature systems |
| **Captive (Tapered) Sourcing** | One primary + several backup suppliers | Balanced risk and partnership | Complex coordination |

### 2.3 Supplier Selection Criteria

In KTU 2024 module 2, the standard criteria used to select suppliers are:

- **Quality** (Capability indices, PPM, certifications like ISO 9001)
- **Cost** (Total Cost of Ownership — not just unit price)
- **Delivery** (On-time delivery %, lead time reliability)
- **Capacity** (Production capacity, scalability)
- **Financial Stability** (Credit rating, years in business)
- **Technical Capability** (R&D, design support)
- **Responsiveness** (Communication, complaint handling)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are the most tested formulas in KTU Sourcing problems. Memorize them.**

**Formula 1: Weighted Vendor Rating Score**

The weighted rating of a supplier is calculated as:

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot R_i
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Total weighted score of the supplier
- $w_i$ = Weight assigned to criterion $i$ (in decimal, e.g., $0.30$ for 30%)
- $R_i$ = Rating of the supplier on criterion $i$ (typically on a 1–10 scale)
- $n$ = Total number of evaluation criteria

**Formula 2: On-Time Delivery (OTD) Percentage**

$$
\text{OTD \%} = \frac{\text{Number of On-Time Deliveries}}{\text{Total Number of Deliveries}} \times 100
$$

**Formula 3: Supplier Rejection Rate (SRR)**

$$
\text{SRR \%} = \frac{\text{Number of Rejected Lots}}{\text{Total Number of Lots Received}} \times 100
$$

**Formula 4: Acceptance Quality Level (AQL) — Sampling-based**

$$
\text{AQL} = \frac{\text{Number of Defective Units Accepted}}{\text{Total Units in Sample}} \times 100
$$

**Formula 5: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)**

$$
\text{TCO} = C_p + C_o + C_d + C_q + C_f
$$

Where:
- $C_p$ = Purchase price
- $C_o$ = Ordering cost
- $C_d$ = Downstream cost of defect (warranty, rework)
- $C_q$ = Quality inspection cost on receipt
- $C_f$ = Failure cost (returns, recalls)

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** KTU examiners often give the *unit purchase price* and expect you to recognize that **TCO** is the correct TQM metric — **not** the lowest unit price. Choosing the lowest-cost supplier can lead to higher TCO.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In industries such as **automotive (Toyota, Tata Motors)**, **aerospace (Boeing, HAL)**, and **electronics (Foxconn, Apple supply chain)**, sourcing decisions determine:
- Product safety (e.g., aircraft components must be defect-free)
- Brand reputation
- Compliance with regulatory standards (ISO/AS 9100, IATF 16949)
- Time-to-market for new products

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example: Weighted Vendor Rating

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pattern:** This type of problem is commonly asked as a 7-mark sub-question under a 14-mark Part B question. It tests **Apply (Bloom Level 3)** and **Analyze (Bloom Level 4)**.

**Problem Statement:**

A TQM-driven company wants to select a supplier for a critical component. Three suppliers — **A, B, and C** — have been evaluated on five criteria. The weights and ratings (on a scale of 1 to 10) are given below:

| Criterion | Weight ($w_i$) | Supplier A Rating | Supplier B Rating | Supplier C Rating |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Quality | 0.35 | 9 | 8 | 7 |
| Cost | 0.20 | 7 | 9 | 8 |
| Delivery | 0.20 | 8 | 7 | 9 |
| Capacity | 0.15 | 7 | 8 | 7 |
| Responsiveness | 0.10 | 9 | 7 | 8 |

**Determine the best supplier using the weighted vendor rating method.**

---

**Step 1: Recall the Formula**

The weighted score for each supplier is computed using:

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot R_i
$$

The supplier with the highest $S$ value wins.

---

**Step 2: Calculate the Weighted Score for Supplier A**

$$
S_A = (0.35 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 9)
$$

$$
S_A = 3.15 + 1.40 + 1.60 + 1.05 + 0.90
$$

$$
S_A = 8.10
$$

---

**Step 3: Calculate the Weighted Score for Supplier B**

$$
S_B = (0.35 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 7)
$$

$$
S_B = 2.80 + 1.80 + 1.40 + 1.20 + 0.70
$$

$$
S_B = 7.90
$$

---

**Step 4: Calculate the Weighted Score for Supplier C**

$$
S_C = (0.35 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8)
$$

$$
S_C = 2.45 + 1.60 + 1.80 + 1.05 + 0.80
$$

$$
S_C = 7.70
$$

---

**Step 5: Compare and Decide**

$$
S_A = 8.10 > S_B = 7.90 > S_C = 7.70
$$

**Decision:** **Supplier A** is selected as it has the highest weighted score.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight for Exam:** Notice that although Supplier B scored the lowest on **Quality**, the heavy weight (0.35) on Quality made Supplier A — which scored highest on Quality — the winner. This demonstrates the **TQM principle** that *quality must dominate cost in supplier selection*.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Strategic vs. Tactical Sourcing

| Dimension | Tactical Sourcing (Traditional) | Strategic Sourcing (TQM-Aligned) |
|-----------|--------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Time horizon | Short-term, transaction-by-transaction | Long-term, multi-year partnerships |
| Supplier count | Many (competitive bidding) | Few, deeply integrated |
| Decision basis | Lowest unit price | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** |
| Quality focus | Inspection on receipt | **Process capability** and prevention |
| Relationship | Adversarial | Collaborative, trust-based |
| Information sharing | Minimal | Two-way, transparent (e.g., EDI, portal) |
| Risk approach | Reactive | **Proactive risk management** |

### 3.3 Analytical Hierarchy: The 4 Levels of Supplier Integration in TQM

1. **Level 1 — Approved Supplier:** Complies with standards; passes incoming inspection.
2. **Level 2 — Preferred Supplier:** Demonstrates consistent quality and on-time delivery.
3. **Level 3 — Strategic Partner:** Involved in joint product development and Kaizen.
4. **Level 4 — Extension of the Enterprise:** Fully integrated; shares forecasting, inventory, and quality data.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The TQM Sourcing Cycle

This diagram depicts the closed-loop process of quality sourcing as per KTU 2024 module framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Quality Need] --> B[Identify Potential Suppliers]
    B --> C[Pre-Qualify and Audit Suppliers]
    C --> D[Evaluate Using Weighted Scoring]
    D --> E[Select Supplier and Sign Quality Agreement]
    E --> F[Monitor Supplier Performance OTD SRR PPM]
    F --> G{Is Quality Acceptable?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Provide Feedback and Reward]
    G -- No --> I[Supplier Development and Corrective Action]
    I --> F
    H --> J[Continuous Improvement Loop]
    J --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Types of Sourcing — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Sourcing Strategy in TQM] --> S1[Single Sourcing]
    S --> S2[Dual Sourcing]
    S --> S3[Multiple Sourcing]
    S --> S4[Strategic Sourcing]
    S --> S5[Captive Sourcing]
    S1 --> R1[Strength: Deep partnership]
    S2 --> R2[Strength: Risk mitigation]
    S3 --> R3[Strength: Price competition]
    S4 --> R4[Strength: Long term value]
    S5 --> R5[Strength: Balanced approach]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Supplier Selection Weighted Scoring Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Begin Selection Process] --> W[Assign Weights to Criteria]
    W --> C1[Quality 35 percent]
    W --> C2[Cost 20 percent]
    W --> C3[Delivery 20 percent]
    W --> C4[Capacity 15 percent]
    W --> C5[Responsiveness 10 percent]
    C1 --> Calc[Compute Weighted Score per Supplier]
    C2 --> Calc
    C3 --> Calc
    C4 --> Calc
    C5 --> Calc
    Calc --> Rank[Rank Suppliers by Score]
    Rank --> Decide[Select Highest Score Supplier]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Make-or-Buy Decision Flow

The make-or-buy decision is closely linked to sourcing strategy and is a high-yield KTU topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the item a core competency?} -->|Yes| Q2{Can we produce it at required quality and cost?}
    Q1 -->|No| Buy[Buy from External Supplier]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Make[Make In House]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is supplier available with TQM compliance?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Buy
    Q3 -->|No| Develop[Develop Supplier Partnership]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The make-or-buy decision must consider **strategic importance**, **quality risk**, **cost**, and **capacity**. The TQM bias is always toward building long-term supplier partnerships rather than short-term transactional buying.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define sourcing in the context of Total Quality Management.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Sourcing in TQM is the strategic process of identifying, evaluating, selecting, and managing external suppliers that provide the materials, components, and services needed by an organization, with the goal of ensuring that all purchased inputs conform to the organization's quality standards. It goes beyond price-based procurement to focus on supplier quality, reliability, and long-term partnership, recognizing that **quality must be sourced in, not inspected in**.

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. Distinguish between Single Sourcing and Multiple Sourcing.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Single Sourcing | Multiple Sourcing |
|--------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Definition | Procuring a particular item from only one approved supplier | Procuring the same item from many competing suppliers |
| Supplier Relationship | Deep, long-term partnership | Transactional, arms-length |
| Risk | High (single point of failure) | Low (risk distributed) |
| Cost | Often lower due to volume discount | Higher due to fragmented orders |
| TQM Suitability | High — preferred for quality-critical items | Low — quality consistency is hard to maintain |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the different types of sourcing strategies used in Total Quality Management. Discuss their advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Three suppliers — P, Q, and R — are being evaluated for a precision component. The criteria, weights, and ratings (out of 10) are:

| Criterion | Weight | P | Q | R |
|-----------|--------|---|---|---|
| Quality | 0.40 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Cost | 0.25 | 7 | 9 | 8 |
| Delivery | 0.20 | 8 | 8 | 7 |
| Capacity | 0.15 | 8 | 7 | 9 |

Using the weighted vendor rating method, determine which supplier should be selected. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Types of Sourcing Strategies:**

1. **Single Sourcing** — One supplier per item. *Advantages:* strong partnership, deeper quality integration, lower administrative cost. *Limitations:* high dependency, supply disruption risk.

2. **Dual Sourcing** — Two qualified suppliers per item. *Advantages:* risk mitigation, benchmarking. *Limitations:* higher coordination effort.

3. **Multiple Sourcing** — Many suppliers per item. *Advantages:* strong price competition. *Limitations:* inconsistent quality, weak partnerships.

4. **Strategic Sourcing** — Data-driven long-term partnership. *Advantages:* aligned goals, continuous improvement. *Limitations:* requires mature quality systems.

5. **Captive (Tapered) Sourcing** — One primary supplier plus backup suppliers. *Advantages:* balance of partnership and risk control. *Limitations:* complexity.

---

**(b) Weighted Vendor Rating Calculation:**

**For Supplier P:**

$$
S_P = (0.40 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 8)
$$

$$
S_P = 3.60 + 1.75 + 1.60 + 1.20 = 8.15
$$

**For Supplier Q:**

$$
S_Q = (0.40 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 7)
$$

$$
S_Q = 2.80 + 2.25 + 1.60 + 1.05 = 7.70
$$

**For Supplier R:**

$$
S_R = (0.40 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 9)
$$

$$
S_R = 3.20 + 2.00 + 1.40 + 1.35 = 7.95
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
S_P = 8.15 > S_R = 7.95 > S_Q = 7.70
$$

**Conclusion:** **Supplier P** should be selected as it has the highest weighted score of **8.15**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the supplier selection process in TQM. Explain the criteria used for evaluating suppliers and justify why quality is given the highest weight in TQM-based supplier selection. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company receives **500 lots** of a component from a supplier in a quarter. Inspection results show **35 lots were rejected** for quality reasons and **40 deliveries were late out of a total of 500 deliveries**. Calculate:
   (i) Supplier Rejection Rate (SRR) in %
   (ii) On-Time Delivery (OTD) percentage **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Supplier Selection Process in TQM:**

The supplier selection process in TQM involves the following stages:

1. **Identify potential suppliers** through market surveys and database searches.
2. **Pre-qualify** them by assessing their certifications (e.g., ISO 9001), financial stability, and process capability indices (Cpk, Pp).
3. **Evaluate** using a weighted scoring model across quality, cost, delivery, capacity, and responsiveness.
4. **Select** the highest-scoring supplier.
5. **Sign a quality agreement** with KPIs and corrective action clauses.
6. **Monitor** using incoming inspection and supplier audits.

**Why quality gets the highest weight:**
- Defective inputs cause downstream rework, warranty claims, and brand damage.
- The cost of preventing a defect at the source is far lower than detecting it later (1-10-100 rule).
- TQM emphasizes **prevention over inspection**, and supplier quality is the foundation of prevention.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Supplier Rejection Rate (SRR):**

$$
\text{SRR \%} = \frac{\text{Rejected Lots}}{\text{Total Lots Received}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{SRR \%} = \frac{35}{500} \times 100 = 7.00\%
$$

**(ii) On-Time Delivery (OTD):**

$$
\text{OTD \%} = \frac{\text{On-Time Deliveries}}{\text{Total Deliveries}} \times 100
$$

Number of late deliveries = 40, so on-time deliveries = $500 - 40 = 460$.

$$
\text{OTD \%} = \frac{460}{500} \times 100 = 92.00\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The supplier's rejection rate of **7%** is high (typically the acceptable limit is ≤ 1–2%) and on-time delivery of **92%** is below the world-class benchmark of ≥ 98%. The supplier requires **supplier development** before contract renewal.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often compute weighted scores by simply averaging ratings without multiplying by weights. **Always multiply each rating by its corresponding weight before summing.**
> - **Common Mistake #2:** When asked to compute OTD, students use the number of *late* deliveries in the numerator instead of the number of *on-time* deliveries.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing **Tactical Sourcing** (transactional, price-driven) with **Strategic Sourcing** (TQM-aligned, partnership-driven). Examiners expect students to clearly distinguish the two.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not showing units or final rounding properly. Always write `7.00%` not `7%`.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sourcing in TQM**

- **Definition:** Sourcing is the strategic process of selecting and managing suppliers to ensure purchased inputs meet TQM quality standards.
- **Core Philosophy:** Quality must be sourced in, not inspected in.
- **Five Types of Sourcing:** Single, Dual, Multiple, Strategic, Captive.
- **Strategic Sourcing** is the most TQM-aligned approach.
- **Supplier Selection Criteria:** Quality (highest weight), Cost, Delivery, Capacity, Responsiveness, Financial Stability, Technical Capability.
- **Key Formulas (must memorize):**
  - Weighted Score: $S = \sum w_i R_i$
  - OTD % = On-Time Deliveries / Total Deliveries × 100
  - SRR % = Rejected Lots / Total Lots × 100
  - TCO = $C_p + C_o + C_d + C_q + C_f$
- **Supplier Integration Levels:** Approved → Preferred → Strategic Partner → Extension of Enterprise.
- **Make-or-Buy Decision:** Based on core competency, quality risk, cost, and capacity.
- **Supplier Development:** Continuous improvement through training, audits, and Kaizen.
- **Taguchi Principle:** Aim for zero-defect sourcing with Cpk ≥ 1.33.
- **World-class Benchmarks:** OTD ≥ 98%, SRR ≤ 1%, PPM ≤ 100.
- **Final Mantra:** In TQM, *the supplier is not a vendor — the supplier is a partner.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supplier Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Supplier Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Supplier Selection** is the strategic process of identifying, evaluating, and approving external vendors capable of delivering materials, components, or services that meet the buyer's specifications of **Quality, Cost, Delivery (QCD)**, and reliability on a consistent, long-term basis. In the context of Total Quality Management (TQM), supplier selection is not a transactional purchase decision but a **strategic partnership decision**, because the quality of an organization's final product is bounded by the quality of its incoming materials (the *Chain-of-Customer* principle).

According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, supplier selection is the **third stage of the supplier management cycle**, which comprises:
1. **Sourcing** (identifying potential vendors)
2. **Vendor Rating** (monitoring performance of existing suppliers)
3. **Supplier Selection** (choosing the best supplier) ← *This Topic*
4. **Supplier Development** (upgrading supplier capability)
5. **Supplier Certification** (long-term recognition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Supplier Selection is studied under the broader umbrella of *Purchasing and Supply Chain Quality*. Examiners frequently frame questions using a 3-stage model: **Identify → Evaluate → Choose**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are choosing a restaurant to host your sister's wedding reception:
- You don't just look at the **price** (one-dimensional view).
- You evaluate **food quality**, **ambience**, **on-time service**, **location**, **capacity**, **past reviews**, and **cost**.
- You assign **weights** to each factor (food quality might matter 40%, cost 20%, ambience 15%, etc.).
- You score each candidate restaurant, multiply scores by weights, add them up, and pick the highest total.

**Supplier selection works exactly the same way** — it is a *Multi-Criteria Decision-Making (MCDM)* exercise where the buyer applies a structured, weighted scoring system to rank competing suppliers and choose the optimal one. Picking a supplier purely on the lowest price is like picking a wedding venue purely on cost — it almost always backfires.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Callout:** *Supplier Selection = a Multi-Criteria Decision-Making (MCDM) process that uses weighted evaluation across quality, cost, delivery, and capability parameters to identify the optimal vendor.*

## 1.3 Strategic Importance in TQM

| Strategic Role | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| **Quality Input Gate** | Defects enter the factory *with* the material. A weak supplier = a weak incoming inspection. |
| **Cost Driver** | Purchased parts typically represent **50%–70%** of product cost in manufacturing. |
| **Delivery Synchronizer** | Poor supplier delivery destroys JIT/Lean systems. |
| **Innovation Partner** | Modern TQM views suppliers as **co-developers**, not adversaries. |
| **Risk Reducer** | Concentration risk, geopolitical risk, financial risk all originate at the supplier interface. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven-Step Supplier Selection Process (KSK Model)

The widely-accepted KTU-aligned framework for supplier selection follows seven structured steps:

1. **Identify Requirements** — Define the technical, commercial, and quality specifications of the material/service needed.
2. **Identify Potential Suppliers** — Search the market (trade directories, exhibitions, references, B2B portals).
3. **Determine Selection Criteria** — Decide the parameters for evaluation (quality, cost, delivery, etc.).
4. **Determine Importance Weights** — Assign relative weights ($w_i$) to each criterion using methods like AHP, Delphi, or simple ranking.
5. **Evaluate Suppliers** — Score each supplier on each criterion (typically on a 1–10 scale or 1–5 scale).
6. **Compute Weighted Scores & Rank** — Apply the weighted point model to obtain a composite score.
7. **Select the Optimal Supplier** — Choose the highest scorer, subject to negotiation and commercial validation.

## 2.2 Categories of Selection Criteria

The **"Why"** behind each criterion category:

- **Quality Criteria** — *Why?* Because TQM's first principle is *fitness for use*. Includes defect rate, PPM, conformance to specs, certification status.
- **Cost Criteria** — *Why?* Because profitability depends on input cost. Includes unit price, lifecycle cost, freight, payment terms.
- **Delivery Criteria** — *Why?* Because reliability of supply underpins production planning. Includes lead time, on-time delivery rate, flexibility.
- **Capability Criteria** — *Why?* Because future-readiness matters. Includes technical expertise, R&D strength, capacity, financial health.
- **Service Criteria** — *Why?* Because problems always arise; recovery matters. Includes responsiveness, warranty support, communication.

## 2.3 The 2-Stage Evaluation Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Concept:** Most KTU questions require a **two-stage filter**:
> - **Stage 1 (Screening):** A simple yes/no filter on *must-have* criteria (e.g., ISO 9001 certification, minimum capacity).
> - **Stage 2 (Weighted Scoring):** Survivors are ranked on *want* criteria using a weighted point model.

## 2.4 Sources of Supplier Selection

| Sourcing Strategy | Definition | When to Use | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Sourcing** | One supplier for a part | Strategic parts, JIT, proprietary tech | High — supply disruption risk |
| **Multiple Sourcing** | Several approved suppliers | Non-critical, commodity items | Lower — competitive leverage |
| **Dual Sourcing** | Two suppliers (one primary, one backup) | Balanced approach | Moderate — split volumes |
| **Outsourcing** | External contractor delivers the function | Non-core activities | Quality / IP control issues |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** Vertical bars $\vert$ are written as `\vert` to prevent markdown table corruption.

| # | Formula / Metric | Mathematical Form | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Weighted Point Score (WPS)** | $S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{ij}$ | Dimensionless (0–10) | Rank alternative suppliers $j$ |
| 2 | **Constraint on Weights** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$ | — | Weights must sum to unity |
| 3 | **Vendor Quality Index (VQI)** | $VQI_j = 1 - \dfrac{\text{No. of rejected lots}_j}{\text{Total lots inspected}_j}$ | 0 to 1 | Measures supplier quality |
| 4 | **On-Time Delivery Rate (OTD)** | $OTD_j = \dfrac{\text{No. of on-time deliveries}_j}{\text{Total deliveries}_j} \times 100$ | % | Delivery reliability |
| 5 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = C_{\text{price}} + C_{\text{transport}} + C_{\text{stock}} + C_{\text{reject}}$ | Currency | True cost of supplier |
| 6 | **Cost Ratio Method (CRS)** | $CRS = \dfrac{C_{\text{lowest}}}{C_j} \times \text{Quality Score}_j$ | Composite | Cost-quality tradeoff |
| 7 | **Normalized Weight** | $w_i^{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{w_i^{\text{raw}}}{\sum w_i^{\text{raw}}}$ | 0 to 1 | Rescale raw weights |

Where:
- $S_j$ = composite score of supplier $j$
- $w_i$ = weight assigned to criterion $i$
- $r_{ij}$ = raw rating of supplier $j$ on criterion $i$
- $n$ = number of criteria
- $C_j$ = cost quoted by supplier $j$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Weighted Point Model (WPM)

The **Weighted Point Model** is the most frequently asked quantitative method in KTU examinations on this topic. Its derivation follows from Multi-Attribute Utility Theory (MAUT):

### Step 1 — Define the Decision Matrix
Let there be $m$ suppliers indexed $j = 1, 2, \dots, m$, and $n$ criteria indexed $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$. Construct the rating matrix:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & \cdots & r_{1m} \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & \cdots & r_{2m} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ r_{n1} & r_{n2} & \cdots & r_{nm} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Each element $r_{ij}$ is the rating (typically 1–10) of supplier $j$ on criterion $i$.

### Step 2 — Define the Weight Vector
Assign a relative importance weight $w_i$ to each criterion such that:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1
$$

If the buyer assigns raw weights (e.g., on a 1–5 scale) that do not sum to 1, normalize them:

$$
w_i^{\text{norm}} = \frac{w_i^{\text{raw}}}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} w_k^{\text{raw}}}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Composite Score
For each supplier $j$, the composite score is the **scalar product** of the weight vector and the $j$-th column of $R$:

$$
S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{ij}
$$

### Step 4 — Rank and Select
Sort $S_1, S_2, \dots, S_m$ in descending order. The supplier with the **maximum $S_j$** is selected:

$$
j^* = \arg\max_{j \in \{1,\dots,m\}} S_j
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Past Exam Pattern)

**Problem Statement:** A manufacturing firm must select a supplier for a critical component. Three suppliers — **A, B, C** — are evaluated on four criteria. The weights (on a 1–5 raw scale) and ratings (on a 1–10 scale) are given below. Use the **Weighted Point Model** to determine the optimal supplier.

| Criterion ($i$) | Raw Weight ($w_i^{\text{raw}}$) | Rating A | Rating B | Rating C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Quality | 5 | 8 | 9 | 7 |
| 2. Cost | 4 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
| 3. Delivery | 3 | 9 | 6 | 8 |
| 4. Service | 2 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
| **Sum** | **14** | — | — | — |

### Step 1 — Normalize the Weights
Apply the normalization formula:

$$
w_i^{\text{norm}} = \frac{w_i^{\text{raw}}}{\sum_{k=1}^{4} w_k^{\text{raw}}}
$$

Compute each:

$$
w_1 = \frac{5}{14} = 0.3571
$$

$$
w_2 = \frac{4}{14} = 0.2857
$$

$$
w_3 = \frac{3}{14} = 0.2143
$$

$$
w_4 = \frac{2}{14} = 0.1429
$$

Verification — weights must sum to 1:

$$
0.3571 + 0.2857 + 0.2143 + 0.1429 = 1.0000 \quad \checkmark
$$

### Step 2 — Compute $S_A$

$$
S_A = (0.3571 \times 8) + (0.2857 \times 7) + (0.2143 \times 9) + (0.1429 \times 6)
$$

$$
S_A = 2.8571 + 2.0000 + 1.9286 + 0.8571
$$

$$
S_A = 7.6428
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $S_B$

$$
S_B = (0.3571 \times 9) + (0.2857 \times 8) + (0.2143 \times 6) + (0.1429 \times 7)
$$

$$
S_B = 3.2143 + 2.2857 + 1.2857 + 1.0000
$$

$$
S_B = 7.7857
$$

### Step 4 — Compute $S_C$

$$
S_C = (0.3571 \times 7) + (0.2857 \times 9) + (0.2143 \times 8) + (0.1429 \times 8)
$$

$$
S_C = 2.5000 + 2.5714 + 1.7143 + 1.1429
$$

$$
S_C = 7.9286
$$

### Step 5 — Rank and Select

$$
S_C = 7.9286 > S_B = 7.7857 > S_A = 7.6428
$$

Therefore:

$$
j^* = C
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** **Supplier C** is selected, even though it scored *lowest on quality*. This is a classic **cost-quality tradeoff** outcome — the high weight given to cost (0.2857) and strong delivery/service performance made C the optimal choice. **This is the exact kind of insight KTU examiners reward in 14-mark answers.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Strengths and Limitations of WPM

| Aspect | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **Simplicity** | Easy to compute and explain to management | Oversimplifies complex tradeoffs |
| **Transparency** | Scoring logic is auditable | Subjectivity in assigning weights and ratings |
| **Comparability** | Produces a single comparable metric | Does not capture inter-criteria correlation |
| **Decision Quality** | Better than single-criterion (price-only) | Cannot handle qualitative criteria rigorously |
| **Use Case** | Routine, tactical supplier selection | Strategic, high-stakes sourcing |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Supplier Selection Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Material Requirements] --> B[Step 2: Identify Potential Suppliers]
    B --> C[Step 3: Apply Screening Filter ISO 9001 Capacity Must Haves]
    C --> D{Supplier Passes Screen}
    D -- No --> E[Reject Vendor]
    D -- Yes --> F[Step 4: Assign Criterion Weights]
    F --> G[Step 5: Rate Each Supplier 1 to 10]
    G --> H[Step 6: Compute Weighted Score S]
    H --> I[Step 7: Rank Suppliers by S]
    I --> J[Step 8: Select Top Ranked Supplier]
    J --> K[Step 9: Negotiate Contract]
    K --> L[Step 10: Onboard and Monitor]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The flow begins with requirement definition and ends with contract onboarding. The diamond shape at node D represents a decision gate — only vendors passing the must-have filter proceed to the weighted scoring stage.

## 4.2 Two-Stage Selection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stage1[STAGE 1 SCREENING FILTER]
        P1[Quality Certification] --> M1{All Met}
        P2[Capacity Threshold] --> M1
        P3[Financial Health] --> M1
    end
    M1 -- No --> R1[Eliminated]
    M1 -- Yes --> Stage2
    subgraph Stage2[STAGE 2 WEIGHTED SCORING]
        Q1[Quality Weight 0.4] --> S[Compute S j]
        Q2[Cost Weight 0.3] --> S
        Q3[Delivery Weight 0.2] --> S
        Q4[Service Weight 0.1] --> S
    end
    S --> Final[Highest S j Selected]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Stage 1 eliminates non-qualifying suppliers via must-have checks. Survivors enter Stage 2 where they are scored on the four weighted criteria and ranked.

## 4.3 Sourcing Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Is the part strategic or proprietary] --> S1{Yes}
    S1 -- Yes --> Single[Choose Single Sourcing]
    S1 -- No --> S2[Is volume high and supply risk moderate]
    S2 -- Yes --> Dual[Choose Dual Sourcing]
    S2 -- No --> S3[Is the part a commodity standard item]
    S3 -- Yes --> Multi[Choose Multiple Sourcing]
    S3 -- No --> Outs[Consider Outsourcing]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each diamond asks a question; each rectangle is a recommended sourcing strategy. This mirrors the strategic sourcing decisions a TQM-oriented purchasing manager would make.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any four important criteria used for supplier selection in a TQM environment.*

**Model Answer (4 × 0.75 ≈ 3 marks):**
The four most important criteria for supplier selection in a TQM environment are:
1. **Quality** — measured by defect rate, PPM, conformance to specification, and certification status (e.g., ISO 9001).
2. **Cost** — total cost of ownership including unit price, freight, inventory carrying cost, and rejection cost.
3. **Delivery Performance** — on-time delivery rate, lead time, and ability to respond to schedule changes.
4. **Supplier Capability** — technical competence, production capacity, financial stability, and R&D strength.

> [!TIP]
> *Examiner's cue:* Mentioning TQM-specific terms like *conformance*, *PPM*, and *total cost of ownership* signals depth.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Single Sourcing and Multiple Sourcing strategies.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Single Sourcing | Multiple Sourcing |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | One supplier is used for a given part | Several suppliers are approved for the same part |
| **Volume** | Entire quantity from one supplier | Quantity split among suppliers |
| **Relationship** | Long-term strategic partnership | Transactional / competitive |
| **Risk** | High (single point of failure) | Lower (supply diversification) |
| **Cost leverage** | Lower bargaining power | Higher competitive pressure |
| **Best suited for** | Proprietary, strategic, or JIT items | Commodity, non-critical items |
| **TQM compatibility** | High (if supplier is certified) | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> *Valuation key:* A clean side-by-side table earns full marks; one-line answers lose 1–1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In 14-mark WPM questions, students frequently **(a) forget to normalize weights**, **(b) round off too early producing wrong ranking**, and **(c) ignore qualitative justification** after the math. The model solutions below show all three safeguards.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
*[(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks]*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **seven-step methodology** for supplier selection in a TQM context. Highlight the role of weighted criteria in this process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company wants to select a supplier for a precision bearing. Four suppliers (P, Q, R, S) are evaluated on five criteria. The raw weights and ratings (on a 1–10 scale) are given below:

| Criterion | Raw Weight | P | Q | R | S |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 5 | 9 | 8 | 7 | 9 |
| Cost | 4 | 7 | 9 | 8 | 6 |
| Delivery | 3 | 8 | 7 | 9 | 8 |
| Technical Capability | 3 | 9 | 8 | 7 | 8 |
| Service | 1 | 7 | 6 | 8 | 7 |

*Use the Weighted Point Model to determine the optimal supplier. Justify your result. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

1. **Step 1 — Identify Requirements** (1 mark): Define the technical, quality, and commercial specifications of the bearing (material grade, dimensional tolerance, load capacity, quantity, delivery schedule).
2. **Step 2 — Identify Potential Suppliers** (1 mark): Search through vendor directories, B2B portals, trade exhibitions, and industry references to create a long list.
3. **Step 3 — Determine Selection Criteria** (1 mark): Choose the evaluation parameters — quality, cost, delivery, technical capability, service.
4. **Step 4 — Determine Importance Weights** (1 mark): Assign raw weights based on strategic priority; normalize so they sum to 1.
5. **Step 5 — Evaluate Suppliers** (1 mark): Score each supplier on each criterion using inspection data, audits, references, and pilot orders.
6. **Step 6 — Compute Weighted Scores and Rank** (1 mark): Apply $S_j = \sum w_i \cdot r_{ij}$ and rank in descending order.
7. **Step 7 — Select Optimal Supplier and Onboard** (1 mark): Choose the top-ranked supplier, negotiate, and integrate into the supply chain.

**Role of Weighted Criteria (interwoven above):** Weights reflect the strategic importance of each criterion; without them, all criteria are treated as equally important, which is rarely true. Weights transform a qualitative judgment into a quantifiable decision.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Weighted Point Calculation**

**Step 1 — Normalize the weights:**

$$
\sum w_i^{\text{raw}} = 5 + 4 + 3 + 3 + 1 = 16
$$

$$
w_1 = \frac{5}{16} = 0.3125 \quad ; \quad w_2 = \frac{4}{16} = 0.2500
$$

$$
w_3 = \frac{3}{16} = 0.1875 \quad ; \quad w_4 = \frac{3}{16} = 0.1875
$$

$$
w_5 = \frac{1}{16} = 0.0625
$$

Verification: $0.3125 + 0.2500 + 0.1875 + 0.1875 + 0.0625 = 1.0000$ ✓

**Step 2 — Compute $S_P$:**

$$
S_P = (0.3125 \times 9) + (0.2500 \times 7) + (0.1875 \times 8) + (0.1875 \times 9) + (0.0625 \times 7)
$$

$$
S_P = 2.8125 + 1.7500 + 1.5000 + 1.6875 + 0.4375 = 8.1875
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $S_Q$:**

$$
S_Q = (0.3125 \times 8) + (0.2500 \times 9) + (0.1875 \times 7) + (0.1875 \times 8) + (0.0625 \times 6)
$$

$$
S_Q = 2.5000 + 2.2500 + 1.3125 + 1.5000 + 0.3750 = 7.9375
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $S_R$:**

$$
S_R = (0.3125 \times 7) + (0.2500 \times 8) + (0.1875 \times 9) + (0.1875 \times 7) + (0.0625 \times 8)
$$

$$
S_R = 2.1875 + 2.0000 + 1.6875 + 1.3125 + 0.5000 = 7.6875
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $S_S$:**

$$
