S_S = (0.3125 \times 9) + (0.2500 \times 6) + (0.1875 \times 8) + (0.1875 \times 8) + (0.0625 \times 7)
$$

$$
S_S = 2.8125 + 1.5000 + 1.5000 + 1.5000 + 0.4375 = 7.7500
$$

**Step 6 — Rank:**

$$
S_P = 8.1875 > S_Q = 7.9375 > S_S = 7.7500 > S_R = 7.6875
$$

**Optimal Supplier: P** with a composite score of **8.1875**. (3 marks for math; 1 mark for justification)

**Justification (1 mark):** Supplier P wins because of its top-tier quality (rating 9) and technical capability (rating 9), which collectively carry the highest weight (0.3125 + 0.1875 = 0.5000, i.e., 50% of the decision). Although P's cost rating (7) is mediocre, the high weights on quality and capability dominate the decision. This is consistent with the TQM philosophy that **quality cannot be compromised for short-term cost savings**.

---

### **Question B (Alternative for Internal Choice — 14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Discuss the various **categorical, weighted point, and cost-ratio methods** of supplier evaluation. Compare their relative merits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A firm must choose between two suppliers X and Y. Quality is the most important criterion (weight 0.5), followed by delivery (0.3) and cost (0.2). The ratings (1–10) are: X → Quality 8, Delivery 9, Cost 6; Y → Quality 9, Delivery 7, Cost 8. Which supplier should be chosen? Discuss whether the firm should consider dual sourcing as a hedge. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**1. Categorical Method (2 marks):** A simple yes/no or graded classification (Excellent / Good / Average / Poor) on each criterion. No mathematical aggregation. Quick, but highly subjective and non-comparable.

**2. Weighted Point Method (3 marks):** The mathematically rigorous method derived in Section 3.1. Assigns numerical weights and ratings; produces a single composite score $S_j = \sum w_i r_{ij}$ per supplier. Most widely used in practice; KTU's flagship method.

**3. Cost-Ratio Method (2 marks):** Combines cost and quality into a single index: $CRS_j = (C_{\text{lowest}} / C_j) \times Q_j$. A supplier that is 20% more expensive but 50% better in quality can still win. Useful when cost-quality tradeoff is dominant.

**Comparative Summary:**

| Method | Math | Objectivity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Categorical | None | Low | Quick screening |
| Weighted Point | High | Medium-High | Routine selection |
| Cost-Ratio | Medium | Medium | Cost-quality tradeoff |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Weighted Point Calculation**

**Verify weights sum to 1:** $0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0$ ✓ (weights are pre-normalized).

**Compute $S_X$:**

$$
S_X = (0.5 \times 8) + (0.3 \times 9) + (0.2 \times 6) = 4.0 + 2.7 + 1.2 = 7.9
$$

**Compute $S_Y$:**

$$
S_Y = (0.5 \times 9) + (0.3 \times 7) + (0.2 \times 8) = 4.5 + 2.1 + 1.6 = 8.2
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
S_Y = 8.2 > S_X = 7.9
$$

The margin is **only 0.3 points (~3.8%)** — a narrow lead.

**Selection: Supplier Y** (3 marks for math, 2 marks for analysis).

**Dual Sourcing Discussion (2 marks):** Given the narrow margin and the dominant weight of quality (0.5), the firm should **seriously consider dual sourcing**: allocate ~60% volume to Y (quality leader) and ~40% to X (delivery leader and lower cost). This hedges against quality failure at Y and delivery failure at X, while leveraging both. In TQM terms, dual sourcing is acceptable when no single supplier dominates across *all* weighted criteria by a significant margin (typically a margin >1.0 is considered decisive).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Supplier Selection**

- **Supplier Selection** = the **Choose** stage in the 5-stage supplier management cycle.
- **TQM view:** Suppliers are *partners*, not adversaries — adopt the *Chain of Customer* concept.
- **Seven-step methodology:** Identify requirements → Identify suppliers → Set criteria → Set weights → Rate → Compute WPM → Select.
- **Two-stage filter:** Screening (must-haves) followed by Weighted Scoring (wants).
- **Five canonical criteria:** Quality, Cost, Delivery, Capability, Service.
- **Weighted Point Formula:** $S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{ij}$, with $\sum w_i = 1$.
- **Weight normalization** is mandatory if raw weights don't sum to 1.
- **Sourcing strategies:** Single (high risk, strategic), Multiple (commodity), Dual (balanced), Outsourcing (non-core).
- **Cost-Ratio Method:** $CRS = (C_{\text{lowest}}/C_j) \times Q_j$ — useful when cost-quality tradeoff dominates.
- **Common exam pitfall:** Forgetting to normalize weights → wrong ranking → ~2 marks lost.
- **Common exam pitfall:** Picking the supplier with the highest quality rating instead of the highest *weighted* score → 0 marks for selection logic.
- **TQM philosophy highlight:** Quality is a *non-negotiable threshold*; cost is a *tradeoff variable* within the quality constraint.
- **Narrow margin?** → Recommend dual sourcing as a hedge in the conclusion.
- **Final answer must include:** Math computation + ranking + 2–3 line qualitative justification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supplier Rating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Supplier Rating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supplier Rating (also called Vendor Rating or Vendor Performance Evaluation)** is a **systematic, continuous, and quantitative assessment process** used by an organization to evaluate, score, and rank its suppliers/vendors based on predefined performance criteria. It provides a structured mechanism to measure the capability and consistency of a supplier in meeting the buyer's quality, cost, delivery, and service expectations over a specified period.

In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**, supplier rating is a vital sub-process under the broader umbrella of **Supplier Quality Management** and **Supply Chain Quality Assurance**. It transforms the abstract concept of "supplier quality" into **measurable, comparable, and actionable data**, enabling the firm to make rational sourcing decisions, build strategic supplier partnerships, and drive continuous improvement across the supply chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **Module 2 – Supplier Management** and supports the Course Outcome *CO2: Apply the principles of TQM in supplier selection, rating, and development to enhance supply chain quality.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **homeowner who regularly orders groceries from three different local shops** (Supplier A, B, and C) for a year. After each purchase, you quietly maintain a small mental (or written) scorecard:

- **How fresh was the produce?** (Quality)
- **Did they deliver on time?** (Delivery reliability)
- **Was the price fair compared to market?** (Price competitiveness)
- **Did they replace damaged items without argument?** (Service / Responsiveness)
- **Did they stock items consistently?** (Availability)

At the end of the year, you naturally **"rate"** the shops. Shop A scored high on freshness but was expensive. Shop B was cheap but often delivered late. Shop C was balanced — good quality, on time, and reasonable service. Next year, you would automatically place larger orders with Shop C and reduce dependence on the others.

**Supplier Rating in TQM works exactly the same way — but with mathematical rigor, formal criteria, weighted scores, and documented records** so that an organization — not just a single person — can make consistent, defensible sourcing decisions.

## 1.3 Why Supplier Rating is Essential in TQM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Quality Chain Rule (Feigenbaum's Principle):** *"Quality is everyone's job, but it is built into the product at the supplier's end. The organization's quality is only as strong as its weakest supplier."*

Key reasons for formal supplier rating:

1. **Objective Decision-Making:** Removes bias, personal favoritism, and gut-feel from procurement.
2. **Continuous Improvement:** Provides structured feedback to suppliers to improve performance.
3. **Risk Mitigation:** Identifies underperforming suppliers before they cause production downtime.
4. **Strategic Sourcing:** Helps identify strategic partners for long-term contracts.
5. **Cost Optimization:** Links quality performance to total cost of ownership.
6. **Compliance & Certification:** Supports ISO 9001 / IATF 16949 supplier monitoring clauses.

## 1.4 Supplier Rating vs Supplier Selection

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction — Frequently asked in KTU exams!**

| Aspect | Supplier Selection | Supplier Rating |
|---|---|---|
| **When performed** | Before awarding the contract (one-time decision) | After contract is awarded (ongoing/periodic) |
| **Purpose** | Choose the *best possible* supplier from a pool of potential vendors | Evaluate the *actual performance* of an existing supplier |
| **Data Source** | Quotations, capability surveys, sample trials, financial audits | Actual delivery data, rejection reports, audit findings |
| **Frequency** | Project-based or once per sourcing cycle | Monthly, quarterly, or per-order basis |
| **Output** | Contract / Purchase Order | Performance score, ranking, action plan |

Both processes are **complementary** and together form the **Supplier Lifecycle Management** framework in TQM.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of Supplier Rating

A well-designed supplier rating system aims to achieve the following:

- **Quantify** supplier performance using measurable metrics.
- **Identify** top-performing, average, and poor suppliers.
- **Provide** documented feedback for corrective and preventive actions (CAPA).
- **Reduce** total cost of poor quality (COPQ) arising from supplier defects.
- **Support** Just-In-Time (JIT) and Total Quality Management philosophies.
- **Enable** reward-penalty frameworks, supplier awards, and long-term partnerships.

## 2.2 Criteria Used for Supplier Rating

The most commonly evaluated parameters (often remembered by the acronym **"QDCST"** — *Quality, Delivery, Cost, Service, Technology*) are:

> [!NOTE]
> **QDCST — The KTU-preferred mnemonic for supplier rating parameters.**

1. **Quality (Q):** Percentage of accepted lots, rejection rate, conformance to specifications, PPM (Parts Per Million) defect rate.
2. **Delivery (D):** On-time delivery percentage, lead-time adherence, schedule reliability.
3. **Cost / Price (C):** Price competitiveness, total cost of ownership, cost-reduction initiatives.
4. **Service (S):** Responsiveness to complaints, technical support, warranty handling, communication quality.
5. **Technology / Capability (T):** Technical competence, R&D capability, process capability indices (Cp, Cpk), innovation.

Additional parameters sometimes included:
- **Financial stability**
- **Compliance with environmental / safety regulations**
- **Capacity / scalability**
- **ISO / quality certification status**

## 2.3 Major Methods of Supplier Rating

There are **three classical methods** taught in the KTU 2024 TQM syllabus. A KTU examiner expects you to be able to **list, define, and apply** all three.

### Method 1: Categorical (Qualitative / Expert Judgment) Method

- Supplier is classified into broad categories such as **Excellent, Good, Average, Poor** based on the subjective judgment of the purchase manager.
- **No numerical scoring** is used; relies on experience and intuition.
- Simple, fast, and inexpensive.
- **Limitation:** Highly subjective, prone to bias, no documented rationale.

### Method 2: Weighted Point (Point-Score) Method — *Most Important for KTU*

- The most widely used and **exam-favorite** method.
- Each evaluation criterion is assigned a **weight** (importance) based on its strategic significance.
- The supplier is **scored** (typically on a 1–5 or 1–10 scale) against each criterion.
- The **Weighted Score** is calculated for each supplier.
- The supplier with the **highest weighted score** is rated best.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This method is the cornerstone of KTU numerical problems. Master the formula below.**

### Method 3: Cost-Based (Cost Ratio) Method

- Suppliers are evaluated primarily on the **total cost impact** they create.
- Cost components include: quoted price, cost of inspection, cost of rejection, cost of downtime, cost of warranty claims, cost of late delivery penalties.
- The supplier with the **lowest total cost ratio** is preferred.
- Particularly useful when **price negotiations** dominate the decision.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas required for solving KTU 2024 numerical problems on Supplier Rating are consolidated here.**

| S.No | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Weighted Score of a Supplier** | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ | Dimensionless score |
| 2 | **Normalized Weight** | $w_j^{norm} = \dfrac{w_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j}$ | Sum of normalized weights = 1 |
| 3 | **Quality Rating (% Acceptance)** | $Q\% = \dfrac{\text{Lots Accepted}}{\text{Total Lots Received}} \times 100$ | Expressed as % |
| 4 | **Delivery Rating (% On-Time Delivery)** | $D\% = \dfrac{\text{On-Time Deliveries}}{\text{Total Deliveries}} \times 100$ | Expressed as % |
| 5 | **Cost Ratio** | $C_{ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Net Price} + \text{Cost of Poor Quality}}{\text{Standard Price}}$ | Ratio (unitless) |
| 6 | **Total Performance Index (Composite Score)** | $TPI = \alpha \cdot Q + \beta \cdot D + \gamma \cdot C$ | Weighted sum of normalized indices |
| 7 | **PPM Defect Rate** | $PPM = \dfrac{\text{Defective Units}}{\text{Total Units Delivered}} \times 10^6$ | Parts per million |
| 8 | **Overall Vendor Performance Rating (Sample Composite)** | $R = \dfrac{\sum w_j \cdot r_j}{\sum w_j} \times 100$ | Percentage rating |

**Where:**
- $S_i$ = Total weighted score of supplier $i$
- $w_j$ = Weight assigned to criterion $j$
- $r_{ij}$ = Score given to supplier $i$ on criterion $j$
- $n$ = Total number of evaluation criteria

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real engineering organizations (automotive, aerospace, electronics), supplier rating systems are:

- Embedded in **ERP systems** (SAP, Oracle) and run automatically from inbound quality data.
- Linked to **Annual Supplier Audits** and **Supplier Scorecards** displayed on dashboards.
- Used as input to **Advanced Product Quality Planning (APQP)** and **PPAP** submissions in IATF 16949 companies.
- Foundation for **Single-Source vs Multi-Source** sourcing strategy decisions.
- Critical for achieving **"Zero Defect"** inbound quality — a core TQM target.

## 2.6 Decision Categories After Rating

Based on the rating, suppliers are typically classified into four action categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Partner / Maintain / Develop / Replace"** — the standard KTU framework for rating-based supplier action.

| Rating Range | Category | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| **90 – 100 %** | **Excellent / Preferred Partner** | Increase share of business, award long-term contracts, treat as strategic partner |
| **70 – 89 %** | **Good / Acceptable** | Maintain relationship, monitor continuously |
| **50 – 69 %** | **Average / Needs Development** | Joint improvement programs, capability development, training |
| **Below 50 %** | **Poor / Unacceptable** | Issue warning, audit, replace or de-list if no improvement |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This section provides fully worked-out numerical examples — the exact style required in KTU 14-mark problems. No step is skipped.**

---

## 3.1 Example 1 — Weighted Point Method (Classic KTU Problem)

### Problem Statement

A manufacturing company purchases a critical component from three suppliers — **A, B, and C**. The purchase manager has identified **four evaluation criteria** with the following weights:

| Criterion | Weight ($w_j$) |
|---|---|
| Quality | **0.40** |
| Delivery | **0.25** |
| Price | **0.20** |
| Service | **0.15** |

A rating team scores each supplier on a scale of **1 to 5** as follows:

| Criterion | Supplier A | Supplier B | Supplier C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quality (0.40) | 4 | 5 | 3 |
| Delivery (0.25) | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| Price (0.20) | 5 | 3 | 4 |
| Service (0.15) | 4 | 4 | 3 |

**Compute the weighted score of each supplier, rank them, and recommend the action category.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Verify the sum of weights.**

$$\sum w_j = 0.40 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 1.00$$

Since the sum equals 1, the weights are already **normalized** (no need to re-normalize). This is a useful time-saver in the exam.

---

**Step 2 — Calculate the weighted score of Supplier A.**

The general formula:

$$S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$$

Substituting the values for Supplier A:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_A &= (0.40 \times 4) + (0.25 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 4) \\
S_A &= 1.60 + 0.75 + 1.00 + 0.60 \\
S_A &= 3.95
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Supplier A scores **3.95 out of 5.00** → 79 % of maximum possible.

---

**Step 3 — Calculate the weighted score of Supplier B.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_B &= (0.40 \times 5) + (0.25 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.15 \times 4) \\
S_B &= 2.00 + 1.00 + 0.60 + 0.60 \\
S_B &= 4.20
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Supplier B scores **4.20 out of 5.00** → 84 % of maximum possible.

---

**Step 4 — Calculate the weighted score of Supplier C.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_C &= (0.40 \times 3) + (0.25 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 3) \\
S_C &= 1.20 + 1.25 + 0.80 + 0.45 \\
S_C &= 3.70
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Supplier C scores **3.70 out of 5.00** → 74 % of maximum possible.

---

**Step 5 — Convert weighted scores to percentage rating for action categorization.**

Using the conversion: $\text{Rating \%} = \dfrac{S_i}{5} \times 100$

| Supplier | Weighted Score $S_i$ | Rating % | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 3.95 | **79.0 %** | 2nd |
| B | 4.20 | **84.0 %** | **1st** ✓ |
| C | 3.70 | **74.0 %** | 3rd |

---

**Step 6 — Recommend the action category.**

- **Supplier B (84 %):** Falls in the **"Good / Acceptable"** category (70 – 89 %). Recommended action → **Maintain** relationship with continuous monitoring.
- **Supplier A (79 %):** Same "Good" category → **Maintain**, with periodic feedback.
- **Supplier C (74 %):** Borderline "Good" → **Maintain**, but initiate a **supplier development program** because the score is close to the lower threshold.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ranking is based on weighted score, not on any single criterion.** Note how Supplier A scored highest on Price (5) but lost to B overall because B's quality score (5) carried a heavier weight (0.40). This illustrates **why the weighted point method is mathematically superior** to the categorical method.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — Quality & Delivery-Based Numerical Rating

### Problem Statement

Over a quarter, Supplier X delivered **200 lots**, out of which **180 lots** passed inspection on first submission and **160 deliveries** were on time. Calculate:
1. Quality Rating ($Q\%$)
2. Delivery Rating ($D\%$)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Quality Rating.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q\% &= \frac{\text{Lots Accepted}}{\text{Total Lots Received}} \times 100 \\
Q\% &= \frac{180}{200} \times 100 \\
Q\% &= 90.00\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Delivery Rating.**

$$\begin{aligned}
D\% &= \frac{\text{On-Time Deliveries}}{\text{Total Deliveries}} \times 100 \\
D\% &= \frac{160}{200} \times 100 \\
D\% &= 80.00\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Inference.**

The supplier has **strong quality (90 %)** but **weaker delivery reliability (80 %)**. The buyer should send a structured **delivery performance improvement** communication while continuing the partnership.

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Cost-Based Supplier Rating (Cost Ratio Method)

### Problem Statement

A buyer is evaluating two suppliers for an item whose **standard market price is ₹100 per unit**. Supplier M quotes **₹95** but incurs **₹8** in inspection/rejection costs. Supplier N quotes **₹98** but incurs **₹3** in such costs. Compute the **Cost Ratio** for each and recommend.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Cost Ratio formula.**

$$C_{ratio} = \frac{\text{Net Price} + \text{Cost of Poor Quality}}{\text{Standard Price}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute for Supplier M.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{ratio}^{M} &= \frac{95 + 8}{100} \\
C_{ratio}^{M} &= \frac{103}{100} = 1.03
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute for Supplier N.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{ratio}^{N} &= \frac{98 + 3}{100} \\
C_{ratio}^{N} &= \frac{101}{100} = 1.01
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Decision.**

Since a **lower cost ratio is better**, Supplier N (1.01) is preferred over Supplier M (1.03), even though M's quoted price was lower. This is the **TQM insight**: *the cheapest quote is not always the lowest-cost supplier.*

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Three Methods

| Comparison Dimension | Categorical Method | Weighted Point Method | Cost-Based Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Numerical rigor** | Low | High | High |
| **Subjectivity** | High | Low – Medium | Low |
| **Cost of implementation** | Very low | Medium | High (requires cost tracking) |
| **Best suited for** | Small firms, single-item procurement | Multi-criteria, strategic items | High-value, defect-cost-sensitive items |
| **Documentation** | Minimal | Detailed scorecard | Detailed cost ledger |
| **TQM alignment** | Weak | Strong | Strong |
| **Exam frequency (KTU)** | Low | **Very High** | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Supplier Rating Process Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Diagram 1:** End-to-end supplier rating lifecycle as practiced in a TQM-driven organization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Evaluation Criteria QDCST] --> B[Assign Weights to Each Criterion]
    B --> C[Define Rating Scale e.g. 1 to 5]
    C --> D[Collect Supplier Performance Data]
    D --> E[Score Each Supplier Against Criteria]
    E --> F[Calculate Weighted Score for Each Supplier]
    F --> G[Convert to Percentage Rating]
    G --> H{Compare Against Rating Thresholds}
    H --> I[Rating 90 to 100 Percent Preferred Partner]
    H --> J[Rating 70 to 89 Percent Maintain Relationship]
    H --> K[Rating 50 to 69 Percent Supplier Development]
    H --> L[Rating Below 50 Percent Replace or De-list]
    I --> M[Feedback to Supplier and Continuous Monitoring]
    J --> M
    K --> N[Joint Improvement Plan and Training]
    N --> M
    L --> O[Issue Warning and Initiate Sourcing of Alternate]
```

## 4.2 Weighted Point Method — Mathematical Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Criterion 1 Weight w1] --> M[Weighted Score Computation]
    S1[Score r1] --> M
    C2[Criterion 2 Weight w2] --> M
    S2[Score r2] --> M
    C3[Criterion 3 Weight w3] --> M
    S3[Score r3] --> M
    M --> F[Final Weighted Score Si]
    F --> R[Ranking and Decision]
```

## 4.3 Supplier Categorization Map (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUP[Supplier Base] --> R{Rating Outcome}
    R --> P1[Strategic Partners]
    R --> P2[Preferred Suppliers]
    R --> P3[Approved Suppliers]
    R --> P4[Conditional Suppliers]
    R --> P5[Disqualified Suppliers]
    P1 --> A1[Long Term Contracts and Joint Development]
    P2 --> A2[Volume Allocation and Preferred Status]
    P3 --> A3[Routine Orders with Monitoring]
    P4 --> A4[Probation with Corrective Action Plan]
    P5 --> A5[De-list and Sourcing from Alternate]
```

## 4.4 Inputs and Outputs of Supplier Rating System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        I1[Incoming Inspection Data]
        I2[On Time Delivery Log]
        I3[Quoted Prices]
        I4[Service Tickets and Complaint Records]
        I5[Audit Findings]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS
        P1[Score and Weight Computation]
        P2[Threshold Comparison]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS
        O1[Supplier Scorecard]
        O2[Action Plan and Feedback]
        O3[Sourcing Strategy Update]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    I4 --> P1
    I5 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> O1
    P2 --> O2
    P2 --> O3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Two model Part A questions with precise model answers — pattern followed in KTU University Exams.**

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question] Define Supplier Rating. List any four criteria used for rating a supplier.

**Model Answer:**

> **Supplier Rating** is a systematic and continuous evaluation process used by a buying organization to **quantitatively measure the performance** of its existing suppliers against predefined criteria such as quality, delivery, cost, and service. It transforms supplier performance into a **score or rank** that supports objective decision-making regarding order allocation, development, or replacement.

**Four commonly used criteria are:**
1. **Quality** — Percentage of accepted lots, rejection rate, PPM.
2. **Delivery** — On-time delivery percentage, lead-time adherence.
3. **Price / Cost** — Price competitiveness and total cost of ownership.
4. **Service** — Responsiveness to complaints, warranty handling, technical support.

*(Examiner's note: Any 4 standard criteria are acceptable.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question] Differentiate between Supplier Selection and Supplier Rating.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Supplier Selection | Supplier Rating |
|---|---|---|
| **Timing** | Done **before** awarding a contract | Done **after** the contract is in place |
| **Purpose** | To **choose** the best supplier from competing bidders | To **evaluate** the ongoing performance of existing suppliers |
| **Data** | Quotations, capability audits, samples | Actual delivery data, rejection reports, audit results |
| **Frequency** | Once per sourcing event | Periodic — monthly or quarterly |
| **Output** | Purchase order / contract | Performance score and action category |

The two processes are **complementary**: selection brings the supplier *in*; rating keeps the supplier *accountable* and drives **continuous improvement**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B questions offer an internal choice. Two independent alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) are provided below — both fully solved.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Weighted Point Method Numerical**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Sessional Pattern]**
>
> A manufacturing company procures a precision component from **three suppliers: P, Q, and R**. The buyer has decided to rate them on **five criteria** with the following weights and supplier scores (rating scale 1 to 10):

| Criterion | Weight | Supplier P | Supplier Q | Supplier R |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 0.35 | 8 | 9 | 7 |
| Delivery | 0.25 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
| Price | 0.20 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Service | 0.10 | 8 | 8 | 6 |
| Technology | 0.10 | 6 | 9 | 7 |

**(a)** Calculate the weighted score for each supplier. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Rank the suppliers and recommend the action category for each based on the standard rating thresholds. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-A**

##### **Part (a) — Weighted Score Calculation**

> [!NOTE]
> **Verify normalization first; if sum of weights = 1, no further normalization is needed.**

$$\sum w_j = 0.35 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.10 = 1.00$$

Weights are already normalized. Apply $S_i = \sum w_j \cdot r_{ij}$.

**Supplier P:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_P &= (0.35 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 6) \\
S_P &= 2.80 + 1.75 + 1.80 + 0.80 + 0.60 \\
S_P &= 7.75
\end{aligned}$$

**Supplier Q:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_Q &= (0.35 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 9) \\
S_Q &= 3.15 + 2.00 + 1.40 + 0.80 + 0.90 \\
S_Q &= 8.25
\end{aligned}$$

**Supplier R:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_R &= (0.35 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 6) + (0.10 \times 7) \\
S_R &= 2.45 + 2.25 + 1.60 + 0.60 + 0.70 \\
S_R &= 7.60
\end{aligned}$$

##### **Part (b) — Ranking and Action Recommendation**

Convert weighted scores (out of 10) into percentage rating:

$$\text{Rating \%} = \frac{S_i}{10} \times 100$$

| Supplier | Weighted Score $S_i$ | Rating % | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|
| P | 7.75 | **77.5 %** | 2nd |
| Q | 8.25 | **82.5 %** | **1st** ✓ |
| R | 7.60 | **76.0 %** | 3rd |

**Action Category Recommendations:**

- **Supplier Q (82.5 %):** Lies in the **70 – 89 % "Good / Acceptable"** band → **Maintain relationship with continuous monitoring.** Eligible for higher share of business.
- **Supplier P (77.5 %):** Same "Good" band → **Maintain with structured feedback**, especially on Technology score.
- **Supplier R (76.0 %):** Same "Good" band, but borderline → **Maintain and initiate a supplier development program** (improve Service rating, which scored only 6).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight from the problem:** Although Supplier P scored highest on Price (9), it lost the overall ranking to Supplier Q because Quality and Technology carried higher weights. This is precisely why TQM uses **weighted scoring** — to align ratings with strategic priorities.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Methods of Supplier Rating (Theory + Numerical)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **three major methods of supplier rating** with their merits and limitations. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A company receives **500 units** from a supplier, of which **25 are rejected** in inspection. **480 units** were scheduled, of which **30 deliveries** were late. The standard price is ₹200; the supplier quoted **₹190** but caused **₹15 in rework and rejection cost** per accepted unit. Calculate the **Quality Rating, Delivery Rating, and Cost Ratio**, and recommend whether the supplier should be retained. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-B**

##### **Part (a) — Three Major Methods of Supplier Rating**

1. **Categorical (Qualitative) Method:**
   - Supplier is classified into broad categories — Excellent, Good, Average, Poor — based on the **subjective judgment** of the purchase manager.
   - *Merits:* Simple, fast, low cost, no complex data needed.
   - *Limitations:* Highly subjective, no documented rationale, prone to bias.

2. **Weighted Point (Point-Score) Method:**
   - Each evaluation criterion is assigned a **weight**; the supplier is **scored** on each criterion; a **weighted total** is calculated.
   - *Merits:* Objective, quantitative, multi-criteria, well-documented, easily auditable.
   - *Limitations:* Requires accurate weight assignment and reliable performance data.

3. **Cost-Based (Cost Ratio) Method:**
   - Supplier is evaluated on the **total cost impact** — quoted price plus cost of poor quality (inspection, rejection, rework, downtime, warranty).
   - *Merits:* Strongest link to financial performance; ideal for cost-sensitive items.
   - *Limitations:* Requires sophisticated cost-tracking systems; can overlook strategic non-cost factors.

##### **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation**

**Step 1 — Quality Rating.**

Total units received = 500, Rejected = 25.
Units accepted = 500 − 25 = 475.

$$Q\% = \frac{475}{500} \times 100 = 95.00\%$$

**Step 2 — Delivery Rating.**

Total scheduled deliveries = 480, Late = 30.
On-time deliveries = 480 − 30 = 450.

$$D\% = \frac{450}{480} \times 100 = 93.75\%$$

**Step 3 — Cost Ratio.**

$$C_{ratio} = \frac{\text{Quoted Price} + \text{Cost of Poor Quality}}{\text{Standard Price}}$$

Cost of poor quality is stated as **₹15 per accepted unit**, so total CoPQ = $475 \times 15 = \text{₹}7{,}125$. To keep it per-unit:

$$C_{ratio} = \frac{190 + 15}{200} = \frac{205}{200} = 1.025$$

**Step 4 — Recommendation.**

- Quality Rating of **95 %** is strong.
- Delivery Rating of **93.75 %** is strong.
- Cost Ratio of **1.025** is slightly above standard (5 % over the standard price when quality cost is included).

**Verdict:** The supplier is performing well on quality and delivery but is **slightly expensive when total cost of ownership is considered**. The recommendation is to **retain the supplier with monitoring** and **negotiate** to either reduce quoted price to ₹185 or jointly reduce the cost of poor quality below ₹10 per unit. This aligns with the **"Maintain"** category in the action framework.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in Supplier Rating questions — and where marks are lost:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize weights** when the sum of weights is not 1.0. Always check $\sum w_j$ first; if not equal to 1, divide each by the sum.
> 2. **Confusing "score out of 5" with "percentage."** Always convert explicitly: $\text{Rating \%} = \dfrac{S_i}{S_{max}} \times 100$ before categorizing into Partner/Maintain/Develop/Replace.
> 3. **Mixing up Supplier Selection and Supplier Rating** in theory questions — this is a very common Part-A trap. Memorize the comparison table.
> 4. **In Cost Ratio problems, students often forget to add the "cost of poor quality"** to the quoted price. The cost ratio is *never* just $\dfrac{\text{Quoted}}{\text{Standard}}$ — it always includes quality-related costs.
> 5. **Not writing the formula before substituting** — KTU examiners award a **dedicated mark for stating the formula**. Always write $S_i = \sum w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ before plugging in numbers.
> 6. **Using inconsistent rating scales** (some criteria on 1–5, others on 1–10). This invalidates the weighted sum. **Normalize all scores to the same scale first.**
> 7. **Skipping the action recommendation.** A numerical problem without the final decision (Partner / Maintain / Develop / Replace) is considered incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — read this just before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Supplier Rating** is the *post-contract, continuous* performance evaluation of suppliers.
- ✅ It is **different from Supplier Selection**, which is a pre-contract activity.
- ✅ The acronym **QDCST** (Quality, Delivery, Cost, Service, Technology) covers the standard evaluation criteria.
- ✅ The **Weighted Point Method** is the most important and exam-frequent method.
- ✅ Key formula: $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ — memorize and apply.
- ✅ Always check that $\sum w_j = 1$ before calculation; if not, **normalize** the weights.
- ✅ Convert weighted score to **percentage** before applying the action category thresholds.
- ✅ Action categories: **90–100 % = Partner**, **70–89 % = Maintain**, **50–69 % = Develop**, **< 50 % = Replace**.
- ✅ Quality Rating $Q\% = \dfrac{\text{Accepted Lots}}{\text{Total Lots}} \times 100$.
- ✅ Delivery Rating $D\% = \dfrac{\text{On-Time Deliveries}}{\text{Total Deliveries}} \times 100$.
- ✅ Cost Ratio $C_{ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Quoted Price} + \text{Cost of Poor Quality}}{\text{Standard Price}}$.
- ✅ TQM philosophy: **The lowest quoted price ≠ the lowest-cost supplier.** Always include cost of poor quality.
- ✅ Categorical method is simple but subjective; Weighted Point is objective; Cost-Based is financially rigorous.
- ✅ Supplier Rating drives **continuous improvement**, **risk mitigation**, and **strategic sourcing decisions** in a TQM environment.
- ✅ In every numerical answer: **State formula → Substitute values → Show calculation → Interpret result → Recommend action.** This five-step structure earns full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relationship Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relationship Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Relationship Development (RD)** in Total Quality Management (TQM) is the strategic, systematic, and continuous process of building, nurturing, and maintaining mutually beneficial long-term partnerships between an organization and its key stakeholders — primarily **customers, suppliers, employees, and alliance partners** — to achieve superior quality outcomes, sustainable competitive advantage, and shared value creation.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, Relationship Development is positioned as a critical pillar of TQM that moves beyond transactional exchanges to collaborative engagement, ensuring that quality is co-created across the value chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Relationship Development encompasses Customer Relationship Management (CRM), Supplier Relationship Management (SRM), and the cultivation of internal and external partnerships that align with the organization's quality philosophy and continuous improvement goals.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying fresh vegetables every day from a street vendor versus having a trusted family grocer who knows your preferences, remembers your budget, sets aside the best produce for you, and even offers credit during tough months.

- **The street vendor** represents a *transactional* relationship — every interaction starts from zero, no loyalty, no trust, easily replaceable.
- **The family grocer** represents a *developed relationship* — built on trust, mutual benefit, emotional connection, and long-term commitment.

In the same way, organizations that invest in **Relationship Development** treat customers, suppliers, and employees not as one-time exchanges but as **partners in a shared quality journey**. When a defect occurs, a developed relationship ensures collaborative problem-solving rather than blame-shifting. This is the essence of TQM — quality is everyone's responsibility, and relationships are the glue that holds the system together.

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Constants in Relationship Development

The following metrics are **standard TQM benchmarks** used to evaluate relationship quality:

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Relationship Metrics (Industry-Standard Values):**
> - **Customer Retention Rate (CRR):** Target ≥ **90%** for mature quality-driven firms
> - **Net Promoter Score (NPS):** Industry benchmark ranges from **+30 to +50**
> - **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV):** High-performing TQM firms achieve CLV growth of **15–25% annually**
> - **Defect Rate (Six Sigma Target):** **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**
> - **Supplier On-Time Delivery:** Target ≥ **98%**
> - **Employee Engagement Index:** World-class benchmark ≥ **70%**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pillars of Relationship Development in TQM

Relationship Development in TQM rests on **four interconnected pillars**:

1. **Customer Relationship Management (CRM)**
   - The strategic process of managing all interactions a company has with current and potential customers.
   - Uses data analysis to improve business relationships, focusing on **customer retention** and **driving sales growth**.

2. **Supplier Relationship Management (SRM)**
   - The systematic approach to managing and optimizing interactions with third-party organizations that supply goods, materials, or services.
   - Emphasizes **collaboration, transparency, and joint quality improvement**.

3. **Internal Relationship Development**
   - Focuses on **employee engagement, cross-functional teamwork, and a culture of trust** within the organization.
   - Recognized as the foundation upon which external relationships are built.

4. **Strategic Partnerships and Alliances**
   - Long-term collaborations with external entities (distributors, technology partners, academic institutions) to **co-create value and share quality standards**.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Pillar

- **Why CRM?** Because acquiring a new customer costs **5–7 times more** than retaining an existing one. Quality is judged not just by product performance but by the **entire customer experience**.

- **Why SRM?** Because in modern supply chains, **up to 80% of a product's quality** is determined by suppliers. You cannot deliver quality to your customer if your supplier delivers defects to you.

- **Why Internal Relationships?** Because disengaged employees cannot deliver engaging customer experiences. The **Service-Profit Chain** (Heskett et al.) proves that internal service quality directly drives external service value.

- **Why Strategic Partnerships?** Because no organization possesses all capabilities. Partnerships extend the organization's quality ecosystem beyond its boundaries.

### 2.3 The Relationship Development Lifecycle

Relationship Development is not a one-time event — it follows a structured lifecycle:

| Stage | Description | TQM Activity | Key Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Identification** | Identifying potential partners/customer segments | Market segmentation, supplier evaluation | Target list |
| **Acquisition** | Initiating the relationship | First contact, needs assessment, quality dialogue | Onboarded partner |
| **Engagement** | Building trust and communication channels | Joint workshops, Kaizen events, QCCs | Established rapport |
| **Development** | Deepening collaboration | Shared quality metrics, joint problem-solving | Co-created value |
| **Retention/Loyalty** | Sustaining the relationship | Loyalty programs, recognition, continuous feedback | Long-term partnership |
| **Advocacy** | Partner becomes promoter | Referral programs, testimonials | Brand ambassadors |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are standard TQM/Quality Management formulas verified against academic literature.**

$$
\text{Customer Retention Rate (CRR)} = \frac{(C_E - C_N)}{C_S} \times 100
$$

Where:
- $C_E$ = Customers at end of period
- $C_N$ = New customers acquired during the period
- $C_S$ = Customers at start of period

$$
\text{Churn Rate} = \frac{C_L}{C_S} \times 100
$$

Where $C_L$ = Customers lost during the period.

$$
\text{Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(R_t - C_t) \times r_t}{(1 + d)^t}
$$

Where:
- $R_t$ = Revenue from customer in period $t$
- $C_t$ = Cost of serving customer in period $t$
- $r_t$ = Retention probability in period $t$
- $d$ = Discount rate
- $T$ = Time horizon

$$
\text{Net Promoter Score (NPS)} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}
$$

$$
\text{Supplier Defect Rate} = \frac{\text{Number of Defective Units Received}}{\text{Total Units Received}} \times 10^6 \text{ (DPMO)}
$$

$$
\text{On-Time Delivery (OTD)\%} = \frac{\text{Deliveries On Time}}{\text{Total Deliveries}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)} = \frac{\sum (\text{Attribute Importance} \times \text{Attribute Satisfaction})}{\sum \text{Attribute Importance}} \times 100
$$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- In the **automotive industry**, Toyota's supplier relationship model (the *Toyota Production System*) demonstrates how SRM drives world-class quality with **supplier defect rates below 0.1 DPMO**.
- In **software engineering**, Microsoft's partner ecosystem shows how strategic alliances multiply quality innovation.
- In **manufacturing**, Boeing's *Partnering for Success* program integrates tier-1 and tier-2 suppliers into a single quality governance framework.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) — Simplified Two-Year Model

Let us derive a simplified CLV formula that students can use in KTU numerical problems.

**Step 1: Define Annual Customer Profit**
The profit generated by a customer in a single year is the difference between revenue and the cost to serve:

$$
\text{Annual Profit} = R - C
$$

**Step 2: Apply Retention Probability**
If a customer has a probability $r$ of being retained each year, the expected profit in year $t$ is:

$$
E(\text{Profit}_t) = (R - C) \cdot r^t
$$

**Step 3: Discount Future Cash Flows**
Money received in the future is worth less today. We apply a discount rate $d$:

$$
\text{Discounted Profit}_t = \frac{(R - C) \cdot r^t}{(1 + d)^t}
$$

**Step 4: Sum Over the Time Horizon**
Total CLV is the sum of all discounted profits over $T$ years:

$$
\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(R - C) \cdot r^t}{(1 + d)^t}
$$

$$
\text{CLV} = (R - C) \cdot \sum_{t=0}^{T} \left(\frac{r}{1 + d}\right)^t
$$

**Step 5: Apply Geometric Series Formula**
For an infinite horizon where $r < (1 + d)$:

$$
\text{CLV}_{\infty} = (R - C) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \frac{r}{1+d}} = (R - C) \cdot \frac{1+d}{1+d-r}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> A company earns an average annual revenue of ₹50,000 per customer. The cost to serve each customer is ₹20,000. The annual retention rate is 85%, and the discount rate is 10%. Calculate the CLV over a 5-year horizon.

**Given:**
- $R = 50{,}000$
- $C = 20{,}000$
- $r = 0.85$
- $d = 0.10$
- $T = 5$

**Step 1: Compute annual profit**

$$
R - C = 50{,}000 - 20{,}000 = 30{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Compute the discount factor ratio**

$$
\frac{r}{1+d} = \frac{0.85}{1.10} = 0.7727
$$

**Step 3: Apply the geometric sum**

$$
\sum_{t=0}^{4} (0.7727)^t = \frac{1 - (0.7727)^5}{1 - 0.7727}
$$

**Step 4: Compute numerator**

$$
(0.7727)^5 \approx 0.2763
$$

$$
1 - 0.2763 = 0.7237
$$

**Step 5: Compute denominator**

$$
1 - 0.7727 = 0.2273
$$

**Step 6: Compute the sum**

$$
\frac{0.7237}{0.2273} \approx 3.184
$$

**Step 7: Multiply by annual profit**

$$
\text{CLV} = 30{,}000 \times 3.184 \approx 95{,}520
$$

**Result:** The Customer Lifetime Value over 5 years is approximately **₹95,520**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Relationship Strategies

| Strategy | Focus Area | Primary Metric | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Transactional Marketing** | One-time sales | Conversion rate | Quick revenue | No loyalty, high churn |
| **CRM (Basic)** | Customer database | Retention rate | Personalization | Costly technology |
| **CRM (Advanced)** | Predictive analytics | CLV, NPS | Proactive engagement | Data privacy concerns |
| **SRM (Collaborative)** | Joint quality improvement | Supplier DPMO | Shared innovation | Dependency risk |
| **Strategic Alliances** | Ecosystem quality | Co-created IP | Market expansion | Coordination complexity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

Define **Customer Relationship Management (CRM)** in the context of Total Quality Management. List any **two benefits** of CRM.

**Model Answer:**

CRM is a strategic approach in TQM that focuses on managing and analyzing all interactions a company has with its current and potential customers to build long-term, mutually beneficial relationships and improve customer retention and satisfaction.

**Two benefits:**
1. Enhanced customer retention leading to increased Customer Lifetime Value (CLV).
2. Improved customer feedback loop enabling continuous product and service quality improvement.

*(Word limit: ~80–100 words for full 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

What is **Supplier Relationship Management (SRM)**? Mention any **two key practices** of SRM.

**Model Answer:**

SRM is the systematic process of managing, evaluating, and collaborating with suppliers to ensure their goods and services align with the organization's quality standards and strategic objectives.

**Two key practices:**
1. Joint quality audits and supplier capability assessments.
2. Long-term contracts with shared quality metrics and continuous improvement targets.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four pillars of Relationship Development** in TQM. Discuss how each pillar contributes to overall quality excellence. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company has the following data:
- Customers at start of year: **2,000**
- New customers acquired: **400**
- Customers at end of year: **2,100**

Calculate:
- (i) Customer Retention Rate (CRR) **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) Churn Rate **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) Briefly comment on the relationship between CRR and profitability. **(2 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The four pillars of Relationship Development in TQM are:

**1. Customer Relationship Management (CRM):** CRM focuses on building long-term relationships with customers through personalized interactions, loyalty programs, feedback systems, and data-driven engagement strategies. It contributes to quality excellence by aligning products and services with evolving customer expectations, reducing defects through customer feedback, and improving the overall customer experience.

**2. Supplier Relationship Management (SRM):** SRM ensures that suppliers are not just vendors but strategic partners. It includes joint quality planning, supplier development programs, shared performance metrics, and collaborative problem-solving. It contributes to quality excellence by ensuring that incoming materials and components meet quality standards, thereby preventing defects at the source.

**3. Internal Relationship Development:** This pillar focuses on employee engagement, cross-functional collaboration, open communication, and a culture of trust. It contributes to quality excellence by ensuring that motivated and empowered employees are committed to quality goals, leading to higher productivity and innovation.

**4. Strategic Partnerships and Alliances:** These are long-term collaborations with external entities such as distributors, technology firms, and academic institutions. They contribute to quality excellence by extending the organization's quality ecosystem, enabling co-innovation, knowledge sharing, and faster time-to-market.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $C_S = 2{,}000$
- $C_N = 400$
- $C_E = 2{,}100$

**(i) Customer Retention Rate:**

$$
\text{CRR} = \frac{C_E - C_N}{C_S} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{CRR} = \frac{2{,}100 - 400}{2{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{CRR} = \frac{1{,}700}{2{,}000} \times 100 = 85\%
$$

**(ii) Churn Rate:**

$$
\text{Customers Lost } (C_L) = C_S - (\text{Retained Customers})
$$

$$
C_L = 2{,}000 - 1{,}700 = 300
$$

$$
\text{Churn Rate} = \frac{300}{2{,}000} \times 100 = 15\%
$$

**(iii) Comment on CRR and Profitability:**

A Customer Retention Rate of **85%** indicates a healthy relationship base, but the 15% churn suggests room for improvement. Research shows that a **5% increase in retention can boost profits by 25–95%** (Bain & Co.). Therefore, the company should invest in CRM initiatives to reduce churn and improve profitability.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - 2023 Supplementary]

**(a)** Discuss the **concept and significance of Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** in TQM. Derive the simplified CLV formula and explain each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **Service-Profit Chain** and how it links internal relationships to customer satisfaction and profitability. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Concept:** Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) is the total net profit a company can expect to earn from a customer over the entire duration of their relationship. It is a critical TQM metric because it shifts organizational focus from short-term transactions to long-term relationship value.

**Significance:**
- Helps prioritize customer segments for quality investment.
- Justifies expenditure on quality improvement and customer service.
- Links customer satisfaction directly to financial outcomes.

**Derivation (refer to Section 3.1 for full derivation):**

$$
\text{CLV} = (R - C) \cdot \frac{1+d}{1+d-r}
$$

**Component Explanation:**
- $R$ = Average annual revenue per customer
- $C$ = Average annual cost to serve the customer
- $r$ = Annual customer retention probability
- $d$ = Annual discount rate (reflects time value of money)

**Model Solution for (b):**

The **Service-Profit Chain** (developed by Heskett, Jones, Loveman, Sasser, and Schlesinger at Harvard) is a strategic framework that links internal service quality to external customer satisfaction and ultimately to profitability.

**The chain operates as follows (refer to the diagram in Section 4.2):**

1. **Internal Service Quality** → A quality-oriented work environment with proper training, tools, and management support.
2. **Employee Satisfaction** → When internal service is high, employees feel valued and satisfied.
3. **Employee Retention** → Satisfied employees stay longer, reducing turnover costs and preserving institutional knowledge.
4. **Employee Productivity** → Experienced, engaged employees are more productive and deliver better output.
5. **External Service Value** → Productive employees create superior value for customers.
6. **Customer Satisfaction** → Customers perceive the value and become satisfied.
7. **Customer Loyalty** → Satisfied customers remain loyal and repeat purchase.
8. **Revenue Growth and Profitability** → Loyalty drives sustainable revenue and profit growth.

**Key Insight:** The chain proves that **internal relationship quality (employee relations) is the root cause of external relationship quality (customer satisfaction) and financial performance.** Organizations that neglect internal relationships cannot sustain external relationship development.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse CRM (Customer Relationship Management) with CMR (Customer Marketing Relations). CRM is the correct acronym.
> - In CLV numerical problems, students often forget to apply the **retention probability** $r^t$ to each year — this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> - When drawing the Service-Profit Chain, examiners expect **all 8 links in correct sequential order** — missing even one link costs 1 mark.
> - For SRM questions, avoid generic answers like *"good communication."* Use **TQM-specific terminology** such as *joint Kaizen, supplier scorecards, poka-yoke at source.*
> - Always state **units** (%, ₹, DPMO) in numerical answers — missing units is a common ½-mark deduction in KTU valuation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Relationship Development in TQM**

- **Definition:** Relationship Development is the strategic process of building long-term, mutually beneficial partnerships with customers, suppliers, employees, and partners to co-create quality.

- **Four Pillars:** CRM, SRM, Internal Relationships, Strategic Alliances — **all four are mandatory to mention** for full marks in essay-type questions.

- **CRM Goal:** Move from transactional to relational engagement → higher **retention, CLV, and NPS**.

- **SRM Goal:** Treat suppliers as partners → use **joint quality planning, supplier scorecards, and Kaizen events** at the source.

- **Service-Profit Chain:** Internal Service Quality → Employee Satisfaction → Retention → Productivity → External Value → Customer Satisfaction → Loyalty → Profit. **Remember all 8 links.**

- **Key Formulas:**
  - $\text{CRR} = \frac{C_E - C_N}{C_S} \times 100$
  - $\text{Churn Rate} = \frac{C_L}{C_S} \times 100$
  - $\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$
  - $\text{CLV} = (R - C) \cdot \frac{1+d}{1+d-r}$

- **Industry Benchmarks:** CRR ≥ 90%, NPS ≥ +30, OTD ≥ 98%, Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO.

- **Real-World Examples:** Toyota Production System (SRM), Apple Supplier Code (SRM + Strategic Alliances), Amazon's customer obsession culture (CRM + Internal Relationships).

- **Golden Rule of KTU Answers:** Always connect the concept back to **TQM philosophy** (customer focus, continuous improvement, employee involvement, data-driven decisions). Generic management answers will not fetch full marks.

- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Relationship Development is **not just customer service.** It is a **strategic, organization-wide philosophy** that includes suppliers, employees, and partners.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Measures - Basic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Performance Measures - Basic Concepts # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Performance Measures (Performance Metrics / KPIs)** are quantifiable indicators used by organizations to evaluate, monitor, and improve the efficiency, effectiveness, and quality of their business processes, products, and services against established strategic objectives and benchmarks. In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**, performance measurement is the systematic process of collecting, analyzing, and reporting data regarding operational outcomes. It forms the **nervous system of any quality initiative**—without measurement, improvement cannot be verified, controlled, or sustained. ### Key Terminology Box > [!NOTE] > **Critical Performance Vocabulary (KTU Syllabus Standard)** > - **Metric:** A standard of measurement (e.g., defects per million). > - **Indicator (KPI):** A metric tied directly to a strategic goal. > - **Benchmark:** A reference point (internal, competitive, or generic) used for comparison. > - **Variance:** The deviation of an actual result from the planned/standard value. > - **Baseline:** The initial measurement value before any improvement intervention. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car Dashboard Imagine you are driving a car on a long highway. The dashboard has several gauges: - **Speedometer** → tells you *current performance* (how fast you are going). - **Fuel Gauge** → tells you *resource consumption* (efficiency). - **Engine Temperature** → tells you *process health* (is something overheating?). - **GPS / Trip Meter** → tells you *progress against the destination* (goal alignment). **Performance Measures in TQM work exactly like this dashboard.** A manager cannot steer a company toward quality excellence without real-time, accurate, and relevant gauges. Just as ignoring the engine temperature light can destroy a car's engine, ignoring key quality metrics can destroy an organization's competitive position. --- ## 1.3 Why Performance Measurement is Critical in TQM > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Quality Trinity — Edwards Deming's Insight:** > *"You cannot manage what you cannot measure. You cannot measure what you cannot define. You cannot define what you do not understand."* The three primary purposes of performance measurement in TQM are: 1. **Control** — Detect deviations from standards early. 2. **Improvement** — Provide data for root cause analysis and PDCA cycles. 3. **Communication** — Align every department with the organization's quality vision. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Performance Measures Performance measures are broadly classified along **two fundamental axes**: ### Axis 1: Process vs. Outcome Measures | Category | Definition | Example | Focus | |---|---|---|---| | **Process Measure** | Measures *how well* an activity is performed | Cycle time, defect rate at station | Efficiency | | **Outcome Measure** | Measures the *final result* delivered to the customer | Customer satisfaction score, warranty claims | Effectiveness | ### Axis 2: Leading vs. Lagging Indicators | Indicator Type | Nature | Predictive Power | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Leading Indicator** | Input / Process-oriented | High (predicts future) | Employee training hours, supplier audit score | | **Lagging Indicator** | Output / Result-oriented | Low (confirms past) | Net profit, annual defect count, customer complaints | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** A balanced TQM system requires **BOTH** leading and lagging indicators. Relying solely on lagging indicators is like driving while looking only in the rear-view mirror. --- ## 2.2 The Traditional vs. Modern Performance Measurement Paradigm | Aspect | Traditional (Pre-TQM) | Modern (TQM Era) | |---|---|---| | **Focus** | Financial only (cost, profit) | Multi-dimensional (financial, customer, process, learning) | | **Time Horizon** | Short-term, retrospective | Long-term, prospective | | **Scope** | Departmental, isolated | Organization-wide, integrated | | **Data Type** | Intuitive, subjective | Objective, statistically validated | | **Flexibility** | Rigid, fixed standards | Adaptive, dynamic KPIs | --- ## 2.3 The Four Pillars of TQM Performance Measurement The **modern TQM framework** (synthesized from Juran, Deming, Crosby, and Kaplan-Norton) measures performance across four interconnected dimensions: 1. **Customer-Centric Measures** — Satisfaction, loyalty, NPS. 2. **Internal Process Measures** — Cycle time, yield, defect rates. 3. **Employee / Learning Measures** — Training hours, engagement, suggestion uptake. 4. **Financial Measures** — Cost of quality, ROI on quality, revenue growth. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these formulas — they are the highest-frequency computational items in KTU ESE questions on this module.** | # | Metric | Formula | Standard Unit / Target | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Defect Rate (DR)** | $\text{DR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units Produced}} \times 100$ | **Percentage (%)** | Manufacturing quality | | 2 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units from First Attempt}}{\text{Total Units Started}} \times 100$ | **Percentage (%)** | Process capability | | 3 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \text{FPY}_1 \times \text{FPY}_2 \times \cdots \times \text{FPY}_n$ | **Decimal $\vert$ Percentage** | Multi-stage processes | | 4 | **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}$ | **Defects / Million** | Six Sigma benchmarking | | 5 | **Sigma Level ($\sigma$)** | $\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}(1 - \text{Defect Rate}) + 1.5$ | **Sigma (World-Class $\geq 4.5$)** | Process excellence | | 6 | **Cost of Quality (COQ)** | $\text{COQ} = P + A + \text{IF} + \text{EF}$ | **Currency (₹ / $)** | Quality economics | | 7 | **Availability** | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime + Downtime}} \times 100$ | **Percentage (%)** | Equipment reliability | | 8 | **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** | $\text{CSI} = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Importance} \times \text{Satisfaction})}{\sum \text{Importance}}$ | **Score (1–10)** | Service quality | | 9 | **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** | $\text{OTD} = \dfrac{\text{Orders Delivered on Time}}{\text{Total Orders}} \times 100$ | **Percentage (%)** | Logistics / supply chain | | 10 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | **Percentage (World-Class $\geq 85\%$)** | Manufacturing productivity | > **Where:** $P$ = Prevention Cost, $A$ = Appraisal Cost, $IF$ = Internal Failure Cost, $EF$ = External Failure Cost. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Manufacturing:** FPY and RTY are used to evaluate assembly line efficiency in automotive and electronics plants (e.g., ISO/TS 16949 audits). - **Software Industry:** DPMO is the foundation of CMMI Level 5 and Six Sigma implementations in companies like Infosys, TCS, and Wipro. - **Healthcare:** CSI and OTD are adapted to measure patient satisfaction and surgical outcomes. - **Civil Engineering:** OEE and Availability are applied to heavy construction equipment (cranes, batching plants). - **Service Sector:** NPS and OTD dominate the airline, banking, and e-commerce industries. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Defect Rate to Sigma Level The relationship between defect rate and sigma level is the **single most important derivation** in TQM performance measurement. Let us derive it step-by-step. ### Step 1 — Start with the Observed Defect Rate $$\text{Defect Rate} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Opportunities}}$$ ### Step 2 — Convert to Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO) To standardize the metric across industries with different process complexity, we multiply by $10^6$: $$\text{DPMO} = \text{Defect Rate} \times 10^{6}$$ ### Step 3 — Relate to the Standard Normal Distribution The number of standard deviations ($\sigma$) that fit between the mean and the nearest specification limit is given by the inverse of the standard normal cumulative distribution function, $\Phi^{-1}$. $$Z = \Phi^{-1}(1 - \text{Defect Rate})$$ ### Step 4 — Apply the Six Sigma 1.5 Sigma Shift Correction To account for long-term process drift observed in real manufacturing environments, **Motorola's Six Sigma methodology** introduced a **1.5-sigma shift** to the right of the mean: $$\boxed{\sigma_{\text{level}} = Z + 1.5}$$ ### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A process produces 9,000 defects per million opportunities. Calculate the **Six Sigma Level**. **Solution:** $$\text{Defect Rate} = \frac{9{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.0090$$ $$Z = \Phi^{-1}(1 - 0.0090) = \Phi^{-1}(0.9910)$$ $$Z \approx 2.37$$ $$\sigma_{\text{level}} = 2.37 + 1.5 = \mathbf{3.87 \, \sigma}$$ **Interpretation:** A process yielding 9,000 DPMO is operating at approximately a **3.87 sigma level**, which is well below the world-class benchmark of $\geq 4.5\sigma$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) Consider a **four-stage manufacturing process** with the following First Pass Yields: | Stage | FPY | |---|---| | Stage 1 — Stamping | 0.98 | | Stage 2 — Welding | 0.96 | | Stage 3 — Painting | 0.95 | | Stage 4 — Assembly | 0.97 | The probability that a unit passes through **all four stages** without any rework is the product of individual probabilities (multiplication rule for independent events): $$\begin{aligned} \text{RTY} &= \text{FPY}_1 \times \text{FPY}_2 \times \text{FPY}_3 \times \text{FPY}_4 \\ &= 0.98 \times 0.96 \times 0.95 \times 0.97 \\ &= 0.8665 \end{aligned}$$ Converting to percentage: $$\text{RTY} = 86.65\%$$ This means that for every **1,000 units entering** the line, only **867 units** will pass through all four stages without requiring any rework. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Hidden Factory Insight:** Even when each stage appears acceptable (>95%), the cumulative effect at four stages drops yield to ~87%. This phenomenon is called the **"hidden factory"** in TQM literature and is a classic KTU question theme. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Cost of Quality (COQ) Computation **Problem:** A manufacturing unit reports the following annual quality costs: - Prevention Cost (Training, Design Reviews) = **₹ 5,00,000** - Appraisal Cost (Inspection, Testing) = **₹ 3,00,000** - Internal Failure Cost (Rework, Scrap) = **₹ 8,00,000** - External Failure Cost (Warranty, Returns) = **₹ 4,00,000** **Compute the Total Cost of Quality and the cost percentages.** **Step 1 — Total COQ:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{COQ} &= P + A + IF + EF \\ &= 5{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 \\ &= \mathbf{₹\, 20{,}00{,}000} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Percentage Breakdown:** $$\begin{aligned} P\% &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 25\% \\ A\% &= \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 15\% \\ IF\% &= \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 40\% \\ EF\% &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 20\% \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Strategic Insight (KTU Critical Thinking):** $$\text{Compliance Cost} = P + A = ₹\, 8{,}00{,}000 \quad (40\%)$$ $$\text{Non-Compliance Cost} = IF + EF = ₹\, 12{,}00{,}000 \quad (60\%)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Optimal COQ Strategy:** As an organization matures in TQM, **Prevention and Appraisal costs should rise** (investing in doing it right the first time), while **Internal and External Failure costs should fall** (fewer defects reaching customers). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Performance Measure to Use? | Business Objective | Recommended Primary Metric | Secondary Metric | |---|---|---| | Reduce manufacturing defects | **DPMO / Sigma Level** | FPY, Defect Rate | | Improve customer loyalty | **CSI / NPS** | Repeat purchase rate | | Optimize equipment usage | **OEE** | Availability, MTBF | | Reduce quality-related spending | **COQ** | Failure cost ratio | | Improve supplier reliability | **Supplier Defect Rate** | OTD from supplier | | Enhance workforce capability | **Training hours / employee** | Suggestion implementation rate | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The TQM Performance Measurement Architecture The following block-level flow diagram illustrates how performance measures cascade from strategic vision down to shop-floor data collection. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Strategic Vision and Mission] --> B[Strategic Objectives] B --> C[Key Performance Indicators KPI] C --> D[Operational Metrics] D --> E[Data Collection Systems] E --> F[Statistical Analysis SPC Six Sigma] F --> G[Performance Review and Reporting] G --> H{Performance Gap?} H -- Yes --> I[Root Cause Analysis] I --> J[Corrective Action PDCA] J --> E H -- No --> K[Continuous Improvement] K --> B ``` ## 4.2 The Four Perspectives of Performance Measurement The **Balanced Scorecard** (Kaplan & Norton, 1992) integrated into the TQM framework provides a holistic view of organizational performance. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Financial Perspective
Cost Revenue ROI] P2[Customer Perspective
Satisfaction Loyalty NPS] P3[Internal Process Perspective
Cycle Time Yield Defects] P4[Learning and Growth Perspective
Training Skills Innovation] P1 --- P2 P2 --- P3 P3 --- P4 P4 --- P1 Center((Organizational
Strategy and Vision)) P1 --- Center P2 --- Center P3 --- Center P4 --- Center ``` ## 4.3 The Cost of Quality (COQ) Categorization ```mermaid flowchart TD COQ[Total Cost of Quality COQ] COQ --> CC[Compliance Cost] COQ --> NC[Non Compliance Cost] CC --> P[Prevention Cost
Training Design Reviews] CC --> A[Appraisal Cost
Inspection Testing Audits] NC --> IF[Internal Failure Cost
Rework Scrap Reinspection] NC --> EF[External Failure Cost
Warranty Returns Loss of Reputation] ``` ## 4.4 Performance Measure Selection Filter (SMART Logic) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q[Candidate Performance Measure] --> S{Specific
Clearly defined?} S -- No --> R1[Refine Definition] R1 --> S S -- Yes --> M{Measurable
Quantifiable data available?} M -- No --> R2[Identify Data Source] R2 --> M M -- Yes --> A{Achievable
Realistic target?} A -- No --> R3[Re baseline Target] R3 --> A A -- Yes --> R{Relevant
Linked to strategy?} R -- No --> R4[Realign with Objective] R4 --> R R -- Yes --> T{Time Bound
Review frequency defined?} T -- No --> R5[Set Review Cadence] R5 --> T T -- Yes --> APPROVED[Approved KPI for Deployment] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]** ### **Q1. Define Performance Measurement. List any four characteristics of a good performance measure.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Performance Measurement is the systematic process of defining, collecting, analyzing, and reporting quantifiable indicators that reflect the critical success factors of an organization in achieving its quality and strategic objectives. **Four Characteristics of a Good Performance Measure (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):** 1. **SMART Compliance** — Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound. 2. **Validity** — The measure must accurately reflect what it intends to measure. 3. **Reliability** — It should yield consistent results under the same conditions. 4. **Actionability** — Data derived from the measure should drive decisions and improvements. --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between Leading and Lagging Indicators with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Leading Indicator | Lagging Indicator | |---|---|---| | **Nature** | Input / Process-oriented | Output / Result-oriented | | **Time Orientation** | Predictive (future-focused) | Confirmatory (past-focused) | | **Controllability** | Highly controllable by management | Difficult to control after the fact | | **Example** | Number of training hours per employee | Annual customer complaint count | | **TQM Use** | Early warning system | Final outcome verification | Leading indicators **forecast** future performance, while lagging indicators **report** what has already occurred. A robust TQM system deploys both for balanced feedback. **(1.5 Marks for differentiation + 1 Mark for examples + 0.5 Mark for concluding statement)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern: Two sub-parts of 7 marks each.** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the **Balanced Scorecard** framework for performance measurement. Discuss its **four perspectives** in detail. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A process produces **2,500 defects per 1,000 units**. Each unit has **10 defect opportunities**. Calculate: (i) Defect Rate (ii) DPMO (iii) Sigma Level **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]** **Introduction (1 Mark):** The Balanced Scorecard, developed by **Robert S. Kaplan and David P. Norton** in 1992 (Harvard Business Review), is a strategic performance management framework that supplements traditional financial metrics with three other perspectives to give managers a more "balanced" view of organizational performance. **Four Perspectives (1.5 Marks each):** 1. **Financial Perspective** — Addresses shareholder value. Metrics: ROI, revenue growth, cost reduction, cash flow. 2. **Customer Perspective** — Focuses on customer satisfaction, retention, and market share. Metrics: CSI, NPS, on-time delivery, customer complaints. 3. **Internal Business Process Perspective** — Examines operational excellence. Metrics: cycle time, yield, defect rate, FPY, RTY, OEE. 4. **Learning and Growth Perspective** — Targets the organization's ability to innovate, improve, and learn. Metrics: employee training hours, R&D investment, employee satisfaction, suggestion system yield. **Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** The four perspectives are interconnected — improvements in learning drive better processes, which enhance customer satisfaction, which ultimately improves financial performance. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]** **Given Data:** - Defects = 2,500 - Units = 1,000 - Opportunities per unit = 10 **Step 1 — Defect Rate (2 Marks):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Defect Rate} &= \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units Produced} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \\ &= \frac{2{,}500}{1{,}000 \times 10} \\ &= \frac{2{,}500}{10{,}000} \\ &= 0.25 \text{ or } 25\% \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — DPMO (2 Marks):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{DPMO} &= \text{Defect Rate} \times 10^{6} \\ &= 0.25 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\ &= \mathbf{250{,}000 \, \text{DPMO}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Sigma Level (3 Marks):** $$\begin{aligned} Z &= \Phi^{-1}(1 - 0.25) = \Phi^{-1}(0.75) \\ Z &\approx 0.6745 \\ \sigma_{\text{level}} &= Z + 1.5 = 0.6745 + 1.5 \\ &= \mathbf{2.17 \, \sigma} \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** A DPMO of 250,000 corresponds to a very low sigma level of **2.17σ**, indicating the process is operating far below the world-class benchmark of $\geq 4.5\sigma$. Immediate quality intervention is required. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]** **(a)** Define **Cost of Quality (COQ)**. Explain its four categories with suitable examples. Discuss how COQ helps in decision-making. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** The following data is collected from a 4-stage production line: | Stage | First Pass Yield (FPY) | |---|---| | Stage 1 | 0.99 | | Stage 2 | 0.97 | | Stage 3 | 0.95 | | Stage 4 | 0.93 | Calculate the **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** and determine the **hidden factory percentage**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]** **Definition (1 Mark):** Cost of Quality (COQ), formalized by **Joseph M. Juran** and popularized by **Philip Crosby**, is the total cost incurred by an organization to ensure product/service conformance plus the cost incurred due to non-conformance. **Four Categories (1 Mark each — 4 Marks total):** 1. **Prevention Costs (P)** — Costs of activities aimed at *preventing* defects. *Examples:* Quality training, supplier capability programs, design reviews, quality planning. 2. **Appraisal Costs (A)** — Costs of activities to *detect* defects. *Examples:* Incoming inspection, in-process testing, final product audits, calibration of equipment. 3. **Internal Failure Costs (IF)** — Costs of defects found *before* reaching the customer. *Examples:* Rework, scrap, reinspection, machine downtime due to defects. 4. **External Failure Costs (EF)** — Costs of defects discovered *after* reaching the customer. *Examples:* Warranty claims, returns, recalls, customer compensation, legal liability, loss of reputation. **Decision-Making Utility (2 Marks):** COQ enables management to: - Identify the **largest cost category** (typically IF or EF in immature quality systems). - Justify investments in **prevention activities** (e.g., training) that reduce far costlier failure costs. - Benchmark year-on-year improvements in the **prevention-to-failure cost ratio** as a TQM maturity indicator. - Allocate budgets optimally across the four categories following Juran's "**goldmine**" principle. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]** **Step 1 — Apply the RTY Formula (4 Marks):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{RTY} &= \text{FPY}_1 \times \text{FPY}_2 \times \text{FPY}_3 \times \text{FPY}_4 \\ &= 0.99 \times 0.97 \times 0.95 \times 0.93 \end{aligned}$$ Computing step-by-step: $$0.99 \times 0.97 = 0.9603$$ $$0.9603 \times 0.95 = 0.9123$$ $$0.9123 \times 0.93 = 0.8484$$ $$\boxed{\text{RTY} = 0.8484 \text{ or } 84.84\%}$$ **Step 2 — Hidden Factory Percentage (3 Marks):** The "hidden factory" represents the proportion of units that **require rework or are scrapped** at any stage: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Hidden Factory} &= 1 - \text{RTY} \\ &= 1 - 0.8484 \\ &= 0.1516 \\ &= \mathbf{15.16\%} \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** Although each individual stage appears highly capable (all $> 0.93$), the cumulative effect produces a hidden factory of **15.16%**, meaning out of every 10,000 units entering production, approximately **1,516 units** will require some form of rework or scrapping. This is a classic example of Juran's "rule of multiplication" in action. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout** > > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:** > > 1. **Confusing Defect Rate and Defects Per Unit.** Always clarify whether the denominator is "units" or "opportunities" before applying the DPMO formula. > 2. **Forgetting the 1.5-sigma shift.** Many students compute $Z$ correctly but stop there. The shift is *mandatory* in Six Sigma — the examiner will deduct at least **1 mark** for omitting it. > 3. **Wrong RTY Application.** RTY is a **multiplication** of FPYs, not an average.
