# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q: List the Seven Tools of Quality and explain the purpose of the Pareto Chart in TQM.**

**Model Answer:**
The Seven Tools of Quality are: (1) Cause-and-Effect Diagram, (2) Check Sheet, (3) Control Chart, (4) Histogram, (5) Pareto Chart, (6) Scatter Diagram, and (7) Flowchart.

The **Pareto Chart** is a graphical tool used to **prioritize problems or causes** based on their frequency or impact. It is based on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)**, which states that approximately **80%** of problems are caused by **20%** of the causes. By ranking defect categories in descending order and plotting cumulative percentages, managers can identify the "vital few" causes to address first, ensuring resources are directed at the most impactful issues. It separates the "vital few" from the "trivial many."

*(3 marks — full marks: 1 for listing all 7, 2 for Pareto explanation)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: Differentiate between common cause variation and special cause variation with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Common Cause Variation | Special Cause Variation |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Source | Inherent to the process | External to the process |
| Frequency | Occurs constantly | Occurs sporadically |
| Pattern | Random, predictable | Unpredictable, assignable |
| Action | Requires process redesign | Requires removal of the specific cause |
| Control chart | Points stay within limits | Points fall outside control limits |
| Example | Slight variation in bolt diameter within ±0.01 mm | A power surge causing a sudden spike in oven temperature |

*(3 marks — full marks: clear tabular differentiation with one example each)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**
**Q: (a)** Explain the construction and interpretation of an $\bar{X}$–$R$ control chart with a suitable diagram. Define UCL, CL, and LCL. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process produces bolts with target diameter 20 mm. Five samples of size 5 are drawn with the following data:

| Sample | $\bar{X}_i$ | $R_i$ |
|--------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | 20.4 | 2.0 |
| 2 | 20.0 | 1.5 |
| 3 | 19.8 | 2.2 |
| 4 | 20.2 | 1.8 |
| 5 | 20.1 | 1.6 |

Given $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_4 = 2.114$, $D_3 = 0$ for $n = 5$, compute the control limits and comment on process stability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Construction of $\bar{X}$–$R$ Control Chart:**

1. **Collect data** in subgroups of equal size $n$ (typically $n = 4$ to $n = 6$) at regular time intervals.
2. **Compute** subgroup mean $\bar{X}_i$ and range $R_i$ for each subgroup.
3. **Compute** grand mean $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and average range $\bar{R}$.
4. **Calculate limits** using tabulated constants.

**Definitions:**

- **Center Line (CL):** The process mean, estimated as $\bar{\bar{X}}$. Represents the expected value of the sample statistic.
- **Upper Control Limit (UCL):** The boundary at $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ for the $\bar{X}$ chart. Points above indicate special cause variation.
- **Lower Control Limit (LCL):** The boundary at $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$. Points below indicate special cause variation.

**Interpretation:** If all sample means fall within UCL and LCL, and all ranges within the $R$-chart limits, the process is **in statistical control** (only common cause variation present). Points outside indicate the process is **out of control** and requires investigation.

*(7 marks — diagram 2 marks, definitions 2 marks, interpretation 3 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1: Compute $\bar{\bar{X}}$.**

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{20.4 + 20.0 + 19.8 + 20.2 + 20.1}{5} = \frac{100.5}{5} = 20.10 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\bar{R}$.**

$$\bar{R} = \frac{2.0 + 1.5 + 2.2 + 1.8 + 1.6}{5} = \frac{9.1}{5} = 1.82 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Compute $\bar{X}$ chart limits.**

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 20.10 + (0.577)(1.82) = 20.10 + 1.05 = 21.15 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = 20.10 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = 20.10 - 1.05 = 19.05 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Compute $R$ chart limits.**

$$\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R} = (2.114)(1.82) = 3.85 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{CL}_R = 1.82 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R} = 0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Check process stability.**

All sample means (20.4, 20.0, 19.8, 20.2, 20.1) lie within **[19.05, 21.15] mm** ✓
All sample ranges (2.0, 1.5, 2.2, 1.8, 1.6) lie within **[0, 3.85] mm** ✓

**Conclusion:** The process is **in statistical control**. No special cause variation is present. The natural variation is acceptable, and the process is operating consistently around the target of 20 mm.

*(7 marks — computation 5 marks, conclusion 2 marks)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Q: (a)** What is a Histogram? Explain the steps to construct a histogram with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data represents the number of customer complaints received per day for 30 days: 2, 3, 5, 4, 6, 3, 2, 4, 5, 7, 3, 4, 5, 2, 6, 4, 3, 5, 4, 3, 2, 5, 6, 4, 3, 5, 4, 2, 3, 6. Construct a frequency distribution and draw a histogram. Comment on the distribution shape. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Definition:** A **Histogram** is a graphical representation of a frequency distribution where data is grouped into contiguous, non-overlapping intervals (called **bins** or **classes**) and plotted as bars. The bar area is proportional to the frequency.

**Construction Steps:**

1. **Determine the range** $R = \text{Max} - \text{Min}$.
2. **Decide the number of bins** $k$ using Sturges' Rule: $k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(N)$.
3. **Calculate bin width** $w = R / k$ (round up for convenience).
4. **Define bin boundaries** starting from the minimum.
5. **Tally** each data point into the appropriate bin.
6. **Plot** bins on x-axis, frequency on y-axis.
7. **Analyze shape** — look for normal, skewed, bimodal, or uniform distributions.

**Example:** Heights of 50 students: minimum = 150 cm, maximum = 185 cm, $R = 35$ cm, $N = 50$, $k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(50) \approx 6$, $w \approx 6$ cm. Bins: 150–156, 156–162, 162–168, 168–174, 174–180, 180–186.

*(7 marks — definition 1, steps 4, example 2)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1: Identify extremes and range.**

$$\text{Min} = 2, \quad \text{Max} = 7, \quad R = 7 - 2 = 5$$

**Step 2: Determine number of bins.**

$$k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(30) = 1 + 3.322 (1.477) = 1 + 4.91 \approx 6 \text{ bins}$$

**Step 3: Calculate bin width.**

$$w = R / k = 5 / 6 \approx 0.83 \rightarrow \text{round up to } 1$$

**Step 4: Tally frequencies.**

| Class Interval | Tally | Frequency $f_i$ |
|----------------|-------|------------------|
| 2–3 | \|\|\|\|\|\|\|\|\| (9) | 9 |
| 3–4 | \|\|\|\|\|\|\|\|\|\|\| (11) | 11 |
| 4–5 | \|\|\|\|\|\|\|\| (7) | 7 |
| 5–6 | \|\|\|\| (4) | 4 |
| 6–7 | \|\|\|\| (4) | 4 |
| 7–8 | $\vert$ (1) | 1 |
| **Total** | | **30** ✓ |

**Step 5: Draw the histogram.**
Plot class intervals on x-axis, frequencies on y-axis. Bars are adjacent (no gaps).

**Step 6: Comment on distribution shape.**
The histogram is **right-skewed (positively skewed)** — the tail extends toward higher values. The mode is in the 3–4 class. The distribution is **not normal**; it suggests that most days see 2–4 complaints, with occasional spikes. Management should investigate the days with 6–7 complaints using a cause-and-effect diagram to identify any special causes (e.g., a specific product defect or service issue).

*(7 marks — computation 3, drawing 2, interpretation 2)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Mixing up control chart constants** — Students frequently swap $A_2$ with $D_4$, or use $D_3$ for the $\bar{X}$ chart. **Remember:** $A_2$ is for the $\bar{X}$ chart; $D_3$ and $D_4$ are for the $R$ chart only.
> 2. **Forgetting the LCL formula sign** — LCL is **subtracted**, not added. The most common arithmetic error.
> 3. **Confusing correlation with causation** — A scatter diagram shows correlation only. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction. You will lose 2 marks for stating "X causes Y" based on a high $r$ value.
> 4. **Skipping the "in statistical control" conclusion** — In every control chart problem, you MUST end with an explicit statement of whether the process is in or out of control. Marks are reserved specifically for this conclusion.
> 5. **Not sorting Pareto data in descending order** — A Pareto chart with unsorted bars receives **zero marks** for the diagram. Sorting is the entire essence of the tool.
> 6. **Stating $\bar{\bar{X}}$ without showing the calculation** — Always show the formula and intermediate values, even if the arithmetic seems obvious.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Seven Tools of Quality (KTU 2024)**
>
> - **Tool 1 — Fishbone Diagram (Ishikawa):** Brainstorming tool; 6M categories (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Environment). Used to find **root causes**, not solutions.
> - **Tool 2 — Check Sheet:** Simple, structured **data collection form** used at the source. No math; just disciplined tallying.
> - **Tool 3 — Control Chart:** Time-series with **UCL, CL, LCL**. Differentiates common vs special cause variation. The most powerful of the seven tools for **ongoing process monitoring**.
> - **Tool 4 — Histogram:** Bar chart of **frequency distribution**. Reveals shape, center, spread, and outliers. Construction requires bins, width, and tallying.
> - **Tool 5 — Pareto Chart:** Bar + cumulative line chart. Sorted **descending** by frequency. Identifies the **vital few** vs **trivial many** using the **80/20 rule**.
> - **Tool 6 — Scatter Diagram:** Paired $(X, Y)$ plot. Quantified by **Pearson correlation coefficient $r$**, ranging from $-1$ to $+1$. **Correlation ≠ Causation**.
> - **Tool 7 — Flowchart:** Process map using **standardized symbols** (oval = start/end, rectangle = process, diamond = decision). Used to standardize and analyze workflows.
> - **3-Sigma Rule:** Control limits at $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ capture **99.73%** of normal variation.
> - **Constants (for $n = 5$):** $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_4 = 2.114$, $D_3 = 0$. These are **must-memorize** values for KTU exams.
> - **Order of problem-solving with the 7 tools:** Identify symptoms (Check Sheet) → Prioritize (Pareto) → Find causes (Fishbone) → Verify stability (Control Chart) → Map process (Flowchart) → Investigate relationships (Scatter) → Visualize data (Histogram).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical Fundamentals - Measures of Central Tendency and Dispersion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Statistical Fundamentals - Measures of Central Tendency and Dispersion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Statistical Analysis in TQM?

Statistical analysis is the **backbone of Total Quality Management (TQM)**. It transforms raw data (defects, dimensions, cycle times, customer complaints) into **actionable intelligence** for process improvement. Without statistics, quality remains a feeling; with statistics, it becomes a **measurable, controllable, and improvable** engineering attribute.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Framework)**
> Statistical Fundamentals constitute the mathematical toolkit that converts process data into decision-ready indices. They are broadly classified into two families: **Measures of Central Tendency** (where does the data "centre"? ) and **Measures of Dispersion** (how "spread out" is the data?).

### 1.2 Measures of Central Tendency (MCT)

A **Measure of Central Tendency** is a single value that attempts to describe a set of data by identifying the **central position** within that data set.

**Formal Definition:** A measure of central tendency is a numerical summary that indicates the *typical*, *central*, or *average* value of a probability distribution or a finite sample of observations.

The three primary measures are:
1. **Mean** (Arithmetic, Geometric, Harmonic)
2. **Median**
3. **Mode**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Classroom Photograph**
> Imagine 50 students standing in a classroom, each at a different height. If you take a photograph:
> - The **Mean** is the height of the *imaginary student* who would balance a see-saw if everyone stood on it (centre of gravity).
> - The **Median** is the height of the *student standing exactly in the middle* when they line up by height.
> - The **Mode** is the height that *appears most frequently* — like a school uniform that most students wear.

### 1.3 Measures of Dispersion (MOD)

A **Measure of Dispersion** quantifies the **degree of variability**, scatter, or spread in a dataset. Two factories might have the same average production (mean = 1000 units/day), but one might swing wildly between 500 and 1500, while the other stays tightly between 950 and 1050. Dispersion tells us **which factory is more consistent**.

**Formal Definition:** A measure of dispersion is a non-negative real number that quantifies the spread of a probability distribution or sample around its central tendency. **Zero dispersion implies all data points are identical**.

Common measures include:
1. **Range**
2. **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)**
3. **Variance** $\sigma^2$
4. **Standard Deviation** $\sigma$
5. **Quartile Deviation (Semi-Interquartile Range)**
6. **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** — relative measure

> [!TIP]
> **Why Two Families Together?**
> Central tendency alone is **dangerous and misleading**. The classic TQM quote: *"Averages are lies, dispersion tells the truth."* The mean without standard deviation is like a ship without a rudder — you know its position, not its stability.

### 1.4 Role in TQM Frameworks (Deming, Six Sigma, SPC)

In **Six Sigma DMAIC** methodology, statistical fundamentals form the **Measure (M)** and **Analyze (A)** phases:
- **Central tendency** → Identifies process targets (e.g., nominal diameter of a shaft).
- **Dispersion** → Defines process capability ($\sigma$ controls defect rates; $6\sigma$ spread = 3.4 DPMO).

In **Statistical Process Control (SPC)**, control limits are set at $\bar{X} \pm 3\sigma$, making **standard deviation the single most important parameter** in modern quality engineering.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Measures of Central Tendency — Theoretical Breakdown

#### A. Arithmetic Mean (AM)
The arithmetic mean is the **sum of all observations divided by the number of observations**. It is the most widely used measure because it uses every data point and has well-understood algebraic properties.

- **Simple (Ungrouped) Arithmetic Mean:** $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$
- **Grouped (Frequency-Weighted) Arithmetic Mean:** $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i}$
- **Combined Mean (Two Sub-groups):** $\bar{x} = \dfrac{n_1 \bar{x}_1 + n_2 \bar{x}_2}{n_1 + n_2}$

**Merits:** (i) Uses all data, (ii) Algebraically tractable, (iii) Rigidly defined.
**Demerits:** (i) Highly sensitive to extreme values (outliers), (ii) Not suitable for open-ended distributions, (iii) Cannot be computed for qualitative data.

#### B. Geometric Mean (GM)
The geometric mean is the **$n$-th root of the product of $n$ observations**. It is the correct average for **ratios, percentages, growth rates, and indices** (e.g., average annual growth rate of a company over 5 years).

$$GM = \left( x_1 \cdot x_2 \cdot x_3 \cdots x_n \right)^{1/n}$$

**Key Identity:** $AM \geq GM \geq HM$ (always, with equality only when all values are identical).

**Use Case in TQM:** Averaging defect rates across multiple inspection stages where each stage's pass-rate is a percentage.

#### C. Harmonic Mean (HM)
The harmonic mean is the **reciprocal of the arithmetic mean of the reciprocals**. It is the appropriate average for **rates** (e.g., average speed when distance is fixed but time varies).

$$HM = \dfrac{n}{\sum \limits_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{x_i}}$$

**Use Case in TQM:** Average defect rate per unit when sample sizes differ across shifts.

#### D. Median
The median is the **middle value** when data is arranged in ascending (or descending) order. It splits the distribution into two equal halves.
- For odd $n$: Middle value at position $\dfrac{n+1}{2}$
- For even $n$: Arithmetic mean of the two middle values
- **Grouped data:** $M_e = L + \left( \dfrac{\frac{N}{2} - F}{f} \right) \times h$

Where $L$ = lower boundary of median class, $F$ = cumulative frequency before median class, $f$ = frequency of median class, $h$ = class width.

#### E. Mode
The mode is the **most frequently occurring value** in the dataset. A dataset may be **unimodal, bimodal, multimodal**, or have **no mode** at all.
- **Grouped data:** $M_o = L + \left( \dfrac{d_1}{d_1 + d_2} \right) \times h$

Where $d_1$ = difference between modal class frequency and preceding class frequency, $d_2$ = difference between modal class frequency and succeeding class frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Empirical Relationship (King's Check for Moderately Skewed Data):**
> $Mode \approx 3 \cdot Median - 2 \cdot Mean$

### 2.2 Measures of Dispersion — Theoretical Breakdown

#### A. Range
Simplest measure: $R = X_{max} - X_{min}$. Useful in quality control charts for small samples (e.g., $R$-chart). Sensitive to outliers; ignores intermediate variability.

#### B. Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)
$$MAD = \dfrac{\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{n}$$

Uses all data; less affected by extreme values than variance because deviations are not squared.

#### C. Variance and Standard Deviation
The **most important** measures in quality engineering.

$$\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n} \quad \text{(Population Variance)}$$

$$s^2 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1} \quad \text{(Sample Variance, unbiased)}$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternate Computational Formula (Raw Score Method):**
> $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - \bar{x}^2$
> This avoids computing deviations one-by-one and is faster for large datasets.

#### D. Quartile Deviation (QD) / Semi-Interquartile Range
$$QD = \dfrac{Q_3 - Q_1}{2}$$

Based on the middle 50% of data; highly **resistant to outliers**. Used when median is the central measure.

- $Q_1$ = First Quartile = 25th percentile
- $Q_3$ = Third Quartile = 75th percentile
- Interquartile Range $IQR = Q_3 - Q_1$

#### E. Coefficient of Variation (CV)
The **only relative (dimensionless) measure** of dispersion. Used to compare variability between two datasets having **different units or vastly different means**.

$$CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%$$

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Master Table for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Measure | Formula (Ungrouped) | Formula (Grouped) | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i}$ | Uses all data, sensitive to outliers |
| 2 | Geometric Mean | $GM = \left( \prod x_i \right)^{1/n}$ | $GM = \left( \prod x_i^{f_i} \right)^{1/N}$ | For ratios, growth rates |
| 3 | Harmonic Mean | $HM = \dfrac{n}{\sum \frac{1}{x_i}}$ | $HM = \dfrac{N}{\sum \frac{f_i}{x_i}}$ | For rates with variable base |
| 4 | Median | Middle value (sorted) | $M_e = L + \left( \dfrac{N/2 - F}{f} \right) h$ | Position-based, outlier-resistant |
| 5 | Mode | Most frequent value | $M_o = L + \left( \dfrac{d_1}{d_1 + d_2} \right) h$ | May not be unique |
| 6 | Range | $R = X_{max} - X_{min}$ | $R = U_{max} - L_{min}$ | Simplest, ignores mid-data |
| 7 | Mean Deviation | $MD = \dfrac{\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{n}$ | $MD = \dfrac{\sum f_i \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{N}$ | Uses all data, $\vert \cdot \vert$ used |
| 8 | Variance | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n}$ | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum f_i (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{N}$ | Squared units, founds $\sigma$ |
| 9 | Std. Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Same unit as data, KPI of TQM |
| 10 | Quartile Deviation | $QD = \dfrac{Q_3 - Q_1}{2}$ | $QD = \dfrac{Q_3 - Q_1}{2}$ | Based on middle 50% |
| 11 | Coeff. of Variation | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100$ | Same | Dimensionless, comparison tool |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where Each Measure Lives

- **Manufacturing (Six Sigma):** $\sigma$ → Drives process capability index $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$
- **Service Quality:** Median response time (skewed distributions).
- **Inventory Management:** EOQ models use geometric/harmonic means for varying demand.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) = arithmetic mean of failure intervals.
- **Quality Cost Analysis:** CV compares defect-rate consistency across product lines with different volumes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Ungrouped Data (Descriptive Statistics)

**Problem Statement:** A precision bearings factory records the following daily production (in hundreds) over 7 days:

$$\text{Data: } 50, \; 55, \; 60, \; 65, \; 70, \; 75, \; 80$$

Compute: (i) Mean, (ii) Median, (iii) Mode, (iv) Range, (v) Mean Deviation, (vi) Variance, (vii) Standard Deviation, (viii) CV.

---

#### (i) Arithmetic Mean

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$$

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{50 + 55 + 60 + 65 + 70 + 75 + 80}{7} = \dfrac{455}{7} = 65$$

**Interpretation:** The average daily production is **65 hundred units**, i.e., 6500 units/day.

---

#### (ii) Median

The data is already sorted (ascending). With $n = 7$, the median is the $\left( \dfrac{n+1}{2} \right) = \left( \dfrac{7+1}{2} \right) = 4^{\text{th}}$ value.

$$M_e = 65$$

**Interpretation:** The middle value of production is **65 hundred units**, identical to the mean (since the data is symmetric).

---

#### (iii) Mode

Each value appears exactly **once** in the dataset. Therefore, the dataset has **no mode** (uniform distribution).

$$M_o = \text{None / No Mode}$$

---

#### (iv) Range

$$R = X_{max} - X_{min} = 80 - 50 = 30$$

**Interpretation:** The spread of production over the week is **30 hundred units**.

---

#### (v) Mean Absolute Deviation

We compute $\vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$ for each observation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \vert 50 - 65 \vert = 15 \\
& \vert 55 - 65 \vert = 10 \\
& \vert 60 - 65 \vert = 5 \\
& \vert 65 - 65 \vert = 0 \\
& \vert 70 - 65 \vert = 5 \\
& \vert 75 - 65 \vert = 10 \\
& \vert 80 - 65 \vert = 15
\end{aligned}
$$

$$MD = \dfrac{15 + 10 + 5 + 0 + 5 + 10 + 15}{7} = \dfrac{60}{7} \approx 8.57$$

---

#### (vi) Variance

We compute $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ for each observation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& (50 - 65)^2 = 225 \\
& (55 - 65)^2 = 100 \\
& (60 - 65)^2 = 25 \\
& (65 - 65)^2 = 0 \\
& (70 - 65)^2 = 25 \\
& (75 - 65)^2 = 100 \\
& (80 - 65)^2 = 225
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\sigma^2 = \dfrac{225 + 100 + 25 + 0 + 25 + 100 + 225}{7} = \dfrac{700}{7} = 100$$

---

#### (vii) Standard Deviation

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2} = \sqrt{100} = 10$$

**Interpretation:** Production typically deviates by $\pm 10$ hundred units from the mean.

**Verification using raw-score formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i^2 & = 50^2 + 55^2 + 60^2 + 65^2 + 70^2 + 75^2 + 80^2 \\
& = 2500 + 3025 + 3600 + 4225 + 4900 + 5625 + 6400 = 30275
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - \bar{x}^2 = \dfrac{30275}{7} - 65^2 = 4325 - 4225 = 100 \;\checkmark$$

---

#### (viii) Coefficient of Variation

$$CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{10}{65} \times 100\% \approx 15.38\%$$

**Interpretation:** The relative variability is **15.38%**, indicating moderate consistency in daily production.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Grouped (Frequency Distribution) Data

**Problem Statement:** A quality control department records the lifetimes (in hours) of 50 electric bulbs. The grouped data is given below:

| Class Interval (hrs) | Frequency ($f$) | Midpoint ($x$) | Cumulative Freq. ($cf$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 – 100 | 5 | 50 | 5 |
| 100 – 200 | 10 | 150 | 15 |
| 200 – 300 | 20 | 250 | 35 |
| 300 – 400 | 10 | 350 | 45 |
| 400 – 500 | 5 | 450 | 50 |
| **Total** | **50** | — | — |

Compute: (i) Mean, (ii) Median, (iii) Mode, (iv) Standard Deviation.

---

#### (i) Arithmetic Mean (Grouped)

First, compute $f \cdot x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 5 \times 50 = 250 \\
& 10 \times 150 = 1500 \\
& 20 \times 250 = 5000 \\
& 10 \times 350 = 3500 \\
& 5 \times 450 = 2250
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\sum f \cdot x = 250 + 1500 + 5000 + 3500 + 2250 = 12500$$

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f x}{\sum f} = \dfrac{12500}{50} = 250 \text{ hours}$$

---

#### (ii) Median (Grouped)

$\dfrac{N}{2} = \dfrac{50}{2} = 25$. The cumulative frequency just greater than or equal to 25 lies in the class **200 – 300** (since $cf = 35$ here, and $cf = 15$ in the previous class).

So, median class: 200 – 300, with $L = 200$, $F = 15$ (cumulative freq. before median class), $f = 20$ (frequency of median class), $h = 100$ (class width).

$$M_e = L + \left( \dfrac{\frac{N}{2} - F}{f} \right) \times h = 200 + \left( \dfrac{25 - 15}{20} \right) \times 100$$

$$M_e = 200 + \left( \dfrac{10}{20} \right) \times 100 = 200 + 50 = 250 \text{ hours}$$

---

#### (iii) Mode (Grouped)

Modal class = class with highest frequency = **200 – 300** ($f = 20$).

- $L = 200$
- $d_1 = f - f_{\text{previous}} = 20 - 10 = 10$
- $d_2 = f - f_{\text{next}} = 20 - 10 = 10$
- $h = 100$

$$M_o = L + \left( \dfrac{d_1}{d_1 + d_2} \right) \times h = 200 + \left( \dfrac{10}{10 + 10} \right) \times 100$$

$$M_o = 200 + \left( \dfrac{10}{20} \right) \times 100 = 200 + 50 = 250 \text{ hours}$$

---

#### (iv) Standard Deviation (Grouped)

Compute $f \cdot x^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 5 \times 50^2 = 5 \times 2500 = 12500 \\
& 10 \times 150^2 = 10 \times 22500 = 225000 \\
& 20 \times 250^2 = 20 \times 62500 = 1250000 \\
& 10 \times 350^2 = 10 \times 122500 = 1225000 \\
& 5 \times 450^2 = 5 \times 202500 = 1012500
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\sum f x^2 = 12500 + 225000 + 1250000 + 1225000 + 1012500 = 3725000$$

Apply the **raw-score formula for grouped data:**

$$\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum f x^2}{\sum f} - \bar{x}^2 = \dfrac{3725000}{50} - 250^2$$

$$\sigma^2 = 74500 - 62500 = 12000$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{12000} = \sqrt{400 \times 30} = 20\sqrt{30} \approx 109.54 \text{ hours}$$

---

#### Coefficient of Variation

$$CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{109.54}{250} \times 100\% \approx 43.82\%$$

**Interpretation:** Bulb lifetimes exhibit **high variability** ($CV > 30\%$), suggesting an inconsistent manufacturing process that warrants Six Sigma intervention.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Which Measure When?

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Quick Decision Matrix**

| Data Characteristic | Best Measure of Center | Best Measure of Spread |
|---|---|---|
| Symmetric, no outliers | Mean | Standard Deviation |
| Skewed or has outliers | Median | Quartile Deviation / IQR |
| Open-ended classes | Median | Quartile Deviation |
| Qualitative (categorical) | Mode | Not applicable |
| Ratios / Percentages | Geometric Mean | CV |
| Rates (speed, defect rate) | Harmonic Mean | CV |
| Two datasets, different units | Not applicable | Coefficient of Variation |
| Small sample ($n \leq 10$) | Median | Range |
| Process Control Charts | Mean (X-bar) | Standard Deviation / Range |

---

### 3.4 Skewness and the Mean-Median-Mode Relationship

> [!NOTE]
> **Karl Pearson's Coefficient of Skewness:**
> $S_k = \dfrac{\text{Mean} - \text{Mode}}{\sigma}$ (or substitute Median if mode is undefined)

- **Symmetric:** Mean = Median = Mode
- **Positively Skewed (Right-tailed):** Mode < Median < Mean
- **Negatively Skewed (Left-tailed):** Mean < Median < Mode
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Statistical Measures

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootNode[Statistical Measures for TQM]
    ctNode[Measures of Central Tendency]
    dispNode[Measures of Dispersion]
    meanNode[Mean Family]
    medianNode[Median]
    modeNode[Mode]
    aMeanNode[Arithmetic Mean]
    gMeanNode[Geometric Mean]
    hMeanNode[Harmonic Mean]
    absDispNode[Absolute Dispersion]
    relDispNode[Relative Dispersion]
    rangeNode[Range]
    mdNode[Mean Deviation]
    varNode[Variance and Std Deviation]
    qdNode[Quartile Deviation]
    cvNode[Coefficient of Variation]
    cqdNode[Coeff of Quartile Deviation]

    rootNode --> ctNode
    rootNode --> dispNode
    ctNode --> meanNode
    ctNode --> medianNode
    ctNode --> modeNode
    meanNode --> aMeanNode
    meanNode --> gMeanNode
    meanNode --> hMeanNode
    dispNode --> absDispNode
    dispNode --> relDispNode
    absDispNode --> rangeNode
    absDispNode --> mdNode
    absDispNode --> varNode
    absDispNode --> qdNode
    relDispNode --> cvNode
    relDispNode --> cqdNode
```

### 4.2 Process Flow of Statistical Computation in a TQM Project

```mermaid
graph LR
    rawDataNode[Raw Quality Data]
    classifyNode[Classify by Type]
    symDataNode[Symmetric Data]
    skewDataNode[Skewed Data]
    catDataNode[Categorical Data]
    rateDataNode[Rates or Ratios]
    meanSelNode[Use Mean and Std Dev]
    medSelNode[Use Median and QD]
    modeSelNode[Use Mode]
    gmSelNode[Use GM and HM]
    insightNode[Statistical Insight for SPC]
    decisionNode[Quality Decision]
    actionNode[CAPA Corrective Action]

    rawDataNode --> classifyNode
    classifyNode --> symDataNode
    classifyNode --> skewDataNode
    classifyNode --> catDataNode
    classifyNode --> rateDataNode
    symDataNode --> meanSelNode
    skewDataNode --> medSelNode
    catDataNode --> modeSelNode
    rateDataNode --> gmSelNode
    meanSelNode --> insightNode
    medSelNode --> insightNode
    modeSelNode --> insightNode
    gmSelNode --> insightNode
    insightNode --> decisionNode
    decisionNode --> actionNode
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Six Sigma Process Capability Linkage

```mermaid
graph TD
    processNode[Manufacturing Process]
    dataNode[Collect Sample Data x1 to xn]
    meanCalcNode[Compute Mean mu]
    sdCalcNode[Compute Std Deviation sigma]
    uslNode[Upper Spec Limit USL]
    lslNode[Lower Spec Limit LSL]
    sixSigmaNode[Six Sigma Spread equals 6 sigma]
    cpkNode[Compute Process Capability Cpk]
    dpmNode[Defects Per Million DPM]
    verdictNode[Process Verdict]

    processNode --> dataNode
    dataNode --> meanCalcNode
    dataNode --> sdCalcNode
    uslNode --> cpkNode
    lslNode --> cpkNode
    meanCalcNode --> cpkNode
    sdCalcNode --> sixSigmaNode
    sixSigmaNode --> dpmNode
    sdCalcNode --> cpkNode
    cpkNode --> verdictNode
    dpmNode --> verdictNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The first diagram maps the **complete taxonomy** of statistical measures covered in KTU Module 3.
> - The second diagram is a **decision flowchart** — given a data type, which measure should the engineer select?
> - The third diagram shows how **Mean and Standard Deviation feed Six Sigma's $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and DPMO** computations in real TQM projects.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**Define the term 'Measure of Central Tendency'. List any three measures of central tendency and state one merit of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A measure of central tendency is a single representative value that describes the centre of a dataset by indicating the typical or average magnitude of the observations.

The three primary measures are:

| Measure | Formula | One Merit |
|---|---|---|
| Mean | $\bar{x} = \sum x_i / n$ | Uses every data point; algebraically tractable |
| Median | Middle value of sorted data | Unaffected by extreme outliers |
| Mode | Most frequent value | Suitable for qualitative/categorical data |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for table with merit)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`
**Distinguish between absolute and relative measures of dispersion with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Absolute Measure | Relative Measure |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Expresses dispersion in the **same unit** as the original data | Expresses dispersion as a **ratio or percentage**, dimensionless |
| Comparability | Cannot compare across units | Enables comparison between different units/scales |
| Example | Standard Deviation $\sigma = 10$ mm | Coefficient of Variation $CV = 15\%$ |

*Examples:* Standard Deviation (absolute) versus Coefficient of Variation (relative). Standard Deviation of bolt diameter (in mm) cannot be directly compared with SD of tensile strength (in MPa) — but their CVs can be compared.

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for definition, comparison, and example)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** The monthly sales (in ₹ lakhs) of 8 sales executives in a manufacturing firm are:
$$45, \; 55, \; 60, \; 62, \; 65, \; 70, \; 75, \; 88$$

Calculate the (i) Arithmetic Mean, (ii) Median, (iii) Mode, (iv) Range, and (v) Standard Deviation of the data.

**(b)** Explain any **four properties** of a good measure of central tendency. Mention when the **median is preferred over the mean** with a real-world example.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Given:** $n = 8$ observations; data sorted in ascending order.

**(i) Arithmetic Mean:**

$$\sum x_i = 45 + 55 + 60 + 62 + 65 + 70 + 75 + 88 = 520$$

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n} = \dfrac{520}{8} = 65 \text{ lakhs}$$

**(ii) Median:**

For even $n = 8$, median = mean of 4th and 5th observations:

$$M_e = \dfrac{x_{(4)} + x_{(5)}}{2} = \dfrac{62 + 65}{2} = \dfrac{127}{2} = 63.5 \text{ lakhs}$$

**(iii) Mode:**

Each value appears exactly once → **No Mode** (uniform distribution).

**(iv) Range:**

$$R = X_{max} - X_{min} = 88 - 45 = 43 \text{ lakhs}$$

**(v) Standard Deviation:**

Compute $(x_i - \bar{x})$ and $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& (45-65)^2 = 400 \\
& (55-65)^2 = 100 \\
& (60-65)^2 = 25 \\
& (62-65)^2 = 9 \\
& (65-65)^2 = 0 \\
& (70-65)^2 = 25 \\
& (75-65)^2 = 100 \\
& (88-65)^2 = 529
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 400 + 100 + 25 + 9 + 0 + 25 + 100 + 529 = 1188$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1188}{8}} = \sqrt{148.5} \approx 12.19 \text{ lakhs}$$

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Four properties of a good measure of central tendency:**

1. **Rigidly Defined:** The measure must have a clear, unambiguous mathematical definition so different analysts obtain the same value. *Example:* Mean has a unique formula; Mode can be ambiguous (bimodal data).

2. **Easy to Understand and Compute:** Should be conceptually simple. Mean and median qualify; percentile-based measures are more complex.

3. **Based on All Data Items (when possible):** Should incorporate every observation. Mean satisfies this; Mode ignores non-modal values.

4. **Least Affected by Sampling Fluctuations:** Should be stable across samples. Mean has the **smallest standard error** among MCTs (least variability between samples).

5. **Capable of Further Algebraic Treatment:** Mean allows easy calculation of combined mean, deviation, etc.

**When Median is Preferred Over Mean (Real-world Example):**

In a **salary survey of 100 employees** at a startup, suppose 99 employees earn ₹30,000/month and the **CEO earns ₹5,00,000/month**. The arithmetic mean becomes:

$$\bar{x} \approx \dfrac{99 \times 30000 + 500000}{100} = \dfrac{2970000 + 500000}{100} = 34700$$

This ₹34,700 figure **misrepresents** the typical worker's salary. The median = ₹30,000 (the 50th and 51st values both equal 30000) — which accurately reflects the "typical" worker.

The mean is **dragged upward by the outlier CEO salary**, whereas the median **remains robust**. Hence for **skewed distributions with outliers**, median is preferred.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**(a)** Define Quartile Deviation and Coefficient of Variation. The marks obtained by 10 students in a Quality Management exam are:
$$42, \; 55, \; 60, \; 65, \; 70, \; 72, \; 75, \; 78, \; 85, \; 92$$

Calculate (i) Quartile Deviation (QD), (ii) Coefficient of Variation (CV), and (iii) Coefficient of Quartile Deviation.

**(b)** The data below represents the diameter (in mm) of 60 bearings produced in a factory. Compute the **Arithmetic Mean and Standard Deviation** using the **grouped frequency distribution method**.

| Diameter (mm) | Number of Bearings |
|---|---|
| 20 – 22 | 5 |
| 22 – 24 | 10 |
| 24 – 26 | 15 |
| 26 – 28 | 20 |
| 28 – 30 | 10 |
| **Total** | **60** |

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Definitions:**

- **Quartile Deviation (QD):** Also called the *Semi-Interquartile Range*, it is half the difference between the third and first quartiles. It measures the spread of the middle 50% of the data and is resistant to outliers.

$$QD = \dfrac{Q_3 - Q_1}{2}$$

- **Coefficient of Variation (CV):** The ratio of standard deviation to the arithmetic mean, expressed as a percentage. It is a **dimensionless relative measure** used to compare variability across datasets with different units or means.

$$CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%$$

**Given Data (sorted):** 42, 55, 60, 65, 70, 72, 75, 78, 85, 92. $n = 10$.

**Compute Mean:**

$$\sum x_i = 42 + 55 + 60 + 65 + 70 + 72 + 75 + 78 + 85 + 92 = 694$$

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{694}{10} = 69.4$$

**Compute Quartiles:**

- $Q_1$ position $= \dfrac{(n+1)}{4} = \dfrac{11}{4} = 2.75^{\text{th}}$ value
- $Q_1 = x_{(2)} + 0.75 \times (x_{(3)} - x_{(2)}) = 55 + 0.75 \times (60 - 55) = 55 + 3.75 = 58.75$

- $Q_3$ position $= \dfrac{3(n+1)}{4} = \dfrac{33}{4} = 8.25^{\text{th}}$ value
- $Q_3 = x_{(8)} + 0.25 \times (x_{(9)} - x_{(8)}) = 78 + 0.25 \times (85 - 78) = 78 + 1.75 = 79.75$

**(i) Quartile Deviation:**

$$QD = \dfrac{Q_3 - Q_1}{2} = \dfrac{79.75 - 58.75}{2} = \dfrac{21}{2} = 10.5$$

**(ii) Coefficient of Variation:**

Compute $\sigma$ first:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (x_i - 69.4)^2 & = (42-69.4)^2 + (55-69.4)^2 + (60-69.4)^2 + (65-69.4)^2 + (70-69.4)^2 \\
& \quad + (72-69.4)^2 + (75-69.4)^2 + (78-69.4)^2 + (85-69.4)^2 + (92-69.4)^2 \\
& = 750.76 + 207.36 + 88.36 + 19.36 + 0.36 + 6.76 + 31.36 + 73.96 \\
& \quad + 243.36 + 510.76 \\
& = 1932.4
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1932.4}{10}} = \sqrt{193.24} \approx 13.90$$

$$CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{13.90}{69.4} \times 100\% \approx 20.03\%$$

**(iii) Coefficient of Quartile Deviation:**

$$CQD = \dfrac{Q_3 - Q_1}{Q_3 + Q_1} = \dfrac{79.75 - 58.75}{79.75 + 58.75} = \dfrac{21}{138.5} \approx 0.1516 \text{ or } 15.16\%$$

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1: Compute Class Midpoints ($x$) and $f \cdot x$ and $f \cdot x^2$:**

| Class | $f$ | Midpoint $x$ | $f \cdot x$ | $f \cdot x^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 20 – 22 | 5 | 21 | 105 | 2205 |
| 22 – 24 | 10 | 23 | 230 | 5290 |
| 24 – 26 | 15 | 25 | 375 | 9375 |
| 26 – 28 | 20 | 27 | 540 | 14580 |
| 28 – 30 | 10 | 29 | 290 | 8410 |
| **Total** | **60** | — | **1540** | **39860** |

**Step 2: Arithmetic Mean:**

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f x}{\sum f} = \dfrac{1540}{60} = 25.667 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Standard Deviation (raw-score formula):**

$$\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum f x^2}{\sum f} - \bar{x}^2 = \dfrac{39860}{60} - (25.667)^2$$

$$\sigma^2 = 664.333 - 658.789 = 5.544$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{5.544} \approx 2.355 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Coefficient of Variation:**

$$CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{2.355}{25.667} \times 100\% \approx 9.18\%$$

**Interpretation:** The bearing diameters have low variability ($CV < 10\%$), indicating a **stable and capable** manufacturing process — well-suited for further Six Sigma optimization.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Arithmetic slip in $\sum f x^2$:** A single wrong multiplication cascades into wrong $\sigma$. Always double-check each $f \cdot x^2$ value.
> 2. **Confusing grouped-data formulas with ungrouped:** For grouped data, use $\bar{x} = \sum f x / \sum f$, NOT $\bar{x} = \sum x / n$.
> 3. **Median Class Identification:** "Cumulative frequency $\geq N/2$" is the rule, NOT "$F \geq N/2$". $F$ is the cumulative frequency *before* the median class.
> 4. **Sample vs. Population:** KTU typically uses population SD (divide by $n$). Writing $n-1$ for KTU gets **partial credit only** unless the question explicitly says "sample".
> 5. **Unit of CV:** CV is **dimensionless/percentage** — writing it with mm or hours loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting to square-root:** Many students correctly compute $\sigma^2$ but report variance as standard deviation. Always end with $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$.
> 7. **Median position formula:** Use $\dfrac{n+1}{2}$ for ungrouped discrete data, but $\dfrac{N}{2}$ for grouped data with the formula given. Mixing these is a frequent error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

**Section A — Measures of Central Tendency:**

- ☐ **Arithmetic Mean:** $\bar{x} = \sum x_i / n$ (ungrouped); $\bar{x} = \sum f_i x_i / \sum f_i$ (grouped). Sensitive to outliers.
- ☐ **Geometric Mean:** $GM = (\prod x_i)^{1/n}$. Used for **growth rates, ratios, indices**. Always: $AM \geq GM \geq HM$.
- ☐ **Harmonic Mean:** $HM = n / \sum (1/x_i)$. Used for **average speed with fixed distance, average rates**.
- ☐ **Median:** Middle value (sorted). Ungrouped: $\dfrac{n+1}{2}$. Grouped: $L + \left( \dfrac{N/2 - F}{f} \right) h$. Outlier-resistant.
- ☐ **Mode:** Most frequent. Grouped: $L + \left( \dfrac{d_1}{d_1+d_2} \right) h$. May be undefined.
- ☐ **Empirical Relationship:** $Mode \approx 3 \cdot Median - 2 \cdot Mean$.

**Section B — Measures of Dispersion:**

- ☐ **Range:** $R = X_{max} - X_{min}$. Simple, but ignores mid-data spread.
- ☐ **Mean Deviation:** $MD = \sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert / n$. Uses all data; uses $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation.
- ☐ **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 / n$. Squared units.
- ☐ **Standard Deviation:** $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$. **Most important** measure in TQM/SPC.
- ☐ **Raw Score Formula:** $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - \bar{x}^2$ (or $\dfrac{\sum f x^2}{\sum f} - \bar{x}^2$ for grouped).
- ☐ **Quartile Deviation:** $QD = (Q_3 - Q_1)/2$. Based on middle 50%. Robust to outliers.
- ☐ **Coefficient of Variation:** $CV = \sigma/\bar{x} \times 100\%$. **Dimensionless** relative measure.
- ☐ **Coefficient of QD:** $CQD = (Q_3 - Q_1)/(Q_3 + Q_1)$. Skewness indicator.

**Section C — Decision Rules:**

- ☐ Symmetric data → **Mean + SD**
- ☐ Skewed data / outliers → **Median + QD**
- ☐ Categorical data → **Mode**
- ☐ Ratios / percentages → **GM**
- ☐ Comparing two datasets of different units → **CV**
- ☐ Six Sigma process capability → uses $\sigma$ as the fundamental unit

**Section D — Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid:**

- ☐ Do NOT use $n-1$ (Bessel's correction) unless "sample" is specified.
- ☐ Do NOT confuse $\sum x_i^2$ with $(\sum x_i)^2$.
- ☐ Do NOT compute mode when each value appears only once.
- ☐ Always write the **unit** for SD/MD/Range; CV is unit-less.
- ☐ In grouped data, **midpoint** is used as $x$, NOT the class boundary.

**Final Memory Anchor — "Mean tells WHERE, SD tells HOW WIDE"** 🎯

In TQM, the **Mean** is your **target**, and the **Standard Deviation** is your **miss distance**. Six Sigma's mission is to make the mean hit the bullseye AND shrink the miss distance to within ±3$\sigma$ on each side of the target.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Population and Sample
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Population and Sample

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)** and statistical quality control, the terms *Population* and *Sample* are foundational to all data-driven decision-making processes described in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Population (Universe):** The **entire set of measurements or observations** that can be made on a particular characteristic of interest. It is the complete collection of items, outcomes, or data points about which information is desired. In Quality Management, the population may be finite (e.g., a lot of 10,000 components) or infinite (e.g., the ongoing production process of a semiconductor fab).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample:** A finite subset of the population, selected through a defined **sampling procedure**, which is actually inspected or measured to gain information about the entire population. A sample is therefore a *representative slice* of the population, used because measuring 100% of the population is usually impossible, uneconomical, or destructive.

The **sampling process** is the bridge between the two — it is the mechanism by which elements of the population are selected for inclusion in the sample, and its design directly determines the validity of any inference made about the population.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the Quality Manager of a tea-bag factory producing **5 million tea bags per day**. You cannot taste-test every single tea bag — that would be impossible (destructive testing) and economically absurd.

Instead, you randomly pick **200 tea bags** from the day's production and weigh them, taste them, and check the string length.

- The **5 million tea bags produced that day** = the **Population**.
- The **200 tea bags you actually tested** = the **Sample**.
- The **process of picking 200 from 5 million** = **Sampling**.

You then use the measurements from the 200 tea bags to make a quality statement (an *inference*) about the entire 5 million. This is the essence of statistical quality control in TQM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in TQM?**
> - Dr. W. Edwards Deming famously said: *"In God we trust, all others must bring data."* That data almost always comes from a **sample**, not the entire population.
> - Acceptance sampling, control charts, process capability studies — all rely on the population–sample paradigm.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Aligned with KTU Syllabus

| Term | Formal Definition | Symbol (Standard) |
|---|---|---|
| Population Size | Number of elements in the entire population | $N$ |
| Sample Size | Number of elements actually measured in the sample | $n$ |
| Population Parameter | Numerical descriptive measure of the population (fixed but usually unknown) | $\mu$ (mean), $\sigma$ (std. dev.) |
| Sample Statistic | Numerical descriptive measure computed from the sample (known, varies sample to sample) | $\bar{x}$ (mean), $s$ (std. dev.) |
| Sampling | The process of selecting $n$ elements from a population of size $N$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Parameter vs. Statistic — The KTU Board loves this distinction.**
> - Parameter describes the **population** (Greek letters: $\mu$, $\sigma$, $\pi$).
> - Statistic describes the **sample** (Latin letters: $\bar{x}$, $s$, $p$).
> - A statistic is used to **estimate** the corresponding parameter.

## 1.4 Why Sampling Instead of 100% Inspection?

KTU examiners often ask: *"Why is sampling preferred over 100% inspection?"* The valid technical reasons are:

1. **Economy of cost and time** — testing every unit is prohibitively expensive.
2. **Destructive testing** — many quality tests (tensile strength, life testing, taste panels) destroy the item; only a sample can be tested.
3. **Fatigue / inspector error** — 100% inspection leads to inspector boredom and rising error rates as the shift progresses.
4. **Practicality in continuous processes** — a flowing stream of product cannot be fully inspected.
5. **Statistical sufficiency** — a well-designed sample provides reliable information about the population with quantifiable error bounds.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Chain: From Population to Inference

The reasoning that links a sample to a population follows a strict logical sequence in TQM:

- **Step 1 — Define the population of interest.** Clearly state *what* is being studied (e.g., "all M10 bolts produced on Machine 7 between 8 AM and 4 PM today").
- **Step 2 — Choose a sampling frame.** This is the accessible list or representation of the population from which the sample is drawn. (In TQM, the *sampling frame* is often the *lot* being inspected.)
- **Step 3 — Select a sampling technique.** Random, stratified, systematic, cluster, or convenience — each with different bias and risk profiles.
- **Step 4 — Draw the sample of size $n$.** Apply the chosen technique rigorously to obtain a sample of $n$ units.
- **Step 5 — Measure the quality characteristic** on each of the $n$ sample units (e.g., diameter, hardness, weight).
- **Step 6 — Compute sample statistics** ($\bar{x}$, $s$, $p$, $R$, etc.) — these are *known numerical values*.
- **Step 7 — Infer about the population parameter** using statistical inference (estimation or hypothesis testing).
- **Step 8 — Quantify the risk** of being wrong using probability (e.g., $\alpha$, $\beta$, confidence level $1-\alpha$).

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'How':** Each step builds on the previous one. A flaw in Step 3 (poor sampling method) cannot be fixed by sophisticated mathematics in Step 7. *"Garbage In, Garbage Out"* applies with full force here.

## 2.2 Types of Population in Quality Management

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognize the following population types:

### (a) Finite Population
- Contains a fixed, countable number of elements $N$.
- Example: a specific lot of **5,000 capacitors** received from a supplier.
- When $n/N > 0.05$, the **finite population correction (FPC) factor** must be applied to standard errors.

### (b) Infinite Population
- Conceptually unbounded — the process continues indefinitely.
- Example: the continuous output of a chemical reactor.
- In this case, $N$ is effectively infinite, and the FPC factor is approximately 1.

## 2.3 Sample Notation: KTU Convention

For every population parameter, there is a corresponding sample statistic. The KTU board follows the standard convention:

| Concept | Population Parameter (Greek) | Sample Statistic (Latin) |
|---|---|---|
| Mean | $\mu$ | $\bar{x}$ |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma$ | $s$ |
| Variance | $\sigma^2$ | $s^2$ |
| Proportion Defective | $p$ (or $\pi$) | $\hat{p}$ (or $p$-bar) |
| Median | $\tilde{\mu}$ | $\tilde{x}$ |
| Range | $R_{pop}$ | $R$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the dual notation?** The Greek symbols are typically *unknown constants* describing the true population. The Latin symbols are *random variables* computed from samples — they vary from sample to sample. This is the foundation of sampling distributions.

## 2.4 Sampling Techniques — The KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Technique | Working Principle | When Used in TQM | Key Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple Random Sampling (SRS)** | Every subset of size $n$ has an equal chance of being chosen | Acceptance sampling, baseline surveys | Requires a complete sampling frame |
| **Systematic Sampling** | Select every $k$-th item ($k = N/n$) from the production line | Continuous production flow | Periodic process cycles can cause bias |
| **Stratified Sampling** | Divide population into homogeneous subgroups (strata), then sample within each | When subgroups differ markedly | Misclassification of strata |
| **Cluster Sampling** | Randomly select *groups* (clusters), then sample within clusters | Geographically dispersed lots | High intra-cluster correlation |
| **Convenience Sampling** | Sample whatever is easiest to access | Rarely justified in TQM | Severe selection bias |

> [!NOTE]
> **Acceptance Sampling** (a TQM cornerstone, covered later in the module) typically uses either **single sampling, double sampling, or multiple sampling plans** — all of which operate on the population–sample paradigm.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The population–sample framework powers the following TQM tools that KTU students will encounter throughout the course:

- **Control Charts (Shewhart charts):** Each subgroup of $n=4$ or $n=5$ is a *sample* drawn from the *population* of the process. The control limits are derived from the population parameter $\mu \pm 3\sigma$.
- **Process Capability Indices ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$):** These use the sample standard deviation $s$ to estimate the population parameter $\sigma$.
- **Acceptance Sampling Plans (MIL-STD-105E, ANSI/ASQ Z1.4):** Decide *accept or reject* a lot (population) based on a small sample.
- **Six Sigma DMAIC:** The *Measure* phase is essentially a sampling-and-inference exercise.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of a population of components is estimated from a sample of failure times.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Population & Sample Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects the **high-yield formulas** that KTU examiners most frequently test in Module 3.

| # | Quantity | Population Formula (Parameter) | Sample Formula (Statistic) | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean | $\mu = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}{N}$ | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$ | Central tendency of process output |
| 2 | Variance | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^2}{N}$ | $s^2 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}$ | Process spread / variability |
| 3 | Std. Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | $s = \sqrt{s^2}$ | Six-sigma control limits |
| 4 | Proportion | $p = \dfrac{X}{N}$ | $\hat{p} = \dfrac{x}{n}$ | Defect rate estimation |
| 5 | Finite Pop. Correction (FPC) | $\text{FPC} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N-n}{N-1}}$ | — | Adjusts variance when $n/N > 0.05$ |
| 6 | Std. Error of Mean | $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ (infinite) | $s_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ (estimated) | Width of confidence interval |
| 7 | Sample Size (mean) | $n = \left( \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2$ | — | Designing the sampling plan |
| 8 | Sample Size (proportion) | $n = \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^2}$ | — | Acceptance sampling design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $n-1$ in the sample variance?**
> This is the **Bessel's correction**. Since $\bar{x}$ (not $\mu$) is used in the deviations, we lose **one degree of freedom**. Dividing by $n-1$ makes $s^2$ an **unbiased estimator** of $\sigma^2$. KTU examiners frequently award 2 marks for a clear statement of this reason.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Population Mean to Sample Mean Estimator

We want to show that $\bar{x}$ is an **unbiased estimator** of $\mu$ — i.e., $E[\bar{x}] = \mu$. This is a derivation KTU examiners value highly.

### Step-by-Step Logical Build

**Step 1 — Definition of the sample mean.**
For a sample of $n$ independent observations $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ drawn from a population with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

**Step 2 — Take the expectation of both sides.**

$$
E[\bar{x}] = E\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Use the linearity of the expectation operator.** Since $\frac{1}{n}$ is a constant, it can be pulled outside the sum:

$$
E[\bar{x}] = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} E[x_i]
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the property that each $x_i$ is a random draw from the same population**, hence $E[x_i] = \mu$ for every $i$:

$$
E[\bar{x}] = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu = \frac{1}{n} \cdot n \cdot \mu
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$
E[\bar{x}] = \mu
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The expected value of the sample mean equals the population mean. Therefore $\bar{x}$ is said to be an *unbiased* estimator of $\mu$. KTU boards love this 4-mark derivation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Variance of the Sample Mean

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of the sample mean.**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the variance operator, again pulling out the constant $\frac{1}{n^2}$:**

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{1}{n^2} \, \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Use the property that the variance of a sum of *independent* random variables equals the sum of variances:**

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(x_i) = \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma^2
$$

**Step 4 — Each $\text{Var}(x_i) = \sigma^2$ (same population), so the sum is $n\sigma^2$:**

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{1}{n^2} \cdot n \sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root to obtain the standard error of the mean:**

$$
\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight — The Square-Root Law of Sampling:**
> To *halve* the standard error of $\bar{x}$, you must *quadruple* the sample size. To *cut it by a factor of 10*, you must increase $n$ by a factor of **100**. This is why sample-size design is a delicate economic trade-off, and KTU Module 3 questions on sample size are common.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Finite Population Correction (FPC) Factor

When sampling **without replacement** from a finite population, the observations are *not independent*. The covariance between pairs is negative, which *reduces* the variance of $\bar{x}$.

**Step 1 — The exact variance of the sample mean for a finite population (sampling without replacement) is:**

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \cdot \frac{N-n}{N-1}
$$

**Step 2 — The FPC factor is therefore:**

$$
\text{FPC} = \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}}
$$

**Step 3 — Effect on the standard error:**

$$
\sigma_{\bar{x},\text{ finite}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule (KTU convention):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $\dfrac{n}{N} \le 0.05$, the FPC factor is close to 1 and may be **ignored**. If $\dfrac{n}{N} > 0.05$, the FPC factor **must** be applied.

**Worked Numerical Example.**

A lot of $N = 500$ resistors is received. A sample of $n = 50$ is drawn (so $n/N = 0.10 > 0.05$). The population standard deviation of resistance is $\sigma = 2.0 \, \Omega$.

$$
\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{2.0}{\sqrt{50}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{500-50}{500-1}} = \frac{2.0}{7.071} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{450}{499}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\bar{x}} = 0.2828 \cdot \sqrt{0.9018} = 0.2828 \cdot 0.9496 = 0.2686 \, \Omega
$$

If the FPC were ignored, the answer would be $0.2828 \, \Omega$ — an **over-estimate of 5.3%**, which would lead to unnecessarily wide control limits in a TQM application.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Sample Size for Estimating a Mean

We want the margin of error $E = Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma_{\bar{x}}$ to be at most a specified value $E$.

**Step 1 — Set up the required inequality:**

$$
Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \le E
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**

$$
\sqrt{n} \ge \frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}
$$

**Step 3 — Square both sides:**

$$
n \ge \left( \frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked numerical example (KTU typical):** A TQM team wants to estimate the mean tensile strength of a wire within $E = 5 \, \text{MPa}$ of the true value, with 95% confidence. From past data, $\sigma = 20 \, \text{MPa}$. $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$.

$$
n = \left( \frac{1.96 \times 20}{5} \right)^2 = (7.84)^2 = 61.47
$$

Always **round up** to the next integer: $n = 62$ wire samples.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Sample Size for Estimating a Proportion

**Step 1 — Standard error of a sample proportion:**

$$
\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}
$$

**Step 2 — Set margin of error:**

$$
E = Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $n$:**

$$
n = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conservative rule of thumb:** When $p$ is unknown, set $p = 0.5$, which maximizes $p(1-p) = 0.25$. This gives the **largest** required $n$ and is the *safest* engineering design choice.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Numerical Problem Patterns

| Problem Type | Given | To Find | Key Formula to Apply |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compute sample mean | $n$ data values | $\bar{x}$ | $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n}$ |
| Compute sample variance | $n$ data values, $\bar{x}$ | $s^2$ | $s^2 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}$ |
| Standard error of mean | $\sigma$, $n$ | $\sigma_{\bar{x}}$ | $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ |
| Apply FPC | $N$, $n$, $\sigma$ | Adjusted $\sigma_{\bar{x}}$ | $\sigma_{\bar{x}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}}$ |
| Sample size for mean | $Z$, $\sigma$, $E$ | $n$ | $n = \left(\frac{Z\sigma}{E}\right)^2$ |
| Sample size for proportion | $Z$, $p$, $E$ | $n$ | $n = \frac{Z^2 p(1-p)}{E^2}$ |
| Confidence interval for $\mu$ | $\bar{x}$, $s$, $n$, confidence level | Lower & upper limit | $\bar{x} \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Population–Sample–Inference Ecosystem in TQM

This block-level functional architecture maps the conceptual flow from the population of items being monitored, through sampling and measurement, to TQM decision-making.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Population N items] --> B[Define Quality Characteristic e.g. diameter]
    B --> C{Choose Sampling Plan}
    C --> D[Simple Random]
    C --> E[Stratified]
    C --> F[Systematic]
    C --> G[Cluster]
    D --> H[Sample of size n]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Measure each of n units]
    I --> J[Compute Sample Statistic xbar, s, p-hat]
    J --> K{Statistical Inference}
    K --> L[Estimate mu with Confidence Interval]
    K --> M[Test Hypothesis about mu]
    K --> N[Acceptance Sampling Decision]
    L --> O[TQM Decision Adjust Process]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Improved Population Quality]
    P --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Parameter vs. Statistic — Conceptual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POP[Population Domain Greek Symbols Unknown Constants]
        P1[mu Population Mean]
        P2[sigma Population Std Dev]
        P3[p Population Proportion]
    end
    subgraph SAMP[Sample Domain Latin Symbols Known Random Variables]
        S1[xbar Sample Mean]
        S2[s Sample Std Dev]
        S3[phat Sample Proportion]
    end
    P1 -->|Estimated by| S1
    P2 -->|Estimated by| S2
    P3 -->|Estimated by| S3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Logic for Applying the FPC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Sampling Plan Design] --> B{Know N and n}
    B -->|No infinite population| C[Ignore FPC use sigma divided by sqrt n]
    B -->|Yes finite population| D{Compute n divided by N}
    D -->|less than or equal 0.05| E[FPC approx 1 Ignore correction]
    D -->|greater than 0.05| F[Apply FPC sqrt of N minus n over N minus 1]
    E --> G[Use sigma divided by sqrt n in formulas]
    F --> H[Use sigma divided by sqrt n times FPC]
    G --> I[Compute control limits or CI]
    H --> I
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Sample Size Trade-off in TQM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Estimation Goal] --> B[Choose confidence level 90 95 99]
    B --> C[Choose tolerable error E]
    C --> D{Is sigma or p known}
    D -->|sigma known| E[Use n equals Z sigma over E squared]
    D -->|p known| F[Use n equals Z squared p 1 minus p over E squared]
    D -->|Neither known| G[Use pilot study or p equals 0.5]
    E --> H[Round up to integer]
    F --> H
    G --> E
    G --> F
    H --> I[Final Sample Size n]
    I --> J{Is n feasible cost time destructive}
    J -->|Yes| K[Execute Sampling]
    J -->|No| L[Relax E or confidence level]
    L --> H
```
