# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]`**
**Differentiate between a population and a sample in the context of Total Quality Management. Why is sampling generally preferred over 100% inspection in modern quality systems?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

A **population** is the entire set of observations or measurements that can be made on a given quality characteristic, while a **sample** is a finite subset of that population which is actually inspected.

Sampling is preferred over 100% inspection because:

1. **Destructive nature of many tests** (tensile, life, fatigue tests) prohibits 100% inspection — only a sample can be tested.
2. **Economic infeasibility** — testing every unit is prohibitively expensive and time-consuming.
3. **Inspector fatigue** — 100% inspection causes human errors to rise sharply toward the end of a shift.
4. **Statistical sufficiency** — a well-designed sample yields reliable estimates with quantified sampling risk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution (KTU pattern):** 1 mark for the population definition, 1 mark for the sample definition, 1 mark for the reasoning behind preferring sampling.

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Distinguish between a population parameter and a sample statistic. Give two examples of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

A **population parameter** is a fixed numerical value describing a characteristic of the *entire* population (usually unknown and denoted by Greek letters). A **sample statistic** is a value computed from the *sample* data (a random variable, denoted by Latin letters).

| Population Parameter (Greek) | Sample Statistic (Latin) |
|---|---|
| Population mean $\mu$ | Sample mean $\bar{x}$ |
| Population standard deviation $\sigma$ | Sample standard deviation $s$ |
| Population proportion $p$ | Sample proportion $\hat{p}$ |

The purpose of statistical inference in TQM is to use sample statistics to *estimate* the unknown population parameters.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B carries **14 marks per question** and offers an internal choice. Either **Question A or Question B** must be answered.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Based on 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A lot of $N = 1000$ electrical resistors is received from a supplier. A simple random sample of $n = 100$ resistors is drawn and their resistance values (in $\Omega$) are summarized as:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{100} x_i = 10\,980 \, \Omega, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{100} x_i^{\,2} = 1\,212\,000 \, \Omega^2
$$

Compute:
(i) The sample mean $\bar{x}$.
(ii) The sample standard deviation $s$.
(iii) The standard error of the mean $\sigma_{\bar{x}}$, applying the finite population correction since $n/N = 0.10$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to a manufacturing process, explain the difference between *parameter* and *statistic*. Construct a 95% confidence interval for the true mean resistance of the lot using the values obtained in part (a) and the standard normal critical value $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$. Comment on what this interval means for the Quality Manager.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a)**

**(i) Sample mean** [2 Marks]:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} = \frac{10\,980}{100} = 109.80 \, \Omega
$$

**(ii) Sample standard deviation** [3 Marks]:

Use the computational identity

$$
s^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\left[\sum x_i^{\,2} - \frac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n}\right]
$$

Substitute values:

$$
s^2 = \frac{1}{99}\left[1\,212\,000 - \frac{(10\,980)^2}{100}\right]
$$

Compute the squared sum term:

$$
\frac{(10\,980)^2}{100} = \frac{120\,560\,400}{100} = 1\,205\,604
$$

Therefore:

$$
s^2 = \frac{1}{99}(1\,212\,000 - 1\,205\,604) = \frac{6396}{99} = 64.606
$$

$$
s = \sqrt{64.606} = 8.038 \, \Omega
$$

**(iii) Standard error of the mean with FPC** [2 Marks]:

Check sampling fraction: $n/N = 100/1000 = 0.10 > 0.05$ ⇒ **FPC must be applied.**

$$
\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}} = \frac{8.038}{\sqrt{100}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1000-100}{1000-1}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\bar{x}} = 0.8038 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{900}{999}} = 0.8038 \cdot \sqrt{0.9009} = 0.8038 \cdot 0.9492 = 0.7629 \, \Omega
$$

**Part (b)**

**Parameter vs. Statistic** [3 Marks]:

A *parameter* is a numerical descriptive measure of the population (e.g., true mean resistance $\mu$ of the lot). It is a **fixed but unknown** constant. A *statistic* is a numerical descriptive measure of the sample (e.g., $\bar{x} = 109.80 \, \Omega$ computed from the 100 sampled resistors). It is a **random variable** that changes from sample to sample. In TQM, sample statistics are used to estimate unknown population parameters via statistical inference.

**95% Confidence Interval** [3 Marks]:

$$
\bar{x} \pm Z_{0.025} \cdot \sigma_{\bar{x}} = 109.80 \pm (1.96 \times 0.7629)
$$

$$
= 109.80 \pm 1.495 = (108.305, \; 111.295) \, \Omega
$$

**Quality Manager's comment** [1 Mark]:

The Quality Manager can be **95% confident** that the true mean resistance of the entire lot of 1000 resistors lies between **108.31 $\Omega$** and **111.30 $\Omega$**. If the design specification is, say, $110 \pm 5 \, \Omega$, this lot is consistent with the specification at the 95% confidence level.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — KTU Examiner's Warning:**
> 1. Many students forget to apply the **finite population correction** when $n/N > 0.05$. The examiner deducts 2 marks for this.
> 2. The denominator in $s^2$ is **$n - 1$**, not $n$. Using $n$ instead yields a *biased* estimator and loses 1 mark.
> 3. In part (b), students often confuse *parameter* with *statistic* definitions — examiners want the **fixed vs. random** distinction explicitly stated.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Based on 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A TQM team wishes to estimate the proportion of defective items in a very large production lot. They want the estimate to be within $\pm 0.03$ of the true proportion with 95% confidence. A pilot study of 50 items indicated 4 defectives.
(i) Estimate the sample proportion $\hat{p}$ and justify whether the FPC is needed.
(ii) Compute the required sample size using $\hat{p}$ from the pilot study and $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$. Comment on whether the pilot sample size (50) was adequate.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the four main types of sampling techniques used in Total Quality Management — *simple random, stratified, systematic,* and *cluster* sampling. For each, give **one TQM application** and **one disadvantage**.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a)**

**(i) Sample proportion from pilot study** [2 Marks]:

$$
\hat{p} = \frac{x}{n} = \frac{4}{50} = 0.08 \quad \text{(i.e., 8% defectives)}
$$

**Justification on FPC** [1 Mark]: Since the production lot is described as "very large", the population is effectively *infinite*, so the **FPC factor is 1** and is not applied.

**(ii) Required sample size** [3 Marks]:

$$
n = \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{E^2} = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot (0.08)(0.92)}{(0.03)^2}
$$

$$
= \frac{3.8416 \times 0.0736}{0.0009} = \frac{0.28274}{0.0009} = 314.16
$$

Always round **up**:

$$
n = 315 \text{ items}
$$

**Comment on pilot adequacy** [1 Mark]: The pilot sample of 50 was used only to *estimate* $\hat{p}$. It was **not** the final sampling inspection — a separate sample of **315 items** must be drawn for the actual estimation of the lot's defective proportion.

**Part (b)** — Sampling Techniques [7 Marks total, 1.75 each, round to 2+2+2+1]:

**1. Simple Random Sampling (SRS):**
- *Principle:* Every possible subset of size $n$ has an equal probability of being chosen.
- *TQM application:* Acceptance sampling under MIL-STD-105E / ANSI Z1.4 for incoming inspection.
- *Disadvantage:* Requires a complete and accurate sampling frame, which is often unavailable in continuous production.

**2. Stratified Sampling:**
- *Principle:* The population is divided into homogeneous subgroups (*strata*), and a random sample is drawn from *each* stratum.
- *TQM application:* Quality inspection across different *shifts* (morning, evening, night) when shift-to-shift variability is suspected.
- *Disadvantage:* Requires prior knowledge of the strata-defining variable; misclassification leads to biased estimates.

**3. Systematic Sampling:**
- *Principle:* Every $k$-th item is selected, where $k = N/n$.
- *TQM application:* Pulling every 50th bottle from a conveyor for quality testing.
- *Disadvantage:* If the production process has a periodic cycle aligned with $k$, the sample becomes biased.

**4. Cluster Sampling:**
- *Principle:* The population is divided into clusters (often geographic), some clusters are randomly selected, and *all* (or some) elements within selected clusters are inspected.
- *TQM application:* Auditing a few randomly selected *shipments* from a supplier who delivers to multiple depots.
- *Disadvantage:* High intra-cluster correlation reduces the effective sample size, inflating the standard error.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question B Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Sample size rounding:** Always round **up** the computed $n$. A sample size of 314.16 must be reported as **315**. Rounding down loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Conservative sample size:** When $p$ is unknown, KTU expects the use of $p = 0.5$ for the *most conservative* design. Failing to mention this convention loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Stratified vs. Cluster confusion:** KTU examiners penalize students who mix up the two. *Stratified* samples *within* every stratum; *Cluster* samples *some* clusters entirely.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Population and Sample**

- **Population** = the *entire* set of measurements (size $N$); **Sample** = a *subset* actually measured (size $n$).
- **Parameter** = fixed-but-unknown population descriptor (Greek: $\mu, \sigma, p$); **Statistic** = computed-from-sample descriptor (Latin: $\bar{x}, s, \hat{p}$).
- The sample mean is an **unbiased estimator** of the population mean: $E[\bar{x}] = \mu$.
- **Standard error of the mean:** $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ — falls only with the *square root* of $n$.
- **Sample variance** uses $n - 1$ in the denominator (**Bessel's correction**) for unbiasedness.
- **Finite Population Correction (FPC):** Apply when $n/N > 0.05$; FPC $= \sqrt{(N-n)/(N-1)}$.
- **Sample size for a mean:** $n = \left( \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}\,\sigma}{E} \right)^2$ — round **up**.
- **Sample size for a proportion:** $n = \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2}\,p(1-p)}{E^2}$ — use $p = 0.5$ if unknown.
- **Sampling techniques:** SRS, Stratified, Systematic, Cluster, Convenience (the last is generally unacceptable in TQM).
- **Why sampling wins over 100% inspection:** cost, time, destructive testing, inspector fatigue, statistical sufficiency.
- **TQM tools rooted in this paradigm:** Control charts, Process Capability ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$), Acceptance Sampling (MIL-STD-105E), Six Sigma DMAIC *Measure* phase.
- **Golden KT rule:** *Garbage In, Garbage Out* — a poor sampling plan cannot be rescued by sophisticated inference.
- **Always state the assumption of independence** of sample observations — this underpins every variance formula.
- **Confidence interval ≠ probability of containing $\mu$** — the *parameter* is fixed; the *interval* is random. State it correctly in exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Charts for Variables and Attributes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control Charts for Variables and Attributes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Control Chart?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (W.A. Shewhart, 1924):**
> A **Control Chart** is a statistical quality-control tool that monitors, controls, and improves a process over time by plotting process data against predetermined upper and lower statistical limits (control limits), thereby distinguishing *common cause* variation (inherent to the process) from *special cause* variation (due to external assignable factors).

In the language of the **KTU 2024 OBE syllabus**, a control chart is the *graphical voice of the process*. Instead of relying on a single inspection of finished goods, it listens to the process continuously and shouts "something is wrong!" the moment an abnormal pattern is detected.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car Speedometer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a control chart like the speedometer and fuel gauge of a moving car.**
> - The **centerline (CL)** is your average comfortable speed on a highway (e.g., 80 km/h).
> - The **Upper Control Limit (UCL)** is the red-line of the speedometer (e.g., 100 km/h) — cross it, and you are in danger.
> - The **Lower Control Limit (LCL)** is the minimum speed to avoid traffic fines (e.g., 60 km/h) — go below it, and you stall the traffic flow.
> - Random speed fluctuations within 60–100 km/h are *normal variation*. A sudden spike to 130 km/h is *assignable-cause variation* and demands immediate driver intervention.

The **process is "in control"** when all points fall randomly within the control limits — no patterns, no trends, no out-of-control points.

## 1.3 Variables vs. Attributes — The Fundamental Classification

Quality characteristics are of two types, and the chart you draw depends entirely on which type you measure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Variables** are *measurable* on a continuous scale (length, weight, temperature, voltage, diameter).
> **Attributes** are *countable* — they either conform or do not, or are counted as defects.

| Aspect | Variables | Attributes |
|---|---|---|
| Data Type | Continuous (e.g., 12.34 mm) | Discrete (e.g., 3 defects, 8 rejects) |
| Sample Statistic | Mean ($\bar{X}$), Range ($R$), Std. Dev. ($S$) | Proportion ($p$), Count ($c$) |
| Charts Used | $\bar{X}$-R, $\bar{X}$-S, I-MR | $p$, $np$, $c$, $u$ |
| Information | *How much* and *which direction* it deviates | Only *whether* it passes or fails |
| Sample Size | Usually small (n = 2 to 10) | Usually large (n ≥ 50) |
| Sensitivity | More sensitive (uses full info) | Less sensitive (binary) |

### 1.4 The Two "Voices" of a Process: Voice of the Customer (VOC) vs. Voice of the Process (VOP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** → defines **specification limits** (USL, LSL) — *what the customer wants.*
> - **Voice of the Process (VOP)** → defines **control limits** (UCL, LCL) — *what the process naturally delivers.*
> A capable process is one where the VOP comfortably sits **inside** the VOC.

## 1.5 The Three-Sigma Logic (The Heart of Shewhart Charts)

All standard Shewhart control charts use the **3σ (Three-Sigma) Limits** because of the empirical rule:

$$P(\mu - 3\sigma \le X \le \mu + 3\sigma) \approx 0.9973$$

This means that if the process is truly stable, only **0.27%** (≈ 3 in 1000) points will fall outside the limits purely by chance. Any point outside is, with **99.73% confidence**, due to an **assignable cause** that must be investigated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Highlight:** KTU examiners repeatedly test the distinction between **Control Limits** (calculated from process data, $\bar{X} \pm 3\sigma$) and **Specification Limits** (given by customer/design, USL/LSL). Never confuse them — they are **independent** lines drawn on possibly the same chart.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Control Chart Structure — Anatomy of Every Chart

Every control chart has five components:

1. **Centerline (CL)** — the grand average of the statistic being plotted.
2. **Upper Control Limit (UCL)** — CL + 3σ (statistical upper bound of natural variation).
3. **Lower Control Limit (LCL)** — CL − 3σ (statistical lower bound).
4. **Plotted Points** — sample statistics in time order.
5. **Zones A, B, C** — divisions between $\bar{X} \pm 1\sigma$, $\pm 2\sigma$, $\pm 3\sigma$ (used in Western Electric / Nelson rules for pattern analysis).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Empirical Rule (68-95-99.73):**
> Within $\pm 1\sigma$ → **68.26%** of points
> Within $\pm 2\sigma$ → **95.46%** of points
> Within $\pm 3\sigma$ → **99.73%** of points

## 2.2 Control Charts for Variables (Continuous Data)

### 2.2.1 The $\bar{X}$-R Chart (Mean and Range Chart)

The most widely used chart pair in industry. The $\bar{X}$-chart tracks the **process average**; the R-chart tracks the **process variability**. *You must monitor both* — a process can have a perfect average yet wild spread.

**Assumptions:**
- Data are normally distributed (or n ≥ 25 by Central Limit Theorem).
- Samples are independent, in *rational subgroups* (subgroups selected such that within-subgroup variation reflects common-cause only, between-subgroup variation reflects special-cause).

### 2.2.2 The $\bar{X}$-S Chart

Used when subgroup size **n > 10** (because R becomes a poor estimator of σ for large n). The standard deviation S is used instead of R.

### 2.2.3 The I-MR Chart (Individuals and Moving Range)

Used when **n = 1** (slow, expensive, or destructive measurements, e.g., chemical batch pH, daily blood-test readings).

## 2.3 Control Charts for Attributes (Discrete Data)

### 2.3.1 The *p*-Chart (Fraction Nonconforming / Defective)

Monitors the **proportion of defective units** in a sample. Variable sample sizes allowed.

### 2.3.2 The *np*-Chart (Number Nonconforming)

Same as *p*-chart but plots the **count** of defectives. Requires **fixed sample size**.

### 2.3.3 The *c*-Chart (Number of Defects per Unit)

Monitors the **count of defects** (not defective units) in a single unit of constant inspection size (e.g., defects per car, per 100 m of fabric, per page of a book).

### 2.3.4 The *u*-Chart (Defects per Unit)

Same as *c*-chart but allows **variable sample size** (e.g., defects per square meter, where area inspected varies).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU numerical revolves around these formulas.**
> Notation: $n$ = subgroup size, $k$ = number of subgroups, $A_2, A_3, B_3, B_4, D_3, D_4, d_2, d_3, d_4$ are tabulated *control-chart constants* (depend on $n$).

### Master Formula Table — All Control Chart Limits

| Chart Type | Centerline (CL) | UCL | LCL |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\bar{X}$ chart ($n \le 10$) | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ |
| $\bar{X}$ chart ($n > 10$) | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_3 \bar{S}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_3 \bar{S}$ |
| R chart | $\bar{R}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ |
| S chart | $\bar{S}$ | $B_4 \bar{S}$ | $B_3 \bar{S}$ |
| I-chart (Individuals) | $\bar{X}$ | $\bar{X} + 3 \dfrac{\overline{MR}}{d_2}$ | $\bar{X} - 3 \dfrac{\overline{MR}}{d_2}$ |
| MR chart | $\overline{MR}$ | $D_4 \overline{MR}$ | (no LCL) |
| *p*-chart (variable n) | $\bar{p}$ | $\bar{p} + 3 \sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | $\bar{p} - 3 \sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ |
| *np*-chart (fixed n) | $n\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | $n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ |
| *c*-chart (fixed size) | $\bar{c}$ | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ |
| *u*-chart (variable size) | $\bar{u}$ | $\bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | $\bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ |

### Tabulated Constants (KTU-Favorite Values)

| n | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $d_2$ | $A_3$ | $B_3$ | $B_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 | 2.659 | 0 | 3.267 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.574 | 1.693 | 1.954 | 0 | 2.568 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 | 1.628 | 0 | 2.266 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 2.326 | 1.427 | 0 | 2.089 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 | 1.287 | 0.030 | 1.970 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 | 1.182 | 0.118 | 1.882 |
| 10 | 0.308 | 0.223 | 1.777 | 3.078 | 0.975 | 0.284 | 1.716 |

### Quick-Reference Mean Estimators

| Statistic | Formula (Estimator of σ) |
|---|---|
| From Range (small $n$) | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ |
| From Std. Dev. | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{S}}{c_4}$ |
| From Moving Range ($n=2$) | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\overline{MR}}{d_2} = \dfrac{\overline{MR}}{1.128}$ |

### Decision Flow: Which Chart Should I Use?

| If data is… | And sample size is… | Use this chart |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous (variable) | Small, fixed ($n \le 10$) | $\bar{X}$-R chart |
| Continuous (variable) | Large, fixed ($n > 10$) | $\bar{X}$-S chart |
| Continuous (variable) | $n = 1$ | I-MR chart |
| Pass/Fail (defective units) | Variable | *p*-chart |
| Pass/Fail (defective units) | Fixed | *np*-chart |
| Number of defects | Constant inspection unit | *c*-chart |
| Number of defects | Variable inspection unit | *u*-chart |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Automotive (Toyota, Ford):** $\bar{X}$-R charts on piston-ring diameters; *p*-charts on weld defects per 100 cars.
> - **Semiconductor (Intel, TSMC):** I-MR charts on wafer-thickness; *c*-charts on particle defects per die.
> - **Pharmaceutical:** $\bar{X}$-S charts on tablet weight; *u*-charts on contamination per vial.
> - **Software (CMMI Level 5):** *u*-charts on defects per KLOC; *p*-charts on test-case failure rates.
> - **Civil Engineering:** $\bar{X}$-R charts on concrete cube compressive strength; *c*-charts on surface cracks per 100 m of bridge deck.

## 2.6 Pattern Analysis — Western Electric / Nelson Rules

> [!WARNING]
> Even if **no point** lies outside the control limits, the process may still be **out of control** due to *non-random patterns*. KTU examiners love these rule-based questions. Key rules:

1. **One point beyond Zone C** (outside 3σ) → *Outlier rule.*
2. **2 of 3 successive points in Zone A or beyond** (beyond 2σ on same side) → *Shift.*
3. **4 of 5 successive points in Zone B or beyond** (beyond 1σ on same side) → *Trend forming.*
4. **8 successive points on one side of CL** → *Process shift.*
5. **6 successive points trending up or down** → *Drift.*

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why 3-Sigma Control Limits?

The Central Limit Theorem states that for a stable process, the sample mean $\bar{X}$ is normally distributed around the process mean $\mu$ with standard error:

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

By the **Empirical Rule**, for a normal distribution:

$$P(\vert \bar{X} - \mu \vert > 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}) \approx 0.0027$$

i.e., only **0.27% chance** of a false alarm. This balance between detecting real shifts quickly (high power) and minimizing false alarms (low Type-I error) makes 3σ the **economically optimal** choice — proven by Shewhart's economic design of control charts.

**Derivation of UCL and LCL for the $\bar{X}$-chart:**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \mu + 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \mu + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \mu - 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \mu - 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$$

Since $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are unknown, we estimate them using sample data:

$$\hat{\mu} = \bar{\bar{X}} \quad \text{(grand mean of subgroup means)}$$

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} \quad \text{(for small } n \text{)}$$

Substituting:

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \cdot \frac{\bar{R}/d_2}{\sqrt{n}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + \frac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} \cdot \bar{R}$$

By definition of the tabulated constant $A_2 = \dfrac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$, we obtain the working formula:

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \quad LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — $\bar{X}$-R Chart (Full KTU-Style Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A quality engineer takes 5 samples of size $n = 4$ each from a machining process. The data (in mm) and computed subgroup means and ranges are given below. Construct the $\bar{X}$-R chart and comment on process control. (For $n = 4$, $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$.)

| Subgroup $i$ | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $X_3$ | $X_4$ | $\bar{X}_i$ | $R_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12.0 | 12.4 | 12.1 | 11.9 | 12.10 | 0.5 |
| 2 | 11.8 | 12.2 | 12.0 | 12.1 | 12.025 | 0.4 |
| 3 | 12.2 | 12.0 | 11.9 | 12.1 | 12.05 | 0.3 |
| 4 | 12.5 | 12.1 | 12.3 | 12.0 | 12.225 | 0.5 |
| 5 | 12.0 | 12.0 | 12.2 | 11.8 | 12.00 | 0.4 |

### Step 1: Compute the Grand Mean ($\bar{\bar{X}}$)

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k} = \frac{12.10 + 12.025 + 12.05 + 12.225 + 12.00}{5}$$

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{60.40}{5} = 12.08 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Average Range ($\bar{R}$)

$$\bar{R} = \frac{\sum R_i}{k} = \frac{0.5 + 0.4 + 0.3 + 0.5 + 0.4}{5} = \frac{2.1}{5} = 0.42 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3: Compute $\bar{X}$-Chart Limits

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 12.08 + (0.729)(0.42)$$

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 12.08 + 0.30618 = 12.386 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 12.08 - 0.30618 = 11.774 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4: Compute R-Chart Limits

$$UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R} = (2.282)(0.42) = 0.958 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R} = (0)(0.42) = 0 \text{ mm}$$

(Note: $LCL = 0$ because $D_3 = 0$ for $n = 4$ — meaning a range of zero is acceptable.)

### Step 5: Plotting and Interpretation

| Subgroup | $\bar{X}_i$ | Within [11.774, 12.386]? | $R_i$ | Within [0, 0.958]? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12.10 | ✓ | 0.5 | ✓ |
| 2 | 12.025 | ✓ | 0.4 | ✓ |
| 3 | 12.05 | ✓ | 0.3 | ✓ |
| 4 | 12.225 | ✓ | 0.5 | ✓ |
| 5 | 12.00 | ✓ | 0.4 | ✓ |

**Conclusion:** All points are inside control limits, no non-random patterns → **Process is IN CONTROL.** The natural process tolerance is $\bar{\bar{X}} \pm 3\hat{\sigma} = 12.08 \pm 3(0.42/2.059) = 12.08 \pm 0.612 = [11.47, 12.69]$ mm.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — *p*-Chart (Variable Sample Size)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A garment factory inspects 10 lots of varying sizes. Compute the *p*-chart limits for each lot and identify out-of-control lots.

| Lot | $n_i$ (inspected) | $d_i$ (defectives) | $p_i = d_i/n_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 8 | 0.080 |
| 2 | 150 | 14 | 0.093 |
| 3 | 120 | 9 | 0.075 |
| 4 | 200 | 22 | 0.110 |
| 5 | 80 | 6 | 0.075 |
| 6 | 180 | 14 | 0.078 |
| 7 | 250 | 28 | 0.112 |
| 8 | 90 | 7 | 0.078 |
| 9 | 110 | 8 | 0.073 |
| 10 | 160 | 13 | 0.081 |

### Step 1: Compute Centerline $\bar{p}$

$$\bar{p} = \frac{\sum d_i}{\sum n_i} = \frac{8+14+9+22+6+14+28+7+8+13}{100+150+120+200+80+180+250+90+110+160}$$

$$\bar{p} = \frac{129}{1440} = 0.0896$$

### Step 2: Compute Variable Limits for Each Lot

For each lot: $UCL_i = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n_i}}$, $LCL_i = \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n_i}}$

The standard error factor $\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})} = \sqrt{0.0896 \times 0.9104} = \sqrt{0.0816} = 0.2856$

Then $3 \cdot 0.2856 = 0.857$, and the lot limit becomes $UCL_i = 0.0896 + 0.857/\sqrt{n_i}$.

| Lot | $n_i$ | $p_i$ | $LCL_i$ | $UCL_i$ | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 0.080 | $-0.000$ | 0.175 | In control |
| 2 | 150 | 0.093 | 0.020 | 0.160 | In control |
| 3 | 120 | 0.075 | 0.011 | 0.168 | In control |
| 4 | 200 | 0.110 | 0.029 | 0.150 | In control |
| 5 | 80 | 0.075 | $-0.006$ | 0.185 | In control |
| 6 | 180 | 0.078 | 0.024 | 0.155 | In control |
| 7 | 250 | 0.112 | 0.036 | 0.144 | **In control** (close to UCL) |
| 8 | 90 | 0.078 | $-0.001$ | 0.180 | In control |
| 9 | 110 | 0.073 | 0.008 | 0.171 | In control |
| 10 | 160 | 0.081 | 0.018 | 0.161 | In control |

*(Negative LCLs are conventionally set to 0 since proportion cannot be negative.)*

**Conclusion:** All lots are within control limits; the process fraction nonconforming $\approx 8.96\%$ is **stable**, though high. Investigate whether this meets customer quality targets — but process variation itself is in statistical control.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — *c*-Chart (Constant Inspection Unit)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A textile mill records surface defects per 100 m of fabric. Data from 15 rolls: 5, 3, 7, 4, 6, 8, 5, 4, 6, 5, 7, 3, 4, 6, 5. Construct a *c*-chart.

### Step 1: Compute Average Defect Count

$$\bar{c} = \frac{\sum c_i}{k} = \frac{5+3+7+4+6+8+5+4+6+5+7+3+4+6+5}{15}$$

$$\bar{c} = \frac{78}{15} = 5.2 \text{ defects per 100 m}$$

### Step 2: Compute Control Limits

$$UCL_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} = 5.2 + 3\sqrt{5.2} = 5.2 + 3(2.280) = 5.2 + 6.840 = 12.04$$

$$LCL_c = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} = 5.2 - 6.840 = -1.64 \rightarrow 0 \text{ (set to 0)}$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

- Maximum observed count = 8 (Roll 6). 
- UCL = 12.04 → 8 < 12.04, hence **no point outside UCL.**
- LCL = 0, so any count > 0 is acceptable.
- **Process is in statistical control.** Average defect rate is 5.2 per 100 m.

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — *u*-Chart (Variable Inspection Unit)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A hospital records infections per ward-month. 8 wards are inspected for variable days.

| Ward | Patient-days $n_i$ | Infections $c_i$ | $u_i = c_i/n_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 500 | 4 | 0.0080 |
| 2 | 600 | 6 | 0.0100 |
| 3 | 400 | 2 | 0.0050 |
| 4 | 800 | 9 | 0.01125 |
| 5 | 700 | 8 | 0.01143 |
| 6 | 550 | 5 | 0.00909 |
| 7 | 450 | 3 | 0.00667 |
| 8 | 650 | 7 | 0.01077 |

### Step 1: Compute $\bar{u}$

$$\bar{u} = \frac{\sum c_i}{\sum n_i} = \frac{4+6+2+9+8+5+3+7}{500+600+400+800+700+550+450+650} = \frac{44}{4650} = 0.00946$$

### Step 2: Variable Limits per Ward

For each ward: $UCL_i = \bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\bar{u}/n_i}$, $LCL_i = \max(0, \bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\bar{u}/n_i})$

| Ward | $n_i$ | $u_i$ | $\sqrt{\bar{u}/n_i}$ | $LCL_i$ | $UCL_i$ | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 500 | 0.0080 | 0.00435 | 0 | 0.0226 | OK |
| 2 | 600 | 0.0100 | 0.00397 | 0 | 0.0214 | OK |
| 3 | 400 | 0.0050 | 0.00486 | 0 | 0.0240 | OK |
| 4 | 800 | 0.01125 | 0.00344 | 0 | 0.0198 | OK |
| 5 | 700 | 0.01143 | 0.00368 | 0 | 0.0205 | OK |
| 6 | 550 | 0.00909 | 0.00415 | 0 | 0.0219 | OK |
| 7 | 450 | 0.00667 | 0.00459 | 0 | 0.0232 | OK |
| 8 | 650 | 0.01077 | 0.00381 | 0 | 0.0209 | OK |

**Conclusion:** All wards are in control. Hospital infection rate is stable at ~0.95 infections per 100 patient-days.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Chart in Exam

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Decision Tree (Memorize!):**
> - Is data measured on a continuous scale? → **Variables chart**
>   - $n = 1$ → **I-MR**
>   - $n \le 10$ → **$\bar{X}$-R**
>   - $n > 10$ → **$\bar{X}$-S**
> - Is data a pass/fail count?
>   - Sample size fixed → ***np***
>   - Sample size variable → ***p***
> - Is data a defect count?
>   - Inspection unit constant → ***c***
>   - Inspection unit variable → ***u***

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Process of Constructing a Control Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Quality Characteristic] --> B{Data Type?}
    B -- Continuous --> C[Collect Rational Subgroups]
    B -- Discrete / Count --> D[Collect Sample Counts]

    C --> E{Subgroup Size n?}
    E -- n equals 1 --> F[I-MR Chart]
    E -- n <= 10 --> G[X-bar R Chart]
    E -- n greater than 10 --> H[X-bar S Chart]

    D --> I{Sample Size?}
    I -- Fixed n --> J{np Chart}
    I -- Variable n --> K[p Chart]

    D --> L{Inspection Unit?}
    L -- Constant --> M[c Chart]
    L -- Variable --> N[u Chart]

    G --> O[Compute CL, UCL, LCL using A2 D3 D4 constants]
    H --> P[Compute CL, UCL, LCL using A3 B3 B4 constants]
    F --> Q[Compute CL, UCL, LCL using d2 D4 constants]
    J --> R[UCL = np-bar plus 3 sqrt np-bar]
    K --> S[UCL = p-bar plus 3 sqrt p-bar 1-p-bar over n]
    M --> T[UCL = c-bar plus 3 sqrt c-bar]
    N --> U[UCL = u-bar plus 3 sqrt u-bar over n]

    O --> V[Plot Points in Time Order]
    P --> V
    Q --> V
    R --> V
    S --> V
    T --> V
    U --> V

    V --> W{All Points Within Limits and Random?}
    W -- Yes --> X[Process In Control]
    W -- No --> Y[Investigate Assignable Cause]
    Y --> Z[Remove Special Cause]
    Z --> X
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between control charts for variables and control charts for attributes. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
Control charts for variables are used when the quality characteristic is measurable on a continuous scale (e.g., diameter in mm, weight in grams). They monitor both the process mean and variability, using charts like the **$\bar{X}$-R chart** and **I-MR chart**. Control charts for attributes are used when the quality characteristic is countable — either conforming/nonconforming (defective) or defect counts — using charts like the **p-chart, np-chart, c-chart, and u-chart**.

| Aspect | Variables | Attributes |
|---|---|---|
| Data type | Continuous | Discrete (count) |
| Example | Diameter of a shaft | Number of defective bulbs in a lot |
| Charts | $\bar{X}$-R, $\bar{X}$-S, I-MR | p, np, c, u |
| Sample size | Usually small (2–10) | Usually large (≥ 50) |

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the formula for UCL and LCL of an R-chart. What is the role of constants D3 and D4?**

**Model Answer:**
For an R-chart with subgroup size $n$:
$$UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}, \qquad LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$$
where $\bar{R}$ is the average of subgroup ranges and $D_3$, $D_4$ are tabulated control-chart constants that depend on $n$.
The constant **$D_4$** scales the upper 3-sigma limit of the range distribution, while **$D_3$** scales the lower limit. They convert the standard deviation of the range into the actual control limits. If $LCL_R$ becomes negative, it is conventionally set to zero since a range cannot be negative.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and interpretation of the **$\bar{X}$-R chart** with neat formulas. Mention the role of the constant $A_2$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The following data represent the diameters (in mm) of 6 samples, each of size 4, taken from a manufacturing process:

| Sample | Obs 1 | Obs 2 | Obs 3 | Obs 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10.2 | 10.4 | 10.1 | 10.3 |
| 2 | 10.0 | 10.2 | 10.3 | 10.1 |
| 3 | 10.4 | 10.5 | 10.3 | 10.4 |
| 4 | 9.9 | 10.1 | 10.0 | 10.2 |
| 5 | 10.3 | 10.2 | 10.4 | 10.1 |
| 6 | 10.5 | 10.4 | 10.3 | 10.2 |

Construct the $\bar{X}$-chart and R-chart. Given for $n = 4$: $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$. Comment on the state of control.

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Construction of $\bar{X}$-R chart:**

The $\bar{X}$-R chart is a pair of control charts used to simultaneously monitor the **process average** ($\bar{X}$-chart) and **process variability** (R-chart) of a variable quality characteristic.

**Step 1 — Collect Rational Subgroups:** Take $k$ samples (typically $k \ge 20$ for a pilot study) of size $n$ (typically $n = 2$ to $10$) from the process at regular intervals.

**Step 2 — Compute Subgroup Statistics:** For each subgroup $i$:
$$\bar{X}_i = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} X_{ij}}{n}, \qquad R_i = X_{i,\max} - X_{i,\min}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Grand Statistics:**
$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i, \qquad \bar{R} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i$$

**Step 4 — Compute Control Limits:**
$$\bar{X}\text{-chart: } UCL = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \quad CL = \bar{\bar{X}}, \quad LCL = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$
$$R\text{-chart: } UCL = D_4 \bar{R}, \quad CL = \bar{R}, \quad LCL = D_3 \bar{R}$$

**Role of $A_2$:** The constant $A_2 = \dfrac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$ converts the average range $\bar{R}$ into the standard error of the mean. It encapsulates the 3-sigma logic for the $\bar{X}$-chart while being tabled for convenience. For $n = 4$, $A_2 = 0.729$.

**Interpretation:** If all points lie within limits with no non-random pattern, the process is **in statistical control**. Any point outside, or a non-random pattern (trend, run, cycle), signals an **assignable cause** requiring investigation.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute subgroup means and ranges:**

| Sample | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $X_3$ | $X_4$ | $\bar{X}_i$ | $R_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10.2 | 10.4 | 10.1 | 10.3 | 10.25 | 0.3 |
| 2 | 10.0 | 10.2 | 10.3 | 10.1 | 10.15 | 0.3 |
| 3 | 10.4 | 10.5 | 10.3 | 10.4 | 10.40 | 0.2 |
| 4 | 9.9 | 10.1 | 10.0 | 10.2 | 10.05 | 0.3 |
| 5 | 10.3 | 10.2 | 10.4 | 10.1 | 10.25 | 0.3 |
| 6 | 10.5 | 10.4 | 10.3 | 10.2 | 10.35 | 0.3 |

**Step 2: Grand Statistics**
$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{10.25+10.15+10.40+10.05+10.25+10.35}{6} = \frac{61.45}{6} = 10.2417 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{0.3+0.3+0.2+0.3+0.3+0.3}{6} = \frac{1.7}{6} = 0.2833 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: $\bar{X}$-Chart Limits**
$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 10.2417 + (0.729)(0.2833) = 10.2417 + 0.2065 = 10.448 \text{ mm}$$
$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 10.2417 - 0.2065 = 10.035 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: R-Chart Limits**
$$UCL_R = (2.282)(0.2833) = 0.647 \text{ mm}, \qquad LCL_R = 0$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**

| Sample | $\bar{X}_i$ | In [10.035, 10.448]? | $R_i$ | In [0, 0.647]? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10.25 | ✓ | 0.3 | ✓ |
| 2 | 10.15 | ✓ | 0.3 | ✓ |
| 3 | 10.40 | ✓ | 0.2 | ✓ |
| 4 | 10.05 | ✓ | 0.3 | ✓ |
| 5 | 10.25 | ✓ | 0.3 | ✓ |
| 6 | 10.35 | ✓ | 0.3 | ✓ |

All sample means and ranges lie within the control limits. **Conclusion:** The process is **in statistical control** with mean $\approx 10.24$ mm and process standard deviation $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2 = 0.2833/2.059 = 0.1376$ mm.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction of a **p-chart** and a **c-chart**. Under what conditions is each used?

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a manufacturing unit, 15 samples of size 100 each are inspected and the number of defectives observed are: 8, 12, 9, 11, 7, 10, 14, 6, 13, 9, 10, 12, 8, 11, 9. Construct a p-chart and state whether the process is in control.

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) p-chart vs c-chart:**

**p-chart (Fraction Nonconforming Chart):**
- **Use when:** Each item is classified as either *defective* or *non-defective* (binary outcome).
- **Sample size:** May be **variable** (e.g., lots of different sizes inspected each hour).
- **Statistic plotted:** Proportion of defective items in sample, $p_i = d_i / n_i$.
- **Underlying distribution:** Binomial with parameters $n$ and $p$.
- **Formulas:**
$$\bar{p} = \frac{\sum d_i}{\sum n_i}, \quad UCL_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}, \quad LCL_p = \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$$
- If sample sizes vary, control limits are recomputed for each sample.

**c-chart (Number of Defects Chart):**
- **Use when:** Each inspected unit may have **multiple defects**, and the total count of defects is recorded.
- **Inspection unit:** **Constant** in size (e.g., per 100 m of fabric, per car, per page).
- **Statistic plotted:** Number of defects $c$ per unit.
- **Underlying distribution:** Poisson with parameter $c$ (mean = variance).
- **Formulas:**
$$\bar{c} = \frac{\sum c_i}{k}, \quad UCL_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}, \quad LCL_c = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$$
- If $LCL_c < 0$, it is set to 0.

| Aspect | p-chart | c-chart |
|---|---|---|
| Counts | Defective *units* | Defects *within* a unit |
| Sample size | Can vary | Must be constant |
| Distribution | Binomial | Poisson |

**(b) Numerical Solution for p-chart:**

**Step 1: Compute $\bar{p}$**

Total defectives $\sum d_i = 8+12+9+11+7+10+14+6+13+9+10+12+8+11+9 = 149$

Total inspected $\sum n_i = 15 \times 100 = 1500$

$$\bar{p} = \frac{149}{1500} = 0.0993$$

**Step 2: Compute Standard Error**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0993 \times 0.9007}{100}} = \sqrt{0.000895} = 0.0299$$

**Step 3: Control Limits (constant, since $n = 100$ for all samples)**

$$UCL_p = 0.0993 + 3(0.0299) = 0.0993 + 0.0897 = 0.1890$$

$$LCL_p = 0.0993 - 0.0897 = 0.0096$$

**Step 4: Compute $p_i$ for Each Sample and Compare**

| Sample | $d_i$ | $p_i$ | In [0.0096, 0.189]? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8 | 0.08 | ✓ |
| 2 | 12 | 0.12 | ✓ |
| 3 | 9 | 0.09 | ✓ |
| 4 | 11 | 0.11 | ✓ |
| 5 | 7 | 0.07 | ✓ |
| 6 | 10 | 0.10 | ✓ |
| 7 | 14 | 0.14 | ✓ |
| 8 | 6 | 0.06 | ✓ |
| 9 | 13 | 0.13 | ✓ |
| 10 | 9 | 0.09 | ✓ |
| 11 | 10 | 0.10 | ✓ |
| 12 | 12 | 0.12 | ✓ |
| 13 | 8 | 0.08 | ✓ |
| 14 | 11 | 0.11 | ✓ |
| 15 | 9 | 0.09 | ✓ |

**Step 5: Interpretation**

All sample proportions fall between $LCL = 0.0096$ and $UCL = 0.1890$. No non-random pattern is evident. **Conclusion:** The process is **in statistical control**, with a stable fraction nonconforming of approximately **9.93%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing defective with defect.** A *defective* unit has at least one defect. The p-chart counts defective units; the c-chart counts defects. Mixing them up = -3 to -5 marks.
> 2. **Wrong constant usage:** Using $A_2$ with $S$ instead of $R$, or forgetting to use $D_3 = 0$ properly. Always verify the constants table for the given $n$.
> 3. **Forgetting to set negative LCL to zero.** Proportions, ranges, and counts cannot be negative.
> 4. **Not plotting both $\bar{X}$ and R charts** — students plot only one and lose 4–5 marks. The R-chart is *equally important* as the $\bar{X}$-chart.
> 5. **Treating specification limits (USL/LSL) as control limits (UCL/LCL).** They are completely different — derived from customer vs. process data.
> 6. **In p-chart with variable sample sizes**, computing one set of limits instead of per-sample limits = -3 marks.
> 7. **Misinterpreting "in control" as "capable."** A process can be in statistical control yet produce defective output (e.g., the 9.93% defective rate above may exceed customer's acceptable quality level — that is a *capability* issue, not a control issue).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Control Charts for Variables and Attributes**
>
> **Variables Charts:**
> - ☐ $\bar{X}$-R chart: most common, for $n \le 10$. Uses $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$.
> - ☐ $\bar{X}$-S chart: for $n > 10$. Uses $A_3$, $B_3$, $B_4$.
> - ☐ I-MR chart: for $n = 1$. Uses $d_2 = 1.128$ and $D_4 = 3.267$.
> - ☐ $A_2 = 3/(d_2\sqrt{n})$; always check the constant table for given $n$.
> - ☐ Both $\bar{X}$ and R charts must be plotted — process must be in control on **both**.
> - ☐ R-chart detects *change in variability*; $\bar{X}$-chart detects *change in average*.
>
> **Attributes Charts:**
> - ☐ p-chart: variable sample size, defective *units*, binomial distribution.
> - ☐ np-chart: fixed sample size, defective *units*, binomial distribution.
> - ☐ c-chart: constant inspection unit, defect *count*, Poisson distribution.
> - ☐ u-chart: variable inspection unit, defect *count*, Poisson distribution.
> - ☐ p ↔ np relation: $np = np$-bar (so $\bar{p} = \bar{np}/n$).
> - ☐ c ↔ u relation: $c = un$ (so $\bar{u} = \bar{c}/n$).
> - ☐ Negative LCLs are conventionally set to zero.
>
> **General Principles:**
> - ☐ Control limits use 3-sigma logic; 0.27% chance of false alarm.
> - ☐ **Control limits ≠ Specification limits.** Control limits are from process data; specification limits are from customer.
> - ☐ "In control" ≠ "capable." In control = stable; capable = meets specifications.
> - ☐ Pattern analysis matters: Western Electric / Nelson rules detect non-random shifts even within limits.
> - ☐ Subgroup selection (rational subgrouping) is critical — minimize within-subgroup variation, maximize between-subgroup variation.
> - ☐ First establish control limits with pilot data (~25 subgroups), then monitor continuously.
> - ☐ Process capability indices (Cp, Cpk) are computed *only after* the process is in statistical control.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Capability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process Capability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):**
> Process Capability is a foundational statistical concept under Statistical Process Control (SPC) that quantifies the inherent ability of a stable process to produce output within customer-specified tolerance limits.

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Capability (Pc)** is defined as the **statistical measure of the inherent variability of a process output (within its natural control limits) compared to the customer-specified tolerance (specification) limits.**

In rigorous statistical terms, Process Capability is the **ratio of the tolerance specified by the design engineer (the "voice of the customer") to the natural spread of the process (the "voice of the process")**, evaluated when the process is operating under a state of **statistical control**.

**Key Metrics (Bolded Standard Indicators):**
- **Cp** – Process Capability Index (potential capability, centered)
- **Cpk** – Process Capability Index (actual capability, accounts for shift)
- **Pp** – Process Performance Index (overall performance, not centered)
- **Ppk** – Process Performance Index (overall performance, accounts for shift)
- **σ (Sigma)** – Standard deviation of the process
- **μ (Mu)** – Process mean

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy ☕

Imagine you are running a **bottled water plant** filling **1-litre bottles (1000 mL)**.

- **Customer Specification (Voice of Customer):** Each bottle must contain between **990 mL (LSL)** and **1010 mL (USL)**. This is a tolerance of ±10 mL.
- **Process Voice (Voice of Process):** Due to variations in valve opening time, water pressure, and temperature, your machine naturally fills bottles with amounts that follow a bell-shaped curve. If the standard deviation of your filling process is **σ = 2 mL**, the natural spread of your process is **±3σ = ±6 mL** (i.e., from 994 mL to 1006 mL when centered at 1000 mL).

**The Question:** *Can your filling process reliably meet the customer's specifications?*

- If the natural spread (6 mL on each side) is **much smaller** than the specification width (10 mL on each side) → **The process is highly capable.**
- If the natural spread is **larger** than the specification width → **The process produces defects.**

> [!TIP]
> **Think of Cp as a "Headroom" Indicator:** It compares how much "room" the customer has given you (the specification width) against how much "wiggle room" your process naturally takes (6σ). A high Cp means your process is precise and consistent relative to what's demanded.

---

## 1.3 Why Process Capability Matters in TQM

| TQM Pillar | Role of Process Capability |
|---|---|
| **Customer Focus** | Translates customer needs (specs) into measurable process performance |
| **Continuous Improvement** | Quantifies "before vs. after" of Kaizen / Six Sigma projects |
| **Decision Making** | Accept / reject machinery, suppliers, or contracts based on Pc indices |
| **Variation Reduction** | Provides numerical target (e.g., achieve Cpk ≥ 1.33) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 The Three Critical Limits in Process Capability

A KTU student must internalize that **three distinct "limits"** govern every capable-process problem:

1. **Lower Specification Limit (LSL):** The minimum acceptable value defined by the customer/design.
2. **Upper Specification Limit (USL):** The maximum acceptable value defined by the customer/design.
3. **Natural Process Limits (NPL):** The inherent spread of the process, calculated as **μ ± 3σ** for a stable, normally distributed process (this captures **99.73%** of all output).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Process Capability:**
> For a process to be considered **capable**, the **Natural Process Limits (μ ± 3σ)** must lie **entirely within** the **Specification Limits (LSL, USL)**. If any portion of the natural curve falls outside the specs, defects are produced.

---

## 2.2 Process Variability vs. Specifications

| Aspect | Voice of the Process | Voice of the Customer |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Machine, method, material, manpower, environment | Design engineer / end-user requirements |
| **Quantified by** | μ ± 3σ (Natural Tolerance) | LSL to USL (Specification Tolerance) |
| **Measured by** | Control charts (X-bar, R-chart) | Engineering drawings, contracts |
| **Goal** | Minimize σ (reduce variability) | Maintain or expand tolerance width |

---

## 2.3 The Logic Behind Cp (Why and How)

**The "Why":** A pure comparison of specification width to process spread is insufficient if the process mean drifts off-center. **Cp** answers the question: *"If this process were perfectly centered, how much room would it have?"*

**The "How":** Cp is computed by dividing the total specification width by the total natural process spread (6σ, which encompasses 99.73% of output).

**Interpretation Logic:**
- **Cp < 1.0** → Process spread > Specification width → Process is **not capable**, regardless of centering.
- **Cp = 1.0** → Process spread equals specification width → Process is at the **edge of capability**.
- **Cp = 1.33** → Industry-accepted minimum for an **existing capable process**.
- **Cp = 1.50** → Industry standard for **new processes** (safety margin).
- **Cp = 2.00** → **Six-Sigma world-class** capability (zero shift, ±6σ inside specs, actually ±12σ between spec limits).

---

## 2.4 The Logic Behind Cpk (Why and How)

**The "Why":** Cp assumes the process is perfectly centered at the midpoint of the specification. In reality, processes drift. **Cpk** accounts for this by measuring the distance from the process mean to the *nearest* specification limit, divided by half the natural spread (3σ).

**The "How":** Two one-sided capability indices are computed, and the **smaller (worst-case) value is reported as Cpk.**

**Interpretation Logic:**
- **Cpk > Cp** → Impossible mathematically (only when Cp is undefined for one-sided specs).
- **Cpk = Cp** → Process is **perfectly centered**.
- **Cpk < Cp** → Process is **off-center**, with the difference indicating the magnitude of shift.
- **Cpk < 1.0** → Process produces defects even if technically "in control."
- **Cpk ≥ 1.33** → Process is **capable and centered**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorize every row. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` instead of `|` to preserve table syntax.

| # | Index | Formula | What It Measures | Key Assumption |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cp (Potential Capability)** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Ratio of spec width to natural spread | Process is stable & centered |
| 2 | **Cpu (Upper Capability)** | $C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$ | Distance from mean to USL, in σ units | One-sided view (upper limit) |
| 3 | **Cpl (Lower Capability)** | $C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$ | Distance from mean to LSL, in σ units | One-sided view (lower limit) |
| 4 | **Cpk (Actual Capability)** | $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$ | Worst-case one-sided capability | Accounts for off-centering |
| 5 | **Pp (Performance Index)** | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s}$ | Uses sample stdev s instead of σ | Long-term performance |
| 6 | **Ppk (Performance Index, shifted)** | $P_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3s}, \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3s}\right)$ | Long-term performance with shift | Sample-based |
| 7 | **Sigma Level (Z-score)** | $Z = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{\sigma}\right)$ | Distance to nearest spec in σ | Direct yield calculator |
| 8 | **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{N \times O}$ | Defect density at Six-Sigma scale | D=defects, N=units, O=opportunities |
| 9 | **Process Yield** | $Yield = \Phi(Z_{upper}) - \Phi(Z_{lower})$ | Proportion of in-spec units | Uses standard normal CDF |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic:**
> - **C** stands for **Capability** (using $\sigma$, the **short-term** within-subgroup standard deviation).
> - **P** stands for **Performance** (using $s$, the **long-term** sample standard deviation).
> - The "k" suffix means **"kept"** (accounts for the shift / k = correction factor).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Process Capability is the **universal language** of quality across engineering domains:

- **Manufacturing (Mechanical):** Machining tolerance verification for shafts, bearings, and engine components.
- **Electronics (VLSI):** Verifying that chip-fabrication processes meet nanometer-level tolerances.
- **Civil Engineering:** Concrete compressive strength testing against design mix requirements.
- **Software Engineering:** Defect density (DPMO) tracking in code releases under CMMI Level 5.
- **Chemical Engineering:** pH, viscosity, and purity control in batch processes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation of Cp

**Starting Premise:**
For a stable, normally distributed process with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, the natural process limits are defined as:

$$
\text{Natural Process Limits} = \mu \pm 3\sigma
$$

This captures **99.73%** of all process output (per the Empirical Rule).

**Total Natural Spread** = Upper NPL − Lower NPL:

$$
\text{Total Natural Spread} = (\mu + 3\sigma) - (\mu - 3\sigma) = 6\sigma
$$

**Total Specification Width** = USL − LSL.

**Cp is defined as the ratio of specification width to natural spread:**

$$
C_p = \dfrac{\text{Specification Width}}{\text{Natural Spread}}
$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$
C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

**Interpretation:** If $C_p = 1.0$, the natural process spread exactly equals the specification width. A $C_p > 1.0$ means the process "fits inside" the specification with room to spare.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Cpk

**Starting Premise:**
$C_p$ assumes the process mean $\mu$ is at the midpoint of the specification, i.e., $\mu = \dfrac{USL + LSL}{2}$.

When the process drifts, the distance from $\mu$ to **each** specification limit becomes asymmetric.

**Distance from μ to the Upper Specification Limit (USL):**

$$
d_{upper} = USL - \mu
$$

**Distance from μ to the Lower Specification Limit (LSL):**

$$
d_{lower} = \mu - LSL
$$

These distances are then normalized by **half the natural spread** (3σ) to obtain one-sided capability indices:

$$
C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} \quad \text{and} \quad C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}
$$

**The actual process capability Cpk is the minimum of the two:**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})
$$

**Why Minimum?** The nearest specification limit is the bottleneck. Even if the mean is far from USL (large $C_{pu}$), if it is close to LSL (small $C_{pl}$), defects will be produced on the lower side. Cpk reflects this worst-case scenario.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example Problem:**
> A shaft manufacturing process has the following parameters:
> - Specification: LSL = 24.90 mm, USL = 25.10 mm
> - Process mean: μ = 25.04 mm
> - Process standard deviation: σ = 0.02 mm
>
> **Calculate Cp, Cpu, Cpl, and Cpk. Comment on the capability.**

**Step 1 — Identify Given Values:**

$$
LSL = 24.90 \text{ mm}, \quad USL = 25.10 \text{ mm}, \quad \mu = 25.04 \text{ mm}, \quad \sigma = 0.02 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Specification Width and Natural Spread:**

$$
\text{Spec Width} = USL - LSL = 25.10 - 24.90 = 0.20 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Natural Spread} = 6\sigma = 6 \times 0.02 = 0.12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cp:**

$$
C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{0.20}{0.12} = 1.667
$$

Since $C_p = 1.667 > 1.33$, the process is **potentially capable**.

**Step 4 — Compute Cpu and Cpl:**

$$
C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.10 - 25.04}{3 \times 0.02} = \dfrac{0.06}{0.06} = 1.00
$$

$$
C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.04 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.02} = \dfrac{0.14}{0.06} = 2.333
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Cpk:**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl}) = \min(1.00, 2.333) = 1.00
$$

**Step 6 — Comment on Capability:**

Since $C_{pk} = 1.00 < 1.33$, the process is **NOT actually capable** despite $C_p = 1.667$, because the process mean is shifted toward the upper specification limit. The bottleneck is the upper side. **Recommendation:** Re-center the process mean toward the midpoint (25.00 mm) to improve Cpk.

---

## 3.4 Six-Sigma Yield and DPMO Calculation

**Setup:**
A process has $C_{pk} = 2.0$. Calculate the expected DPMO.

**Step 1 — Convert Cpk to Z-score (distance to nearest spec in σ units):**

For a centered process, $Z_{bench} = 3 \times C_{pk}$. For an uncentered process, use the actual Z from Section 2.5.

$$
Z = 3 \times C_{pk} = 3 \times 2.0 = 6.0
$$

**Step 2 — Find Area Beyond Z = 6.0 Using Standard Normal Table:**

$$
P(Z > 6.0) \approx 9.87 \times 10^{-10} \approx 1 \times 10^{-9}
$$

For two-sided tolerance with $Z = 6$ on each side:

$$
\text{Tail Probability (both sides)} = 2 \times 10^{-9}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute DPMO:**

$$
DPMO = \text{Tail Probability} \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 2 \times 10^{-9} \times 10^{6} = 0.002 \text{ DPMO}
$$

**Interpretation:** A Six-Sigma process produces, on average, **0.002 defects per million opportunities** — essentially "zero defects" in practical terms.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix for KTU Problems

| Condition | Decision |
|---|---|
| $C_p \geq 1.33$ AND $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ | Process is **capable and centered** — Maintain |
| $C_p \geq 1.33$ BUT $C_{pk} < 1.33$ | Process is **off-center** — Re-center the mean |
| $C_p < 1.00$ regardless of $C_{pk}$ | Process is **fundamentally incapable** — Reduce σ |
| $C_p \approx C_{pk}$ | Process is **well-centered** |
| $C_p - C_{pk} > 0.50$ | Process has **significant shift** — Investigate assignable causes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Process Capability Evaluation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Collect Process Data] --> nodeB[Compute Mean μ and Stdev σ]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Is Process in Statistical Control?}
    nodeC -- No --> nodeD[Use Control Charts to Identify and Remove Special Causes]
    nodeD --> nodeB
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeE[Identify LSL and USL from Specs]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Calculate Cp = Spec Width divided by 6 sigma]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Cp >= 1.33?}
    nodeG -- No --> nodeH[Process Incapable: Reduce Variability sigma]
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeI[Calculate Cpu and Cpl]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Take Minimum as Cpk]
    nodeJ --> nodeK{Cpk >= 1.33?}
    nodeK -- No --> nodeL[Process Off-Center: Re-center Mean]
    nodeK -- Yes --> nodeM[Process Capable and Centered: Monitor and Maintain]
    nodeM --> nodeN[End: Report Capability to Management]
```

---

## 4.2 Cp vs Cpk — Conceptual Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph scenarioA[Scenario 1: Centered Process]
        a1[LSL] --> a2[Process Mean μ at Center]
        a2 --> a3[USL]
        a2 -.-> a4[Cp equals Cpk: Both equal 1.5]
    end

    subgraph scenarioB[Scenario 2: Off-Center Process]
        b1[LSL] --> b2[Process Mean μ Shifted Right]
        b2 --> b3[USL]
        b2 -.-> b4[Cp equals 1.5 but Cpk equals 1.0]
    end

    subgraph scenarioC[Scenario 3: High Variability]
        c1[LSL] --> c2[Process Mean μ]
        c2 --> c3[USL]
        c2 -.-> c4[Natural Spread Exceeds Specs: Cp less than 1.0]
    end

    scenarioA --> scenarioB
    scenarioB --> scenarioC
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Six Sigma Capability Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input1[Customer Specification: USL and LSL] --> block1[Define Tolerance Window]
    block1 --> block2[Measure Process: μ and σ]

    subgraph computeBlock[Capability Computation Engine]
        block2 --> block3[Compute Cp Block]
        block2 --> block4[Compute Cpu and Cpl Blocks]
        block3 --> block5[Combine into Cpk Block]
        block4 --> block5
    end

    block5 --> output1[Capability Report: Cp, Cpk]
    block5 --> output2[Yield and DPMO Metrics]
    block5 --> output3[Improvement Actions: Re-center or Reduce σ]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Six-Sigma Distribution Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph specBlock[Specification Window]
        LSL_marker[LSL Boundary] --> USL_marker[USL Boundary]
    end

    subgraph processBlock[Natural Process Distribution ±6 Sigma]
        mu_minus_6[μ - 6 sigma] --> mu_minus_3[μ - 3 sigma: LSL Region]
        mu_minus_3 --> mu_center[μ: Process Mean]
        mu_center --> mu_plus_3[μ + 3 sigma: USL Region]
        mu_plus_3 --> mu_plus_6[μ + 6 sigma]
    end

    specBlock --> processBlock
    processBlock --> resultBlock[Six Sigma: Zero Defects Boundary]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagram:** In a true Six-Sigma process, the **entire ±6σ spread of the process fits within the specification limits**, and even with a **1.5σ drift**, defects remain virtually zero. This is the architectural target of Motorola's original Six-Sigma program (1986).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are designed for the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) Part A. Answers should be precise, definition-focused, and limited to 3–4 lines.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**Q: Define Process Capability Index (Cp) and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Process Capability Index (Cp) is defined as the ratio of the specification width (USL − LSL) to the natural process spread (6σ).
> Mathematically: $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$.
> It measures the **potential capability** of a stable process assuming it is perfectly centered.
> A Cp value of **1.33** is the industry-accepted minimum for an existing process; **1.50** is the recommended minimum for new processes, and **2.00** corresponds to Six-Sigma world-class capability.
> Higher Cp indicates greater headroom between the natural process spread and the customer specifications, leading to fewer defects.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: Differentiate between Cp and Cpk with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> | Aspect | Cp | Cpk |
> |---|---|---|
> | Definition | Compares spec width to 6σ | Compares nearest spec distance to 3σ |
> | Centering Assumption | Assumes process is centered | Does not assume centering |
> | Formula | $C_p = \dfrac{USL-LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ |
> | Example | Cp = 1.5 indicates potential capability | Cpk = 1.0 indicates the bottleneck side is at risk |
>
> When the process is centered, **Cp = Cpk**; when off-center, **Cpk < Cp**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 3 questions appear as 14-mark questions with internal choice. Each question below carries 7 + 7 = 14 marks across two sub-parts.

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**Q: (a) A CNC turning process produces shafts with specification LSL = 24.90 mm and USL = 25.10 mm. After collecting 25 subgroups of 5 measurements each, the average sample mean is $\bar{x}$ = 25.03 mm and the average within-subgroup standard deviation is $\bar{R}/d_2$ = 0.025 mm. Calculate Cp, Cpk, and comment on the process capability. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$$
LSL = 24.90 \text{ mm}, \quad USL = 25.10 \text{ mm}, \quad \mu = \bar{x} = 25.03 \text{ mm}, \quad \sigma = 0.025 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 1 — Specification Width:**
$$
USL - LSL = 25.10 - 24.90 = 0.20 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Natural Spread:**
$$
6\sigma = 6 \times 0.025 = 0.15 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cp:**
$$
C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{0.20}{0.15} = 1.333
$$

Since $C_p = 1.33$, the process meets the **minimum capability threshold**.

**Step 4 — Compute Cpu and Cpl:**
$$
C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.10 - 25.03}{3 \times 0.025} = \dfrac{0.07}{0.075} = 0.933
$$

$$
C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.03 - 24.90}{0.075} = \dfrac{0.13}{0.075} = 1.733
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Cpk:**
$$
C_{pk} = \min(0.933, 1.733) = 0.933
$$

**Step 6 — Comment:**
Since $C_{pk} = 0.933 < 1.00$, the process is **NOT actually capable**. Although $C_p = 1.33$ indicates potential capability, the mean has shifted toward the USL, causing the bottleneck on the upper side. **Recommendation:** Re-center the process at $\mu_{target} = 25.00$ mm and re-evaluate.

---

**(b) Explain the Six-Sigma concept of Process Capability. How does a 1.5σ shift affect the yield calculation? Show the derivation of DPMO for a process with $C_{pk}$ = 2.0. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Six-Sigma Concept:**
Six-Sigma is a quality philosophy pioneered by Motorola in 1986. It defines a process as "world-class" when the natural process spread (6σ) fits comfortably inside the specification limits, leaving room for a **1.5σ drift** in the mean without producing defects. For a centered process, this corresponds to $C_p = 2.0$ (since $Z = 6$ corresponds to $C_p = 2.0$).

**Step 2 — Effect of 1.5σ Shift:**
Without shift: Mean is at center, Z = 6 on each side.
With 1.5σ shift: Effective distance reduces to $Z_{eff} = 6 - 1.5 = 4.5$ on the nearest side.

**Step 3 — Calculate DPMO with $C_{pk}$ = 2.0 (Centered):**

For a centered process with $C_p = 2.0$:
$$
Z = 3 \times C_p = 6
$$

Tail probability at $Z = 6$:
$$
P(Z > 6) \approx 1 \times 10^{-9}
$$

For two-sided specification:
$$
\text{Defect Probability} = 2 \times 10^{-9}
$$

DPMO:
$$
DPMO = 2 \times 10^{-9} \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 0.002 \text{ DPMO}
$$

**Step 4 — DPMO with 1.5σ Shift:**

Effective $Z = 4.5$.
$$
P(Z > 4.5) \approx 3.4 \times 10^{-6}
$$

For one side (one-tailed): 3.4 ppm. For two-sided: 6.8 ppm ≈ **3.4 DPMO**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
Even with a 1.5σ shift, a Six-Sigma process produces only **3.4 DPMO**, which corresponds to the practical industry standard.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q: (a) Define Cpk. A pharmaceutical tablet manufacturing process has LSL = 95 mg and USL = 105 mg of active ingredient. After 30 batches, the mean is found to be 98 mg with σ = 1.5 mg. Calculate Cp, Cpk, and determine if the process is capable. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$$
LSL = 95 \text{ mg}, \quad USL = 105 \text{ mg}, \quad \mu = 98 \text{ mg}, \quad \sigma = 1.5 \text{ mg}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute Cp:**
$$
C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{105 - 95}{6 \times 1.5} = \dfrac{10}{9} = 1.111
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cpu and Cpl:**
$$
C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{105 - 98}{3 \times 1.5} = \dfrac{7}{4.5} = 1.556
$$

$$
C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{98 - 95}{4.5} = \dfrac{3}{4.5} = 0.667
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cpk:**
$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.556, 0.667) = 0.667
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
Since $C_p = 1.111 < 1.33$ and $C_{pk} = 0.667 < 1.00$, the process is **NOT capable**. The natural spread is too large relative to the specifications, and the mean is shifted toward the LSL. **Recommendation:** Reduce σ by improving process equipment (e.g., better dosing pump precision) AND re-center the mean.

---

**(b) Explain the relationship between Cp and Cpk. Using a graphical representation in Mermaid, show how a process appears when (i) Cp is high but Cpk is low, and (ii) both are high. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Conceptual Relationship:**

- **Cp** measures *potential* capability assuming perfect centering.
- **Cpk** measures *actual* capability with the current mean position.
- The relationship $C_p \geq C_{pk}$ always holds when both are defined.
- When the process is perfectly centered, $C_p = C_{pk}$.
- When the process drifts, $C_{pk} < C_p$, and the difference quantifies the shift.

**Mathematical Statement:**
$$
\Delta = C_p - C_{pk} = \dfrac{\vert\mu - M\vert}{\sigma}
$$
where $M = \dfrac{USL + LSL}{2}$ is the specification midpoint.

**Step 2 — Graphical Representation (Mermaid Block Diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph caseOne[Case i: High Cp but Low Cpk]
        L1[LSL] --> center1[Specification Midpoint M]
        center1 --> U1[USL]
        meanA[Shifted Process Mean μ] --> note1[Cp is High but Cpk is Low due to Off-Centering]
    end

    subgraph caseTwo[Case ii: Both High]
        L2[LSL] --> center2[Specification Midpoint M]
        center2 --> U2[USL]
        meanB[Process Mean μ equals M] --> note2[Cp equals Cpk: World-Class Centered Process]
    end

    caseOne --> caseTwo
```

**Step 3 — Engineering Interpretation:**

- **Case (i):** Indicates that the **process variability is low** but the **mean is drifting**. Action: Implement statistical process control (SPC) feedback loops to re-center the mean.
- **Case (ii):** Indicates a **mature, well-managed process**. Action: Maintain current controls and monitor for any drift using X-bar and R-charts.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Using σ vs. s incorrectly:** Cp uses **σ** (short-term, within-subgroup standard deviation via $\bar{R}/d_2$ or $S/\bar{c_4}$). Pp uses **s** (long-term sample standard deviation). Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "k" in Cpk:** Students often compute Cp but call it Cpk, ignoring centering. If the mean is shifted, both must be computed and **the minimum** must be reported.
>
> 3. **Confusing "Process in Control" with "Process Capable":** A process can be in statistical control (no points outside control limits) but still produce defects (low Cpk). These are **independent** conditions.
>
> 4. **Units in Final Answer:** Always carry units (mm, mg, °C) in the final answer. Missing units = –0.5 mark penalty per KTU valuation norms.
>
> 5. **Interpreting Cpk < 1.0 as "Zero Capability":** Cpk = 0.5 still produces some good output; it is not "zero capability." Use precise language: "Not capable of meeting specifications consistently."
>
> 6. **Six-Sigma Misconception:** Writing "Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO" without explaining that this is the **post-1.5σ shift yield** (not the centered yield). Examiners look for this nuance.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Process Capability** quantifies the inherent ability of a stable process to produce output within specification limits.
- **Cp** = (USL − LSL) / 6σ → potential capability, assumes centered.
- **Cpk** = min[(USL − μ)/3σ, (μ − LSL)/3σ] → actual capability, accounts for shift.
- **Pp** and **Ppk** use sample standard deviation s (long-term performance).
- **Cp ≥ 1.33** → Existing process minimum capability.
- **Cp ≥ 1.50** → New process design target.
- **Cp ≥ 2.00** → Six-Sigma world-class.
- **Cpk = Cp** → Process is centered; **Cpk < Cp** → Process is off-center.
- **In control ≠ Capable:** Statistical control (SPC) is necessary but not sufficient.
- **Six-Sigma** = 6σ inside specs, with 1.5σ shift allowance → 3.4 DPMO.
- **DPMO** = (Defects × 10⁶) / (Units × Opportunities per unit).
- **Z-score (Sigma level)** is the distance from process mean to nearest specification limit in σ units.
- **Reducing σ** improves both Cp and Cpk; **shifting μ** only improves Cpk.
- **Natural Process Limits** = μ ± 3σ → covers 99.73% of output (Empirical Rule).
- For KTU exams, always **show all intermediate calculations**; partial marks are awarded per step.
- **Industry benchmark** for process acceptance: **Cpk ≥ 1.33** for existing processes, **Ppk ≥ 1.67** for safety-critical (automotive, aerospace, medical) per AIAG/ISO/TS standards.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Six Sigma
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Concept of Six Sigma # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Six Sigma** is a highly disciplined, statistical, and data-driven methodology originally developed by **Engineer Bill Smith** at **Motorola Inc. in 1986** (officially launched by CEO Bob Galvin in 1987). It was later popularized globally by **Jack Welch at General Electric (GE)** in 1995. The methodology focuses on **eliminating defects** (non-conformities), **reducing process variation**, and **improving process capability** so that products and services can be delivered with near-perfect quality. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):** > *"Six Sigma is a business strategy and statistical methodology that aims to improve the quality of process outputs by identifying and removing the causes of defects (errors) and minimizing variability in manufacturing and business processes. A process operating at Six Sigma quality produces **3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**."* The term "**Sigma**" (represented by the Greek letter **σ**) is borrowed from **statistics**, where it represents the **Standard Deviation** of a process. A Six Sigma process is one in which **±6 standard deviations (6σ)** of a normal distribution fit within the customer's specification limits (USL − LSL), thereby accounting for a possible **1.5σ process shift** over the long term. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a chef running a large restaurant. Every dish you serve must have exactly **5 grams of salt** (this is the customer specification: Lower Spec Limit LSL = 4.5g, Upper Spec Limit USL = 5.5g). Now: - A **2-Sigma chef** might serve a perfectly salted dish only **69.1%** of the time — about **309,000 wrong dishes per million orders**. - A **4-Sigma chef** gets it right **99.379%** of the time — only **6,210 wrong dishes per million**. - A **6-Sigma chef** is so consistent that the salt is almost always within 0.5 grams of the target — only **3.4 wrong dishes per million** — a near-flawless level of culinary precision. In a factory or a service, the "salt" becomes any **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** characteristic: the weight of a tablet, the waiting time for a customer call, the diameter of a piston, or the number of bugs in a software release. **Six Sigma** is the philosophy, statistical toolset, and cultural framework that pushes an organization from the level of a sloppy cook toward the level of a Michelin-star master — consistently, measurably, and profitably. ## 1.3 Statistical Origin of the "3.4 DPMO" Number > [!NOTE] > The seemingly magical number **3.4 DPMO** comes from a standard normal distribution analysis. If a process has specification limits set at **±6σ** from the mean, the *theoretical* defect rate is **0.002 parts per billion (PPB)**. However, the **Motorola founders** added a **1.5σ long-term mean shift** (because real-world processes drift over time). The area under the tail of the normal curve beyond 4.5σ on one side equals approximately **3.4 defects per million opportunities**. This is the universally accepted benchmark for "Six Sigma Quality." ## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Sigma (σ)** | Greek letter representing the standard deviation of a process | | **DPMO** | Defects Per Million Opportunities | | **DPO** | Defects Per Opportunity (decimal fraction) | | **DPU** | Defects Per Unit | | **CTQ** | Critical-to-Quality: the output characteristic the customer values most | | **COPQ** | Cost of Poor Quality: money lost due to defects, rework, warranty, etc. | | **Yield (Y)** | Percentage of defect-free units produced by a process | | **RTY** | Rolled Throughput Yield: cumulative yield across all sub-processes | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Statistical Foundation: The Normal Distribution Six Sigma is built on the **Gaussian (Normal) Distribution** bell curve, characterized by two parameters: - **μ (mu):** the process mean - **σ (sigma):** the process standard deviation The property exploited by Six Sigma is that for a **normally distributed process**: - **68.27%** of observations fall within **±1σ** - **95.45%** of observations fall within **±2σ** - **99.73%** of observations fall within **±3σ** - **99.9937%** of observations fall within **±4σ** - **99.999943%** of observations fall within **±5σ** - **99.9999998%** of observations fall within **±6σ** A higher sigma capability = a tighter bell curve = fewer defects leaking outside the spec limits. ## 2.2 The Core Philosophy: The 6 Core Principles of Six Sigma Six Sigma is more than statistics; it is a **business philosophy**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes these foundational principles: 1. **Genuine Focus on the Customer** — Every improvement must begin and end with the Voice of the Customer (VOC). 2. **Data and Fact-Driven Decision Making** — Intuition is replaced by statistical evidence. 3. **Process Focus** — Defects are caused by *processes*, not by *people*. Fix the process, not the blame. 4. **Proactive Management** — Prevention over detection (Pareto: 80% of defects come from 20% of causes). 5. **Boundaryless Collaboration** — Break down silos between departments; work as cross-functional teams. 6. **Aim for Perfection, Tolerate Failure in Learning** — A Six Sigma culture accepts small pilot failures as long as the lessons are learned and rolled out. ## 2.3 The DMAIC Roadmap (The Heart of Six Sigma) > [!IMPORTANT] > **DMAIC is the universal problem-solving roadmap of Six Sigma.** It is a 5-phase methodology, and KTU examiners frequently ask students to explain each phase in 14-mark questions. | Phase | Full Name | Key Objective | Key Tools | |---|---|---|---| | **D** | **Define** | Identify the problem, the customer, and the project scope (CTQ) | Project Charter, VOC, SIPOC, COPQ Analysis | | **M** | **Measure** | Quantify the current process performance; establish a baseline | Pareto Chart, Process Mapping, Gage R&R, Capability Indices (Cp, Cpk) | | **A** | **Analyze** | Identify the root causes of variation and defects | Fishbone (Ishikawa), 5-Why Analysis, Hypothesis Testing, Regression | | **I** | **Improve** | Implement solutions that eliminate root causes; pilot and validate | DOE (Design of Experiments), Kaizen, Poka-Yoke, FMEA | | **C** | **Control** | Standardize the improvements; sustain the gains with controls | Control Charts, Statistical Process Control (SPC), SOPs, Mistake-Proofing | ## 2.4 The Six Sigma Belts — Organizational Roles Six Sigma projects are executed by a hierarchy of trained professionals known as **Belts** (a martial-arts metaphor borrowed from karate): - **Executive Leadership** (CEO, Senior VPs): Own the strategy, allocate resources, sponsor projects. - **Champions**: Senior managers who identify, prioritize, and sponsor projects; remove organizational roadblocks. - **Master Black Belts (MBB)**: Elite experts in Six Sigma tools; mentor Black Belts; teach the methodology. - **Black Belts (BB)**: Full-time project leaders; lead 4–6 projects per year; advanced statistical competency. - **Green Belts (GB)**: Part-time project leaders; lead smaller projects or assist Black Belts; basic statistical skills. - **Yellow Belts**: Team members; process participants;
