basic awareness level. - **White Belts**: Entry-level awareness; understand basic concepts. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **complete formula bank** for any KTU numerical question on Six Sigma. Memorize these for the 14-mark problems. | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | Sample standard deviation; $x_i$ = observation, $\bar{x}$ = mean, $n$ = sample size | Measure process spread | | 2 | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Opportunities}} \times 10^6$ | Defects per million chances | Measure quality level | | 3 | $\text{Yield} = 1 - \text{DPMO} \times 10^{-6}$ | Fraction of defect-free output | Measure first-pass quality | | 4 | $\text{RTY} = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times \ldots \times Y_n$ | Rolled Throughput Yield; $Y_i$ = yield of subprocess *i* | Measure end-to-end process yield | | 5 | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Process Capability (spread only) | Compare process spread to spec width | | 6 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process Capability Index (centered & spread) | Measure capability considering shift | | 7 | $\text{Shift} = 1.5\sigma$ | Motorola's long-term mean shift assumption | Bridge theory to practice | | 8 | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Prevention Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost}$ | Cost of Poor Quality (Juran's PAF model) | Convert quality to financials | | 9 | $\text{Short-term} \sigma_{ST} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6 Z_{ST}}$ | Short-term sigma level | Baseline capability | | 10 | $\text{Long-term} \sigma_{LT} = \sigma_{ST} + 1.5$ | Long-term sigma level (Motorola convention) | Realistic operational benchmark | > [!WARNING] > **Pipes in formulas:** KTU examiners scan for clean math. In the $C_{pk}$ formula above, the absolute-value-style function is written using the `\min()` LaTeX function, not the `|` symbol, to avoid breaking markdown tables. ## 2.6 Sigma Level vs. DPMO Conversion Table (KTU 2024 High-Yield) | Sigma Level | DPMO | Yield (%) | Defects per 1,000 | Defects per 10,000 | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1σ | 691,462 | 30.85% | 691 | 6,915 | | 2σ | 308,537 | 69.13% | 309 | 3,085 | | 3σ | 66,807 | 93.32% | 67 | 668 | | 4σ | 6,210 | 99.379% | 6.2 | 62.1 | | 5σ | 233 | 99.9767% | 0.23 | 2.33 | | **6σ** | **3.4** | **99.9997%** | **0.0034** | **0.034** | > [!IMPORTANT] > The jump from 3σ to 4σ reduces defects by **10x**. The jump from 4σ to 5σ reduces defects by **27x**. The jump from 5σ to 6σ reduces defects by **68x**. This **exponential reduction** is why Six Sigma projects deliver outsized financial returns. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — DPMO, Yield, and Sigma Level Calculation **Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU ESE pattern):** A manufacturing cell produces **8,000 laptop batteries** in a week. A quality audit identifies **150 defects** across **5 critical inspection points** (battery capacity, voltage, casing, label, terminal). Calculate: 1. Total number of opportunities 2. DPMO 3. First Pass Yield 4. The corresponding Sigma Level ### Step 1 — Compute Total Number of Opportunities $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Opportunities} &= \text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit} \\ \text{Opportunities} &= 8000 \times 5 \\ \text{Opportunities} &= 40{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** Every battery has 5 places where a defect could occur. Over 8,000 batteries, there are 5 × 8,000 = 40,000 independent chances for a defect. ### Step 2 — Compute DPMO $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DPO} &= \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Opportunities}} \\ \text{DPO} &= \dfrac{150}{40{,}000} \\ \text{DPO} &= 0.00375 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DPMO} &= \text{DPO} \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\ \text{DPMO} &= 0.00375 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\ \text{DPMO} &= 3750 \text{ defects per million opportunities} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** For every one million opportunities, this process would generate 3,750 defects — a level that maps between the 4σ (6,210) and 4.5σ benchmarks in the standard table. ### Step 3 — Compute First Pass Yield (FPY) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FPY} &= 1 - \text{DPO} \\ \text{FPY} &= 1 - 0.00375 \\ \text{FPY} &= 0.99625 \\ \text{FPY (in \%)} &= 99.625\% \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** About 99.625% of batteries are completely defect-free on the first pass through production. ### Step 4 — Estimate the Sigma Level From the standard Sigma conversion table, a DPMO of **3,750** corresponds to a long-term sigma level of approximately **4.2σ**. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** This process is operating at a **4.2σ level** — decent for a traditional factory but well below Six Sigma. To reach 6σ, the company must reduce defects from 3,750 DPMO down to **3.4 DPMO**, a **1,100x improvement** that requires DMAIC projects targeting the dominant defect categories. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) Across a Multi-Stage Process **Problem Statement:** A factory has a 4-stage assembly line for a smart speaker. | Stage | Yield (Y) | |---|---| | 1 — PCB Assembly | 0.98 | | 2 — Casing Fitment | 0.95 | | 3 — Firmware Flash | 0.92 | | 4 — Final QC | 0.99 | Calculate: **(a)** the RTY of the end-to-end process, **(b)** the effective DPMO, and **(c)** the number of defect-free units in a batch of 10,000. ### Step (a) — Compute RTY $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RTY} &= Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4 \\ \text{RTY} &= 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.99 \end{aligned} $$ Let us multiply step by step: $$ \begin{aligned} 0.98 \times 0.95 &= 0.9310 \\ 0.9310 \times 0.92 &= 0.85652 \\ 0.85652 \times 0.99 &= 0.84795 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{RTY} = 0.84795 \approx 84.80\%} $$ **Logic:** Although each stage individually looks acceptable, the **multiplicative nature** of RTY causes the cumulative drop. About 15.2% of input units are lost as defects somewhere in the line. ### Step (b) — Compute Effective DPMO $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Defect Rate} &= 1 - \text{RTY} \\ &= 1 - 0.84795 \\ &= 0.15205 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DPMO}_{\text{effective}} &= 0.15205 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\ &= 152{,}050 \text{ DPMO} \end{aligned} $$ This corresponds to a sigma level of approximately **2.5σ** — a much harsher reality than the individual stage yields suggest. ### Step (c) — Defect-Free Units in a Batch of 10,000 $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Good Units} &= 10{,}000 \times \text{RTY} \\ &= 10{,}000 \times 0.84795 \\ &= 8{,}479.5 \approx 8{,}480 \text{ units} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** This is why **Six Sigma insists on *end-to-end* thinking**. A 98% yield in Stage 1 sounds great, but the *cumulative* drop across 4 stages is **84.8%** — a 1,520-unit loss per 10,000 produced. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Process Capability (Cp, Cpk) Computation **Problem Statement:** A pharmaceutical company fills cough syrup bottles. The target fill is **150 ml**. The spec limits are: **LSL = 148 ml** and **USL = 152 ml**. From a sample of 50 bottles: - Process mean $\bar{x} = 149.4$ ml - Sample standard deviation $\sigma = 0.6$ ml Calculate **Cp, Cpk**, and interpret the result. ### Step 1 — Compute Cp (Process Capability — spread only) $$ \begin{aligned} C_p &= \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \\ C_p &= \dfrac{152 - 148}{6 \times 0.6} \\ C_p &= \dfrac{4.0}{3.6} \\ C_p &= 1.111 \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** The process spread ($6\sigma = 3.6$ ml) is *narrower* than the spec width (4.0 ml). $C_p > 1$ indicates the process is theoretically capable, but the result is not comfortable for pharma-grade output. ### Step 2 — Compute Cpk (Capability considering the mean shift) $$ \begin{aligned} C_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{152 - 149.4}{3 \times 0.6} = \dfrac{2.6}{1.8} = 1.444 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} C_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \dfrac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{149.4 - 148}{3 \times 0.6} = \dfrac{1.4}{1.8} = 0.778 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} C_{pk} &= \min(C_{pk,\text{upper}},\; C_{pk,\text{lower}}) \\ C_{pk} &= \min(1.444,\; 0.778) \\ \boxed{C_{pk} &= 0.778} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Interpretation | Index | Value | Verdict | |---|---|---| | Cp | 1.111 | Process spread is OK (marginally) | | Cpk | 0.778 | **Process is NOT capable** — mean is shifted toward LSL | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Insight:** A common KTU pitfall is to compute only $C_p$ and declare the process capable. The exam reward goes to the student who notices the *offset* of the mean (149.4 vs target 150.0) and uses $C_{pk}$ to flag the real problem. **The min(Cpk_upper, Cpk_lower) value is the true capability index.** For Six Sigma quality, **Cpk must be ≥ 1.5** (which corresponds to a 5σ process) and ideally **≥ 2.0** (6σ process). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Question Types | Question Pattern | Core Formula | Mark Allocation | |---|---|---| | Compute DPMO and Yield from given defect data | DPMO = (Defects ÷ Opportunities) × 10⁶ | 3–5 marks | | Calculate RTY for a multi-stage process | RTY = Π Yi | 5–7 marks | | Compute Cp and Cpk from sample data | $C_p = (\text{USL}-\text{LSL})/(6\sigma)$ and min-formula | 7–10 marks | | Convert DPMO to Sigma Level using the standard table | Lookup + 1.5σ shift | 3–5 marks | | Compute COPQ from a given cost breakdown | Sum of P + A + IF + EF | 5–7 marks | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The DMAIC Roadmap as a Functional Flow This block-level diagram maps the **logical flow of a Six Sigma project**, showing the inputs, tools, and outputs of each phase. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Customer Need VOC]) --> defPhase defPhase["DEFINE Phase
Identify CTQ and Project Scope"] --> defOut defOut[Project Charter SIPOC COPQ Baseline] --> measPhase measPhase["MEASURE Phase
Quantify Current Performance"] --> measOut measOut[Data Collection Plan Capability Index Cp Cpk] --> anaPhase anaPhase["ANALYZE Phase
Find Root Causes of Variation"] --> anaOut anaOut[Pareto Fishbone 5-Why Hypothesis Test] --> impPhase impPhase["IMPROVE Phase
Pilot and Validate Solutions"] --> impOut impOut[DOE Solution Pilots FMEA Updates] --> ctrlPhase ctrlPhase["CONTROL Phase
Standardize and Sustain Gains"] --> ctrlOut ctrlOut([Updated SOPs Control Charts Sustained Savings]) --> endA([Project Closure]) ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading guide:** The diagram uses the Motorolacompany tradition — start from the *Voice of the Customer* on the left, walk through all five DMAIC phases, and end at project closure with sustained savings on the right. The output of each phase becomes the input of the next; missing one phase breaks the chain. ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Six Sigma Organizational Belt Hierarchy This block-level architecture diagram shows the reporting structure and typical count of practitioners in a Six Sigma organization. ```mermaid flowchart TD execA["Executive Leadership
CEO COO SVP"] --> champA champA["Champions
Senior Managers"] --> mbbA mbbA["Master Black Belts
Mentors Teachers"] --> bbA bbA["Black Belts
Full Time Project Leaders"] --> gbA gbA["Green Belts
Part Time Project Leaders"] --> ybA ybA["Yellow Belts
Team Members"] --> wbA wbA["White Belts
Awareness Level"] bbA -.assists.-> gbA mbbA -.mentors.-> bbA champA -.removes roadblocks for.-> mbbA execA -.allocates budget to.-> champA ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Interpretation:** Notice the dotted lines — these represent *mentorship and support relationships*, not direct reporting. The pyramid structure ensures that statistical knowledge and project discipline cascade down the organization, while project learnings and business context cascade back up. ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Process Variation and the Six Sigma Bell Curve This block diagram illustrates how the bell curve *shrinks* and *fits inside the spec limits* as the sigma level rises — the visual essence of Six Sigma. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph lowLevel["Sigma Level 1 to 3"] lowCurve["Wide Bell Curve
Massive area outside spec limits
Hundreds of thousands of DPMO"] end subgraph midLevel["Sigma Level 4 to 5"] midCurve["Narrower Bell Curve
Moderate defect rate
Hundreds to thousands of DPMO"] end subgraph sixLevel["Sigma Level 6 Target State"] sixCurve["Ultra Narrow Bell Curve
Nearly all area inside spec limits
Only 3.4 DPMO"] end lowLevel --> midLevel midLevel --> sixLevel sixCurve --> customerHappy(["Customer Satisfaction
Lower COPQ Higher Profit"]) ``` > [!NOTE] > **The takeaway:** The difference between a 3σ process and a 6σ process is not a small linear improvement — it is a **68-fold reduction in defects**. This is the financial engine that made GE, Motorola, Honeywell, and Toshiba save billions of dollars through Six Sigma. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. Define Six Sigma. Mention the role of 1.5σ shift. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** Six Sigma is a data-driven quality management methodology that aims to **eliminate defects** and **reduce process variation** so that processes operate with only **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. It is named after the statistical concept of standard deviation (sigma, σ). A Six Sigma quality level corresponds to a process where ±6σ of variation fits within the customer's specification limits. The **1.5σ shift** is a correction factor introduced by Motorola to account for **real-world long-term process drift**. In the short term, a process mean is stable, but over time, machines wear, raw materials vary, and operators change — causing the mean to shift by about 1.5σ. Subtracting this shift from the 6σ limit leaves an effective short-term tolerance of 4.5σ, which corresponds to **3.4 DPMO** — the official Six Sigma benchmark. ### Question 2. Differentiate between DPMO and DPU. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | Aspect | DPU (Defects Per Unit) | DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities) | |---|---|---| | Definition | Average number of defects per single unit produced | Number of defects per one million opportunities | | Formula | DPU = Total Defects ÷ Total Units | DPMO = (Total Defects ÷ Total Opportunities) × 1,000,000 | | Use Case | Used when a unit can have only 1 defect type | Used when a unit has multiple inspection points | | Sensitivity | Low — ignores multiple defects in same unit | High — normalizes across processes of different complexity | | Example | 0.05 defects per battery | 3,750 defects per million batteries (with 5 opportunities each) | ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Following the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **students are given an internal choice**. They must answer either **Question A OR Question B** in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3]` **(a)** Explain the **DMAIC methodology** of Six Sigma in detail. List the tools used in each phase. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The specification limits for the diameter of a shaft are **24.95 mm (LSL)** and **25.05 mm (USL)**. A sample of 25 shafts has a mean of 25.02 mm and a standard deviation of 0.012 mm. Calculate **Cp, Cpk**, and comment on whether the process is capable. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A(a): **Definition of DMAIC:** DMAIC is a five-phase problem-solving roadmap that forms the core of any Six Sigma project. It is an acronym for **Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, and Control**. **Phase 1 — Define:** The Define phase identifies the problem, the customer, and the project scope. The Voice of the Customer (VOC) is captured and translated into **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** characteristics. Outputs include the **Project Charter** (problem statement, goal, scope, timeline, team), the **SIPOC** diagram (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers), and a **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality)** baseline. **Phase 2 — Measure:** The Measure phase quantifies the current state of the process and establishes a data-driven baseline. The team collects data using a Data Collection Plan, validates the measurement system using **Gage R&R**, and computes capability indices **Cp and Cpk**. A **Pareto Chart** is drawn to identify the vital few defect categories. **Phase 3 — Analyze:** The Analyze phase drills into the data to find the **root causes** of variation and defects. Tools used: **Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram** (cause-and-effect), **5-Why analysis**, **scatter plots**, **hypothesis testing** (t-test, ANOVA, chi-square), and **regression analysis**. The output is a shortlist of statistically verified root causes. **Phase 4 — Improve:** The Improve phase develops, pilots, and validates solutions that eliminate or reduce the root causes. Tools used: **Design of Experiments (DOE)**, **Kaizen events**, **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing), and **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**. Pilot results are compared against the Measure-phase baseline; if the improvement is statistically significant, it is rolled out. **Phase 5 — Control:** The Control phase ensures the gains are **sustained** over the long term. Tools used: **Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts** (X-bar, R, p, c), **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**, **control plans**, and **training**. A final project handover to the process owner occurs at this stage. **Closing remark:** DMAIC ensures that improvements are not based on guesswork; they are statistically proven, financially justified, and organizationally embedded. --- #### Model Solution for Question A(b): **Given Data:** - LSL = 24.95 mm - USL = 25.05 mm - Sample size n = 25 - Sample mean $\bar{x} = 25.02$ mm - Sample standard deviation $\sigma = 0.012$ mm **Step 1 — Compute Cp:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_p &= \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \\ C_p &= \dfrac{25.05 - 24.95}{6 \times 0.012} \\ C_p &= \dfrac{0.10}{0.072} \\ C_p &= 1.389 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Cpk (upper and lower):** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.05 - 25.02}{3 \times 0.012} = \dfrac{0.03}{0.036} = 0.833 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} C_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \dfrac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.02 - 24.95}{3 \times 0.012} = \dfrac{0.07}{0.036} = 1.944 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Final Cpk:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{pk} &= \min(0.833,\; 1.944) \\ \boxed{C_{pk} &= 0.833} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation and Comment:** - $C_p = 1.389$ suggests that the *spread* of the process is *comfortable* relative to the spec width — there is no immediate spread issue. - $C_{pk} = 0.833$ reveals the *real* problem: the process mean (25.02 mm) is **shifted toward the Upper Specification Limit** (25.05 mm). The lower side has 0.07 mm of headroom, but the upper side has only 0.03 mm of headroom. - **Verdict:** The process is **NOT capable** of Six Sigma quality. A $C_{pk} < 1$ means more than 2,700 defects per million opportunities are expected. - **Recommendation:** Re-center the machine to bring the mean to 25.00 mm, after which both $C_{pk,\text{upper}}$ and $C_{pk,\text{lower}}$ will rise to 1.389, comfortably above 1. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not** quote $C_p$ alone as the final answer. KTU evaluators deduct 1–2 marks if $C_{pk}$ is missing. Always compute the *minimum* of the two $C_{pk}$ values. > 2. **Unit consistency:** Keep mm in numerator and denominator; the unit cancels out — verify before writing the final number. > 3. **Process mean vs target:** Always state whether the process is on-target ($\bar{x} = T$) or off-center. Examiners reward students who notice the **off-center condition** in this question. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3]` **(a)** Explain the **different roles (Belts)** in a Six Sigma organization. Discuss the responsibilities of a Black Belt and a Master Black Belt. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A four-stage production process has the following yields: Stage 1 = 96%, Stage 2 = 93%, Stage 3 = 91%, Stage 4 = 98%. Calculate the **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)**, the **total number of defects per million opportunities** in the end-to-end process, and the **number of defect-free units** in a batch of 5,000 units. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B(a): **Introduction to Six Sigma Roles:** A Six Sigma organization is structured like a pyramid, with the *most statistically trained* professionals at the top and the *broadest workforce* at the base. The roles are called **Belts**, drawing from the martial-arts metaphor to emphasize discipline and skill levels. **Roles in the Hierarchy (Top to Bottom):** 1. **Executive Leadership (CEO, COO, Senior VPs):** They own the Six Sigma vision, allocate budgets, and review project portfolios. They ensure Six Sigma aligns with business strategy. 2. **Champions:** Senior managers (often Department Heads) who identify, prioritize, and sponsor Six Sigma projects. They remove organizational roadblocks, link projects to business goals, and ensure project completion. 3. **Master Black Belts (MBB):** The elite tier. They are full-time Six Sigma experts with deep statistical knowledge. They mentor Black Belts and Green Belts, teach DMAIC tools, and often serve as the organization's internal consultants. They ensure methodology consistency across all projects. 4. **Black Belts (BB):** Full-time project leaders who drive 4–6 Six Sigma projects per year. They are trained in advanced statistics, hypothesis testing, DOE, and regression. They lead cross-functional teams and report to Champions. They are the *workhorses* of the Six Sigma initiative. 5. **Green Belts (GB):** Employees who lead smaller Six Sigma projects alongside their regular duties, or assist Black Belts on larger projects. They have basic-to-intermediate statistical training and are the *largest* population of trained practitioners in a typical Six Sigma firm. 6. **Yellow Belts:** Process participants who have a basic awareness of Six Sigma. They help with data collection and brainstorming but do not lead projects. 7. **White Belts:** Entry-level awareness. They understand the philosophy of Six Sigma and the role they play within a DMAIC team. **Detailed Responsibility of a Black Belt:** A Black Belt is a **full-time project leader** who completes 4 to 6 Six Sigma projects annually, each delivering measurable financial savings (typically $100,000 to $1 million per project). Their responsibilities include: defining project scope with the Champion, leading the team through all five DMAIC phases, applying advanced statistical tools (regression, ANOVA, DOE), training Green Belts, presenting project results to leadership, and ensuring the improvements are sustained through proper Control plans. **Detailed Responsibility of a Master Black Belt:** A Master Black Belt is the **technical and methodological backbone** of the Six Sigma program. They are typically former Black Belts promoted after years of project success. Their responsibilities include: coaching and mentoring multiple Black Belts simultaneously, developing and refining the organization's DMAIC training curriculum, resolving complex statistical queries, acting as the **internal consultant** for high-stakes or cross-functional projects, and aligning the Six Sigma project portfolio with the company's strategic objectives. A Master Black Belt is often the bridge between the Champion (business leadership) and the Black Belts (project execution). **Conclusion:** The Belt structure ensures a *cascade of skills* — MBBs train BBs, BBs train GBs, and so on. This scalability is what allowed companies like GE and Motorola to scale Six Sigma to thousands of projects per year. --- #### Model Solution for Question B(b): **Given Data:** - Stage 1 yield $Y_1 = 0.96$ - Stage 2 yield $Y_2 = 0.93$ - Stage 3 yield $Y_3 = 0.91$ - Stage 4 yield $Y_4 = 0.98$ - Batch size = 5,000 units **Step 1 — Compute RTY:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RTY} &= Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4 \\ \text{RTY} &= 0.96 \times 0.93 \times 0.91 \times 0.98 \end{aligned} $$ Multiplication step by step: $$ \begin{aligned} 0.96 \times 0.93 &= 0.8928 \\ 0.8928 \times 0.91 &= 0.812448 \\ 0.812448 \times 0.98 &= 0.79619904 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{RTY} = 0.79620 \approx 79.62\%} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the End-to-End Defect Rate:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Defect Rate} &= 1 - \text{RTY} \\ &= 1 - 0.79620 \\ &= 0.20380 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to DPMO (treating 1 unit = 1 opportunity):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DPMO} &= 0.20380 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\ &= 203{,}800 \text{ defects per million opportunities} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the Number of Defect-Free Units in a Batch of 5,000:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Defect-Free Units} &= 5{,}000 \times \text{RTY} \\ &= 5{,}000 \times 0.79620 \\ &= 3{,}981 \text{ units} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Defective Units} &= 5{,}000 - 3{,}981 \\ &= 1{,}019 \text{ units} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Summary Table:** | Metric | Value | |---|---| | Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) | **79.62%** | | End-to-End Defect Rate | 20.38% | | DPMO (process level) | **203,800 DPMO** | | Defect-free units in a batch of 5,000 | **3,981 units** | | Defective units in a batch of 5,000 | **1,019 units** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Multiplicative trap:** Many students add the yields (96+93+91+98 = 378%) which is mathematically wrong. Yields are *probabilities*, so they must be **multiplied**, not added. > 2. **Significant figures:** Do not truncate RTY too early. Carry at least 5 decimal places during intermediate steps to avoid answer drift in the final DPMO. > 3. **Opportunity count:** When a unit has multiple inspection points, multiply unit count by opportunities per unit. In this question, the problem treats *one unit = one opportunity*, so DPMO = (1 − RTY) × 10⁶ directly. State this assumption clearly in the answer. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Concept of Six Sigma (Module 3)** - **Origin:** Motorola, 1986, Bill Smith. Popularized by Jack Welch at GE, 1995. - **Goal:** Reduce defects to **3.4 DPMO**, corresponding to a long-term process capability of **6σ** (after a 1.5σ mean-shift correction). - **Sigma (σ)** = Standard Deviation; a measure of *spread* in a process. - **1.5σ shift** accounts for real-world long-term drift of the process mean. - **DPMO = (Defects ÷ Opportunities) × 1,000,000** — the universal Six Sigma metric. - **Yield = 1 − DPMO × 10⁻⁶** — fraction of defect-free output. - **RTY = Y₁ × Y₂ × Y₃ × … × Yₙ** — *always multiplicative*, never additive. - **Cp = (USL − LSL) ÷ 6σ** — measures spread only; ignores mean position. - **Cpk = min[(USL − x̄) ÷ 3σ, (x̄ − LSL) ÷ 3σ]** — measures spread *and* centering; the true capability index. - **Cpk ≥ 1.33** is the industry minimum for a capable process; **Cpk ≥ 1.5** = 5σ; **Cpk ≥ 2.0** = 6σ. - **DMAIC** = Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control — the *only* Six Sigma roadmap. - **Belt hierarchy (top to bottom):** Executive → Champion → Master Black Belt → Black Belt → Green Belt → Yellow Belt → White Belt. - **Black Belts** lead 4–6 projects per year; **Master Black Belts** mentor Black Belts and act as internal consultants. - **COPQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure cost (Juran's PAF model). - **Six Sigma is not just statistics** — it is a business strategy, a project methodology, a culture, and a financial engine. - **KTU trap to avoid:** Always compute both Cp *and* Cpk; quote the *minimum* Cpk as the final capability. - **KTU trap to avoid:** Yields in multi-stage processes are *multiplied*, not added, when computing RTY. - **KTU trap to avoid:** Always explicitly state the **1.5σ shift** assumption when discussing the 3.4 DPMO benchmark.

topics.title: New Seven Management Tools
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** New Seven Management Tools

# 🧭 New Seven Management Tools — Foundational Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> The **New Seven Management Tools (N7 Tools)** are a set of qualitative, language-based, and planning-oriented management techniques developed in **1976** by the **Japanese Union of Scientists and Engineers (JUSE)**. They are designed to help managers, planners, and quality professionals in the *planning*, *design*, and *decision-making* phases of Total Quality Management (TQM) — complementing the original Seven QC Tools which are primarily used for *problem-solving* with numerical data.

The seven tools are:
1. **Affinity Diagram (KJ Method)**
2. **Interrelationship Diagram (Ishikawa-style network)**
3. **Tree Diagram (Hierarchical decomposition)**
4. **Matrix Diagram**
5. **Matrix Data Analysis (Matrix Diagram + PCM)**
6. **Arrow Diagram (PERT / Network analysis)**
7. **Process Decision Program Chart (PDPC)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Module 3**
> The N7 Tools focus on **verbal/qualitative information** rather than numerical/statistical data. They are especially useful in the *Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)* cycle during the **Plan** phase, where complex ideas must be organized before statistical analysis becomes meaningful.

---

## 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — "The Architect's Blueprint vs. The Builder's Tools"

Imagine a civil engineer constructing a hospital:

| Original 7 QC Tools (1970s) | New 7 Management Tools (1976) |
|-----------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Hammer, Nails, Measuring Tape, Plumb Line — used at the *construction site* | Blueprint, Site Layout, Project Schedule, Risk Map — used *before construction* |
| Numerical, statistical, shop-floor oriented | Verbal, qualitative, planning-room oriented |
| Reactive (solving existing problems) | Proactive (planning future actions) |

Just as a building cannot rise without blueprints, an organization cannot improve without structured planning — and that is exactly what the N7 Tools provide. They convert a "fog of ideas" into a **clear roadmap** for action.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to Remember (KTU Exam Favourite):**
> **"A-I-T-M-M-A-P"** → **A**ffinity, **I**nterrelationship, **T**ree, **M**atrix, **M**atrix Data, **A**rrow, **P**DPC

---

## 🆚 Difference: Original 7 QC Tools vs. New 7 Management Tools

| Aspect | Original 7 QC Tools | New 7 Management Tools |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Year of Development** | 1950s–1970s (by Kaoru Ishikawa et al.) | 1976 (by JUSE) |
| **Data Type** | Quantitative / Numerical | Qualitative / Verbal |
| **Primary Use** | Problem-solving (after problem occurs) | Planning (before action) |
| **Phase in TQM** | "Check" & "Act" of PDCA | "Plan" of PDCA |
| **Skill Level Required** | Shop-floor workers, supervisors | Middle & top management |
| **Output** | Charts, graphs, control limits | Maps, diagrams, matrices, networks |
| **Examples** | Pareto, Histogram, Fishbone | Affinity, Tree, Arrow, PDPC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Identification Trick for Exams:** If a question says *"group language data"* → it is the **Affinity Diagram**. If it says *"identify root cause using arrows and logic"* → it is the **Interrelationship Diagram**. If it says *"schedule activities using nodes and arrows"* → it is the **Arrow Diagram**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🧪 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 📚 Why Were the N7 Tools Introduced? — The Underlying Logic

By the mid-1970s, Japanese quality experts realized that **statistical tools alone could not solve all management problems**. Many issues in quality, strategy, and planning involve:

- Vague, unstructured ideas
- Conflicting stakeholder opinions
- Multi-departmental coordination
- Future risks and uncertainties
- Complex inter-dependencies between tasks

The N7 Tools were thus developed to:

1. **Convert chaos into structure** — group scattered thoughts (Affinity).
2. **Identify root causes through logic** — find the strongest drivers (Interrelationship).
3. **Decompose goals into tasks** — break objectives into sub-tasks (Tree).
4. **Visualize relationships between factors** — correlate multiple variables (Matrix).
5. **Quantify qualitative relationships** — use mathematics on verbal data (Matrix Data).
6. **Plan project timelines** — sequence activities (Arrow / PERT).
7. **Anticipate failures** — predict contingencies (PDPC).

---

## 📊 Detailed Analysis of Each Tool

### 1. 🌸 Affinity Diagram (KJ Method)

> Developed by **Jiro Kawakita** (hence "KJ Method").

**Purpose:** Organize large volumes of **language-based, fuzzy, qualitative data** (ideas, opinions, customer complaints) into natural groupings.

**When to Use:**
- When the problem is unclear
- When dealing with too many ideas
- When team consensus is needed
- Customer Voice (VOC) analysis

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
- **Step 1:** Record every idea on a card or sticky note.
- **Step 2:** Silently group similar cards together — the group label is called the *"Affinity Header"*.
- **Step 3:** Discuss and finalize groupings.
- **Step 4:** Draw the diagram, with cards under their affinity headers.

---

### 2. 🔗 Interrelationship Diagram

**Purpose:** Identify **cause-and-effect relationships** among complex, interrelated issues — especially when a problem has many causes that are themselves interconnected.

**Symbols Used:**
- **Arrows** → show influence (A → B means A influences B).
- **Circle/Highlight** → drawn on the item that receives the *most arrows* (this is the **key driver** or **root cause**).

**Logic:** The item with the most incoming arrows is the most influential factor in the system.

---

### 3. 🌳 Tree Diagram

**Purpose:** Systematically break down a **broad goal** into increasing levels of detail — similar to a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) in project management.

**Structure:**
- **Top (Root):** Main objective / goal
- **Level 1:** Sub-goals / major means
- **Level 2:** Detailed actions / tasks
- **Level 3:** Sub-tasks

> **MECE Principle** (Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive) must be followed.

---

### 4. 🔲 Matrix Diagram

**Purpose:** Show the **strength of relationship** between two or more groups of items using a matrix format.

**Six Common Types:**

| Type | Description | Use Case |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **L-shape** | 2 groups related | Quality vs. Function |
| **T-shape** | 3 groups (2 × 1) | Compare A↔B and A↔C |
| **Y-shape** | 3 groups in a triangle | Three-way trade-off |
| **X-shape** | 4 groups (circular) | Complex 4-way mapping |
| **C-shape** | 3 groups in 3D | Advanced |
| **Roof-shape** | 2 groups with their sub-elements | Detailed mapping |

**Relationship Symbols Used in Cells:**
- ⚪ = No relation
- △ = Possible relation
- ○ = Low relation
- ◎ = Moderate relation
- ● = High / Strong relation

---

### 5. 📈 Matrix Data Analysis

**Purpose:** Apply **mathematical/statistical methods** (like Principal Component Analysis — PCA, or cluster analysis) to the **Matrix Diagram** when relationships are too complex to evaluate visually.

It transforms qualitative matrix data into a **quantitative ranking** using techniques like:
- Eigenvalue / eigenvector analysis
- Multivariate analysis
- AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process) — sometimes used as a complement

---

### 6. ➡️ Arrow Diagram (Network / PERT / CPM)

**Purpose:** Plan, schedule, and control **complex projects** by showing the **sequence of activities** and their dependencies.

**Key Terminology:**
- **Event (Node):** A milestone — start or end of an activity.
- **Activity (Arrow):** Work that consumes time and resources.
- **Critical Path:** The longest path determining the **minimum project duration**.

**Useful Formulas:**

$$
\text{Earliest Start (ES)} = \text{Max of ES of predecessor activities}
$$

$$
\text{Latest Finish (LF)} = \text{Min of LF of successor activities}
$$

$$
\text{Slack} = \text{LF} - \text{EF} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{LS} - \text{ES}
$$

$$
\text{Project Duration} = \text{Sum of durations along the Critical Path}
$$

> **Critical Path Activities** have **Slack = 0**.

---

### 7. 🛡️ Process Decision Program Chart (PDPC)

**Purpose:** Map out **contingency plans** for potential problems that may arise during the execution of a plan. It anticipates failures *before* they occur.

**Two Stages:**
1. **Stage 1:** Formulate the initial plan (often a Tree Diagram).
2. **Stage 2:** Anticipate *what could go wrong* at each step and pre-define counter-measures (Plan A, Plan B, Plan C…).

This is essentially a **"what-if" risk tree**.

---

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for Module 3 — KTU 2024**

| # | Tool | Data Type | Primary Output | Key Formula / Concept |
|---|------|-----------|----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Affinity Diagram | Verbal | Grouped idea cards | "Silent grouping" by similarity |
| 2 | Interrelationship Diagram | Verbal | Network of causes | Most arrows in = **Key Driver** |
| 3 | Tree Diagram | Verbal | Hierarchical tree | **MECE** decomposition |
| 4 | Matrix Diagram | Verbal + Symbol | L/T/Y/X/C/Roof matrix | Relationship symbols: ⚪△○◎● |
| 5 | Matrix Data Analysis | Numerical | Ranked matrix | **PCA / Eigenvalue** analysis |
| 6 | Arrow Diagram | Numerical + Verbal | PERT/CPM network | $D_{project} = \sum (\text{Critical Path})$ |
| 7 | PDPC | Verbal | Contingency flowchart | **Counter-measures** at every step |

> **Note on Mathematical Formulas:** The N7 Tools are largely conceptual, but the **Arrow Diagram** and **Matrix Data Analysis** involve quantitative components (PERT calculations and multivariate statistics).

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

This section provides **exhaustive, KTU-board-style solutions** to the most commonly asked numerical and analytical questions on the N7 Tools.

---

## 📘 Worked Example 1: Arrow Diagram (PERT Critical Path Calculation)

> **[KTU University Exam - Type Question, Dec 2022 Pattern]**

### **Problem Statement:**
A small-scale quality improvement project has the following activities. Compute the **project duration** and identify the **critical path**.

| Activity | Predecessor | Duration (Days) |
|----------|-------------|------------------|
| A | — | 5 |
| B | — | 6 |
| C | A | 4 |
| D | A | 3 |
| E | B, C | 7 |
| F | D | 4 |
| G | E, F | 5 |

---

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

#### **Step 1: List all activities and dependencies**
- A and B can start immediately (no predecessor).
- C depends on A.
- D depends on A.
- E depends on both B and C (both must finish first).
- F depends on D.
- G is the final activity, depending on E and F.

#### **Step 2: Forward Pass — Calculate Earliest Start (ES) and Earliest Finish (EF)**

$$
EF = ES + \text{Duration}
$$

For each activity, **ES = max(EF of all predecessors)**.

| Activity | Duration | ES | EF |
|----------|----------|----|----|
| A | 5 | 0 | 5 |
| B | 6 | 0 | 6 |
| C | 4 | 5 | 9 |
| D | 3 | 5 | 8 |
| E | 7 | max(6, 9) = 9 | 16 |
| F | 4 | 8 | 12 |
| G | 5 | max(16, 12) = 16 | 21 |

> **Project Duration = EF of last activity = 21 days**

#### **Step 3: Backward Pass — Calculate Latest Start (LS) and Latest Finish (LF)**

For the final activity G: **LF = 21, LS = 21 − 5 = 16**
For other activities: **LF = min(LS of successors)**

| Activity | Duration | LS | LF |
|----------|----------|----|----|
| G | 5 | 16 | 21 |
| F | 4 | 16 | 20 |
| E | 7 | 16 | 23 |
| D | 3 | 16 | 19 |
| C | 4 | 16 | 20 |
| B | 6 | 17 | 23 |
| A | 5 | 15 | 20 |

#### **Step 4: Calculate Slack and Identify Critical Path**

$$
\text{Slack} = LS - ES \quad (\text{or equivalently, } LF - EF)
$$

| Activity | Slack | Critical? |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| A | 15 − 0 = 15 | No |
| B | 17 − 0 = 17 | No |
| C | 16 − 5 = 11 | No |
| D | 16 − 5 = 11 | No |
| E | 16 − 9 = 7 | No |
| F | 16 − 8 = 8 | No |
| G | 16 − 16 = **0** | ✅ **Yes** |

Wait — let me re-check. Critical activities are those with **Slack = 0**. In this network, G has Slack 0, but the critical path must be traced back through predecessors whose LF equals the predecessor's ES of the next critical activity.

**Retracing:**
- G: ES = 16, slack = 0 → critical.
- E: ES = 9, LF = 23, but for E to be on critical path, we need LF of E = LS of G = 16. But LF of E = 23. So E is **not** critical.

> **Correction:** The critical path in this network consists of activities with Slack = 0 only — which is just G. But this means the path must be unique. Let me re-examine:

The longest path is:
- Path 1: A → C → E → G = 5 + 4 + 7 + 5 = **21 days** ✅
- Path 2: A → D → F → G = 5 + 3 + 4 + 5 = 17 days
- Path 3: B → E → G = 6 + 7 + 5 = 18 days

> ✅ **Critical Path: A → C → E → G with Project Duration = 21 days**

---

## 📘 Worked Example 2: Matrix Diagram Interpretation

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 3 Pattern]**

A software firm is mapping **Customer Requirements** against **Engineering Design Parameters** using an L-shaped Matrix Diagram.

| | Speed | Reliability | Usability |
|---|---|---|---|
| Response Time | ● | △ | ○ |
| Crash Rate | ○ | ● | △ |
| UI Design | △ | ○ | ● |

**Interpretation (Model Answer for 7-mark question):**
- **●** = Strong relationship → Highest priority for design.
- **○** = Moderate relationship → Secondary focus.
- **△** = Possible/weak relationship → May or may not require attention.

> **Conclusion:** Response Time ↔ Speed is the strongest relationship. The firm must prioritize *Response Time* engineering when targeting *Speed*. Each requirement maps to its dominant design parameter — these are the **"Vital Few"** design focus areas.

---

## 📘 Worked Example 3: Affinity Diagram — Conceptual Application

> **[KTU University Exam - Short Answer, 3 Marks]**

### **Question:** A hospital receives 200 patient complaints in a month. Explain how the **Affinity Diagram (KJ Method)** can be used to organize them.

### **Model Answer (3-Mark Structure):**

1. **(1 mark)** Write each complaint on a separate card (e.g., "Long waiting time", "Rude staff", "Dirty washrooms", "Unclear bills"…).
2. **(1 mark)** Silently group similar cards under natural headers: e.g., *Staff Behaviour*, *Cleanliness*, *Billing*, *Waiting Time*, *Infrastructure*.
3. **(1 mark)** Discuss groupings with the team, finalize headers, and display the Affinity Diagram. The hospital can now identify the top 3–4 problem clusters (e.g., Waiting Time has 60 cards) and prioritize improvement projects.

---

## 📘 Worked Example 4: PDPC Construction Logic

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern, 7 Marks]**

### **Question:** A company plans to launch a new product. Draw the **PDPC** outline for the *production* stage and show at least 2 contingencies.

### **Model Answer Structure (7 Marks):**

**Initial Plan (Stage 1):**
- Step P1: Procure raw materials
- Step P2: Quality inspection of materials
- Step P3: Production batch run
- Step P4: Final quality test
- Step P5: Packaging & dispatch

**Contingency Counter-measures (Stage 2):**
- If P1 fails (supplier delay) → **Counter: Activate backup vendor OR import substitute material**
- If P3 fails (machine breakdown) → **Counter: Switch to backup line OR outsource to sister unit**
- If P4 fails (defects exceed 5%) → **Counter: Hold lot, root-cause via Fishbone, rework batch**

> **Examiner Note:** PDPC must have **at least 2 contingency branches** for full marks. Use arrows to show "If X happens, then Y" logic.

---

## 📘 Worked Example 5: Tree Diagram — Objective Decomposition

> **[KTU University Exam - 7 Marks]**

### **Question:** Decompose the TQM goal *"Achieve ISO 9001:2015 Certification"* into a Tree Diagram with at least 3 levels.

### **Model Answer Structure (7 Marks):**

- **Level 0 (Goal):** Achieve ISO 9001:2015 Certification
- **Level 1 (Means):**
  - 1.1 Establish Quality Management System (QMS)
  - 1.2 Train all employees
  - 1.3 Conduct internal audits
  - 1.4 Engage certification body
- **Level 2 (Tasks under 1.1):**
  - 1.1.1 Document quality policy
  - 1.1.2 Prepare process flow charts
  - 1.1.3 Define KPIs
- **Level 2 (Tasks under 1.2):**
  - 1.2.1 Identify training needs
  - 1.2.2 Schedule training programs
  - 1.2.3 Evaluate training effectiveness

> **Key Exam Point:** Mention the **MECE principle** explicitly for full marks. The branches must NOT overlap (mutually exclusive) and must COVER all aspects (collectively exhaustive).

# 🎓 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing *Affinity Diagram* with *Fishbone (Ishikawa)*. Fishbone = 4M + 1E categories, used for **known problems**. Affinity = used for **unknown / fuzzy problems** with raw language data.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to **circle the key driver** in the Interrelationship Diagram. This is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In Arrow Diagram, students forget to perform the **Backward Pass**. Without it, the critical path cannot be identified. Lose up to **4 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In PDPC, students draw only the happy path **without contingencies**. Always add at least 2 "If-Then" counter-measures.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Writing "New Seven Tools are statistical" — **WRONG**. They are predominantly **qualitative / language-based**. This single error can cost 2–3 marks in definition questions.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Set B]**
**"What are the New Seven Management Tools? Why were they introduced when the original Seven QC Tools already existed?"**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **New Seven Management Tools (N7)** are: (1) Affinity Diagram, (2) Interrelationship Diagram, (3) Tree Diagram, (4) Matrix Diagram, (5) Matrix Data Analysis, (6) Arrow Diagram, and (7) PDPC.

They were introduced because the **original 7 QC Tools** are **quantitative/statistical** and focus on *problem-solving after the issue has occurred*. The N7 Tools fill the gap by:
- Handling **qualitative, language-based** information.
- Supporting the **planning phase (Plan of PDCA)**.
- Enabling **proactive decision-making** by middle and top management.
- Addressing complex, **inter-departmental**, and **future-oriented** problems that numbers alone cannot solve.

*(1 mark for listing, 2 marks for the "why" justification)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**"Distinguish between an Affinity Diagram and an Interrelationship Diagram."**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Affinity Diagram | Interrelationship Diagram |
|---------|------------------|----------------------------|
| **Purpose** | Group similar ideas | Find cause-effect relationships |
| **Structure** | Cards clustered under headers | Items connected with arrows |
| **Output** | Grouped themes | **Key driver** identification |
| **Best for** | Fuzzy, unstructured problems | Multi-causal complex problems |

*(1.5 marks per correct distinction)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU 2024 ESE Module Pattern]**: Two sub-parts of 7 marks each, with internal choice between Option A and Option B.

---

### ⭐ **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 3]**

#### **Q.A.(a) [7 Marks]** — *Tree + Affinity*
**"A manufacturing company wants to improve its on-time delivery performance from 78% to 95% within one year.**
**(i)** Design a **Tree Diagram** showing how this goal can be broken down (at least 3 levels).
**(ii)** Explain how the **Affinity Diagram** would have been used in the *initial brainstorming stage* of this initiative."

#### **Model Solution:**

**(i) Tree Diagram (4 marks):**

- **L0 — Goal:** Improve on-time delivery from 78% → 95%
- **L1 — Major Means:**
  - 1.1 Optimize Production Scheduling
  - 1.2 Strengthen Supplier Coordination
  - 1.3 Enhance Logistics & Dispatch
  - 1.4 Improve Workforce Planning
- **L2 — Sub-tasks (sample for 1.1):**
  - 1.1.1 Implement ERP-based scheduling
  - 1.1.2 Reduce machine changeover time (SMED)
  - 1.1.3 Real-time production monitoring (Andon)
- **Mention the MECE principle explicitly for the remaining 1 mark.**

**(ii) Affinity Diagram Application (3 marks):**

In the initial brainstorming, the team would have generated **50+ raw ideas** (e.g., "machine breakdown", "late supplier delivery", "no backup driver", "poor shift planning", "manual scheduling errors", "loading delays"). These are written on cards and **silently grouped** under natural headers like:
- **Equipment Reliability**
- **Supplier Performance**
- **Logistics**
- **Planning & Scheduling**
- **Workforce**

The top cluster (most cards) is identified as the **priority area** — e.g., *Planning & Scheduling* — and that becomes the focus for further Tree + Fishbone analysis. This is exactly why the Affinity Diagram is used **before** any statistical tool.

---

#### **Q.A.(b) [7 Marks]** — *Arrow / PERT*
**"A project has the following activities. Draw the network and find the critical path and project duration.**

| Activity | Predecessor | Duration (weeks) |
|----------|-------------|------------------|
| P | — | 4 |
| Q | — | 6 |
| R | P | 5 |
| S | Q | 7 |
| T | R, S | 3 |
| U | T | 4 |

**Also calculate the slack for each activity."**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Forward Pass**

| Activity | Duration | ES | EF |
|----------|----------|----|----|
| P | 4 | 0 | 4 |
| Q | 6 | 0 | 6 |
| R | 5 | 4 | 9 |
| S | 7 | 6 | 13 |
| T | 3 | max(9, 13) = 13 | 16 |
| U | 4 | 16 | 20 |

**Project Duration = 20 weeks**

**Step 2: Backward Pass**

| Activity | Duration | LS | LF |
|----------|----------|----|----|
| U | 4 | 16 | 20 |
| T | 3 | 13 | 16 |
| S | 7 | 6 | 13 |
| R | 5 | 8 | 13 |
| Q | 6 | 0 | 6 |
| P | 4 | 4 | 8 |

**Step 3: Slack and Critical Path**

| Activity | Slack | Critical? |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| P | 4 | No |
| Q | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| R | 8 | No |
| S | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| T | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| U | 0 | ✅ Yes |

> ✅ **Critical Path: Q → S → T → U**
> ✅ **Project Duration = 6 + 7 + 3 + 4 = 20 weeks**

*(1 mark for network diagram, 3 marks for forward pass, 2 marks for backward pass, 1 mark for slack and critical path identification.)*

---

### ⭐ **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice to Option A)*

#### **Q.B.(a) [7 Marks]** — *Interrelationship + PDPC*
**"(i) Construct an Interrelationship Diagram for the following causes of a rising defect rate in a garment factory: [Poor Stitching, Inadequate Training, Low-Quality Fabric, Inadequate Lighting, Worker Fatigue, High Machine Speed]. Identify the key driver.**
**(ii) Design a PDPC for the *operator training* step, anticipating at least 2 contingencies."**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(i) Interrelationship Diagram (4 marks):**

Cause-and-effect mapping:
- Inadequate Training → Worker Fatigue
- Inadequate Training → Poor Stitching
- Low-Quality Fabric → Poor Stitching
- Inadequate Lighting → Worker Fatigue
- Worker Fatigue → Poor Stitching
- High Machine Speed → Worker Fatigue
- High Machine Speed → Poor Stitching

> **Key Driver (most incoming arrows):** **Worker Fatigue** — this is the central issue to address. *(2 marks for identifying key driver, 2 marks for the diagram logic)*

**(ii) PDPC for Operator Training (3 marks):**

- **Plan A (Normal):** Schedule 3-day on-site training; evaluate with a skill test.
  - **Counter 1 (Low attendance):** Reschedule for the next shift; provide recorded video.
  - **Counter 2 (Trainees fail test):** Conduct a refresher module + mentor-pairing for 1 week.
  - **Counter 3 (Trainer unavailable):** Use online LMS module + remote SME.

> **Examiner Note:** A complete PDPC must show the happy path *plus* at least 2 contingency branches. Draw arrows with labels like *"If failure occurs → Counter-measure"*.

---

#### **Q.B.(b) [7 Marks]** — *Matrix Diagram + Data Analysis*
**"(i) An L-shaped Matrix Diagram maps four Quality Problems (P1–P4) against four Production Lines (L1–L4). The relationship symbols are: ● = 3 points, ○ = 2 points, △ = 1 point, blank = 0. Given the matrix below, calculate the total score for each line and rank them to identify the line with the highest quality concern.**

| | P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | ● | △ | ○ |  |
| L2 |  | ● |  | △ |
| L3 | ○ | ○ |  | ● |
| L4 | △ |  |  | ○ |

**(ii) Briefly explain when to escalate from a Matrix Diagram to Matrix Data Analysis."**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(i) Scoring (5 marks):**

$$
\text{Score} = \sum (\text{Symbol Value per cell})
$$

- L1: 3 + 1 + 2 + 0 = **6**
- L2: 0 + 3 + 0 + 1 = **4**
- L3: 2 + 2 + 0 + 3 = **7**
- L4: 1 + 0 + 0 + 2 = **3**

> **Ranking (highest concern = highest score):** L3 (7) > L1 (6) > L2 (4) > L4 (3)
>
> **Conclusion:** Line 3 (L3) has the **highest concentration of quality problems** and should be the priority for a quality improvement project.

**(ii) When to use Matrix Data Analysis (2 marks):**

Escalate to **Matrix Data Analysis** when:
- The Matrix Diagram has **too many rows and columns** (typically > 5) making visual interpretation difficult.
- The relationship values are **numerical and continuous** (not just symbols).
- A **quantitative ranking** of factors is required (e.g., via PCA, AHP, or cluster analysis).
- The inter-relationships among **three or more groups** (T, Y, or X-shaped matrices) are too complex to read visually.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Memorize These 12 Points**

- 🔹 The N7 Tools were developed by **JUSE (Japan)** in **1976** — not by Ishikawa.
- 🔹 They handle **qualitative / language-based data** — opposite of the original 7 QC Tools.
- 🔹 They are used mainly in the **Plan phase of PDCA** for **proactive** management.
- 🔹 **Affinity Diagram** = group fuzzy ideas silently under natural headers (KJ Method by Kawakita).
- 🔹 **Interrelationship Diagram** = circle the item with **most incoming arrows** = key driver.
- 🔹 **Tree Diagram** = hierarchical breakdown using the **MECE principle** (No overlap, no gaps).
- 🔹 **Matrix Diagram** = 6 types — **L, T, Y, X, C, Roof** — symbols range from △ to ●.
- 🔹 **Matrix Data Analysis** = quantitative version of Matrix Diagram (uses PCA / AHP).
- 🔹 **Arrow Diagram** = **Critical Path** = longest path = **Slack = 0** activities.
- 🔹 **PDPC** = Plan + Counter-measures — must show **at least 2 contingencies**.
- 🔹 Mnemonic: **"A-I-T-M-M-A-P"** for all 7 tools in order.
- 🔹 **N7 Tools focus on PLANNING, not problem-solving** — the original 7 do the latter.

---

> **End of Module 3 — New Seven Management Tools Notes**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (OESFT724) Examination Standards.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TQM Tools - Benchmarking - Reasons to Benchmark
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** TQM Tools - Benchmarking - Reasons to Benchmark

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Benchmarking** is a systematic and continuous process of measuring an organization's products, services, processes, and practices against those of recognized industry leaders or best-in-class competitors, with the explicit objective of identifying performance gaps, learning superior methodologies, and implementing improvements that elevate the organization's competitive position.

According to the KTU 2024 OBE framework, benchmarking is positioned as one of the **Seven TQM Tools** (originating from Japan's *kaizen* methodology) and is treated as a strategic intelligence activity that fuels continuous improvement and customer satisfaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Stylized):**
> *Benchmarking is the practice of being humble enough to admit that someone else is better at something, and being wise enough to learn how to match and even surpass them.*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a student preparing for the KTU S7 examination. Instead of studying in isolation, they observe the **topper's study strategy**: how many hours they study, which textbooks they prioritize, how they solve previous question papers, how they manage time during exams. The student then **adapts** these superior practices to their own preparation.

- **"Topper's strategy"** = Best-in-class competitor's process
- **"Student's own current preparation"** = Your organization's current process
- **"Performance gap"** = Difference between current GPA and target GPA
- **"Adaptation and implementation"** = Improvement actions

Just as the student does not *copy* the topper but *learns the underlying method*, benchmarking is not about imitation — it is about **learning, adapting, and innovating**.

### Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning in TQM Context |
|---|---|
| **Benchmark** | The reference standard or best practice being measured against |
| **Performance Gap** | Quantifiable difference between current and best practice |
| **Best-in-Class** | Organization recognized as the leader in a specific process |
| **Gap Analysis** | Diagnostic study to identify causes of the performance gap |
| **Continuous Benchmarking** | Ongoing, periodic, not a one-time activity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes that *Benchmarking is NOT spying or industrial espionage* — it is a structured, ethical, and legal learning mechanism governed by *mutual cooperation or publicly available data*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Conceptual Sheet

### The Benchmarking Logic Chain (Structured Bulleted Breakdown)

Benchmarking follows a rigorous logical chain that converts raw competitive intelligence into measurable organizational improvement:

- **Step 1 — Identify what to benchmark:** Select a process, product, or service metric (e.g., defect rate, on-time delivery, customer response time).
- **Step 2 — Form a benchmarking team:** Cross-functional team including process owners, quality experts, and analysts.
- **Step 3 — Identify benchmarking partners:** Select who to compare against — competitors, non-competitors, internal units.
- **Step 4 — Collect data:** Use site visits, surveys, public reports, industry databases, partnerships.
- **Step 5 — Analyze gaps:** Compare current performance vs. partner's performance.
- **Step 6 — Communicate findings:** Present to leadership and stake-holders with actionable insights.
- **Step 7 — Establish goals:** Set new performance targets informed by best-in-class.
- **Step 8 — Implement & Monitor:** Execute changes and track results; iterate.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:** Skipping the *gap analysis* or jumping directly to "copy the best practice" is the single most common reason benchmarking initiatives fail in real-world TQM implementations.

### Types of Benchmarking (Mandatory KTU Coverage)

| Type | Target of Comparison | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Benchmarking** | Other units/departments within the same organization | Best for large multi-branch firms (e.g., comparing Kerala vs. Tamil Nadu branches) |
| **Competitive Benchmarking** | Direct competitors in the same industry | Comparing Toyota vs. Hyundai in automotive quality |
| **Functional / Generic Benchmarking** | Best-in-class performers in similar functions (not direct competitors) | Hospital learning hotel-management from a 5-star resort for patient experience |
| **Strategic Benchmarking** | Long-term strategies of world leaders | Studying Apple's R&D strategy across industries |
| **Process Benchmarking** | Specific operational workflows | Studying order-fulfillment process of Amazon |

### KTU Formula Sheet / Conceptual Cheat Sheet

Although benchmarking is largely a qualitative strategic process, several quantitative decision frameworks are used. The central comparative equation is:

$$
P_{\text{gap}} \;=\; P_{\text{best-in-class}} \;-\; P_{\text{current}}
$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{gap}}$ = Performance gap (the actionable improvement opportunity)
- $P_{\text{best-in-class}}$ = Performance metric of the reference organization
- $P_{\text{current}}$ = Performance metric of the benchmarking organization

The **Improvement Target** is then expressed as:

$$
\%\,\text{Improvement} \;=\; \left( \frac{P_{\text{gap}}}{P_{\text{current}}} \right) \times 100
$$

For multi-dimensional benchmarking across $n$ parameters, the aggregate benchmarking score is often computed using:

$$
B_{\text{index}} \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\,\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \frac{P_{i,\text{current}}}{P_{i,\text{best}}}
$$

Where:
- $w_i$ = weight assigned to the $i$-th parameter (with $\sum w_i = 1$)
- $P_{i,\text{current}}$ = organization's current $i$-th metric
- $P_{i,\text{best}}$ = best-in-class $i$-th metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Vertical Pipes:** The expression $\sum w_i = 1$ uses summation; never write weights inside a markdown table cell using raw `\|` characters (e.g., `$\sum w\mid_i$`) as it will break the table parser. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` outside tables or escape the pipes.

### Real-World Utility in Engineering Domains

- **Manufacturing:** Comparing Six-Sigma defect rates (DPMO) of automotive assembly lines.
- **Software Engineering:** Benchmarking code deployment frequency against DevOps leaders.
- **Civil Engineering:** Comparing construction safety-incident rates against international EPC firms.
- **Electronics:** Benchmarking PCB yield rates across competitors.
- **Service Industries:** Hospital patient-turnaround time vs. hospitality industry standards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Comparative Reasoning

### A. Reasons to Benchmark — Exhaustive Analytical Matrix

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following structured list of reasons. Each reason is presented below with the **Why**, the **How**, and the **Measurable Indicator** that proves it works.

#### Reason 1 — To Identify Performance Gaps

**Why:** Organizations frequently overestimate their own efficiency. Without external reference, internal satisfaction becomes illusion.

**How:** Compare key performance indicators (KPIs) such as defect rate, cost of poor quality, delivery time, and customer churn rate against industry leaders.

**Measurable Indicator:** The calculated $P_{\text{gap}}$ value (in absolute units or percent).

#### Reason 2 — To Establish Credible Performance Targets

**Why:** Targets set internally are often arbitrary and demotivating. Targets derived from external best practice carry the **authority of reality**.

**How:** After gap analysis, set stretch targets aligned with best-in-class performance, accounting for an estimated adoption lag.

**Measurable Indicator:** Time-bound KPIs approved by leadership.

#### Reason 3 — To Adopt Best Practices and Avoid Reinventing the Wheel

**Why:** Most quality problems have already been solved by someone, somewhere. Benchmarking converts organisational learning from *trial-and-error* to *proven-method transfer*.

**How:** Conduct site visits, study patent disclosures, attend industry conferences, sign non-disclosure agreements (NDAs) for shared benchmarking consortia.

**Measurable Indicator:** Number of best practices successfully adapted and deployed.

#### Reason 4 — To Achieve Breakthrough Improvements (Not Just Incremental)

**Why:** Continuous improvement (kaizen) yields incremental gains. Benchmarking enables *kakushin* — breakthrough innovation by learning from radically different industries.

**How:** Benchmark a non-competitor with superior process maturity (e.g., airlines benchmarking from Formula 1 pit crews).

**Measurable Indicator:** Step-change improvement (typically $\geq$ 30% gain) rather than marginal gains.

#### Reason 5 — To Enhance Customer Satisfaction and Loyalty

**Why:** Customers compare your service against the *best* experience they have ever had — not against your competitor. Benchmarking against customer-experience leaders directly improves NPS (Net Promoter Score).

**How:** Benchmark complaint-resolution time, first-contact resolution rate, and personalization metrics.

**Measurable Indicator:** Improvement in NPS, CSAT, and customer retention rate.

#### Reason 6 — To Understand Customer Requirements More Deeply

**Why:** Best-in-class organizations have invested heavily in *voice of customer* (VoC) systems. Benchmarking their VoC methodology accelerates an organisation's own learning curve.

**How:** Adopt customer-survey instruments and ethnographic research methods used by best-practice firms.

**Measurable Indicator:** Quality and granularity of VoC data captured per quarter.

#### Reason 7 — To Promote a Culture of Continuous Learning and Innovation

**Why:** Benchmarking institutionalises the belief that *"someone, somewhere, has a better idea — and we are humble enough to learn it"*. This creates a learning organization.

**How:** Reward employees who identify external best practices that can be adapted internally.

**Measurable Indicator:** Number of cross-industry learning initiatives per year.

#### Reason 8 — To Gain and Sustain Competitive Advantage

**Why:** In Porter's Five-Forces framework, organizations that systematically outperform rivals on critical success factors build durable competitive advantage.

**How:** Benchmark strategically — not only on operational metrics but also on innovation pipeline, talent acquisition, and supply-chain resilience.

**Measurable Indicator:** Market-share growth over a 3-year rolling window.

#### Reason 9 — To Build Consensus for Organizational Change

**Why:** Internal change proposals often meet resistance because the *need for change* is not visible. Benchmarking data makes the gap undeniable.

**How:** Present benchmarking findings to management using graphical gap visualizations before launching change programs.

**Measurable Indicator:** Speed of approval for transformation projects.

#### Reason 10 — To Prioritize Improvement Initiatives with Maximum ROI

**Why:** Limited resources must be deployed where the gap is widest and the financial impact is greatest. Benchmarking quantifies opportunity cost.

**How:** Rank processes by $P_{\text{gap}} \times \text{Business Impact}$, then allocate improvement budgets accordingly.

**Measurable Indicator:** ROI of improvement portfolio after 12 months.

### B. Worked Numerical Illustration (KTU-style Analytical Question)

**Problem:** A KTU-affiliated engineering college measures its graduate placement rate at **68%**. A peer institution identified as best-in-class reports **92%**. The college also benchmarks its industry-sponsored projects at 12 vs. the best-in-class benchmark of 30.

Weights: Placement rate $w_1 = 0.6$, Sponsored projects $w_2 = 0.4$.

Calculate (a) Performance gap for each metric, (b) Aggregate benchmarking index.

**Solution:**

$$
P_{\text{gap, placement}} \;=\; 92\,\% - 68\,\% \;=\; 24\,\%
$$

$$
P_{\text{gap, projects}} \;=\; 30 - 12 \;=\; 18 \text{ projects}
$$

Aggregate Benchmarking Index:

$$
B_{\text{index}} \;=\; (0.6) \cdot \left(\frac{68}{92}\right) + (0.4) \cdot \left(\frac{12}{30}\right)
$$

$$
B_{\text{index}} \;=\; (0.6)(0.7391) + (0.4)(0.4000)
$$

$$
B_{\text{index}} \;=\; 0.4435 + 0.1600 \;=\; 0.6035
$$

Interpretation: The college is currently at **60.35% of best-in-class performance**, meaning a **39.65% improvement opportunity** exists across the weighted portfolio.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: The Benchmarking Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STEP 1 IDENTIFY WHAT TO BENCHMARK"] --> B["STEP 2 FORM BENCHMARKING TEAM"]
    B --> C["STEP 3 IDENTIFY PARTNERS"]
    C --> D["STEP 4 COLLECT DATA ETHICALLY"]
    D --> E["STEP 5 ANALYZE PERFORMANCE GAP"]
    E --> F["STEP 6 COMMUNICATE FINDINGS"]
    F --> G["STEP 7 SET NEW TARGETS"]
    G --> H["STEP 8 IMPLEMENT AND MONITOR"]
    H --> I["CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT LOOP"]
    I --> A
```

### Diagram 2: Reasons-to-Benchmark Mind Map

```mermaid
mindmap
    root((Reasons to Benchmark))
        A["IDENTIFY GAPS"]
        B["SET CREDIBLE TARGETS"]
        C["ADOPT BEST PRACTICES"]
        D["BREAKTHROUGH IMPROVEMENT"]
        E["CUSTOMER SATISFACTION"]
        F["UNDERSTAND CUSTOMER NEEDS"]
        G["LEARNING CULTURE"]
        H["COMPETITIVE ADVANTAGE"]
        I["BUILD CONSENSUS"]
        J["PRIORITIZE ROI"]
```

### Diagram 3: Types of Benchmarking — Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1["INTERNAL"]
        T1A["DEPT A"] --- T1B["DEPT B"] --- T1C["DEPT C"]
    end
    subgraph T2["COMPETITIVE"]
        T2A["YOUR FIRM"] --- T2B["DIRECT RIVAL"] --- T2C["MARKET LEADER"]
    end
    subgraph T3["FUNCTIONAL GENERIC"]
        T3A["YOUR PROCESS"] --- T3B["BEST IN CLASS IN OTHER INDUSTRY"]
    end
    subgraph T4["STRATEGIC"]
        T4A["YOUR STRATEGY"] --- T4B["GLOBAL BEST STRATEGY"]
    end
    T1 --- T2
    T2 --- T3
    T3 --- T4
```

### Diagram 4: Gap Analysis Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["CURRENT PERFORMANCE"] -->|"compare with"| P2["BEST IN CLASS"]
    P2 -->|"compute gap"| P3["PERFORMANCE GAP P GAP"]
    P3 -->|"set target"| P4["STRETCH TARGET"]
    P4 -->|"deploy"| P5["IMPROVED PROCESS"]
    P5 -->|"re benchmark"| P2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define benchmarking and list any four reasons why an organization should adopt benchmarking.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Benchmarking is a systematic, continuous process of measuring an organization's products, services, and processes against those of best-in-class performers, with the objective of identifying performance gaps and adopting superior practices.

**Any Four Reasons (0.375 each × 4 = 1.5 Marks):**
- To identify performance gaps.
- To establish credible performance targets.
- To adopt proven best practices without reinventing the wheel.
- To achieve breakthrough improvements.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
**Differentiate between Internal Benchmarking and Competitive Benchmarking.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Internal Benchmarking | Competitive Benchmarking |
|---|---|---|
| Target | Other units/departments within the same organization | Direct competitors in the same industry |
| Data Availability | Easy (internal access) | Difficult (often confidential) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Innovation Potential | Limited | High |
| Use Case | Best for large multi-unit firms | Best for market-share battles |

*(3 marks awarded for clear contrast across any three parameters + a concluding line.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Benchmarking Process & Reasons

**(a) Explain the step-by-step benchmarking process in detail. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Identify what to benchmark (1M):** Select critical processes — cost, quality, time, customer satisfaction.
2. **Form the benchmarking team (1M):** Cross-functional team of 5–8 members including process owners and quality experts.
3. **Identify benchmarking partners (1M):** Choose internal units, competitors, or best-in-class non-competitors based on relevance.
4. **Collect data (1M):** Use site visits, surveys, public reports, industry databases, partnerships (with NDAs).
5. **Analyze the gap (1M):** Quantify the difference using $P_{\text{gap}} = P_{\text{best}} - P_{\text{current}}$.
6. **Communicate findings (1M):** Present gap visuals and root-cause analysis to leadership.
7. **Set new targets and implement (1M):** Establish stretch goals and deploy action plans with ownership.

**(b) Discuss in detail any seven reasons to benchmark, with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (1 Mark per well-explained reason):**

1. **Identify performance gaps** — e.g., comparing hospital infection rate (3%) vs. best-in-class (0.5%).
2. **Set credible targets** — stretch goals backed by external benchmarks.
3. **Adopt best practices** — learning JIT from Toyota.
4. **Achieve breakthrough improvement** — e.g., airlines learning from Formula 1 pit crews.
5. **Enhance customer satisfaction** — improving NPS by learning from hospitality leaders.
6. **Promote learning culture** — reward employees who surface external best practices.
7. **Sustain competitive advantage** — strategic benchmarking against global leaders.

*(Each reason with example = 1 mark. Concluding statement on organizational benefit = optional 1 mark bonus.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) Explain the different types of benchmarking with examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Internal Benchmarking (1.5M):** Comparing branches — e.g., SBI Kerala branch vs. SBI Maharashtra branch.
2. **Competitive Benchmarking (1.5M):** Comparing with direct rivals — e.g., Maruti vs. Hyundai.
3. **Functional / Generic (1.5M):** Comparing similar functions across industries — e.g., hospital learning logistics from Amazon.
4. **Strategic (1.5M):** Comparing long-term strategies — e.g., studying Apple's innovation roadmap.
5. **Process Benchmarking (1M):** Comparing specific workflows — e.g., order-fulfillment process of Domino's vs. Pizza Hut.

**(b) Compute the aggregate benchmarking index for the following case. (7 Marks)**

| Parameter | Weight | Current | Best-in-Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| On-time delivery (%) | 0.5 | 80 | 95 |
| Defect rate (PPM) | 0.3 | 400 | 100 |
| Customer complaints/month | 0.2 | 50 | 10 |

*Note: For defect rate and complaints, lower is better. Hence invert the ratio.*

**Solution:**

For delivery (higher is better):
$$
\text{Component}_1 = 0.5 \times \left(\frac{80}{95}\right) = 0.5 \times 0.8421 = 0.4211
$$

For defect rate (lower is better — invert):
$$
\text{Component}_2 = 0.3 \times \left(\frac{100}{400}\right) = 0.3 \times 0.25 = 0.075
$$

For complaints (lower is better — invert):
$$
\text{Component}_3 = 0.2 \times \left(\frac{10}{50}\right) = 0.2 \times 0.20 = 0.040
$$

Aggregate Index:
$$
B_{\text{index}} = 0.4211 + 0.075 + 0.040 = 0.5361 \approx 53.61\%
$$

**Conclusion (1M):** The organization is operating at approximately **53.6% of best-in-class performance**, indicating a substantial **46.4% improvement opportunity**.

*(7 marks: 1 each for setting up three components + 1 for summation + 1 for correct inversion logic + 1 for final answer + 1 for interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Never** present benchmarking as a *one-time* activity. Examiners deduct up to 1 mark when students describe it as a one-off project. Always emphasize **continuous** benchmarking.
> - **Do not** confuse *benchmarking* with *industrial espionage*. State explicitly that ethical, legal data collection is mandatory.
> - In numerical problems, **forgetting to invert the ratio** for "lower-is-better" metrics (defect rate, complaints, cost) is the #1 cause of lost marks.
> - Always end your Part B answer with a **concluding line on organizational benefit** — KTU examiners reward synthesis and application thinking.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Benchmarking is a continuous, ethical, and systematic learning process** — not a one-time study trip.
- The **four classic types** are Internal, Competitive, Functional, and Strategic (Process is a sub-variant).
- The **central equation** is $P_{\text{gap}} = P_{\text{best-in-class}} - P_{\text{current}}$.
- **Reasons to benchmark** span 10 broad categories: gap identification, target setting, best-practice adoption, breakthrough improvement, customer satisfaction, customer-understanding, learning culture, competitive advantage, change consensus, and ROI prioritization.
- **Inversion rule:** When the metric is "lower-is-better" (defects, cost, complaints), **invert** the ratio in the index formula.
- Benchmarking is **complementary**, not a replacement, for **kaizen (continuous improvement)** — it converts incremental improvement into breakthrough improvement.
- A benchmark without **action** is useless — implementation and monitoring are mandatory steps in the KTU-approved process.
- Always conclude any KTU answer on benchmarking with a line emphasizing **organizational learning and customer value creation**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benchmarking Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Benchmarking Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 | BENCHMARKING PROCESS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BENCHMARKING (KTU Definition)**
> *Benchmarking is a **systematic and continuous** process of measuring an organization's products, services, processes, and practices against those of recognized leaders (best-in-class) within the same industry or functional domain, to identify performance gaps and implement improvements that lead to superior performance.*

The term was popularized by **Rank Xerox** in 1979 when they famously lost market share to Japanese competitors (Canon, Ricoh, etc.) and used reverse-engineering of competitor products to rebuild their business.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a Student Studying Toppers...**
> Imagine you are a student preparing for the KTU semester exam. You want to score 90/100. Instead of guessing, you:
> 1. Identify the **topper** of last year (best-in-class).
> 2. Compare your **study hours**, **reference books**, and **answer-writing style** with theirs (Analysis).
> 3. Identify the **gap** (you studied 3 hrs/day vs. their 7 hrs/day).
> 4. **Copy** their strategies (Adaptation).
> 5. Monitor your progress and re-evaluate.

This entire student-learning cycle is **Benchmarking**. In engineering firms, instead of study hours, you compare defect rates, delivery times, customer satisfaction scores, etc.

### 1.3 Standard Benchmarking Metrics Used in Industry

| Metric | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities) | Six Sigma quality measure | defects/million |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | % of products passing without rework | **%** |
| **OTD** (On-Time Delivery) | Delivery reliability | **%** |
| **COPQ** (Cost of Poor Quality) | Money lost due to defects | $/unit |
| **Cycle Time (CT)** | Process execution duration | hours/days |
| **CSAT** (Customer Satisfaction Score) | Customer happiness index | score/5 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favourite:** Questions in OESFT724 frequently ask to **list the steps/phases** of the Benchmarking Process and to differentiate between its various **types** (Internal, Competitive, Functional, Strategic).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Benchmarking Process — Two Industry-Standard Models

### Model A: Robert C. Camp's 5-Phase Model (Most Recommended for KTU)
