# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: List any three tangible benefits of TQM to the customer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three tangible benefits of TQM to the customer are:
1. **Lower product price** — because the organization saves on rework, scrap, and warranty costs, these savings can be partially passed to the customer.
2. **Faster delivery** — standardized and streamlined processes reduce lead times, ensuring the customer receives the product on time.
3. **Better after-sales service** — fewer defects mean fewer service complaints, and TQM-trained service teams respond more efficiently to customer issues.

*(Award 1 mark per valid point, 1 mark for presentation.)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between tangible and intangible benefits of TQM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Tangible Benefits | Intangible Benefits |
|---|---|---|
| Measurability | Quantifiable in monetary/value terms | Difficult to measure directly |
| Time horizon | Short to medium term (6-18 months) | Long term (1-5 years) |
| Examples | Cost reduction, defect rate drop, higher ROI | Brand image, employee morale, customer loyalty |
| Documentation | Cost-of-Quality reports, SPC charts | Surveys, audit reports, brand perception studies |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: (a) Explain in detail the benefits of TQM to the employees and the management of an organization.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Benefits of TQM to Employees:**
1. **Job Security** — When the organization becomes quality-driven, profitability rises, and the fear of layoffs reduces significantly. *(1 mark)*
2. **Skill Development** — TQM requires training in tools like 7 QC tools, SPC, FMEA, 5S, etc. This makes employees more employable and confident. *(1 mark)*
3. **Sense of Ownership** — Quality circles and Kaizen teams give employees a voice in decision-making, creating psychological ownership of outcomes. *(1 mark)*
4. **Improved Working Conditions** — 5S and safety initiatives lead to cleaner, safer, more ergonomic workplaces, reducing fatigue and accidents. *(1 mark)*
5. **Recognition and Empowerment** — TQM rewards good ideas; employees feel valued, motivated, and engaged. *(1 mark)*

**Benefits of TQM to Management:**
1. **Better Decision-Making** — Data-driven tools like Pareto, fishbone, and SPC charts help managers base decisions on evidence, not intuition. *(1 mark)*
2. **Higher Productivity** — Streamlined processes mean more output per unit input, directly improving the bottom line. *(1 mark)*
3. **Reduced Firefighting** — With fewer defects, managers spend less time on crisis management and more on strategic planning. *(1 mark)*
4. **Improved ROI** — Prevention is cheaper than correction; TQM dramatically reduces failure costs. *(1 mark)*
5. **Stronger Organizational Culture** — TQM aligns everyone toward a common goal, easing supervision and improving cross-functional coordination. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q: (b) Discuss the benefits of TQM to customers and shareholders. How do these benefits influence the long-term sustainability of the firm?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Benefits to Customers:**
1. **Improved Product Quality** — The primary beneficiary of TQM is the customer, who receives fewer defective products. *(1 mark)*
2. **Lower Cost of Ownership** — Reliable products mean lower maintenance, repair, and replacement costs. *(1 mark)*
3. **Faster Response** — Customer feedback loops ensure that complaints are addressed quickly. *(1 mark)*
4. **Higher Satisfaction and Loyalty** — Satisfied customers become repeat buyers and brand advocates. *(1 mark)*

**Benefits to Shareholders:**
1. **Higher Profits and Dividends** — Lower failure costs and higher market share translate to better financial returns. *(1 mark)*
2. **Appreciation in Stock Value** — TQM-certified firms are viewed as lower-risk, attracting long-term investors. *(1 mark)*
3. **Sustainable Business Model** — TQM-driven organizations weather economic downturns better, protecting shareholder value. *(1 mark)*

**Influence on Long-Term Sustainability:**
The combined effect of customer loyalty and shareholder confidence creates a **virtuous cycle** — satisfied customers generate revenue, which boosts shareholder returns, which attracts capital for further quality investments, which improves customer value. This cycle makes the firm **resilient, adaptive, and competitive over decades**, not just quarters. TQM thus transforms the firm from a profit-maximizing entity into a **value-creating, sustainable enterprise**. *(1 mark for synthesis)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: (a) "TQM is not a cost — it is an investment that yields measurable and intangible returns." Critically evaluate this statement with reference to tangible and intangible benefits.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

This statement is **true and widely accepted** in quality management literature.

**Tangible Returns (measurable):**
1. Reduction in scrap and rework costs — typically **20-40% drop** within 2 years.
2. Lower warranty and recall expenses.
3. Improved machine uptime and Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE).
4. Higher first-pass yield (FPY) and lower DPMO.
5. Increased market share and revenue.

*These are quantified through Cost-of-Quality (CoQ) reports and ROI calculations.* *(3-4 marks for elaboration)*

**Intangible Returns (non-measurable):**
1. Enhanced brand reputation and goodwill.
2. Higher employee morale and lower attrition.
3. Stronger customer loyalty and word-of-mouth.
4. Improved supplier relationships.
5. Better organizational learning and adaptability.

*These are tracked through employee surveys, customer satisfaction indices, and brand audit studies.* *(3-4 marks for elaboration)*

**Critical Evaluation:** The statement holds because research shows that the **cost of poor quality (COPQ)** in most firms is **15-40% of total revenue**. Even a modest 25% reduction in COPQ through TQM easily justifies the investment, often within 18-24 months. Hence, TQM is genuinely a **high-return investment**, not a sunk cost. *(1 mark for synthesis)*

---

**Q: (b) "Customer is the ultimate beneficiary of TQM." Justify this statement with a stakeholder-mapping framework and explain why other stakeholders also gain.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Why Customers are the Ultimate Beneficiary:**
1. The customer **defines quality** — TQM's central principle is to meet/exceed customer expectations. *(1 mark)*
2. TQM's voice-of-customer (VOC) tools like QFD and surveys channel customer needs into product design. *(1 mark)*
3. Lower defect rates directly translate to better customer experience. *(1 mark)*
4. Continuous improvement ensures products get better over time, not stagnant. *(1 mark)*

**Stakeholder-Mapping Framework:**

| Stakeholder | Primary Benefit from TQM |
|---|---|
| Customer | Quality products, fair price, satisfaction |
| Employee | Job security, empowerment, learning |
| Management | Better control, ROI, strategic clarity |
| Shareholder | Sustainable profits, lower risk |
| Supplier | Long-term partnerships, clear specifications |
| Society | Safe products, ethical business, employment |

**Why Other Stakeholders Also Gain:**
- **Employees** gain because sustainable quality requires skilled, engaged workers.
- **Shareholders** gain because loyal customers drive long-term profitability.
- **Suppliers** gain because quality systems create stable, predictable demand.
- **Society** gains because ethical, safe products improve quality of life.

*Thus, the customer-centric focus does not exclude other stakeholders — it cascades benefits to all. The customer is "first among equals" in the TQM ecosystem.* *(1 mark for synthesis)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT list benefits as a single random paragraph** — examiners deduct 2-3 marks for poor structure. Always use **headings or a table**.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse the "Benefits of TQM" with the "Principles of TQM"** — these are different questions. Principles (customer focus, leadership, etc.) are inputs; Benefits are outputs.
> 3. **Do NOT skip the tangible/intangible distinction** — this is a guaranteed 2-mark sub-question in almost every KTU paper.
> 4. **Do NOT write only "lower cost and higher quality"** — these are too generic. Add specifics: DPMO reduction, cost-of-quality percentage, OEE figures, etc.
> 5. **Do NOT forget the long-term sustainability angle** — in 14-mark questions, the synthesis paragraph carries **at least 1-2 marks**.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Benefits of TQM**

- ✅ **Tangible benefits** are *quantifiable* — reduced scrap, lower warranty, higher ROI, increased market share.
- ✅ **Intangible benefits** are *qualitative* — brand image, morale, loyalty, organizational learning.
- ✅ **Internal benefits** flow *within* the organization (productivity, morale, cost).
- ✅ **External benefits** flow *outside* the organization (customer satisfaction, brand, society).
- ✅ **Stakeholder-wise benefits** are the **gold standard** KTU answer format: Customer, Employee, Management, Shareholder, Supplier, Society.
- ✅ **TQM benefits are time-lagged** — typically 18-36 months for visible impact; 3-5 years for full cultural transformation.
- ✅ The benefit-cost equation: $S_{net} = (C_{Old,Failure} - C_{New,Failure}) - C_{TQM-Investment}$ — must be positive.
- ✅ **PDCA cycle** multiplies benefits: $B_{cumulative} = B_0 \times M^{n_{cycles}}$ — explains why TQM compounds over time.
- ✅ **DPMO reduction** is a key metric: from 5000 → 3000 represents a **2.7σ improvement**.
- ✅ **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** in most firms is **15-40% of revenue** — even small TQM gains yield large savings.
- ✅ TQM is a **journey, not a destination** — benefits accrue cumulatively, not instantly.
- ✅ The customer is the **ultimate beneficiary**, but the benefits cascade to **all six stakeholder groups**.
- ✅ **Six benefits must be remembered:** Lower cost, higher quality, faster delivery, better morale, stronger brand, sustainable profits.
- ✅ **For 14-mark questions:** Use a table for stakeholder benefits, a separate section for tangible/intangible, and a concluding synthesis paragraph on long-term sustainability.
- ✅ **For 3-mark questions:** A clear definition + 3 distinct points + 1 example = full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Taguchi Quality Loss Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Taguchi Quality Loss Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Taguchi Quality Loss Function (QLF)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taguchi's Quality Loss Function (QLF)** is a mathematical model proposed by **Genichi Taguchi** that quantifies the financial and societal loss incurred when a product's quality characteristic deviates from its **target value (m)**. The loss is not binary (i.e., "pass/fail" or "within/outside specification"); rather, **every deviation from the target, however small, causes a loss to the customer and to society**.

The general quadratic form of the loss function is:

$$L(x) = k(x - m)^2$$

Where:
- $L(x)$ = Quality Loss (in monetary units, e.g., ₹ or $)
- $k$ = Quality Loss Coefficient (a constant unique to the product/process)
- $x$ = Actual measured value of the quality characteristic
- $m$ = Target value (the ideal, loss-free value)

> [!NOTE]
> **Paradigm Shift:** Traditional Western quality thinking says: *"If it's within specification, there is no loss."* Taguchi's revolutionary view says: *"The customer always experiences some loss — even within the specification band — proportional to the square of the deviation from target."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Real-World Analogy — The Car's Tyre Pressure** 🚗
Imagine a car manufacturer specifies the **ideal tyre pressure as 32 PSI (target m = 32)**. The traditional specification might allow anything from **30 to 34 PSI** (tolerance ±2).

| Tyre Pressure (x) | Traditional QC View | Taguchi's View (Customer Reality) |
|---|---|---|
| 32 PSI (target) | OK | **Zero loss** (best fuel economy, even wear) |
| 33 PSI | OK | Small loss (slightly uneven wear) |
| 30 PSI (LSL) | Barely OK | High loss (tire wear increases, mileage drops) |
| 28 PSI (out of spec) | Reject | Very high loss (safety risk, warranty claim) |

**The Insight:** A driver whose tyres are at 30 PSI suffers a *real* loss (shorter tyre life, more fuel cost) even though the car "passed" inspection. Taguchi formalised this hidden cost.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Target Value (m):** The nominal/ideal value of a quality characteristic that minimises loss.
- **Functional Limit (Δ₀):** The deviation at which the product becomes functionally useless or causes customer dissatisfaction.
- **Loss at Functional Limit (A₀):** The cost (in money) incurred by the customer when the product reaches the functional limit.
- **Quality Loss Coefficient (k):** A proportionality constant linking squared deviation to monetary loss.
- **Specification Limits (USL, LSL):** Customer-defined acceptable bounds (different from functional limits in Taguchi's philosophy).
- **Signal-to-Noise (S/N) Ratio:** A logarithmic measure used by Taguchi to design robust products.
- **Robust Design:** Engineering a product so that its performance is insensitive to "noise" (variation in usage/environment).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Philosophical Foundations — Loss to Society

Taguchi redefined quality as **"the loss a product causes to society after it is shipped, other than any losses caused by its intrinsic functions."** This loss has three components:

1. **Loss to the Manufacturer** (rework, scrap, warranty claims, reputation damage)
2. **Loss to the Customer** (poor performance, early failure, maintenance costs)
3. **Loss to Society** (environmental damage, resource wastage, disposal costs)

> [!NOTE]
> Taguchi's view is **loss-based**, not specification-based. A product that "meets spec" can still cause massive loss if its target value is poorly chosen.

## 2.2 The Three Quality Characteristics

Taguchi classified quality characteristics into three categories, each requiring a different loss function treatment:

| Type | Description | Target (m) | Loss Increases When | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nominal-the-Best (NB)** | Target value exists; any deviation is bad | m = specified centre | Deviates from m in **either** direction | Voltage of a battery (1.5V), length of a shaft, viscosity of oil |
| **Smaller-the-Better (STB)** | Lower values are always better | m = 0 (theoretical) | Value **increases** above 0 | Defects per million (DPMO), pollution, leakage |
| **Larger-the-Better (LTB)** | Higher values are always better | m = ∞ (theoretical) | Value **decreases** below ∞ | Strength, reliability, fuel efficiency, yield strength |

## 2.3 Derivation of the Quality Loss Coefficient (k)

The coefficient **k** is determined using the **functional limit approach**:

- Step 1: Estimate the cost **A₀** (in monetary units) that the customer suffers when the product reaches the functional limit.
- Step 2: Identify the functional tolerance **Δ₀** (the deviation from target at the functional limit).
- Step 3: Apply the boundary condition $L(\Delta_0) = A_0$:

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}$$

This is the most frequently tested numerical in KTU university exams.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table** — it covers 90% of the numerical problems asked in KTU ESE for this topic.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Use Case | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Quadratic Loss Function (NB)** | $L(x) = k(x-m)^2$ | Symmetric deviation around target | ₹ (or $) |
| 2 | **Quality Loss Coefficient** | $k = A_0 / \Delta_0^2$ | Calculate k from known cost at functional limit | ₹ per unit² |
| 3 | **Expected Loss (mean & variance)** | $E[L] = k(\sigma^2 + (\mu - m)^2)$ | When output varies with mean μ and std dev σ | ₹ |
| 4 | **S/N Ratio — Nominal-the-Best** | $\eta = 10 \log_{10}(\mu^2 / \sigma^2)$ dB | Maximise robustness around target | decibels (dB) |
| 5 | **S/N Ratio — Smaller-the-Best** | $\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right)$ dB | Minimise undesirable effect | dB |
| 6 | **S/N Ratio — Larger-the-Best** | $\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum \frac{1}{y_i^2}\right)$ dB | Maximise desirable effect | dB |
| 7 | **Loss from tolerance (simple)** | $L = k \cdot \Delta^2$ | Loss at deviation Δ from target | ₹ |
| 8 | **Average Loss over range** | $\bar{L} = \int_{LSL}^{USL} L(x) \, f(x) \, dx$ | Continuous distributions | ₹ |

> [!NOTE]
> Note the use of `\vert` or `\mid` in exam answers when denoting "magnitude of" — KTU examiners reward correct mathematical notation.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of the Quality Loss Function

- **Cost-of-Quality Estimation:** Translates engineering tolerances into rupees.
- **Tolerance Design:** Justifies tighter tolerances by showing the loss reduction outweighs the manufacturing cost.
- **Supplier Evaluation:** Compares suppliers based on S/N ratios rather than just averages.
- **Robust Design (Taguchi Method):** Optimises design to minimise sensitivity to noise factors.
- **Six Sigma & TQM Linkage:** Forms the financial backbone of continuous improvement projects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Quadratic Loss Function

Taguchi started with three axioms for an ideal loss function:

1. $L(m) = 0$ (loss is zero at target)
2. $L(x) \geq 0$ (loss is non-negative)
3. $L(x)$ is **monotonically increasing** as $\vert x - m \vert$ increases.

The simplest mathematical form satisfying these (with smooth differentiability and symmetry) is the **quadratic** function. We can derive it via Taylor's expansion around the target:

$$L(x) = L(m) + L'(m)(x - m) + \frac{L''(m)}{2!}(x - m)^2 + \ldots$$

Applying the axioms:

- $L(m) = 0$ (eliminates the constant term)
- $L'(m) = 0$ (minimum at the target — first derivative is zero)
- Define $k = L''(m)/2$ (the constant second derivative, scaled)

Therefore, the higher-order terms (cubic, quartic) are ignored to obtain the simplest acceptable form:

$$L(x) = k(x - m)^2$$

## 3.2 Expected Loss with Mean and Variance

When multiple units are produced with mean **μ** and standard deviation **σ**, the expected (average) loss is derived as:

$$E[L(x)] = E[k(x - m)^2] = k \cdot E[(x - m)^2]$$

Expanding $(x - m)^2 = (x - \mu + \mu - m)^2 = (x - \mu)^2 + 2(x - \mu)(\mu - m) + (\mu - m)^2$.

Taking expectations:

$$E[(x - m)^2] = E[(x - \mu)^2] + 2(\mu - m) \cdot E[x - \mu] + (\mu - m)^2$$

Since $E[(x - \mu)^2] = \sigma^2$ and $E[x - \mu] = 0$:

$$E[L(x)] = k[\sigma^2 + (\mu - m)^2]$$

This is the **two-component loss** — Taguchi's proof that **both variation and off-target mean contribute to loss**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

### Worked Example 1 — Finding k from Functional Limit Data

> **Problem:** A manufacturer of electrical resistors determines that a deviation of 0.5 Ω from the target value of 100 Ω causes a functional failure resulting in a customer loss of **₹ 400**. Determine the quality loss coefficient **k**.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Given:
- Target value: $m = 100 \, \Omega$
- Functional limit deviation: $\Delta_0 = 0.5 \, \Omega$
- Customer loss at functional limit: $A_0 = \text{₹ } 400$

Using the boundary condition $L(\Delta_0) = A_0$:

$$L(\Delta_0) = k \cdot \Delta_0^2 = A_0$$

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} = \frac{400}{(0.5)^2} = \frac{400}{0.25}$$

$$\boxed{k = 1600 \, \text{₹ per } \Omega^2}$$

### Worked Example 2 — Calculating Loss for a Given Deviation

> **Problem:** Using the value of k from Example 1, calculate the quality loss for a resistor with an actual resistance of (a) 100.2 Ω, (b) 99.5 Ω, and (c) 101.0 Ω.

**Solution Framework:**
$$L(x) = 1600 \cdot (x - 100)^2$$

**(a)** For $x = 100.2 \, \Omega$:

$$L = 1600 \cdot (100.2 - 100)^2 = 1600 \cdot (0.2)^2 = 1600 \cdot 0.04 = \text{₹ } 64$$

**(b)** For $x = 99.5 \, \Omega$:

$$L = 1600 \cdot (99.5 - 100)^2 = 1600 \cdot (-0.5)^2 = 1600 \cdot 0.25 = \text{₹ } 400$$

**(c)** For $x = 101.0 \, \Omega$:

$$L = 1600 \cdot (101.0 - 100)^2 = 1600 \cdot (1.0)^2 = 1600 \cdot 1.0 = \text{₹ } 1600$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Note that even a tiny deviation (0.2 Ω) incurs a loss — the loss is never zero unless the product is exactly on target. This is Taguchi's central message.

### Worked Example 3 — Expected Loss with Mean and Variance

> **Problem:** A process produces components with mean μ = 100.05 Ω and standard deviation σ = 0.1 Ω. Target m = 100 Ω and k = 1600 ₹/Ω². Calculate the expected quality loss.

**Solution:**

$$E[L] = k \cdot [\sigma^2 + (\mu - m)^2]$$

$$E[L] = 1600 \cdot [(0.1)^2 + (100.05 - 100)^2]$$

$$E[L] = 1600 \cdot [0.01 + (0.05)^2] = 1600 \cdot [0.01 + 0.0025]$$

$$E[L] = 1600 \cdot 0.0125 = \text{₹ } 20$$

### Worked Example 4 — S/N Ratio (Nominal-the-Best)

> **Problem:** A process yields measurements with mean μ = 50.2 and standard deviation σ = 1.1. Calculate the S/N ratio (in dB) using the Nominal-the-Best formula.

**Solution:**

$$\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\mu^2}{\sigma^2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{(50.2)^2}{(1.1)^2}\right)$$

$$= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2520.04}{1.21}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(2082.68)$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 33.19 \, \text{dB}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A **higher S/N ratio** is always better — it means the signal (target) dominates the noise (variation).

### Worked Example 5 — S/N Ratio (Smaller-the-Best)

> **Problem:** Five measurements of contamination levels are: 2.1, 2.4, 1.9, 2.3, 2.0 (ppm). Compute S/N ratio for "Smaller-the-Best" characteristic.

**Solution:**

First, compute the sum of squares:

$$\sum y_i^2 = (2.1)^2 + (2.4)^2 + (1.9)^2 + (2.3)^2 + (2.0)^2$$

$$= 4.41 + 5.76 + 3.61 + 5.29 + 4.00 = 23.07$$

Mean of squares:

$$\frac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2 = \frac{23.07}{5} = 4.614$$

Apply formula:

$$\eta = -10 \log_{10}(4.614) = -10 \times 0.6641$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx -6.64 \, \text{dB}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For STB, the S/N ratio is **always negative** (because it is the negative log of a positive quantity). Less negative = better.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Quality Loss Function Framework

The following block diagram illustrates the input–process–output flow of Taguchi's QLF methodology, mapping how engineering data is converted into monetary loss and quality improvement decisions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["STEP 1 DEFINE TARGET\nIdentify Nominal Value m\nQuality Characteristic Type"] --> B["STEP 2 ESTIMATE A0\nCustomer Cost at\nFunctional Failure Point"]
    B --> C["STEP 3 DETERMINE DELTA0\nFunctional Tolerance\nfrom Engineering Limits"]
    C --> D["STEP 4 CALCULATE k\nApply k equals A0 over Delta0 squared"]
    D --> E["STEP 5 COMPUTE LOSS\nL of x equals k times x minus m squared"]
    E --> F["STEP 6 MEASURE PROCESS\nSample mean mu and std dev sigma\nfrom production data"]
    F --> G["STEP 7 EXPECTED LOSS\nE of L equals k times sigma squared plus mu minus m squared"]
    G --> H["STEP 8 S/N RATIO\nCalculate Signal to Noise\nfor Robustness Evaluation"]
    H --> I["STEP 9 TOLERANCE DESIGN\nOptimize Specs to\nMinimize Total Loss"]
    I --> J["OUTPUT CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT\nReduced Loss to Society"]
    A -.-> A1["Quality Type: NB or STB or LTB"]
    A1 -.-> A
    G -.-> K["DECISION POINT\nIs Loss Acceptable"]
    K -->|NO| L["REDESIGN PROCESS"]
    L --> A
    K -->|YES| J
```

**How to read this diagram (for KTU viva):**
- Each block represents a **sequential stage** in applying QLF.
- The **dashed arrows** indicate feedback loops — QLF is iterative.
- The **Decision Point (K)** is the heart of robust design: if loss is unacceptable, return to Step 1 and re-engineer the process.

## 4.2 Visual Representation of the Quadratic Loss Curve

The following is a **text-based schematic** of the parabola $L(x) = k(x - m)^2$, which is the visual signature of the QLF concept often asked in viva:

```
QUALITY LOSS (L)
     ^
     \vert
     |               *           <-- L (USL) = k(USL - m)^2
     |             *     *
     |           *         *       <-- Loss grows quadratically
     |          *           *
 L ->|         *             *      <-- Specification band
     |        *               *        (still incurs loss!)
     |       *                 *
     |     *     *          *    *   <-- L (LSL) = k(LSL - m)^2
     |   *         *      *       *
     | *             *  *          *
     |*                *            *
     |________________________________-> x
                   ^        ^      ^
                 LSL      m      USL
                            (TARGET = ZERO LOSS)
```

**Reading the curve:**
- The **lowest point** of the parabola touches the x-axis at the target **m** → zero loss.
- The curve is **symmetric** about the target → deviation in either direction causes equal loss (for Nominal-the-Best).
- **Loss increases with the SQUARE of the deviation** — not linearly. Hence, a deviation of 2 units costs **4 times** the loss of a 1-unit deviation.

## 4.3 Comparison: Traditional vs. Taguchi View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAD["TRADITIONAL QUALITY VIEW"]
        T1["Within Spec = OK\nNo Loss"] --> T2["Outside Spec = REJECT\nTotal Loss"]
    end
    subgraph TAGU["TAGUCHI QUALITY VIEW"]
        Q1["At Target = Zero Loss"] --> Q2["Slight Deviation = Small Loss\nQuadratic Growth"]
        Q2 --> Q3["At Functional Limit = Customer Cost A0"]
    end
    TRAD -.CONTRAST.-> TAGU
```

> [!NOTE]
> The right-hand parabola is the **continuous** loss function — even "in-spec" products carry hidden cost. The left-hand step function represents the obsolete "conformance-only" view.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*"Define Taguchi's Quality Loss Function. Explain how it differs from the traditional 'specification-based' view of quality."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** The Quality Loss Function (QLF) is a quadratic mathematical model, $L(x) = k(x - m)^2$, that quantifies the monetary loss to society as a function of deviation of a quality characteristic from its target value.
- (1 mark) **Key difference:** Traditional QC treats quality as a binary condition (within spec = no loss; outside spec = total loss). QLF views quality as **continuous** — any deviation, however small, causes a quantifiable loss.
- (1 mark) **Implication:** QLF encourages manufacturers to target the nominal value and minimise variation, even if the product is well within specification limits.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*"Explain the three categories of quality characteristics in Taguchi's methodology with one industrial example each."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 mark) **Nominal-the-Best (NB):** A specific target value exists; deviation in either direction increases loss. *Example: Diameter of a piston (50 mm ± 0.01 mm).*
- (1 mark) **Smaller-the-Better (STB):** Lower values are always preferred; the ideal target is zero. *Example: Defects per million opportunities (DPMO) in a PCB.*
- (1 mark) **Larger-the-Better (LTB):** Higher values are always preferred; ideal target is infinity. *Example: Tensile strength of a steel rod, or battery life in hours.*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students write $L(x) = k(x - m)$ (linear) instead of $L(x) = k(x - m)^2$ (quadratic). This is a **2-mark deduction trap**. Always square the deviation. Also, units of $k$ must include the squared dimension in the denominator (e.g., ₹/mm²), not just ₹.

---

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the Taguchi Quality Loss Function in its quadratic form. State and justify the axioms used. (7 Marks)

**(b)** An automotive cylinder bore has a target diameter of **80.00 mm**. The functional limit of deviation is **0.4 mm**, and the customer's loss when the product reaches this functional limit is estimated at **₹ 2,000**.
  - (i) Calculate the quality loss coefficient **k**.
  - (ii) Determine the loss for an actual diameter of **79.85 mm**.
  - (iii) Find the expected loss if a batch has mean μ = 80.02 mm and standard deviation σ = 0.05 mm.
  (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to Question Choice A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — State the axioms (2 Marks):**
- Axiom 1: Loss $L(m) = 0$ at the target value $m$.
- Axiom 2: Loss $L(x) \geq 0$ for all $x$ (non-negative).
- Axiom 3: Loss is a monotonically increasing function of $\vert x - m \vert$.

**Step 2 — Taylor Series Expansion around the target (2 Marks):**

$$L(x) = L(m) + L'(m)(x - m) + \frac{L''(m)}{2!}(x - m)^2 + \frac{L'''(m)}{3!}(x - m)^3 + \ldots$$

**Step 3 — Apply axioms and simplify (2 Marks):**
- Since $L(m) = 0$, the constant term vanishes.
- Since $m$ is a minimum, $L'(m) = 0$, eliminating the linear term.
- For the simplest acceptable form, we truncate after the second-order term and define:

$$k = \frac{L''(m)}{2}$$

Thus we obtain the **quadratic loss function**:

$$\boxed{L(x) = k(x - m)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Justification (1 Mark):**
- Quadratic form is the lowest-order polynomial satisfying all three axioms.
- It is differentiable, symmetric, and computationally simple — suitable for engineering applications.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $m = 80.00 \, \text{mm}$, $\Delta_0 = 0.4 \, \text{mm}$, $A_0 = \text{₹ } 2000$, $x = 79.85 \, \text{mm}$, $\mu = 80.02 \, \text{mm}$, $\sigma = 0.05 \, \text{mm}$.

**(i) Calculate k (2 Marks):**

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} = \frac{2000}{(0.4)^2} = \frac{2000}{0.16}$$

$$\boxed{k = 12{,}500 \, \text{₹ per mm}^2}$$

**(ii) Loss at x = 79.85 mm (2 Marks):**

$$L(79.85) = 12{,}500 \times (79.85 - 80.00)^2 = 12{,}500 \times (-0.15)^2$$

$$= 12{,}500 \times 0.0225 = \text{₹ } 281.25$$

**(iii) Expected Loss (3 Marks):**

$$E[L] = k \cdot [\sigma^2 + (\mu - m)^2]$$

$$= 12{,}500 \times [(0.05)^2 + (80.02 - 80.00)^2]$$

$$= 12{,}500 \times [0.0025 + 0.0004]$$

$$= 12{,}500 \times 0.0029$$

$$\boxed{E[L] = \text{₹ } 36.25}$$

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 4 Alternate]**

**(a)** Explain the three types of quality characteristics (Nominal-the-Best, Smaller-the-Best, Larger-the-Best) and write the corresponding Taguchi Signal-to-Noise (S/N) ratio formulas. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A bearing manufacturer measures the surface roughness (Ra) of five bearings and records the following values: **0.42, 0.38, 0.45, 0.40, 0.41 μm**. Lower roughness is better.
  - (i) State the S/N ratio formula to be used.
  - (ii) Calculate the S/N ratio in dB.
  - (iii) If the process is improved to give readings **0.32, 0.30, 0.31, 0.29, 0.33 μm**, compute the new S/N ratio. Comment on which process is better.
  (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to Question Choice B

**Part (a) — Explanation & Formulas (7 Marks):**

| Type | Target | Loss Characteristic | S/N Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nominal-the-Best (NB)** | Fixed value m | Loss on either side of m | $\eta = 10 \log_{10}(\mu^2 / \sigma^2)$ |
| **Smaller-the-Best (STB)** | 0 (theoretical) | Loss grows as value increases | $\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right)$ |
| **Larger-the-Best (LTB)** | ∞ (theoretical) | Loss grows as value decreases | $\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum \frac{1}{y_i^2}\right)$ |

- (3 Marks) Description of each type with one example.
- (3 Marks) Correct formulas with notation.
- (1 Mark) Statement: *Higher S/N ratio always indicates a more robust product.*

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**(i) Formula to be used (1 Mark):**
Since lower roughness is better, this is a **Smaller-the-Best (STB)** characteristic:

$$\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right)$$

**(ii) S/N Ratio for Original Process (3 Marks):**

Compute the sum of squares:

$$\sum y_i^2 = (0.42)^2 + (0.38)^2 + (0.45)^2 + (0.40)^2 + (0.41)^2$$

$$= 0.1764 + 0.1444 + 0.2025 + 0.1600 + 0.1681 = 0.8514$$

Mean:

$$\frac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2 = \frac{0.8514}{5} = 0.17028$$

Apply S/N formula:

$$\eta_1 = -10 \log_{10}(0.17028) = -10 \times (-0.7687)$$

$$\boxed{\eta_1 \approx 7.69 \, \text{dB}}$$

**(iii) S/N Ratio for Improved Process (3 Marks):**

$$\sum y_i^2 = (0.32)^2 + (0.30)^2 + (0.31)^2 + (0.29)^2 + (0.33)^2$$

$$= 0.1024 + 0.0900 + 0.0961 + 0.0841 + 0.1089 = 0.4815$$

Mean:

$$\frac{1}{5} \times 0.4815 = 0.09630$$

Apply S/N formula:

$$\eta_2 = -10 \log_{10}(0.09630) = -10 \times (-1.0164)$$

$$\boxed{\eta_2 \approx 10.16 \, \text{dB}}$$

**Comment:** Since $\eta_2 (10.16 \, \text{dB}) > \eta_1 (7.69 \, \text{dB})$, the **improved process is better** — it has a higher S/N ratio, indicating less variability around a smaller (better) mean value.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in STB and LTB S/N formulas — this gives a wrong sign and can cost **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing the type of characteristic** — if you mistakenly use the NB formula for a STB problem, the entire calculation is invalidated.
> 3. **Not commenting on the comparison** — KTU examiners allocate **1 mark** explicitly for the conclusion/interpretation in comparison-based questions.
> 4. **Rounding errors** — always retain **at least 4 decimal places** in intermediate steps.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ The **QLF** is: $L(x) = k(x - m)^2$ — a **quadratic** (not linear) function.
- ✅ **k** is calculated as: $k = A_0 / \Delta_0^2$ — using customer loss at the functional limit.
- ✅ **Three characteristic types:** NB (target m), STB (target 0), LTB (target ∞).
- ✅ **Expected Loss** when output varies: $E[L] = k[\sigma^2 + (\mu - m)^2]$ — both variation AND off-target mean contribute.
- ✅ **S/N Ratio (NB):** $\eta = 10 \log_{10}(\mu^2 / \sigma^2)$ — always positive, higher is better.
- ✅ **S/N Ratio (STB):** $\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right)$ — always negative, less negative is better.
- ✅ **S/N Ratio (LTB):** $\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum 1/y_i^2\right)$ — always negative, less negative is better.
- ✅ **Units of k** must be **₹ per (unit²)** — e.g., ₹/mm², ₹/Ω², ₹/V².
- ✅ **Taguchi's philosophy** = Loss to Society, not just Loss to Manufacturer.
- ✅ **Quadratic growth** — a 2× deviation gives a 4× loss; a 3× deviation gives a 9× loss.
- ✅ **Tolerance design rule:** Reduce $\sigma$ first (process improvement), then move μ → m (mean adjustment). Both reduce loss, but variance reduction has a larger effect when $\sigma > \vert \mu - m \vert$.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** Tolerance is NOT the same as functional limit. Tolerance is a manufacturing target; functional limit is a customer-failure boundary.
- ✅ **KTU Frequently Tested:** Calculating k from $A_0$ and $\Delta_0$ (3–4 marks almost every semester).

**Master these 12 points and you will comfortably score full marks on any Taguchi QLF question in the KTU 2024 Scheme examination.** 📚
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) - Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) - Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** is defined as *productive maintenance carried out by all employees through small group activities, with the active involvement of top management, aimed at achieving zero breakdowns, zero defects, and zero accidents by maximizing the Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) of production systems.*

The term was coined and formalized by **Seiichi Nakajima** in 1971 at **Nippondenso** (a Toyota group company), evolving the older American concept of *Preventive Maintenance* into a holistic, organization-wide philosophy. The Japan Institute of Plant Maintenance (JIPM) institutionalized it and established the **TPM Excellence Award** in **1982**.

**Three Tense Phrase Interpretation of TPM:**
- **Total** → *Total participation* (all departments, all levels, all employees)
- **Productive** → *Productive output* (zero loss, zero waste, full efficiency)
- **Maintenance** → *Maintenance of equipment* (upkeep of production assets in perfect condition)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Family Car System**
> 
> Imagine you own a family car. If you ignore oil changes, tire pressure, and strange noises, the car will break down on a highway, causing huge inconvenience and expense. 
> 
> - **Traditional Maintenance** = A mechanic fixes the car only when it breaks down (**Breakdown Maintenance**).
> - **Preventive Maintenance** = You take the car to a service center every 6 months, whether it has a problem or not.
> - **TPM** = The driver (operator) himself checks oil daily, cleans the engine, monitors tire pressure, and reports minor issues. The mechanic handles the deeper technical work. **Both share responsibility.** Result: The car never breaks down, runs like new, has zero accidents, and lasts decades.
> 
> **The car is the "machine," the family is the "organization," and the driver-mechanic partnership is the essence of TPM.**

## 1.3 Core Objectives & Guiding Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Three Zeros" of TPM (Foundational Mantra of JIPM):**
> 
> 1. **Zero Breakdowns** — No unplanned machine stoppages
> 2. **Zero Defects** — No defective products produced
> 3. **Zero Accidents** — No workplace injuries or safety incidents
> 
> All activities in TPM converge towards achieving these three zeros.

**Supporting Quantitative Goal:**
$$\text{World-Class OEE Target} \ge \mathbf{85\%}$$
A *good* industry benchmark is **85%**; TPM aims to push organizations towards this benchmark as a baseline, with ideal targets approaching **95%+**.

## 1.4 Distinguishing TPM from TQM

| Aspect | TQM (Total Quality Management) | TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Focus** | Quality of products and services | Effectiveness of equipment/machinery |
| **Origin** | USA (Deming, Juran, Crosby) | Japan (Nakajima, 1971) |
| **Core Metric** | Customer satisfaction, defect rate | Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) |
| **Key Methodology** | PDCA, Six Sigma, Kaizen | 8 Pillars, 5S, OEE Analysis |
| **End Goal** | Zero customer complaints | Zero breakdowns, zero defects, zero accidents |
| **Synergy** | **Both require total employee participation and continuous improvement (Kaizen)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** TPM is often considered a *subsystem* or *pillar* of the broader TQM framework, but is treated as an independent philosophy in management studies because of its specialized focus on equipment and plant maintenance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Evolution of Maintenance Philosophy (Why TPM Exists)

Maintenance thinking evolved through five generations:

1. **Breakdown Maintenance (BM)** — Fix only when it breaks. (Pre-1950s, reactive)
2. **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** — Time-based scheduled servicing. (1950s, US origin)
3. **Predictive/Condition-Based Maintenance (PdM/CBM)** — Service based on actual condition (vibration, temperature sensors). (1970s)
4. **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)** — Risk-based maintenance strategy. (1970s-80s)
5. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** — Holistic, employee-driven, life-cycle approach. (1971 onwards)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** Questions often ask *"How is TPM different from Preventive Maintenance?"*
> **Answer:** PM is *time-based* and *technician-driven*. TPM is *condition-based, employee-driven, and total* (covering the entire equipment life cycle and involving everyone in the organization).

## 2.2 The 8 Pillars of TPM (JIPM Framework)

The TPM architecture is built on **8 interconnected pillars**. Each pillar supports the three zeros.

### **Pillar 1: 5S — The Foundation**
The five Japanese words beginning with 'S' that create a clean, organized, disciplined workplace — a prerequisite for any TPM activity.

| Step | Japanese | English | Meaning |
|------|----------|---------|---------|
| 1 | **Seiri** | Sort | Remove all unnecessary items from the workplace |
| 2 | **Seiton** | Set in Order | Arrange needed items so they are easy to find and use |
| 3 | **Seiso** | Shine | Clean the workplace thoroughly; no dust, no oil leaks |
| 4 | **Seiketsu** | Standardize | Establish procedures and standards for the first 3S |
| 5 | **Shitsuke** | Sustain | Train and discipline workers to follow 5S consistently |

### **Pillar 2: Jishu Hozen (Autonomous Maintenance)**
Operators take daily care of their own machines — cleaning, lubrication, tightening, inspection — instead of relying entirely on the maintenance department. *"The operator is the owner of the machine."*

### **Pillar 3: Keikaku Hozen (Planned Maintenance)**
A systematic, schedule-based program combining **Preventive Maintenance** and **Predictive Maintenance** to avoid breakdowns, with activities planned in 6 categories: daily checkup, periodic checkup, lubrication, tightening, replacement, and improvement.

### **Pillar 4: Quality Maintenance (Hinshitsu Hozen)**
Applying TPM principles to achieve *zero defects*. Focus is on **condition monitoring** to detect the source of defects before they occur (e.g., machine wear affecting dimensional accuracy).

### **Pillar 5: Kobetsu Kaizen (Focused Improvement)**
Cross-functional small-group activities (Quality Circles) targeting specific losses and inefficiencies identified through OEE analysis. Uses the classic **PDCA** cycle and statistical tools (7 QC tools).

### **Pillar 6: Education and Training**
Operators and maintenance staff are trained to develop multi-skill capabilities. The principle: *"Don't operate a machine you don't understand."* Training matrix is used to record skill levels.

### **Pillar 7: Office TPM**
Extends TPM to administrative and support functions (HR, finance, marketing, sales). The goal is to eliminate losses in *information flow* and *administrative processes* that indirectly affect production.

### **Pillar 8: Safety, Health, and Environment (SHE)**
A cross-functional pillar ensuring **zero accidents** by creating a safe working environment. Often considered a *horizontal pillar* because it is woven through all other 7 pillars.

## 2.3 Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) — The Core Metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OEE is the single most important quantitative metric in TPM.** It quantifies how effectively a manufacturing operation is utilized.

$$\boxed{\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}}$$

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula Component | Definition | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Availability** | Fraction of *Planned Production Time* that the equipment is actually running | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ |
| **Performance** | How close actual output is to theoretical maximum at ideal cycle time | $\text{Performance} = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}}$ |
| **Quality** | Fraction of pieces produced that are good (sellable) | $\text{Quality} = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}$ |
| **Planned Production Time** | Total scheduled time minus planned downtime (breaks, planned maintenance) | $\text{PPT} = \text{Shift Time} - \text{Planned Stops}$ |
| **Operating Time** | Planned Production Time minus Unplanned Downtime (breakdowns, setup) | $\text{OT} = \text{PPT} - \text{Downtime}$ |
| **Six Big Losses** | The 6 categories of waste that OEE captures (see below) | Categorized into A, P, Q groups |

### The 6 Big Losses (Mapped to OEE Components)

| OEE Factor | Loss Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Availability** | 1. Equipment Failure / Breakdown Losses | Sudden stops, unplanned breakdowns |
| **Availability** | 2. Setup and Adjustment Losses | Time lost during changeovers, tooling changes |
| **Performance** | 3. Idling and Minor Stoppages | Brief halts (sensor trips, jams, blocked feeds) |
| **Performance** | 4. Reduced Speed Losses | Machine running below design speed |
| **Quality** | 5. Defect / Rework Losses | Production of defective items during steady-state |
| **Quality** | 6. Startup / Yield Losses | Defective items during the warm-up/ramp-up phase |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Plants:** Continuous-process industries (automotive, FMCG, semiconductors) use TPM to maximize expensive capital equipment uptime.
- **Aerospace & Defense:** TPM-derived reliability principles reduce Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF).
- **Hospitals & Data Centers:** Office TPM and predictive techniques borrowed from TPM keep critical equipment (MRI machines, servers) at peak availability.
- **Cost Impact:** Industry studies show that *unplanned downtime* can cost **$10,000–$250,000 per hour** in heavy industry. TPM directly attacks this cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section builds the OEE formula from first principles and provides a complete worked-out numerical example, plus the analytical breakdown of the **12 Steps of TPM Implementation** (JIPM model).

## 3.1 Derivation of the OEE Formula

### Step 1 — Start with Total Calendar Time
$$\text{Shift Time} = T_{\text{shift}}$$
This is the *gross* time the factory is open.

### Step 2 — Subtract Planned Stops (Breaks, Lunch, Scheduled Maintenance)
$$T_{\text{PPT}} = T_{\text{shift}} - T_{\text{planned stops}}$$
This is the *Planned Production Time* — the time the equipment is *supposed* to run.

### Step 3 — Compute Availability by Subtracting Unplanned Downtime
$$T_{\text{OT}} = T_{\text{PPT}} - T_{\text{downtime (breakdowns + setup)}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Availability} = \dfrac{T_{\text{OT}}}{T_{\text{PPT}}}}$$

### Step 4 — Define Ideal Cycle Time
The ideal cycle time ($T_{\text{ideal}}$) is the *fastest* time the machine can produce one good piece when running at *design speed* with *no stoppages*. It is set by the equipment manufacturer.

### Step 5 — Compute Performance Ratio
The theoretical maximum number of pieces that *could* be produced in the operating time is $T_{\text{OT}} / T_{\text{ideal}}$. The *actual* number of pieces produced is $N_{\text{total}}$.

$$\boxed{\text{Performance} = \dfrac{T_{\text{ideal}} \times N_{\text{total}}}{T_{\text{OT}}}}$$

### Step 6 — Compute Quality Ratio
Some of the $N_{\text{total}}$ pieces are defective. Let $N_{\text{good}}$ = pieces meeting quality specifications.

$$\boxed{\text{Quality} = \dfrac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}}}$$

### Step 7 — Multiply the Three Ratios
$$\boxed{\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}}$$

Substituting the full forms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} &= \dfrac{T_{\text{OT}}}{T_{\text{PPT}}} \times \dfrac{T_{\text{ideal}} \times N_{\text{total}}}{T_{\text{OT}}} \times \dfrac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \\
&= \dfrac{N_{\text{good}}}{T_{\text{PPT}} \times \left(\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{ideal}}}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Notice that $T_{\text{OT}}$ and $N_{\text{total}}$ *cancel out*, leaving OEE as a pure ratio of **actual good pieces produced** to **theoretical maximum good pieces possible** in the planned production time at ideal speed. This makes OEE a **dimensionless percentage** between 0% and 100%.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem Statement (from typical KTU question pattern):**
> 
> A CNC machine operates for an 8-hour shift. The shift has 30 minutes of planned breaks. During the shift, the machine experienced 45 minutes of unplanned breakdown and 15 minutes of setup. Total pieces produced = 360. Ideal cycle time = 1 minute. Defective pieces = 24. Calculate OEE and identify the loss category for each component.

### Given Data
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{shift}} &= 8 \text{ hours} = 480 \text{ min} \\
T_{\text{planned stops}} &= 30 \text{ min} \\
T_{\text{breakdown}} &= 45 \text{ min} \\
T_{\text{setup}} &= 15 \text{ min} \\
N_{\text{total}} &= 360 \text{ pieces} \\
T_{\text{ideal}} &= 1 \text{ min per piece} \\
N_{\text{defective}} &= 24 \text{ pieces}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step A — Planned Production Time
$$T_{\text{PPT}} = 480 - 30 = 450 \text{ min}$$

### Step B — Total Unplanned Downtime
$$T_{\text{downtime}} = 45 + 15 = 60 \text{ min}$$

### Step C — Operating Time
$$T_{\text{OT}} = 450 - 60 = 390 \text{ min}$$

### Step D — Availability
$$\text{Availability} = \dfrac{390}{450} = 0.8667 = \mathbf{86.67\%}$$

### Step E — Performance
$$\text{Performance} = \dfrac{1 \times 360}{390} = \dfrac{360}{390} = 0.9231 = \mathbf{92.31\%}$$

### Step F — Good Pieces
$$N_{\text{good}} = 360 - 24 = 336 \text{ pieces}$$

### Step G — Quality
$$\text{Quality} = \dfrac{336}{360} = 0.9333 = \mathbf{93.33\%}$$

### Step H — Final OEE
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} &= 0.8667 \times 0.9231 \times 0.9333 \\
&= 0.8667 \times 0.8616 \\
&= 0.7467 \\
&= \mathbf{74.67\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step I — Interpretation
- The machine is running at **74.67%** of its theoretical ideal capacity.
- **World-class benchmark** is **≥ 85%**, so this plant has a TPM improvement opportunity of approximately **10.33 percentage points**.
- The biggest loss component here is **Availability** (down 13.33% from 100%), primarily due to breakdowns and setup — typical **Availability losses** in the Six Big Losses framework.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — The 12 Steps of TPM Implementation (JIPM)

The Japan Institute of Plant Maintenance (JIPM) prescribes a **12-step roadmap** for implementing TPM. The table below is the analytical matrix KTU examiners expect students to reproduce.

| Step | Phase | Activity | Purpose |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **1** | Preparation | Top management announces TPM decision publicly | Demonstrates commitment, removes ambiguity |
| **2** | Preparation | Launch TPM campaign — posters, slogans, awareness | Create organization-wide enthusiasm |
| **3** | Preparation | Formulate master plan for TPM roll-out | Set timelines, milestones, KPIs |
| **4** | Introduction | Begin **5S** activities at the workplace | Establish clean, organized foundation |
| **5** | Introduction | Set up **Autonomous Maintenance** (Jishu Hozen) | Train operators to own their machines |
| **6** | Introduction | Set up **Planned Maintenance** (Keikaku Hozen) | Build preventive + predictive systems |
| **7** | Introduction | Conduct operator **Education and Training** | Develop multi-skilled workforce |
| **8** | Implementation | Set up **Early / Life-Cycle Equipment Management** | Design new equipment for maintainability |
| **9** | Implementation | Set up **Quality Maintenance** programs | Achieve zero-defect conditions |
| **10** | Implementation | Set up **Focused Improvement** (Kobetsu Kaizen) | Target-specific loss-reduction projects |
| **11** | Implementation | Set up **Office TPM** | Extend TPM to administrative departments |
| **12** | Mastery | Apply for **TPM Excellence Award** from JIPM | External recognition, continuous motivation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 12 steps?** The 12 steps are designed to be **sequential** — skipping a step (e.g., implementing Focused Improvement before 5S) almost always leads to TPM failure. This is a common KTU 2-mark conceptual question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This Mermaid diagram shows how the 8 pillars converge on the **Three Zeros** goal.

```mermaid
    p7["Pillar 7 Office TPM"]

    p1 --> goal
    p2 --> goal
    p3 --> goal
    p4 --> goal
    p5 --> goal
    p6 --> goal
    p7 --> goal
    p8 --> goal
    
    p1 -.Foundation.-> p2
    p1 -.Foundation.-> p3
    p1 -.Foundation.-> p5
```

## 4.3 OEE Composition Tree — Availability × Performance × Quality

```mermaid
graph TD
```
