| **Procedures** | Step-by-step activity instructions | Detailed recipe procedures |
| **Work Instructions** | Task-level detailed guidance | Specific cooking techniques |
| **Records** | Evidence of performance | Daily sales log |
| **Quality Audit** | Systematic review | Health inspector's visit |
| **Management Review** | Top-level evaluation | Restaurant owner's monthly meeting |

### 1.4 Standard Quality Metrics

- **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** – used in Six Sigma evaluation
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** – percentage of units passing without rework
- **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** – measured via feedback surveys
- **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** – prevention + appraisal + failure costs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **ISO 9000 family** is the most widely implemented quality system framework globally, with over **1 million certifications issued across 170+ countries**, making it the single most important standard for any KTU-level answer on quality systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The ISO 9000 Family of Standards

The ISO 9000 family is a set of international standards developed by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** to help organizations ensure their products and services consistently meet customer and regulatory requirements.

#### 2.1.1 Core Standards in the ISO 9000 Family

- **ISO 9000:2015** – Fundamentals and vocabulary. Establishes the foundational concepts and language of quality management.
- **ISO 9001:2015** – Requirements. This is the **only standard in the family that can be certified against**. Specifies the requirements for a Quality Management System (QMS).
- **ISO 9004:2018** – Guidelines for performance improvements. Provides guidance on achieving sustained success through QMS enhancement.
- **ISO 19011:2018** – Guidelines for auditing management systems. Provides the framework for conducting quality and environmental audits.

#### 2.1.2 Seven Quality Management Principles (ISO 9001:2015)

1. **Customer Focus** – Primary focus is meeting customer needs and exceeding expectations.
2. **Leadership** – Leaders establish unity of purpose and direction.
3. **Engagement of People** – Competent, empowered people at all levels enhance the organization's capability.
4. **Process Approach** – Activities are understood and managed as interrelated processes.
5. **Improvement** – Continual improvement is an essential objective.
6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making** – Decisions are based on analysis of data and information.
7. **Relationship Management** – Relationships with interested parties (suppliers, partners) are managed.

### 2.2 Steps in Implementation of a Quality System

#### 2.2.1 Phase 1: Planning and Preparation

- **Top Management Commitment** – Without executive sponsorship, any QMS initiative fails.
- **Define Scope** – Identify which processes, products, services, and locations are covered.
- **Gap Analysis** – Compare current practices against ISO 9001:2015 requirements to identify gaps.
- **Establish Quality Policy and Objectives** – Set measurable, customer-focused goals.

#### 2.2.2 Phase 2: Documentation

- Prepare the **Quality Manual**.
- Develop **Documented Procedures** (e.g., control of documents, internal audit, control of nonconforming product, corrective action).
- Create **Work Instructions** for operational tasks.
- Define **Records** required for evidence.

#### 2.2.3 Phase 3: Implementation

- Communicate the quality policy to all employees.
- Conduct **Training** on procedures and standards.
- Deploy the system in day-to-day operations.
- Establish **measurement and monitoring** of key processes.

#### 2.2.4 Phase 4: Verification

- Conduct **Internal Quality Audits** to check compliance.
- Perform **Management Review** meetings.
- Take **Corrective Actions** for identified non-conformities.
- Verify effectiveness through follow-up audits.

#### 2.2.5 Phase 5: Continual Improvement

- Use audit results, customer feedback, and performance data to drive improvement.
- Apply the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) Cycle** continuously.
- Pursue third-party **Certification Audit** if external recognition is required.

### 2.3 Quality Manual Structure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Quality Manual** is the master document of the QMS. While ISO 9001:2015 does not mandate a specific format, a typical KTU-expected Quality Manual structure includes:

1. **Title page and approval signatures**
2. **Table of contents**
3. **Scope of the QMS**
4. **Exclusions and justifications (if any)**
5. **Quality Policy**
6. **Quality Objectives**
7. **Description of the organization and its context**
8. **Process map and interaction**
9. **Reference to documented procedures**
10. **Reference to supporting documents**

### 2.4 Quality Audit (Detailed)

A **Quality Audit** is a systematic, independent, and documented process for obtaining objective evidence and evaluating it objectively to determine the extent to which audit criteria are fulfilled.

#### 2.4.1 Types of Quality Audits

| Type | Conducted By | Purpose | Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Internal Audit (First-Party)** | Organization's own trained auditors | Verify own QMS compliance | At least once per year |
| **Supplier Audit (Second-Party)** | Customer or buyer's auditors | Evaluate supplier's capability | Before awarding contracts |
| **Certification/Registration Audit (Third-Party)** | External certification body (e.g., TUV, BIS, BSI) | Grant/withhold ISO certification | Every 3 years (with surveillance audits annually) |

#### 2.4.2 Audit Process

- **Stage 1 Audit (Documentation Review)** – Auditors review the QMS documentation for adequacy.
- **Stage 2 Audit (On-site Audit)** – Auditors verify implementation through interviews, observation, and records review.
- **Non-Conformity Reports (NCR)** – Issued for any deviation; classified as **Major** (system absence) or **Minor** (isolated incident).
- **Corrective Action** – Organization must address root cause within an agreed timeframe.
- **Certification Decision** – Awarded only when all major NCRs are closed.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **DPMO (Six Sigma)** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Opportunities} \times \text{Number of Units}} \times 10^6$ | Defects per million |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units passed first time}}{\text{Total units started}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \text{FPY}_1 \times \text{FPY}_2 \times \dots \times \text{FPY}_n$ | Decimal or Percentage |
| **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $\text{CoQ} = \text{Prevention Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost}$ | Currency units |
| **Sigma Level** | $\sigma = \dfrac{\text{DPMO conversion}}{1}$ | Sigma points (3.4 DPMO = 6σ) |
| **Customer Satisfaction Score** | $\text{CSAT} = \dfrac{\text{Sum of all ratings}}{\text{Maximum possible rating}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |

> [!WARNING]
> For KTU answers, always state that **ISO 9001:2015 introduced risk-based thinking** and replaced the previous 2008 version's "Preventive Action" requirement with a focus on **risks and opportunities** in Clause 6.1.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Manufacturing Engineering** – ISO 9001 certification is often a pre-requisite for supplying parts to automotive, aerospace, and defense industries.
- **Software Engineering** – ISO 9001 combined with **CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration)** ensures process maturity in software development organizations.
- **Construction Engineering** – Quality systems ensure structural safety, regulatory compliance, and reduced rework on-site.
- **Service Industries** – Banks, hospitals, and IT service providers use ISO 9001 frameworks to standardize service delivery and improve customer satisfaction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Implementation Framework

This section walks through the **exhaustive logical sequence** of implementing an ISO 9001:2015-based quality system. Each step is a logical transition that builds on the previous one.

#### Step 1: Top Management Commitment and Leadership

```
Step 1 → Step 2 transition logic:
Without visible executive ownership, employees will not prioritize
the quality initiative over their daily production targets.
```

- Top management must provide **documented evidence** of commitment (memos, board minutes, allocated budgets).
- They must communicate the **importance of customer focus** and the role of each employee.
- They must ensure the **QMS achieves its intended results**.

#### Step 2: Define the Scope and Context of the Organization

- Identify **internal issues** (organizational culture, workforce competence, infrastructure).
- Identify **external issues** (regulatory environment, market trends, technology changes).
- Identify **interested parties** (customers, suppliers, regulators, employees) and their requirements.

```
Transition: Once context is clear, the organization can determine
which clauses of ISO 9001 apply and what processes must be controlled.
```

#### Step 3: Gap Analysis

- Conduct a baseline assessment comparing current practices to ISO 9001:2015 clauses (4–10).
- Document each gap as a **Non-Conformity** with severity (Major/Minor).
- Develop an **Implementation Plan** with milestones, responsible persons, and target dates.

#### Step 4: Process Identification and Mapping

- Identify all **core, support, and management processes**.
- Determine **process inputs, outputs, sequence, performance indicators**.
- Create a **Process Interaction Map** (often called the "Turtle Diagram" or "Sipoc Diagram" in KTU-level coursework).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SIPOC Diagram** stands for **Suppliers – Inputs – Process – Outputs – Customers**, which is a standard tool for documenting processes before drafting procedures.

#### Step 5: Documentation Hierarchy Development

```
Tier 1: Quality Manual (broad, policy-level)
   ↓
Tier 2: Documented Procedures (process-level)
   ↓
Tier 3: Work Instructions (task-level)
   ↓
Tier 4: Records (evidence of performance)
```

- Apply the **"Say What You Do, Do What You Say, Prove It"** principle.
  - **Say What You Do** → Document the procedures.
  - **Do What You Say** → Follow the procedures.
  - **Prove It** → Maintain records as evidence.

#### Step 6: Training and Awareness

- Develop **role-based training modules** for each function.
- Maintain **training records** including competency assessment results.
- Ensure every employee understands:
  - The **Quality Policy**.
  - **Relevant Quality Objectives** for their role.
  - The **implications of non-conformance**.

#### Step 7: System Deployment

- Officially launch the QMS on a defined **"Go-Live" date**.
- Conduct **pilot runs** of critical processes.
- Implement **monitoring and measurement systems** for key quality indicators.

#### Step 8: Internal Audit

- Train internal auditors as per **ISO 19011:2018** guidelines.
- Develop an **Annual Audit Programme** covering all QMS processes.
- Use a structured audit approach:
  1. Plan the audit (audit plan, checklist).
  2. Conduct the opening meeting.
  3. Gather and verify information.
  4. Generate audit findings.
  5. Conduct the closing meeting.
  6. Issue the **Audit Report**.

#### Step 9: Management Review

- Conduct at least **one management review per year** (typically every 6 months).
- Inputs include: audit results, customer feedback, process performance, status of corrective actions, follow-up from previous reviews, changes affecting the QMS, recommendations for improvement.
- Outputs include: opportunities for improvement, need for changes to the QMS, and resource requirements.

#### Step 10: Continual Improvement and PDCA Cycle

$$\text{PLAN} \rightarrow \text{DO} \rightarrow \text{CHECK} \rightarrow \text{ACT} \rightarrow \text{PLAN (next cycle)}$$

- **Plan:** Establish objectives and processes to deliver results (quality objectives, process design).
- **Do:** Execute the planned processes (training, implementation).
- **Check:** Monitor and measure performance (internal audits, KPI tracking).
- **Act:** Take actions to improve performance (corrective actions, preventive measures based on risk).

### 3.2 Mathematical Derivation: Linking DPMO to Sigma Level

#### Given:

- Number of defects observed = **350**
- Number of opportunities per unit = **5**
- Sample size = **50,000 units**

#### Step-by-Step Calculation:

**Step 1: Calculate the total number of opportunities.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Opportunities} &= \text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per unit} \\
&= 50{,}000 \times 5 \\
&= 250{,}000 \text{ opportunities}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Apply the DPMO formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Total Opportunities}} \times 10^6 \\
&= \dfrac{350}{250{,}000} \times 10^6 \\
&= 0.0014 \times 10^6 \\
&= 1{,}400 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Convert DPMO to Process Sigma using a yield-to-sigma conversion table.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Yield} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6} \\
&= 1 - \dfrac{1{,}400}{1{,}000{,}000} \\
&= 1 - 0.0014 \\
&= 0.9986 \\
&= 99.86\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Look up the Sigma Level from the yield-to-sigma conversion table.**

- 99.86% yield corresponds to approximately **4.5σ** (using standard six-sigma conversion tables).

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU board answers, always remember that **6σ corresponds to 3.4 DPMO** (which equals **99.99966% yield**). This is the benchmark quality level targeted by leading global corporations like Motorola and GE.

### 3.3 Cost of Quality (CoQ) Calculation Example

#### Given:

- Prevention Cost = ₹2,00,000
- Appraisal Cost = ₹1,50,000
- Internal Failure Cost = ₹80,000
- External Failure Cost = ₹70,000

#### Solution:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CoQ} &= P + A + IF + EF \\
&= 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 70{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

#### Interpretation:

- Prevention + Appraisal = **₹3,50,000 (70%)** – this is the "cost of conformance."
- Failure Costs (Internal + External) = **₹1,50,000 (30%)** – this is the "cost of non-conformance."
- **Insight:** Increasing prevention spending by ₹50,000 could potentially reduce failure costs by more than that amount, demonstrating the **optimal quality cost model**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of Audit Types

| Parameter | Internal Audit (1st Party) | Supplier Audit (2nd Party) | Certification Audit (3rd Party) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conducted by** | Organization's own auditors | Customer's auditors | External certification body |
| **Independence** | Moderate | High | Highest |
| **Purpose** | Self-verification | Supplier capability assessment | Formal certification |
| **Frequency** | At least once per year | Pre-contract and periodic | Initial + surveillance every 6–12 months |
| **Outcome** | Corrective actions internally | Approved/rejected supplier | Certificate granted/maintained |
| **KTU Exam Weight** | High | Moderate | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Flow Diagram: Quality System Implementation Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Top Management Commitment] --> B[Define Scope and Context]
    B --> C[Gap Analysis against ISO 9001:2015]
    C --> D[Process Mapping using SIPOC]
    D --> E[Documentation Hierarchy Design]
    E --> E1[Quality Manual]
    E --> E2[Documented Procedures]
    E --> E3[Work Instructions]
    E --> E4[Records and Forms]
    E1 --> F[Training and Awareness Program]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[System Deployment and Go-Live]
    G --> H[Internal Audit ISO 19011]
    H --> I[Management Review]
    I --> J{Non-Conformities?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Corrective Action Process]
    K --> H
    J -- No --> L[Continual Improvement PDCA]
    L --> M[Third-Party Certification Audit]
    M --> N[ISO 9001 Certificate Issued]
    N --> O[Surveillance Audits Annually]
    O --> L
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: PDCA Cycle within Quality System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PLAN: Set Quality Objectives and Processes] --> D1[DO: Implement and Train]
    D1 --> C1[CHECK: Monitor, Measure, and Audit]
    C1 --> A1[ACT: Corrective Action and Improvement]
    A1 --> P1
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Documentation Structure (Tier Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T1[Tier 1: Quality Manual] --> T2[Tier 2: Documented Procedures]
    T2 --> T3[Tier 3: Work Instructions]
    T3 --> T4[Tier 4: Records and Forms]
```

### 4.4 Process Interaction Diagram (SIPOC-Based Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Supplier] --> I[Inputs] --> Pr[Process] --> O[Outputs] --> Cu[Customer]
    I -. feedback .-> S
    O -. feedback .-> Pr
    Cu -. satisfaction data .-> Pr
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram: Quality Audit Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stage 1 Documentation Review] --> A2[Stage 2 On-Site Audit]
    A2 --> A3[Audit Findings and NCR]
    A3 --> A4{NCR Severity}
    A4 -- Major --> A5[Corrective Action Plan]
    A4 -- Minor --> A6[Opportunity for Improvement]
    A5 --> A7[Verification Audit]
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8{Certification Granted?}
    A8 -- Yes --> A9[Certificate Issued]
    A8 -- No --> A10[Re-audit Required]
    A10 --> A2
```

### 4.6 Conceptual Block Diagram: Cost of Quality Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q[Total Cost of Quality] --> P[Prevention Costs]
    Q --> AP[Appraisal Costs]
    Q --> IF[Internal Failure Costs]
    Q --> EF[External Failure Costs]
    P --> P1[Training, Quality Planning, Design Review]
    AP --> AP1[Inspection, Testing, Audits]
    IF --> IF1[Rework, Scrap, Re-inspection]
    EF --> EF1[Warranty Claims, Returns, Lost Goodwill]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** Define a **Quality System**. List any **four** components of a Quality System as per ISO 9001:2015. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Quality System** is a formalized, documented set of policies, processes, procedures, and responsibilities that an organization uses to direct and control activities related to quality, ensuring that products and services consistently meet customer and regulatory requirements.

Four components of a Quality System:

1. **Quality Policy** – the top management's documented commitment to quality.
2. **Quality Manual** – the master document describing the QMS scope and structure.
3. **Documented Procedures** – step-by-step instructions for carrying out activities.
4. **Records** – objective evidence that procedures have been followed.

> [!TIP]
> For a full 3-mark answer, always include a brief **definition (1 mark)** + **four points with one-line explanations (2 marks)**. This is the standard KTU valuation pattern.

---

**Q2.** What is a **Quality Audit**? Mention the **three types** of quality audits. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Quality Audit** is a systematic, independent, and documented process for obtaining objective evidence and evaluating it to determine the extent to which audit criteria are fulfilled.

Three types of quality audits:

1. **First-Party (Internal) Audit** – conducted by the organization's own trained auditors to verify internal QMS compliance.
2. **Second-Party (Supplier) Audit** – conducted by customers on their suppliers to evaluate capability and compliance.
3. **Third-Party (Certification) Audit** – conducted by an external certification body (e.g., TUV, BSI) to grant or maintain ISO certification.

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **ISO 9000 family of standards**. Discuss the **seven quality management principles** of ISO 9001:2015. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step implementation process** of a Quality Management System in an organization. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Answer:**

**(a) ISO 9000 Family of Standards & Seven Quality Management Principles:**

The ISO 9000 family is a globally recognized set of standards published by the International Organization for Standardization for implementing and auditing Quality Management Systems (QMS).

**Core Standards:**

- **ISO 9000:2015** – Defines the fundamental concepts, vocabulary, and principles of QMS.
- **ISO 9001:2015** – Specifies the requirements for a QMS; this is the **only auditable and certifiable** standard in the family.
- **ISO 9004:2018** – Provides guidelines for sustained success and broader performance improvement beyond ISO 9001 requirements.
- **ISO 19011:2018** – Provides guidelines for auditing any management system (quality and environmental).

**Seven Quality Management Principles (ISO 9001:2015):**

1. **Customer Focus** – The primary goal is to understand and meet customer needs and exceed expectations.
2. **Leadership** – Leaders establish a unified direction and create conditions for people to achieve quality objectives.
3. **Engagement of People** – Competent, empowered, and engaged employees at all levels enhance organizational capability.
4. **Process Approach** – Consistent and predictable results are achieved more efficiently when activities are managed as interrelated processes.
5. **Improvement** – Successful organizations maintain a continual focus on improvement.
6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making** – Effective decisions are based on the analysis of data and information.
7. **Relationship Management** – Relationships with relevant interested parties (suppliers, partners, regulators) are managed for sustained success.

**(b) Step-by-Step Implementation of a Quality System:**

**Step 1 – Top Management Commitment:** Top management must visibly lead the QMS initiative by allocating resources, defining the quality policy, and communicating its importance.

**Step 2 – Define Scope and Context:** Identify internal and external issues, interested parties, and their requirements, as well as the boundaries and applicability of the QMS.

**Step 3 – Gap Analysis:** Compare the current state of organizational practices against ISO 9001:2015 clauses to identify non-conformities and prioritize actions.

**Step 4 – Process Identification and Mapping:** Identify all processes, determine their sequence, interactions, inputs, outputs, owners, and KPIs. SIPOC diagrams are commonly used.

**Step 5 – Documentation Hierarchy:** Develop the four-tier documentation structure: Quality Manual → Procedures → Work Instructions → Records.

**Step 6 – Training and Awareness:** Train all employees on the quality policy, their role-specific procedures, and the consequences of non-conformance.

**Step 7 – System Deployment:** Roll out the QMS on a defined go-live date with pilot runs of critical processes.

**Step 8 – Internal Audit:** Conduct internal audits as per ISO 19011:2018 to verify compliance. Document findings and non-conformities.

**Step 9 – Management Review:** Top management reviews QMS performance, customer feedback, audit results, and recommends actions for improvement.

**Step 10 – Continual Improvement and Certification:** Apply the PDCA cycle continuously. Pursue third-party certification audit if external recognition is desired.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Many students **confuse ISO 9000 (vocabulary) with ISO 9001 (requirements)** – remember only ISO 9001 is certifiable.
> 2. In the seven principles question, **listing only the principle names without one-line explanations** leads to a loss of 2–3 marks. Always explain briefly.
> 3. In implementation questions, students often **skip Steps 1 (Leadership) and 9 (Management Review)**, which are mandatory clauses of ISO 9001 (Clauses 5 and 9 respectively). Always include them.
> 4. Avoid writing **"ISO certification is mandatory"** – it is voluntary. The correct statement is *"ISO 9001 certification is voluntary but often contractually required."*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) - Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Explain the structure and contents of a **Quality Manual** with reference to ISO 9001:2015. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Discuss the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** and explain how it supports continual improvement in a quality system. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Quality Manual – Structure and Contents:**

The **Quality Manual** is the master document that describes the QMS scope, exclusions, quality policy, objectives, and reference to documented procedures. While ISO 9001:2015 does not mandate a specific format, the typical structure used by organizations is:

1. **Title Page and Approval** – Includes organization name, manual title, version number, and approval signatures of top management.
2. **Table of Contents** – Lists all sections with page numbers for easy navigation.
3. **Scope of the QMS** – Defines which products, services, processes, and locations are covered.
4. **Exclusions and Justifications** – If any ISO 9001 clause is excluded, a justification must be provided (justified only if it does not affect the organization's ability to ensure conformity).
5. **Quality Policy** – Top management's documented commitment aligned with strategic direction.
6. **Quality Objectives** – Measurable goals consistent with the quality policy.
7. **Context of the Organization** – Internal/external issues, needs of interested parties (Clause 4).
8. **Process Map and Interactions** – Visual representation of all processes and their interactions.
9. **Reference to Documented Procedures** – Lists all Tier-2 procedures (e.g., Document Control, Internal Audit, Control of Non-Conforming Product, Corrective Action).
10. **Reference to Records** – Lists all records retained as evidence of performance.

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Say What You Do, Do What You Say, Prove It"** principle applies here: the manual documents intent (Say What), employees follow it (Do What), and records provide evidence (Prove It).

**(b) PDCA Cycle and Continual Improvement:**

The **PDCA Cycle** is a four-stage iterative management method used for the continual improvement of processes and products. It is the central engine of ISO 9001-based QMS.

**P – Plan:** Establish objectives, processes, and procedures to deliver results in accordance with customer requirements and the organization's quality policy. Develop training plans, allocate resources, and design the process map.

**D – Do:** Execute the planned processes as per the documented procedures. Implement training, deploy resources, and produce the products/services under controlled conditions.

**C – Check:** Monitor and measure process performance against the quality objectives, policies, and customer requirements. Use internal audits, KPI dashboards, and customer feedback analysis.

**A – Act:** Take corrective actions to address non-conformities, eliminate root causes, and implement improvements. Update procedures, retrain staff, and enhance controls.

**How PDCA Supports Continual Improvement:**

- The cycle is **iterative** – each completed cycle raises the maturity of the system.
- It ensures that improvements are **evidence-based** (Check) and not arbitrary.
- It links **top-level objectives (Plan)** with **frontline execution (Do)**.
- It drives the **ISO 9001 requirement for continual improvement** (Clause 10.3).

**Practical Example:** A software company plans to reduce bug density (Plan), trains developers in unit testing (Do), measures post-release defect rates (Check), and introduces stricter code reviews (Act). In the next cycle, the target is reduced further.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall – PDCA Question:**
> 1. Students often describe PDCA in **one line per stage** – this fetches only 50% marks. Provide **3–4 lines of detailed explanation per stage** for full 7 marks.
> 2. The PDCA cycle is sometimes confused with the **Shewhart cycle (which is essentially the same, but PDCA is the Deming terminology)** – either term is acceptable, but mention that Deming popularized it.
> 3. For an extra mark, link PDCA directly to **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 10 (Improvement)**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **Quality System** is a formalized set of policies, procedures, and responsibilities that ensure products/services meet customer and regulatory requirements.
- The **ISO 9000 family** is the globally recognized framework for QMS, with **ISO 9001:2015** being the only certifiable standard.
- The **Seven Quality Management Principles** (Customer Focus, Leadership, Engagement, Process Approach, Improvement, Evidence-Based Decision Making, Relationship Management) form the philosophical foundation of ISO 9001:2015.
- **Implementation follows 10 logical steps** starting from Top Management Commitment and ending with Continual Improvement and Certification.
- The **Documentation Hierarchy** has 4 tiers: Quality Manual → Procedures → Work Instructions → Records.
- A **Quality Manual** is the master QMS document covering scope, policy, objectives, and references to procedures.
- **Quality Audits** are of three types: First-Party (Internal), Second-Party (Supplier), and Third-Party (Certification).
- The **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) Cycle** is the cornerstone of continual improvement in any quality system.
- **DPMO, FPY, RTY, and CoQ** are the standard quantitative metrics used to measure quality system performance.
- **6σ quality level corresponds to 3.4 DPMO** (99.99966% yield), the gold standard of process excellence.
- **ISO 9001:2015 replaced "Preventive Action"** with **risk-based thinking** in Clause 6.1.
- **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure Costs.
- For KTU answers, always **link theory to ISO 9001:2015 clauses** wherever possible – examiners reward this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Documentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Documentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Documentation in Total Quality Management (TQM)** refers to the systematic, controlled creation, maintenance, distribution, retention, and disposal of all written, electronic, or recorded information that defines, implements, maintains, and continually improves an organization's **Quality Management System (QMS)**. It serves as the primary **objective evidence** required by ISO 9001:2015 standards and KTU's outcome-based engineering practices.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 of OESFT724, documentation is treated as the **"nervous system of a QMS"** — it transforms tacit organizational knowledge into explicit, auditable, and reproducible quality practices.

### Sub-Categories of TQM Documentation

| Sub-Document | Primary Purpose | Mandatory under ISO 9001:2015 |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality Manual** | Top-level policy & QMS scope | Yes |
| **Quality System Procedures (QSP)** | Departmental/Process-level instructions | Yes |
| **Standard Operating Procedures (SOP)** | Task-level work instructions | Recommended |
| **Work Instructions** | Step-by-step operational guidance | Recommended |
| **Quality Records** | Objective evidence of conformity | Yes |
| **Forms / Checklists / Templates** | Data capture instruments | Recommended |
| **External Documents** | Standards, regulations, supplier docs | Yes |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Documentation is the DNA of an Organization"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Restaurant Kitchen**
> Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen. The **Master Chef (Quality Manager)** runs the kitchen with the help of:
> - A **Recipe Book** → This is the **Quality Manual** (overall cooking philosophy).
> - **Sub-Recipes per Cuisine** → These are **Quality System Procedures** (how Indian dishes are made vs. Italian).
> - **Step-by-step kitchen cards pinned near each station** → These are **Work Instructions** (chop onions thinly, sauté for 3 min).
> - **Customer bills & complaint logs** → These are **Quality Records** (proof the food was prepared and served properly).
> - **The Kitchen Manager's clipboard checklist** → These are **Forms and Checklists**.

If a new chef joins, the kitchen works **the same way regardless of who is cooking** because the documents exist. That is exactly what **Documentation does in TQM** — it makes quality **independent of people, memory, or mood**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Standards & Document Metrics (Industry-Recognised Values)

> [!NOTE]
> The following are universally accepted **document control metrics** in a compliant QMS:

- **Document Retention Period:** typically **7 years** for quality records (pharma/medical devices) and **3 years** minimum for general ISO 9001 records.
- **Page Identification Standard:** every page must carry the 4-element identifier — **Document No., Revision No., Effective Date, Page X of Y**.
- **Approval Threshold:** all controlled documents must have at least **two signatures** (Prepared by + Approved by).
- **Maximum Acceptable Revision Cycle:** **≤ 12 months** for critical procedures in regulated industries.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Documentation Hierarchy (Pyramid Model)

The **ISO-documented QMS structure** follows a strict 4-tier pyramid:

```
                ▲  LEVEL 1: QUALITY MANUAL
               ╱ ╲     (Policy + Scope + Exclusions)
              ╱───╲
             ╱ QSP ╲   LEVEL 2: QUALITY SYSTEM PROCEDURES
            ╱───────╲    (Process-level "what & who")
           ╱─────────╲
          ╱ WORK INST ╲ LEVEL 3: WORK INSTRUCTIONS / SOPs
         ╱─────────────╲  (Task-level "how")
        ╱─────────────────╲
       ╱   QUALITY RECORDS   ╲ LEVEL 4: RECORDS / FORMS
      ╱_______________________╲ (Objective Evidence)
```

Each tier **expands the previous one** — a single Quality Manual clause can generate 3–5 procedures, each procedure can have 5–10 work instructions, each instruction can spawn multiple records.

---

## 2.2 The Document Life Cycle (5-Phase Model)

A controlled document passes through these phases:

1. **DRAFTING** → Authored by the process owner.
2. **REVIEW** → Cross-functional team validates accuracy.
3. **APPROVAL** → Authorized signatory releases the document.
4. **DISTRIBUTION & USE** → Document is accessible at point-of-use (master + controlled copies).
5. **REVISION / OBSOLESCENCE** → Document is updated, replaced, or archived.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners frequently ask the difference between *Revision* (minor change, same Rev No., e.g., 1.0 → 1.1) and *Re-issue* (major change, new Rev No., e.g., 1.0 → 2.0). Master revisions must be tracked in a **Document Master List (DML)**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Document Effectiveness Ratio (DER)** | $DER = \dfrac{\text{No. of Controlled Documents in Use}}{\text{Total No. of Documents in System}} \times 100\%$ | Measures document discipline; target ≥ 95% |
| 2 | **Record Compliance Rate (RCR)** | $RCR = \dfrac{\text{No. of Audit-Compliant Records}}{\text{Total Records Audited}} \times 100\%$ | Used in Internal Quality Audits |
| 3 | **Document Revision Cycle (DRC)** | $DRC = \text{Issue Date} - \text{Previous Issue Date} \;\;(\text{months})$ | Critical SOPs: $DRC \leq 12$ months |
| 4 | **Document Control Cost (DCC)** | $DCC = \text{Drafting Cost} + \text{Distribution Cost} + \text{Training Cost} + \text{Archival Cost}$ | TQM cost-of-quality metric |
| 5 | **Obsolete Document Risk (ODR)** | $ODR = \dfrac{\text{No. of Obsolete Docs Found in Use}}{\text{Total Docs Sampled}}$ | Should be 0 in compliant QMS |
| 6 | **Distribution Lag Time (DLT)** | $DLT = T_{\text{received}} - T_{\text{released}}$ (in hours) | Should be $\leq 24$ hours for critical docs |
| 7 | **Retrieval Time Standard (RTS)** | $RTS \leq 30$ seconds per document | Used in 5S audits of document rooms |

---

## 2.4 Why Documentation is Critical — The "Why" Behind the "What"

> [!NOTE]
> **The 7 Engineering Reasons for Documentation:**

1. **Traceability** — Every product can be traced back to its raw material, operator, and machine.
2. **Continuity** — Knowledge survives employee attrition.
3. **Consistency** — Reduces process variation; statistical process control (SPC) needs uniform procedures.
4. **Auditability** — External auditors (ISO, NABCB, KTU academic audits) need documented proof.
5. **Legal Defence** — In case of product liability or recall, records serve as legal evidence.
6. **Training Foundation** — Onboarding becomes structured, not ad-hoc.
7. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — You cannot improve what you have not documented and measured.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Application

| Engineering Sector | Typical Document | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical (Production Shop)** | CNC Operation SOP | Ensures repeatability of tolerance-critical parts |
| **Civil (Construction Site)** | Concrete Pour Checklist | Records slump test, temperature, batch No. |
| **Electrical (PCB Assembly)** | Reflow Profile Document | Documents soldering thermal profile |
| **Software (IT Services)** | Test Case Document | Validates defect-free code delivery |
| **Biomedical** | Sterilization Validation Record | Mandatory FDA/Central Drugs Standard Control evidence |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Content

## 3.1 The Standard Document Control Procedure — Step-by-Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU ESE question on Documentation may require you to outline this procedure. Memorize the **flow**.

**Step 1 — Need Identification**
The process owner identifies the need for a new or revised document based on:
- Process change
- Customer complaint
- Audit non-conformance
- Regulatory update

**Step 2 — Drafting**
The author (process owner) drafts the document using the standard **4-element header template**:

$$
\text{Header} = \{ \text{Logo} + \text{Doc No.} + \text{Rev. No.} + \text{Effective Date} + \text{Page X of Y} \}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-Functional Review**
A **Document Review Committee (DRC)** — comprising Quality Manager, Production Head, and one user — verifies:
- Technical accuracy
- Compliance with standards
- Clarity of instructions

**Step 4 — Approval & Authorization**
The **Management Representative (MR)** signs the document. A two-signature principle is followed:

$$
\text{Document Approval} = \{\text{Prepared By (Author)} + \text{Approved By (MR)}\}
$$

**Step 5 — Numbering & Master Filing**
A unique document number is assigned using a standard numbering scheme:

$$
\text{Doc No.} = \underbrace{DEPT}_{\text{Department}} - \underbrace{XX}_{\text{Process Code}} - \underbrace{NNN}_{\text{Sequence}} - \underbrace{R}_{\text{Rev. Letter}}
$$

**Example:** `QA-WLD-014-R2` = Quality Assurance / Welding Process / 14th Document / Revision 2.

**Step 6 — Distribution**
- **Master Copy** → retained in Document Control Room (DCR).
- **Controlled Copies** → distributed with a **copy number stamp** (e.g., Copy No. 03 of 10).
- **Uncontrolled Copy** → given for reference only (e.g., marketing, external).

**Step 7 — Training & Implementation**
Affected employees must sign a **Training Acknowledgement Form** before the document is enforced.

$$
\text{Implementation Date} = \text{Approval Date} + \text{Training Period (typically 7-15 days)}
$$

**Step 8 — Periodic Review**
Documents are reviewed at least **once every 12 months**, or upon:
- Major process change
- Customer complaint
- Audit finding

**Step 9 — Revision / Obsolescence**
Obsolete documents are:
- **Withdrawn** from all points-of-use.
- **Marked** with a red stamp "**OBSOLETE — DO NOT USE**".
- **Archived** in the obsolete records register for the legal retention period.

**Step 10 — Disposal**
After retention period, documents are shredded / incinerated with a witness-signed disposal record.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Document Types

| Parameter | Quality Manual | Procedure (QSP) | Work Instruction | Quality Record |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Level in Pyramid** | Level 1 | Level 2 | Level 3 | Level 4 (Base) |
| **Audience** | Top Management + Auditors | Process Owners | Operators | Auditors |
| **Question Answered** | *Why? What?* | *Who? When?* | *How?* | *What happened?* |
| **Length** | 10–30 pages | 3–10 pages | 1–3 pages | Variable |
| **Review Frequency** | Every 24 months | Every 12 months | Every 6–12 months | Never (retained) |
| **Approval Authority** | Managing Director | MR + HOD | Process Owner | Auto-generated |
| **Mandatory under ISO 9001** | Yes (Clause 7.5) | Yes (Clause 7.5) | Recommended | Yes (Clause 7.5.3) |
| **Example** | ABC Industries QMS Manual | Procurement Procedure | CNC Calibration Steps | Incoming Inspection Report |
| **Format** | Narrative | Narrative + Flowchart | Numbered steps | Form / Database |

---

## 3.3 Document Numbering System — Detailed Algorithm

A robust document numbering system follows this logic:

$$
\boxed{\text{Doc No.} = \text{Org Code} - \text{Dept Code} - \text{Type} - \text{Sequence} - \text{Revision}}
$$

**Expanded Form:**

$$
\text{Doc No.} = \underbrace{ABC}_{\text{Org Identifier}} - \underbrace{QA}_{\text{Quality Dept}} - \underbrace{P}_{\text{Procedure Type}} - \underbrace{007}_{\text{7th in series}} - \underbrace{R3}_{\text{3rd Revision}}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**
Suppose an organization "**Kerala Engineering Pvt. Ltd.**" needs its **15th SOP for the Maintenance Department** and it's the **2nd revision**.

$$
\text{Doc No.} = \underbrace{KEPL}_{\text{Org}} - \underbrace{MNT}_{\text{Dept}} - \underbrace{SOP}_{\text{Type}} - \underbrace{015}_{\text{Sequence}} - \underbrace{R2}_{\text{Revision}}
$$

**Final Doc No.:** `KEPL-MNT-SOP-015-R2` ✓

---

## 3.4 Document Effectiveness Audit — Numerical Problem Template

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Favourite:** A quality audit reveals the following data. Calculate the Document Effectiveness Ratio (DER) and Record Compliance Rate (RCR) of the QMS.

**Given:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Documents in System} &= 240 \\
\text{Controlled Documents in Active Use} &= 222 \\
\text{Total Records Audited} &= 150 \\
\text{Audit-Compliant Records} &= 138
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution:**

$$
DER = \dfrac{222}{240} \times 100\% = \mathbf{92.5\%}
$$

$$
RCR = \dfrac{138}{150} \times 100\% = \mathbf{92.0\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** Both ratios fall below the industry benchmark of **95%**, indicating a need for refresher training and stricter document control. This is a **NC (Non-Conformance)** in the next external audit.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Documentation Hierarchy Pyramid (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["LEVEL 1: QUALITY MANUAL - Defines QMS Scope and Policy"]
    B["LEVEL 2: QUALITY SYSTEM PROCEDURES - Defines Process-level Workflows"]
    C["LEVEL 3: WORK INSTRUCTIONS AND SOPs - Defines Task-level Steps"]
    D["LEVEL 4: QUALITY RECORDS AND FORMS - Objective Evidence of Conformance"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a4a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#0b2a4a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#0b2a4a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style D fill:#deebf7,stroke:#0b2a4a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.2 Document Life Cycle — Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["PHASE 1: DRAFTING - Author prepares first version"]
    A2["PHASE 2: REVIEW - Cross-functional DRC verifies accuracy"]
    A3["PHASE 3: APPROVAL - MR authorizes release"]
    A4["PHASE 4: DISTRIBUTION - Controlled copies issued"]
    A5["PHASE 5: REVISION OR OBSOLESCENCE - Update or Retire"]

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5
    A5 -.Revision Required.-> A1
    A5 -.Obsolete.-> A6["ARCHIVE IN OBSOLETE REGISTER - Retain for 7 Years"]
    A6 --> A7["DISPOSAL - Shred or Incinerate with Witness"]

    style A1 fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#385723,color:#000000
    style A2 fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#385723,color:#000000
    style A3 fill:#548235,stroke:#385723,color:#ffffff
    style A4 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#385723,color:#ffffff
    style A5 fill:#e2efda,stroke:#385723,color:#000000
    style A6 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000
    style A7 fill:#fbe5d6,stroke:#833c0c,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.3 Document Control — Functional Architecture Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["NEW OR REVISED DOCUMENT TRIGGER"]
    process["PROCESS OWNER AUTHORS DOCUMENT"]
    review["DOCUMENT REVIEW COMMITTEE EVALUATES"]
    approved{"APPROVED?"}
    yes["MR SIGNS AND ASSIGNS DOC NUMBER"]
    no["RETURN TO AUTHOR FOR CORRECTIONS"]
    train["TRAIN OPERATORS AND USERS"]
    distribute["DISTRIBUTE CONTROLLED COPIES"]
    use["DOCUMENT IN ACTIVE USE AT POINT OF USE"]
    periodic{"PERIODIC REVIEW DUE?"}
    revise["INITIATE REVISION CYCLE"]
    archive["MARK OBSOLETE AND ARCHIVE FOR 7 YEARS"]

    start --> process --> review --> approved
    approved --> yes
    approved -- No --> no --> process
    yes --> train --> distribute --> use --> periodic
    periodic -- Yes --> revise --> process
    periodic -- No --> use
    use --> archive

    style start fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000
    style approved fill:#ffd966,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000
    style periodic fill:#ffd966,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000
    style yes fill:#548235,stroke:#385723,color:#ffffff
    style no fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
    style archive fill:#808080,stroke:#404040,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.4 Document Distribution & Numbering Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    master["MASTER COPY - LOCKED IN DOCUMENT CONTROL ROOM"]
    c1["CONTROLLED COPY 01 - PRODUCTION DEPT"]
    c2["CONTROLLED COPY 02 - QUALITY DEPT"]
    c3["CONTROLLED COPY 03 - MAINTENANCE DEPT"]
    uc["UNCONTROLLED COPY - EXTERNAL OR REFERENCE ONLY"]

    master --> c1
    master --> c2
    master --> c3
    master -.Reference Only.-> uc

    c1 -.Updates.-> master
    c2 -.Updates.-> master
    c3 -.Updates.-> master

    style master fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
    style c1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#0b2a4a,color:#ffffff
    style c2 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#0b2a4a,color:#ffffff
    style c3 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#0b2a4a,color:#ffffff
    style uc fill:#bfbfbf,stroke:#595959,color:#000000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define documentation in the context of Total Quality Management. List the four levels of the documentation hierarchy.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
> Documentation in TQM is the systematic creation, control, distribution, and maintenance of all written or electronic records that define, implement, and provide evidence of the effective operation of an organization's Quality Management System. It serves as the auditable backbone of continuous improvement and conformance to ISO 9001 standards.

**Four Levels (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{Level 1: Quality Manual} \rightarrow \text{Level 2: Quality System Procedures} \rightarrow \text{Level 3: Work Instructions / SOPs} \rightarrow \text{Level 4: Quality Records}
$$

---

### **Q2. What is a Document Master List (DML)? Why is it important in document control?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Document Master List (DML)** is a controlled register that contains the complete inventory of all documents in the QMS, along with their **document number, current revision number, effective date, owner, and status** (active / obsolete / under review).

**Importance (2 Marks):**
1. Provides **single point of reference** for auditors to verify QMS scope.
2. Enables **traceability of obsolete vs. current** versions, preventing use of outdated documents.
3. Supports **change management** — every revision is logged.
4. Required by **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 7.5.3** as part of control of documented information.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 4 Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **lose 3 marks** by confusing *Quality Manual* with *Quality Plan*. A Quality Manual is **permanent + organization-wide**; a Quality Plan is **project-specific + temporary**.
> - Students **lose 2 marks** by forgetting to mention the **two-signature principle** (Prepared by + Approved by).
> - Students **lose 2 marks** by not explaining the **difference between Revision and Re-issue**.
> - Always draw the **documentation pyramid** in the answer sheet — it fetches **2 easy marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

#### **Q. (a) Explain in detail the different types of documents used in a Quality Management System. How does each level support ISO 9001:2015 requirements? (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A Quality Management System relies on a structured hierarchy of documents to ensure consistency, traceability, and conformance. The major types are:

**1. Quality Manual (2 Marks):**
The top-level document that defines the organization's QMS scope, exclusions, and high-level policies. It maps every ISO 9001 clause to a responsible process. It answers the *why* of the QMS.

**2. Quality System Procedures (QSPs) (2 Marks):**
These are process-level documents that describe *who* does *what*, *when*, and *how* interactions occur between departments. Example: *Procedure for Handling Customer Complaints* (Clause 8.7 of ISO 9001).

**3. Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) / Work Instructions (1.5 Marks):**
These are task-level documents providing step-by-step instructions to operators. Example: *SOP for Operating CNC Lathe*. They answer *how* and are the most numerous documents in any QMS.

**4. Quality Records (1 Mark):**
Provide **objective evidence** that the QMS is functioning. They include inspection reports, calibration logs, training records, audit reports, and management review minutes. ISO 9001:2015 (Clause 7.5.3) mandates their control.

**5. Forms, Templates, and Checklists (0.5 Mark):**
Standardized data-capture instruments ensuring uniform information collection.

**ISO 9001 Mapping:**

$$
\text{Quality Manual} \rightarrow \text{Clause 4.3 (Scope of QMS)}
$$

$$
\text{QSPs} \rightarrow \text{Clauses 7.5.1, 7.5.2}
$$

$$
\text{Records} \rightarrow \text{Clause 7.5.3}
$$

---

#### **Q. (b) Describe the step-by-step procedure for Document Control in an ISO 9001:2015 compliant organization. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Need Identification (0.5 Mark):**
Triggered by process change, audit NC, customer complaint, or regulatory update.

**Step 2 — Drafting (0.5 Mark):**
Author drafts using the standard template with the 4-element header (Doc No., Rev No., Date, Page X of Y).

**Step 3 — Review (1 Mark):**
A Document Review Committee (DRC) — comprising Quality Manager, HOD, and a user — verifies accuracy, clarity, and compliance.

**Step 4 — Approval (1 Mark):**
The **Management Representative (MR)** approves. Two signatures mandatory:

$$
\text{Prepared By (Author)} + \text{Approved By (MR)}
$$

**Step 5 — Numbering & Master Filing (1 Mark):**
Unique document number assigned using:

$$
\text{Doc No.} = \text{Org} - \text{Dept} - \text{Type} - \text{Sequence} - \text{Revision}
$$

Master copy stored in Document Control Room (DCR).

**Step 6 — Distribution (1 Mark):**
Controlled copies distributed with **copy-number stamps** (Copy 01 of 10). Uncontrolled copies marked "FOR REFERENCE ONLY".

**Step 7 — Training & Implementation (1 Mark):**
Operators sign **Training Acknowledgement Forms** before use. Implementation date = Approval date + Training period.

**Step 8 — Periodic Review & Obsolescence (1 Mark):**
Reviewed every 6–12 months. Obsolete documents marked "**OBSOLETE — DO NOT USE**", withdrawn, archived for **7 years**, then disposed.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

#### **Q. (a) Compare and contrast the Quality Manual, Quality Plan, and Quality Record. When would you use each in a project lifecycle? (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (5 Marks):**

| Parameter | Quality Manual | Quality Plan | Quality Record |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Organization-wide | Project / Contract-specific | Specific event / activity |
| **Duration** | Permanent | Temporary (project life) | Permanent (retained) |
| **Purpose** | Defines QMS framework | Defines project-specific quality practices | Provides evidence of conformance |
| **Author** | MR + Top Management | Project Manager | Auto-generated / Operator |
| **Mandatory under ISO 9001** | Yes | Yes (Clause 8.5.1) | Yes (Clause 7.5.3) |
| **Example** | ISO 9001 Manual for ABC Industries | Quality Plan for Bridge Construction | Concrete Cube Test Report |

**Lifecycle Use (2 Marks):**

- **Quality Manual** → Used at the **strategic / organizational level** during QMS design and audit cycles.
- **Quality Plan** → Used at the **project initiation phase** to define acceptance criteria, inspection points, and resource allocation for that specific project.
- **Quality Records** → Used at the **execution and closure phases** to capture evidence of what was actually done.

---

#### **Q. (b) An audit of your organization reveals 222 controlled documents in active use out of 240 total documents. Out of 150 records audited, 138 are compliant. Calculate the Document Effectiveness Ratio (DER) and Record Compliance Rate (RCR), and recommend corrective actions. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Calculate DER (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DER &= \dfrac{\text{Controlled Documents in Active Use}}{\text{Total Documents}} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{222}{240} \times 100\% \\
&= \mathbf{92.5\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate RCR (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RCR &= \dfrac{\text{Audit-Compliant Records}}{\text{Total Records Audited}} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{138}{150} \times 100\% \\
&= \mathbf{92.0\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Corrective Actions (1 Mark):**

> [!NOTE]
> Both ratios fall **below the industry benchmark of 95%**. Recommended actions:
> - Conduct **root-cause analysis** for the 18 non-active documents (likely obsolete docs found in use or missing master copies).
> - Organize **refresher training** for record-fillers on the 12 non-compliant records.
> - Update the **Document Master List (DML)** to ensure traceability.
> - Schedule a **follow-up internal audit within 30 days** to verify closure of NCs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep:**

- ✅ Documentation = **Written evidence** of QMS operation, mandatory under **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 7.5**.
- ✅ The **4-tier pyramid**: Manual → QSP → SOP/Work Instruction → Records.
- ✅ Every document needs **3 elements**: Doc No. + Rev. No. + Effective Date + Page X of Y.
- ✅ Two-signature rule: **Prepared by + Approved by** (MR).
- ✅ **Master Copy** is the original; **Controlled Copies** are stamped with copy numbers.
- ✅ **Uncontrolled copies** = "FOR REFERENCE ONLY" stamp.
- ✅ Obsolete docs must be stamped **"OBSOLETE — DO NOT USE"** in red and withdrawn.
- ✅ Retention period: typically **7 years** (regulated industries) / **3 years** (general ISO 9001).
- ✅ **Document Master List (DML)** is mandatory and must be current.
- ✅ **DER benchmark** ≥ 95%; **RCR benchmark** ≥ 95%.
- ✅ Document numbering formula: `ORG-DEPT-TYPE-SEQUENCE-REVISION`.
- ✅ Revision is *minor change*; Re-issue is *major change* (changes the revision number).
- ✅ Periodic review every **≤ 12 months** for critical procedures.
- ✅ Records provide **objective evidence** — they are not revised, only retained.
- ✅ Quality Plan ≠ Quality Manual. Plan is **project-specific**; Manual is **organization-wide**.
- ✅ KTU ESE loves asking: *"Explain document control procedure with a flowchart"* — always draw the **10-step cycle** for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Auditing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT724 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality Auditing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Quality Audit** is a systematic, independent, and documented examination of an organization's quality system, processes, products, or services to determine whether they conform to specified requirements (such as ISO 9001 standards, customer requirements, or regulatory norms) and to evaluate the effectiveness of the implemented quality management system (QMS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Quality Audit is a structured, evidence-based verification process used to ascertain the degree of compliance of a quality system with reference standards (e.g., **ISO 9001:2015**, ISO 19011:2018). It is *not* an inspection of products; it is an *audit of the system that makes the products*.

The governing standard for auditing management systems is **ISO 19011:2018** — "Guidelines for auditing management systems." This is the globally accepted framework referenced in KTU 2024 scheme assessments.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Health Check-up"**
> Think of a **Quality Audit** exactly like a **doctor's annual health check-up**:
> - The **doctor** is the **auditor**.
> - The **hospital's procedures, medical equipment, hygiene, and record-keeping** are the **Quality Management System (QMS)**.
> - The **patient** is the **organization being audited**.
> - The doctor does **not heal the patient**; they only *report* findings (diagnose). The patient (organization) must take corrective action (treatment).
> - Blood tests, scans, and check-ups are the **audit evidence** collected.
> - The **medical report** is the **audit report**.
> - Regular follow-ups to verify recovery are the **audit follow-up / closure process**.

Just as a hospital may receive accreditation (NABH) only after passing such audits, organizations achieve **ISO certification** only after successfully passing a third-party quality audit.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standardized Vocabulary)

| Term | Formal Meaning |
|------|----------------|
| **Audit Criteria** | The set of policies, procedures, or requirements (e.g., ISO 9001 clauses) used as a reference against which audit evidence is compared. |
| **Audit Evidence** | Records, statements of fact, or other verifiable information that are relevant to the audit criteria. |
| **Audit Findings** | Results of the evaluation of collected audit evidence against audit criteria. Can be Conformity or Non-conformity. |
| **Non-Conformity (NC)** | Non-fulfillment of a requirement. Classified as **Major NC** (systemic failure) or **Minor NC** (isolated incident). |
| **Observation** | A statement of fact made during an audit that does not constitute a non-conformity but is noted for improvement. |
| **Opportunity for Improvement (OFI)** | A suggestion provided by the auditor to enhance the QMS, without being a deficiency. |
| **Auditor** | A person with demonstrated personal attributes and competence to conduct an audit. |
| **Lead Auditor** | The auditor who leads the audit team, manages the audit, and is responsible for the audit report. |
| **Auditee** | The organization or person being audited. |
| **Corrective Action (CA)** | Action to eliminate the cause of a detected non-conformity and prevent recurrence. |
| **Preventive Action (PA)** | Action to eliminate the cause of a potential non-conformity or other undesirable situation. |

## 1.4 Objectives of Quality Auditing (KTU High-Yield Point)

The five primary objectives examiners frequently test are:

1. **Verify Conformance:** Determine whether the QMS conforms to planned arrangements, ISO standard requirements, and organizational policies.
2. **Evaluate Effectiveness:** Assess whether the implemented QMS is *effectively* maintained and *continually improving*.
3. **Identify Gaps:** Detect non-conformities, risks, and opportunities for improvement.
4. **Enable Certification:** Provide the basis for third-party certification to standards like ISO 9001.
5. **Drive Continual Improvement:** Supply management with objective data to make informed decisions (Deming's "Evaluate" stage in PDCA).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Quality Audits (Hierarchical Decomposition)

Quality audits are classified along **three orthogonal dimensions** — *by party, by scope, and by subject*.

### 2.1.1 By Party Conducting the Audit (Most Important — KTU Hot Topic)

| Audit Type | Conducted By | Audits Whom? | Primary Purpose |
|------------|--------------|--------------|-----------------|
| **First-Party Audit (Internal Audit)** | The organization itself (own employees) | Internal departments / own QMS | Self-assessment, management review input, continual improvement |
| **Second-Party Audit (Supplier Audit)** | A customer or by a customer on its behalf | Suppliers / contractors | Supplier selection, supplier monitoring, contract compliance |
| **Third-Party Audit (External / Certification Audit)** | Independent external body (e.g., BIS, TUV, BSI) | The certified organization | Certification, accreditation, regulatory compliance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick — "1-2-3 Self-Cust-Cert":**
> - **1st Party** → **Self** (Internal)
> - **2nd Party** → **Customer** (Supplier audit)
> - **3rd Party** → **Certifier** (Independent body)

### 2.1.2 By Scope of Audit

- **System Audit:** Evaluates the entire QMS against a standard (e.g., ISO 9001).
- **Process Audit:** Focuses on a single process (e.g., the welding process in manufacturing).
- **Product Audit:** Examines a specific product type for conformance to specifications.
- **Compliance Audit:** Verifies adherence to laws, regulations, or contractual obligations.
- **Combined Audit:** Conducts audits of two or more management systems together (e.g., QMS + EMS = ISO 9001 + ISO 14001).

### 2.1.3 By Subject / Domain

- **Quality System Audit** (most common in TQM)
- **Environmental Audit** (ISO 14001)
- **Safety Audit** (OHSAS / ISO 45001)
- **Energy Audit** (ISO 50001)
- **Information Security Audit** (ISO 27001)

## 2.2 Audit Principles (ISO 19011:2018 — KTU Mandatory)

There are **seven auditing principles** that every professional auditor must follow:

1. **Integrity** — Foundation of professionalism; auditor must be honest and truthful.
2. **Fair Presentation** — Audit findings must reflect the true state of activities; nothing concealed.
3. **Due Professional Care** — Apply diligence and judgment during the audit.
4. **Confidentiality** — Protect sensitive information obtained during the audit.
5. **Independence** — Auditors must be independent of the activity being audited (no bias).
6. **Evidence-Based Approach** — Findings must be backed by verifiable evidence.
7. **Risk-Based Approach** — Focus audit attention on areas of highest risk to the QMS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Questions like *"List the seven principles of auditing as per ISO 19011"* or *"Explain 'evidence-based approach' in auditing"* are guaranteed in every ESE. Memorize these seven verbatim.

## 2.3 The Audit Process (Six-Stage Lifecycle)

The audit lifecycle follows a structured, sequential flow:

### Stage 1: Initiating the Audit
- Define audit objectives, scope, and criteria.
- Select the audit team and lead auditor.
- Establish initial contact with the auditee.

### Stage 2: Preparing the Audit Activities
- Document review (quality manual, procedures, prior audit reports).
- Prepare the **audit plan** (dates, locations, departments).
- Prepare **working documents**: audit checklists, question lists, forms for recording evidence.

### Stage 3: Conducting the Audit (On-Site Activities)
- **Opening Meeting:** Introduce team, confirm scope, clarify logistics.
- **Collecting Evidence:** Through interviews, observation of activities, review of documents and records.
- **Generating Findings:** Classify as Conformity, Observation, or Non-Conformity.
- **Closing Meeting:** Present findings to auditee's top management.

### Stage 4: Preparing and Distributing the Audit Report
- Lead auditor drafts the report.
- Distribute within an agreed timeframe (typically 2–4 weeks).
- The report must contain: objectives, scope, criteria, findings, conclusions, and recommendations.

### Stage 5: Completing the Audit
- Distribute the audit report to relevant parties.
- Retain audit records per documented procedures.

### Stage 6: Conducting Audit Follow-Up
- Verify that the auditee has taken **corrective action** for all non-conformities.
- Verify effectiveness of corrective action.
- Close out non-conformities formally.

## 2.4 Audit Findings Classification (Decision Logic)

$$
\text{Audit Finding} = f(\text{Evidence}, \text{Criteria})
$$

- If Evidence $\geq$ Criteria → **Conformity (C)**
- If Evidence < Criteria AND systemic → **Major Non-Conformity (NC-Major)** — issued when there is:
  - Total absence of a required system/element, OR
  - Multiple related minor NCs against one clause, OR
  - A situation that raises significant doubt about the ability of the QMS to achieve intended outcomes.
- If Evidence < Criteria AND isolated → **Minor Non-Conformity (NC-Minor)**
- If no NC but improvement possible → **Observation** or **Opportunity for Improvement (OFI)**

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although auditing is qualitative, the following **ratios and indices** are testable in 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

| Concept / Formula | Expression | Use in Audit Context |
|-------------------|------------|---------------------|
| **Audit Coverage Ratio** | $ACR = \dfrac{\text{No. of processes audited}}{\text{Total no. of processes}} \times 100\%$ | Measures the breadth of audit scope |
| **Non-Conformity Density (NCD)** | $NCD = \dfrac{\text{Number of NCs raised}}{\text{Number of man-hours audited}}$ | Benchmarks audit severity between departments |
| **Closure Rate** | $CR = \dfrac{\text{NCs effectively closed}}{\text{Total NCs raised}} \times 100\%$ | Tracks audit follow-up effectiveness |
| **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $CoQ = P_c + A_c + I_c + F_c$ | $P_c$ = Prevention cost; $A_c$ = Appraisal cost; $I_c$ = Internal failure cost; $F_c$ = External failure cost. **Audit cost is part of Appraisal cost $A_c$** |
| **Sampling Size (Audit)** | $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$ | Statistical sample size for audit evidence collection |
| **Risk Priority Number (FMEA)** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Identifies high-risk areas to prioritize in audit |

> **KTU High-Yield Line:** *"Quality Audit cost falls under **Appraisal Cost** in Juran's Cost of Quality model — NOT under Prevention Cost."*

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Quality Auditing

- **Manufacturing:** Audits verify process capability (Cpk ≥ 1.33) and Six-Sigma discipline.
- **Software Engineering:** CMMI and ISO 9001 audits govern software development processes.
- **Healthcare:** NABH audits (analogous to ISO 9001) ensure patient safety protocols.
- **Automotive:** IATF 16949 audits are mandatory for Tier-1 suppliers to global OEMs.
- **Service Sector:** Banks undergo ISO 9001 audits for service consistency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Audit vs. Inspection vs. Surveillance vs. Review

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most frequently asked differentiation question** in KTU Module 4 (often a guaranteed 7-mark sub-question).

| Attribute | **Quality Audit** | **Inspection** | **Surveillance** | **Management Review** |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Purpose** | Verify QMS conformance & effectiveness | Verify product conformance to specs | Continuous monitoring of a certified entity | Review QMS by top management for suitability |
| **Scope** | System / Process | Product only | The certified system's ongoing performance | Entire QMS |
| **Conducted By** | Trained auditors | Inspectors / QC personnel | Certification body (periodic) | Top management |
| **Frequency** | Planned (usually annual) | Every batch / sample | Periodic (e.g., 6/12 months) | At least once per year |
| **Output** | Audit report with NCs | Pass / Fail decision | Continued / Suspended / Withdrawn certification | Review minutes + improvement actions |
| **Focus** | "Are we doing what we said?" | "Is the product good?" | "Are they still compliant?" | "Is the QMS suitable, adequate, effective?" |
| **Standard** | ISO 19011 | Sampling plans (e.g., ANSI/ASQ Z1.4) | ISO 17021, ISO 17065 | ISO 9001 Clause 9.3 |

**Memory Hook:** *Audit = System, Inspection = Product, Surveillance = Continuing compliance, Review = Strategic.*

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Risk-Based Audit Planning (KTU Analytical Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"How is the risk-based approach applied in audit planning? Derive the prioritization formula."*

### Step 1: Define Risk Components
For each process $P_i$ in the QMS, three risk factors are scored on a scale of 1 to 5:

- **Severity (S):** Impact of failure on the customer.
- **Likelihood (L):** Probability of non-conformance occurring.
- **Detectability Gap (D):** Inability of current controls to detect the failure (higher = worse).

### Step 2: Compute Risk Priority Number (RPN) for Each Process

$$
RPN_i = S_i \times L_i \times D_i
$$

### Step 3: Normalize and Rank Processes
Normalize each RPN to a 0–1 scale:

$$
NRPN_i = \dfrac{RPN_i - RPN_{\min}}{RPN_{\max} - RPN_{\min}}
$$

### Step 4: Allocate Audit Duration Proportionally
Let $T_{\text{total}}$ be the total audit time budget. The audit time for process $P_i$ is:

$$
T_i = T_{\text{total}} \times \dfrac{NRPN_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} NRPN_j}
$$

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

Suppose an organization has **3 processes** to audit with the following scores:

| Process | S | L | D | RPN |
|---------|---|---|---|-----|
| P1 (Welding) | 5 | 4 | 3 | 60 |
| P2 (Packaging) | 3 | 2 | 2 | 12 |
| P3 (Dispatch) | 2 | 3 | 1 | 6 |

**Step 5a — Find Max and Min RPN:**

$$
RPN_{\max} = 60, \quad RPN_{\min} = 6
$$

**Step 5b — Normalize:**

$$
NRPN_1 = \frac{60 - 6}{60 - 6} = 1.000
$$

$$
NRPN_2 = \frac{12 - 6}{60 - 6} = \frac{6}{54} = 0.111
$$

$$
NRPN_3 = \frac{6 - 6}{60 - 6} = 0.000
$$

**Step 5c — Sum of NRPNs:**

$$
\sum NRPN = 1.000 + 0.111 + 0.000 = 1.111
$$

**Step 5d — If Total Audit Time = 8 hours, allocate per process:**

$$
T_1 = 8 \times \frac{1.000}{1.111} = 7.20 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
T_2 = 8 \times \frac{0.111}{1.111} = 0.80 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
T_3 = 8 \times \frac{0.000}{1.111} = 0.00 \text{ hours (excluded from this audit cycle)}
$$

**Interpretation:** The high-risk welding process consumes ~90% of audit hours — a textbook application of **risk-based thinking** (Clause 6.1 of ISO 9001:2015).

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Cost of Quality (CoQ) with Audit Component

Juran's classic CoQ equation is the most tested formula in TQM:

$$
CoQ_{\text{Total}} = P_c + A_c + I_c + F_c
$$

**Detailed breakdown:**

- $P_c$ (Prevention Cost) — Cost of activities to *prevent* defects (training, QMS design, FMEA, supplier capability programs).
- $A_c$ (Appraisal Cost) — Cost of *detecting* defects (inspection, testing, **quality audits**, calibration).
- $I_c$ (Internal Failure Cost) — Cost of defects found *before* delivery (rework, scrap, re-inspection).
- $F_c$ (External Failure Cost) — Cost of defects found *after* delivery (warranty, returns, recall, lawsuits).

> **KTU Key Insight:** As prevention cost $P_c$ increases, internal and external failure costs decrease dramatically. The **optimal point** is where the total CoQ curve reaches its minimum. Quality audits, although an *appraisal cost* today, can be reframed as *prevention cost tomorrow* by feeding findings into improvement actions.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Audit Finding Decision Tree (Logic Walkthrough)

A correct response to *"Classify the following finding as Major/Minor NC, Observation, or OFI"* uses this reasoning:

1. **Is there a documented requirement?**
   - **No** → not auditable, but may become an Observation.
   - **Yes** → proceed.
2. **Is there objective evidence of non-fulfillment?**
   - **No** → Conformity.
   - **Yes** → proceed.
3. **Does the issue indicate systemic failure or absence of control?**
   - **Yes** → **Major NC**.
   - **No** → proceed.
4. **Is the non-fulfillment an isolated lapse with documented control?**
   - **Yes** → **Minor NC**.
   - **No** → proceed.
5. **Is there no NC but a noteworthy area?**
   - **Yes** → **Observation** (mandatory) or **OFI** (optional).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Complete Quality Audit Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1 Initiate Audit] --> B[Stage 2 Prepare Audit Activities]
    B --> B1[Document Review]
    B --> B2[Prepare Audit Plan]
    B --> B3[Prepare Checklists]
    B1 --> C[Stage 3 Conduct Audit Onsite]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Opening Meeting]
    C1 --> C2[Collect Evidence Interviews Observation Records]
    C2 --> C3[Generate Findings]
    C3 --> C4{Conformity Check}
    C4 -->|Yes| C5[Mark as Conformity]
    C4 -->|No| C6[Classify NC Major Minor Observation OFI]
    C5 --> C7[Closing Meeting]
    C6 --> C7
    C7 --> D[Stage 4 Prepare Audit Report]
    D --> E[Stage 5 Complete Audit and Distribute Report]
    E --> F[Stage 6 Audit Follow Up]
    F --> F1[Verify Corrective Action]
    F1 --> F2[Verify Effectiveness]
    F2 --> G{All NCs Closed}
    G -->|Yes| H[Audit Cycle Complete]
    G -->|No| I[Escalate to Top Management]
    I --> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Three-Party Audit Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Quality Audits] --> B[First Party Internal Audit]
    A --> C[Second Party Customer Audit]
    A --> D[Third Party Certification Audit]
    B --> B1[Conducted by own staff]
    B --> B2[Purpose Self assessment and continual improvement]
    C --> C1[Conducted by customer]
    C --> C2[Purpose Supplier evaluation and contract compliance]
    D --> D1[Conducted by external certification body]
    D --> D2[Purpose ISO Certification and accreditation]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Audit Findings Classification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Audit Evidence Collected] --> Q1{Is there a requirement?}
    Q1 -->|No| NotAud[Not an audit finding]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Does evidence fulfill requirement?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Conf[Conformity]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Systemic failure or absence of control?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Major[Major Non Conformity]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Isolated lapse with documented control?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Minor[Minor Non Conformity]
    Q4 -->|No| Obs[Observation or OFI]
    Major --> CA1[Immediate Corrective Action Required]
    Minor --> CA2[Corrective Action within time bound]
    Obs --> PA[Improvement Suggestion]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Audit Roles & Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client[Client or Top Management] -->|Mandate| CB[Certification Body]
    CB --> LA[Lead Auditor]
    LA --> AT1[Auditor Team Member 1]
    LA --> AT2[Auditor Team Member 2]
    LA --> AT3[Technical Expert Optional]
    AT1 --> Auditee1[Auditee Department 1]
    AT2 --> Auditee2[Auditee Department 2]
    AT3 --> Auditee3[Auditee Department 3]
    Auditee1 --> Evidence[Audit Evidence Records]
    Auditee2 --> Evidence
    Auditee3 --> Evidence
    Evidence --> Analysis[Evidence Analysis and Triangulation]
    Analysis --> Findings[Audit Findings Conformity NC Observation]
    Findings --> Report[Audit Report]
    Report --> Client
    Report --> CB
    CB --> Decision[Certification Decision Granted Suspended Withheld]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Cost of Quality with Audit Position

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CoQ[Total Cost of Quality] --> Pc[Prevention Cost Pc]
    CoQ --> Ac[Appraisal Cost Ac]
    CoQ --> Ic[Internal Failure Cost Ic]
    CoQ --> Fc[External Failure Cost Fc]
    Ac --> AuditPos[Quality Audits and Inspection Costs]
    Ac --> TestPos[Testing and Calibration Costs]
    Ac --> RevPos[Review of Documents and Records]
    Pc --> Train[Training]
    Pc --> Design[QMS Design and FMEA]
    Pc --> Supp[Supplier Development]
    Ic --> Rework[Rework]
    Ic --> Scrap[Scrap]
    Fc --> Warr[Warranty Claims]
    Fc --> Recall[Product Recall]
    Fc --> Lawsuit[Legal Liabilities]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Quality Audit and list any two objectives of conducting a quality audit in an organization.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Quality Audit** is a systematic, independent, and documented process for obtaining audit evidence and evaluating it objectively to determine the extent to which audit criteria (such as ISO 9001:2015) are fulfilled. *(2 marks)*

**Two objectives:** *(1 mark)*
1. To verify that the QMS conforms to the requirements of the relevant standard.
2. To evaluate the effectiveness of the QMS in achieving stated quality objectives and driving continual improvement.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between First-Party, Second-Party, and Third-Party audits with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Type | Conducted By | Example |
|------|--------------|---------|
| **First-Party Audit** | The organization itself | A company's internal QMS audit conducted by its own quality team before the management review. |
| **Second-Party Audit** | Customer or its representative | An automobile OEM auditing its Tier-1 brake supplier before awarding an annual contract. |
| **Third-Party Audit** | Independent certification body | TUV SUD certifying an IT firm to ISO 9001:2015 standards. *(1 mark each)*
